model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_001",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I scaled the shed and slipped through my window just as dawn began to break. My fresh garments were covered in grease and mud, and I was exhausted.

CHAPTER III.

I received a stern lecture the next morning from Miss Watson regarding my attire; however, the widow refrained from scolding, choosing instead to wipe away the dirt and oil. She appeared so saddened that I felt inclined to try and act properly for a time. Miss Watson then led me into a closet to pray, yet nothing resulted from it. She instructed me to pray daily, promising that whatever I requested would be granted. That was not the case, however. I tested it. I once obtained a fishing line, but lacked the hooks. It was useless without them. I attempted to secure the hooks on several occasions, but it never succeeded. Eventually, I asked Miss Watson to pray on my behalf, but she called me a fool. She never explained why, and I could not fathom the reason.

I sat alone in the woods once and pondered the matter deeply. I reasoned that if a person could receive anything they prayed for, why did Deacon Winn not recover the money he lost on pork? Why could the widow not retrieve her stolen silver snuffbox? Why could Miss Watson not gain weight? No, I concluded, there is nothing to it. I mentioned this to the widow, and she explained that the things one could obtain through prayer were “spiritual gifts.” This was confusing, but she clarified that I must assist others, prioritize their needs constantly, and never consider myself. I assumed this included Miss Watson. I retreated to the woods to contemplate this for a long while, but I could see no benefit in it—except for the other people; so, I finally decided to stop worrying and let it be. Sometimes the widow would pull me aside and speak of Providence in a way that sounded appealing; yet, the following day, Miss Watson would intervene and ruin it all. I figured there must be two Providences, and a poor fellow might fare well with the widow’s version, but if he fell under Miss Watson’s, there was no hope for him. I thought it through and decided I would belong to the widow’s if he wanted me, though I could not see how he would benefit, given how uneducated, low-down, and mean I was.

Father had not been spotted for over a year, which was a relief; I had no desire to see him again. He used to beat me whenever he was sober and could catch me, though I usually hid in the woods whenever he was nearby. Well, around this time, he was discovered drowned in the river, roughly twelve miles upstream, or so the townspeople claimed."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_001",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "I climbed up the shed and crept into my window just before daybreak. My new clothes were covered in grease and clay, and I was exhausted.

CHAPTER III.

Well, Miss Watson gave me a hard time in the morning because of my clothes, but the widow didn’t scold me. She just cleaned off the grease and clay and looked so sad that I decided I would try to behave for a while if I could. Then Miss Watson took me into the closet to pray, but nothing came of it. She told me to pray every day, and that whatever I asked for, I would receive. But that wasn't true. I tried it. Once I got a fishing line, but no hooks. It was useless to me without hooks. I tried for the hooks three or four times, but somehow I couldn't make it work. Eventually, I asked Miss Watson to pray for them for me, but she called me a fool. She never explained why, and I couldn't figure it out at all.

I sat down in the woods one time and had a long think about it. I said to myself, if a person can get anything they pray for, why doesn't Deacon Winn get back the money he lost on pork? Why can't the widow get back her stolen silver snuffbox? Why can't Miss Watson gain some weight? No, I told myself, there is nothing to it. I went and told the widow about it, and she said that the things a person could get by praying were “spiritual gifts.” This was too much for me, but she explained what she meant—I had to help other people, do everything I could for them, look out for them constantly, and never think about myself. I assumed this included Miss Watson. I went out into the woods and turned it over in my mind for a long time, but I couldn't see any advantage to it—except for the other people; so finally, I decided I wouldn't worry about it anymore and just let it go. Sometimes the widow would take me aside and talk about Providence in a way that made it sound appealing, but the next day Miss Watson would step in and ruin it all. I figured there must be two Providences, and a poor guy might have a decent chance with the widow’s Providence, but if Miss Watson’s got hold of him, there was no hope left. I thought it all through and decided I would belong to the widow’s if he wanted me, though I couldn't see how he would be any better off than before, considering how ignorant, low-down, and ornery I was.

My father hadn't been seen for over a year, which was a relief; I didn't want to see him again. He used to beat me whenever he was sober and could get his hands on me, though I usually hid in the woods whenever he was around. Well, around this time, he was found drowned in the river about twelve miles above town, or so people said."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_002",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "At times he thinks he will depart, and then again he believes he will remain. The wisest course is to remain calm and allow the old man to follow his own path. A pair of angels are circling him. One is luminous and white, while the other is dark. The white one encourages him to behave for a short spell, then the black one intervenes and ruins everything. It is impossible to say yet which one will claim him in the end. But you are fine. You are destined to experience significant hardship in your life, along with significant happiness. Occasionally you will be injured, and occasionally you will fall ill; yet each time you will recover. Two women are hovering around your future. One is fair and the other is dark. One is wealthy and the other is destitute. You will wed the poor one first and the rich one later on. You ought to avoid the water as much as possible and take no chances, because it is written in your fate that you are destined to be hanged.

When I ignited my candle and ascended to my chamber that evening, there sat Pap himself!

CHAPTER V.

I had closed the door. Then I pivoted and there he stood. I was habitually terrified of him, as he whipped me so frequently. I assumed I was frightened now, as well; but within a moment I realized I was mistaken—that is, following the initial shock, so to speak, when my breathing faltered because his presence was so unforeseen; but immediately afterward I perceived I was not afraid of him in any meaningful way.

He was nearly fifty, and he appeared every bit of it. His hair was lengthy, matted, and oily, hanging down so that his eyes gleamed through as if he were peering from behind vines. It was entirely black, without a trace of gray; the same was true of his long, tangled beard. There was no color in his complexion, where it was visible; it was white; not like the complexion of any other man, but a white that would sicken a person, a white that would make one’s skin creep—a tree-toad white, a fish-belly white. Regarding his attire—merely tatters, that was all. He had one ankle resting upon the other knee; the boot on that foot was split, and a pair of his toes protruded, which he flexed occasionally. His hat rested upon the floor—a battered black slouch with the crown collapsed, like a lid.

I remained observing him; he sat there observing me, with his chair tipped back slightly. I placed the candle down. I observed the window was raised; so he had climbed in by way of the shed. He continued to examine me from head to toe. Eventually he remarked:

“Fancy clothes—very. You fancy yourself quite the important person, don’t you?”

“Perhaps I am, perhaps I am not,” I replied.

“Do not offer me any of your insolence,” he stated. “You have adopted quite a few airs since I have been gone. I will humble you before I am finished with you. You are schooled, as well, they claim—can read and write. You believe you are superior to your father, now, do you not, because he cannot?”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_002",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Sometimes he thinks he will leave, and then again he thinks he will stay. The best way is to rest easy and let the old man take his own path. There are two angels hovering around him. One of them is white and shiny, and the other is black. The white one gets him to do right for a little while, then the black one swoops in and ruins it all. A person cannot tell yet which one is going to claim him in the end. But you are all right. You are going to have considerable trouble in your life, and considerable joy. Sometimes you are going to get hurt, and sometimes you are going to get sick; but every time you are going to get well again. There are two girls hovering around you in your life. One of them is light and the other is dark. One is rich and the other is poor. You are going to marry the poor one first and the rich one later on. You want to stay away from the water as much as you can, and do not take any risks, because it is written in the cards that you are going to be hanged.

When I lit my candle and went up to my room that night, there sat Pap himself!

CHAPTER V.

I had shut the door. Then I turned around and there he was. I used to be scared of him all the time, because he beat me so much. I figured I was scared now, too; but in a minute I saw I was mistaken—that is, after the first shock, as you might say, when my breath sort of caught, him being so unexpected; but right away after, I saw I was not scared of him worth worrying about.

He was almost fifty, and he looked it. His hair was long, tangled, and greasy, and hung down, and you could see his eyes shining through like he was behind vines. It was all black, no gray; so were his long, matted whiskers. There was no color in his face, where his skin showed; it was white; not like another man’s white, but a white to make a person sick, a white to make a person’s flesh crawl—a tree-toad white, a fish-belly white. As for his clothes—just rags, that was all. He had one ankle resting on the other knee; the boot on that foot was busted, and two of his toes stuck through, and he wiggled them now and then. His hat was lying on the floor—an old black slouch with the top caved in, like a lid.

I stood looking at him; he sat there looking at me, with his chair tilted back a little. I set the candle down. I noticed the window was open; so he had climbed in by the shed. He kept looking me all over. By and by he says:

“Fancy clothes—very. You think you’re a big deal, don’t you?”

“Maybe I am, maybe I ain’t,” I says.

“Don’t you give me any of your lip,” says he. “You’ve put on quite a few airs since I’ve been away. I’ll take you down a peg before I’m done with you. You’re educated, too, they say—can read and write. You think you’re better than your father, now, don’t you, because he can’t?”"
}

The white one gets him to do right for a little while, then the black one sails in and ruins it all. A person cannot tell yet which one is going to claim him in the end. But you are all right. You are going to have considerable trouble in your life, and considerable joy. Sometimes you are going to get hurt, and sometimes you are going to get sick; but every time you are going to get well again. There are two girls flying about you in your life. One of them is light and the other one is dark. One is rich and the other is poor. You are going to marry the poor one first and the rich one later on. You want to keep away from the water as much as you can, and do not take any risks, because it is written in the cards that you are going to be hanged.

When I lit my candle and went up to my room that night, there sat Pap himself!

CHAPTER V.

I had shut the door. Then I turned around and there he was. I used to be scared of him all the time, because he beat me so much. I thought I was scared now, too; but in a minute I saw I was mistaken—that is, after the first shock, when my breath sort of hitched because he was so unexpected. But right away after that, I saw I was not scared of him worth worrying about.

He was almost fifty, and he looked it. His hair was long, tangled, and greasy, and hung down, and you could see his eyes shining through like he was behind vines. It was all black, with no gray; so were his long, messy whiskers. There was no color in his face where it showed; it was white, not like another man’s white, but a white to make a person sick, a white to make a person’s flesh crawl—a tree-toad white, a fish-belly white. As for his clothes, they were just rags. He had one ankle resting on the other knee; the boot on that foot was broken, and two of his toes stuck through, and he wiggled them now and then. His hat was lying on the floor—an old black slouch hat with the top caved in.

I stood looking at him; he sat there looking at me, with his chair tilted back a little. I set the candle down. I noticed the window was open; so he had climbed in by the shed. He kept looking me all over. By and by he says:

“Fancy clothes—very. You think you are a big deal, don’t you?”

“Maybe I am, maybe I am not,” I says.

“Don’t you give me any of your lip,” says he. “You have put on quite a few airs since I have been away. I will take you down a peg before I am done with you. You are educated, too, they say—can read and write. You think you are better than your father now, don’t you, because he cannot?”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_003",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I anticipated finding someone lying inside, as individuals frequently acted that way to deceive others, popping up to mock any fellow who rowed a boat out to investigate. However, that was not the case here. It was truly a drifting canoe, so I climbed aboard and rowed it to the bank. I figured my father would be pleased to see it, as it was valued at ten dollars. Yet, when I reached the shore, my father was nowhere to be seen. As I maneuvered the craft into a small, vine-and-willow-covered creek, a new thought occurred: I decided to conceal it well. Instead of fleeing into the woods when I eventually ran away, I would travel fifty miles downriver, settle in one spot, and avoid the hardship of traveling on foot.

It was quite near the cabin, and I constantly imagined I heard my father approaching, but I managed to hide the boat. I stepped out and peered around some willow bushes to see him down the path, aiming his rifle at a bird. He had not noticed anything.

By the time he arrived, I was busy retrieving a trotline. He scolded me briefly for my sluggishness, but I claimed I had fallen into the river, which explained my delay. I knew he would notice my wet clothes and start questioning me. We collected five catfish from the lines and returned home.

While we rested after breakfast to recover our strength, both of us being exhausted, I pondered that if I could devise a method to prevent my father and the widow from pursuing me, it would be more reliable than relying on luck to get far away before they noticed my absence; after all, anything could happen. I saw no solution for a while, but eventually, my father sat up to drink more water and remarked, "Next time a man comes prowling around here, you wake me up, you hear? That fellow was up to no good. I would have shot him. Next time, you wake me up, you hear?"

He then lay back down and resumed sleeping, but his words provided the exact idea I needed. I told myself that I could now arrange things so that no one would consider chasing me.

Around noon, we got up and walked along the riverbank. The water was rising rapidly, carrying plenty of driftwood. Soon, a section of a log raft appeared, consisting of nine logs lashed together. We took the skiff out and towed it to shore. Then we ate dinner. Anyone other than my father would have waited to see what else the day might bring, but that was not his way. Nine logs sufficed; he had to head straight to town to sell them. He locked me inside, took the skiff, and departed with the raft in tow around three-thirty. I concluded he would not return that night."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_003",

  "rewritten_text": "I expected someone might be lying inside, as people often did that to trick others, and when a guy rowed a skiff out to it, they would sit up and laugh at him. But that wasn't the case this time. It was definitely a stray canoe, so I climbed in and paddled it to the bank. I thought my father would be happy when he saw it, since it was worth ten dollars. But when I reached the shore, my father was nowhere to be seen. As I was steering it into a small creek that looked like a gully, covered in vines and willows, I had another idea: I decided I would hide it well, and then, instead of heading into the woods when I ran away, I would go down the river about fifty miles and camp in one spot permanently, avoiding the hardship of traveling on foot.

It was quite near the shack, and I kept thinking I heard my father coming, but I got the canoe hidden. Then I stepped out and looked around a cluster of willows, and there was my father down the path, aiming his gun at a bird. So, he hadn't seen anything.

When he arrived, I was busy pulling up a trotline. He scolded me a bit for being so slow, but I told him I had fallen into the river, which was why I took so long. I knew he would see I was wet and start asking questions. We pulled five catfish off the lines and went home.

While we rested after breakfast to catch up on sleep, both of us being exhausted, I started thinking that if I could find a way to keep my father and the widow from trying to track me down, it would be more certain than relying on luck to get far enough away before they noticed I was gone; after all, anything could happen. I didn't see a way for a while, but eventually, my father sat up for a moment to drink more water and said, "Next time a man comes prowling around here, you wake me up, you hear? That man wasn't here for any good reason. I would have shot him. Next time, you wake me up, you hear?"

Then he lay back down and went to sleep again, but what he said gave me the exact idea I needed. I told myself I could arrange things now so that nobody would think of following me.

Around noon, we got up and walked along the bank. The river was rising quickly, and plenty of driftwood was floating by. Eventually, a section of a log raft came along—nine logs tied together. We went out in the skiff and towed it ashore. Then we had dinner. Anyone else but my father would have waited and spent the day there to catch more items, but that wasn't his style. Nine logs were enough for one time; he had to head straight to town to sell them. So, he locked me in, took the skiff, and left, towing the raft around three-thirty. I figured he wouldn't be back that night."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_004",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Then a thought occurred to me. I figured that the widow, the minister, or someone else must have prayed for this bread to locate me, and it had actually succeeded. Clearly, there is some truth to that practice—at least when someone like the widow or the minister prays; it does not seem to work for me, and I suspect it only functions for the right sort of person.

I ignited my pipe, enjoyed a lengthy smoke, and continued my observation. The ferry was drifting with the river, and I anticipated a chance to identify the passengers as it passed, since it would steer near the spot where the bread had floated. Once it drew reasonably close, I extinguished my pipe, moved to the location where I had retrieved the bread, and stretched out behind a log in a small clearing. Through a gap in the log’s branches, I could peer out.

Eventually, the boat arrived, drifting so near that they could have extended a gangplank and stepped onto the bank. Nearly everyone was aboard: Pap, Judge Thatcher, Bessie Thatcher, Jo Harper, Tom Sawyer, his elderly Aunt Polly, Sid, Mary, and many others. Everyone was discussing the murder, but the captain interrupted, saying, "Keep a sharp lookout; the current pulls in closest here, and perhaps he has washed up and become caught in the brush along the bank. I certainly hope so."

I did not share that hope. They all gathered at the rails, leaning over almost directly above me, remaining silent and watching intently. I had a clear view of them, but they could not spot me. Then the captain shouted, "Stand back!" and the cannon fired a blast so close that the sound deafened me and the smoke nearly blinded me, leading me to believe I was finished. Had they loaded it with bullets, I suspect they would have secured the corpse they were seeking. Fortunately, I realized I was unharmed. The boat drifted onward, disappearing around the island’s bend. I could hear the distant booming for a while, but after an hour, it ceased entirely. The island stretched for three miles. I assumed they had reached the end and were abandoning the search. However, they persisted a while longer. They circled the foot of the island and began steaming up the channel along the Missouri side, firing occasionally as they traveled. I crossed to that side to watch them. Once they were level with the head of the island, they stopped firing, steered toward the Missouri shore, and returned to town.

I knew I was safe at last. No one else would come searching for me. I retrieved my belongings from the canoe and established a comfortable camp within the dense forest. I fashioned a makeshift tent from my blankets to shelter my gear from the rain. I caught a catfish, cleaned it with my saw, and as evening approached, I lit my campfire and ate my supper."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_004",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Then it hit me. I thought, I bet the widow or the preacher or someone prayed that this bread would find me, and here it actually did. So there is no doubt there is something to that—at least, there is something to it when a person like the widow or the preacher prays, but it doesn’t work for me, and I figure it only works for the right kind of people.

I lit a pipe, had a long smoke, and kept watching. The ferryboat was drifting with the current, and I figured I would have a chance to see who was on board when it passed, because it would come in close, right where the bread had. When it got pretty far down toward me, I put out my pipe and went to where I had fished out the bread, lying down behind a log on the bank in a small clearing. I could peek through the fork in the log.

Before long, the boat came along, drifting in so close they could have run out a plank and walked ashore. Almost everyone was on the boat: Pap, Judge Thatcher, Bessie Thatcher, Jo Harper, Tom Sawyer, his old Aunt Polly, Sid, Mary, and plenty more. Everyone was talking about the murder, but the captain interrupted and said, “Look sharp, now; the current pulls in closest here, and maybe he’s washed ashore and gotten tangled in the brush at the water’s edge. I hope so, anyway.”

I didn’t hope so. They all crowded up and leaned over the rails, nearly in my face, and stayed quiet, watching with all their might. I could see them perfectly, but they couldn’t see me. Then the captain shouted, “Stand back!” and the cannon fired such a blast right in front of me that it made me deaf from the noise and nearly blind from the smoke, and I thought I was done for. If they had loaded it with bullets, I figure they would have gotten the corpse they were after. Well, I saw I wasn’t hurt, thank goodness. The boat floated on and went out of sight around the bend of the island. I could hear the booming now and then, further and further away, and eventually, after an hour, I didn’t hear it anymore. The island was three miles long. I figured they had reached the end and were giving up. But they didn’t stop yet. They turned around the foot of the island and started up the channel on the Missouri side, under steam, firing once in a while as they went. I crossed over to that side and watched them. When they got level with the head of the island, they stopped shooting, headed over to the Missouri shore, and went home to town.

I knew I was safe now. Nobody else would come hunting for me. I got my gear out of the canoe and set up a nice camp in the thick woods. I made a kind of tent out of my blankets to put my things under so the rain couldn’t reach them. I caught a catfish and hacked it open with my saw, and toward sundown, I started my campfire and had supper."
}

Everyone was talking about the murder, but the captain interrupted and said, “Look sharp, now; the current pulls in closest here, and maybe he has washed ashore and gotten tangled in the brush at the water’s edge. I hope so, anyway.”

I certainly did not hope so. They all crowded up and leaned over the rails, nearly in my face, and stayed quiet, watching with all their might. I could see them perfectly, but they could not see me. Then the captain shouted, “Stand away!” and the cannon fired such a blast right in front of me that it made me deaf and nearly blinded me with smoke. I thought I was done for. If they had put bullets in, I believe they would have gotten the corpse they were looking for. Fortunately, I realized I was not hurt. The boat floated on and went out of sight around the bend of the island. I could hear the booming now and then, further and further away, and after an hour, I heard it no more. The island was three miles long. I figured they had reached the end and were giving up. But they did not stop yet. They turned around the foot of the island and started up the channel on the Missouri side, using steam and firing the cannon occasionally. I crossed over to that side and watched them. When they were level with the head of the island, they stopped shooting, headed toward the Missouri shore, and went back to town.

I knew I was safe now. No one else would come hunting for me. I took my belongings out of the canoe and set up a nice camp in the thick woods. I made a tent out of my blankets to keep my things dry from the rain. I caught a catfish and cut it open with my saw, and toward sundown, I started my campfire and had supper."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_005",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I figured I could land almost anywhere, but the banks were too steep. I was nearly at the island's end before I discovered a suitable spot. I retreated into the forest, deciding to avoid rafts since they were moving the lantern about. I had my pipe, a piece of tobacco, and matches tucked in my cap, which stayed dry, so I was set.

“So you haven’t had any bread or meat this whole time? Why didn’t you catch mud-turtles?”

“How would you manage that? You can’t sneak up and snatch them, and how could anyone hit them with a stone? How could a person do that in the dark? Besides, I wasn’t about to reveal myself on the shore during the day.”

“That’s true. You had to stay hidden in the trees, naturally. Did you hear them firing the cannon?”

“Oh, yes. I knew they were hunting you. I saw them pass by here, watching through the brush.”

Some young birds appeared, fluttering a few feet at a time before landing. Jim claimed it indicated rain was coming. He noted that young chickens acted that way, so he assumed it meant the same for wild birds. I intended to capture a few, but Jim forbade it, calling it a death omen. He explained that when his father was gravely ill, someone caught a bird, and his grandmother predicted his father would pass away, which is exactly what happened.

Jim also warned against counting items intended for dinner, as it invited misfortune. The same applied to shaking a tablecloth after sunset. He added that if a beekeeper passed away, the bees had to be informed before dawn, or they would grow weak, stop working, and perish. Jim insisted bees wouldn’t sting fools, though I doubted that, having tested it myself many times without getting stung.

I had heard of some of these omens, but not all. Jim was an expert on signs and claimed to know nearly everything. I remarked that most signs seemed to involve bad luck, so I asked if there were any for good fortune. He replied:

“Very few, and they aren’t much help. Why would you want to know when good luck is arriving? To keep it away?” He continued, “If you have hair on your arms and chest, it’s a sign you’ll be wealthy. Now, that’s a useful sign because it looks so far ahead. You see, you might be poor for a long time and get discouraged enough to end your life if you didn’t have the sign to know you’d be rich eventually.”

“Do you have hairy arms and a hairy chest, Jim?”

“Why ask that? Can’t you see for yourself?”

“Well, are you rich?”

“No, but I was wealthy once, and I will be wealthy again.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_005",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "I thought I could land almost anywhere, but I couldn’t because the bank was too steep. I was nearly at the foot of the island before I found a good spot. I went into the woods and decided I wouldn’t mess with rafts anymore, not while they were moving the lantern around like that. I had my pipe, a plug of tobacco, and some matches in my cap, and since they weren’t wet, I was doing fine.

“So you haven’t had any meat or bread to eat this whole time? Why didn’t you catch mud turtles?”

“How are you going to catch them? You can’t sneak up on them and grab them, and how is a person supposed to hit them with a rock? How could anyone do that at night? Besides, I wasn’t going to show myself on the bank during the day.”

“Well, that’s true. You’ve had to stay in the woods the whole time, of course. Did you hear them firing the cannon?”

“Oh, yes. I knew they were after you. I saw them go by here and watched them through the bushes.”

Some young birds came along, flying a yard or two at a time and landing. Jim said it was a sign that it was going to rain. He said it was a sign when young chickens flew that way, so he figured it was the same when young birds did it. I was going to catch some of them, but Jim wouldn’t let me. He said it meant death. He said his father was once very sick, and some people caught a bird, and his grandmother said his father would die, and he did.

Jim also said you shouldn’t count the things you’re going to cook for dinner because that brings bad luck. It’s the same if you shake the tablecloth after sunset. He said if a man owned a beehive and died, the bees had to be told about it before sunrise the next morning, or else they would weaken, stop working, and die. Jim said bees wouldn’t sting idiots, but I didn’t believe that because I had tested them many times myself, and they wouldn’t sting me.

I had heard about some of these things before, but not all of them. Jim knew all kinds of signs. He said he knew almost everything. I mentioned that it seemed like all the signs were about bad luck, so I asked him if there weren’t any good-luck signs. He replied:

“Very few, and they aren’t any use to a person. Why would you want to know when good luck is coming? Do you want to keep it away?” He added, “If you have hairy arms and a hairy chest, it’s a sign that you’re going to be rich. Now, there’s some use in a sign like that, because it’s so far in the future. You see, you might be poor for a long time first, and you could get discouraged and kill yourself if you didn’t know by the sign that you were going to be rich eventually.”

“Do you have hairy arms and a hairy chest, Jim?”

“What’s the point of asking that? Don’t you see that I do?”

“Well, are you rich?”

“No, but I was rich once, and I’m going to be rich again.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_006",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“You ought to remain here for the night. Remove your bonnet.”

“No,” I replied; “I believe I will just rest briefly and continue on. I am not frightened of the darkness.”

She insisted she would not permit me to travel alone, but mentioned her husband would return shortly, perhaps in ninety minutes, and she would have him accompany me. She then began discussing her spouse, her kin residing upriver and downriver, and how much more prosperous they had been previously, and how they questioned if moving to our town had been an error rather than leaving well enough alone—and she continued on in that fashion until I worried that I had made a mistake visiting her to discover the local news; however, eventually she shifted the conversation to my father and the killing, and then I was quite content to let her chatter away. She recounted the story of Tom Sawyer and me discovering the six thousand dollars (though she inflated it to ten) and spoke at length about my father and what a rough character he was, and what a rough character I was, until she finally reached the subject of my own murder. I asked:

“Who committed it? We have heard quite a bit regarding these events down in Hookerville, but we do not know who it was that killed Huck Finn.”

“Well, I imagine there are a great many people here who would like to know who killed him. Some suspect old Finn did it himself.”

“No—is that true?”

“Nearly everyone believed that initially. He will never realize how close he came to being lynched. But before evening, they shifted their opinions and decided it was the work of a runaway slave named Jim.”

“Why, he—”

I paused. I decided it was wiser to remain silent. She continued speaking, never noticing that I had interjected at all:

“The slave fled the very evening Huck Finn was killed. Consequently, there is a reward offered for him—three hundred dollars. And there is a reward for old Finn as well—two hundred dollars. You see, he arrived in town the morning following the murder, spoke about it, and joined the search party on the ferry-boat, and immediately afterward he departed. Before nightfall, they intended to lynch him, but he had vanished, you see. Well, the following day they discovered the slave was missing; they learned he had not been seen since ten o’clock the night the murder occurred. So then they blamed him, you see; and while they were preoccupied with that, the next day, old Finn returned and went crying to Judge Thatcher to obtain funds to search for the slave throughout Illinois. The judge provided him some, and that evening he became intoxicated and spent time past midnight with a pair of very rough-looking strangers, then left with them. Well, he has not returned since, and they are not expecting him back until this situation settles down somewhat, for people now believe he killed his son and arranged matters so others would assume robbers were responsible, allowing him to claim Huck’s money without enduring a lengthy legal battle. People certainly say he was capable of such a thing.”"
}

Then she started talking about her husband, her relatives up the river and down the river, how much better off they used to be, and how they weren’t sure if they had made a mistake moving to our town instead of leaving well enough alone—and on and on, until I was afraid I had made a mistake coming to her to find out what was happening in town. But eventually, she got around to talking about my father and the murder, and then I was perfectly happy to let her keep talking. She told me about Tom Sawyer and me finding the six thousand dollars (though she exaggerated it to ten) and all about my father and what a rough character he was, and what a rough character I was, and finally she got to the part about me being murdered. I said:

“Who did it? We’ve heard quite a bit about these events down in Hookerville, but we don’t know who killed Huck Finn.”

“Well, I imagine there are plenty of people here who would like to know who killed him. Some think old Finn did it himself.”

“No—is that so?”

“Almost everyone thought so at first. He’ll never know how close he came to being lynched. But before nightfall, they changed their minds and decided it was done by a runaway slave named Jim.”

“Why, he—”

I stopped. I figured I had better keep quiet. She kept talking and never noticed I had said anything at all:

“The slave ran off the very night Huck Finn was killed. So there is a reward out for him—three hundred dollars. And there is a reward out for old Finn, too—two hundred dollars. You see, he came to town the morning after the murder, told everyone about it, and joined them on the ferry-boat search, and then immediately left. Before night, they wanted to lynch him, but he was already gone. Well, the next day they found out the slave was gone; they discovered he hadn’t been seen since ten o’clock the night the murder happened. So then they blamed him, you see; and while they were obsessed with that the next day, old Finn came back and went crying to Judge Thatcher to get money to hunt for the slave all over Illinois. The judge gave him some, and that evening he got drunk and was out past midnight with a couple of very rough-looking strangers, and then left with them. Well, he hasn’t come back since, and they aren’t expecting him back until this whole thing blows over, because people now think he killed his boy and staged it to look like robbers did it, so he could get Huck’s money without having to deal with a long lawsuit. People say he was certainly capable of doing it.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_007",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Each evening around ten o’clock, I would sneak to the bank near some small town to purchase a few cents’ worth of bacon, meal, or other provisions. Occasionally, I would also snatch a chicken that seemed to be resting poorly. My father always insisted that one should grab a chicken whenever possible, arguing that if you didn’t need it, someone else surely would, and a kind act is never forgotten. I never knew my father to turn down a chicken for himself, but that was his usual justification.

Before dawn, I would creep into fields to take a pumpkin, a watermelon, a cantaloupe, or some fresh corn. My father claimed it was not wrong to borrow items if you intended to pay for them eventually, though the widow maintained that was merely a polite term for theft and that no respectable person would engage in it. Jim suggested that both the widow and my father were partially correct, so we decided the best approach was to select a few items from our list to stop taking, which would make borrowing the rest acceptable. We spent an entire night drifting down the river debating whether to give up watermelons, cantaloupes, or other produce. By morning, we reached a satisfactory agreement to stop taking persimmons and crabapples. We felt uneasy before this decision, but afterwards, we were at ease. I was pleased with the outcome, as crabapples are never tasty, and the persimmons would not be ready for several months.

Occasionally, we would shoot a waterfowl that rose too early or stayed out too late. All things considered, we were eating quite well.

On the fifth night past St. Louis, a severe storm struck after midnight, bringing intense thunder and lightning while rain fell in heavy sheets. We remained inside our shelter and allowed the raft to drift. Whenever lightning illuminated the scene, we could see a wide, straight stretch of river flanked by tall, rocky cliffs. Eventually, I called out, “Look there, Jim!” It was a steamboat that had wrecked against a rock, and we were drifting directly toward it. The lightning revealed the vessel clearly; it was tilted, with a portion of the upper deck above the surface. During the flashes, I could see every detail, including the chimney stays and a chair near the large bell with an old slouch hat draped over it.

Given the late hour, the storm, and the general sense of mystery, I felt exactly as any boy would upon seeing that mournful, lonely wreck sitting in the middle of the river. I wanted to board her and explore a bit. I said, “Let’s land on her, Jim.”

Jim was strongly opposed at first. He replied, “I don’t want to go fooling around any wreck.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_007",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Every night now, I would sneak ashore around ten o’clock at some small village to buy ten or fifteen cents’ worth of cornmeal, bacon, or other food. Sometimes I would grab a chicken that wasn’t roosting comfortably and take it with me. My father always said to take a chicken whenever you had the chance, because if you didn’t want it yourself, you could easily find someone who did, and a good deed is never forgotten. I never saw my father when he didn’t want the chicken himself, but that is what he used to say anyway.

In the mornings before dawn, I would slip into cornfields and borrow a watermelon, a muskmelon, a pumpkin, some fresh corn, or things like that. My father always said it wasn’t wrong to borrow items if you intended to pay them back eventually, but the widow insisted that was just a polite term for stealing and that no decent person would do it. Jim thought the widow was partly right and my father was partly right, so he suggested the best approach would be for us to pick two or three items from our list and agree not to borrow them anymore, which he figured would make it acceptable to borrow the rest. We talked it over all night while drifting down the river, trying to decide whether to stop taking watermelons, cantaloupes, muskmelons, or something else. By daybreak, we had settled it to our satisfaction and decided to stop taking crabapples and persimmons. We hadn’t felt quite right before that, but everything was comfortable now. I was happy with the outcome, too, because crabapples are never good, and the persimmons wouldn’t be ripe for another two or three months.

We occasionally shot a waterfowl that got up too early in the morning or didn’t go to bed early enough in the evening. All things considered, we lived quite well.

On the fifth night past St. Louis, we hit a massive storm after midnight with intense thunder and lightning, and the rain poured down in a solid sheet. We stayed inside the shelter and let the raft take care of itself. When the lightning flashed, we could see a wide, straight stretch of river ahead with high, rocky bluffs on both sides. Eventually, I said, “Hello, Jim, look over there!” It was a steamboat that had wrecked on a rock. We were drifting straight toward it. The lightning revealed it very clearly. It was leaning over with part of its upper deck above water, and you could see every little chimney wire distinctly, along with a chair by the big bell with an old slouch hat hanging on the back of it whenever the flashes occurred.

Well, since it was late at night, stormy, and all so mysterious, I felt just the way any other boy would have felt seeing that wreck lying there so mournful and lonely in the middle of the river. I wanted to get on board, sneak around a bit, and see what was there. So I said, “Let’s land on her, Jim.”

But Jim was completely against it at first. He said, “I don’t want to go fooling around any wreck.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_008",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "He remarks:

“I didn’t realize there were so many of them. I’ve hardly heard of any, except for old King Solomon, unless you count the kings found in a deck of cards. How much money does a king earn?”

“Earn?” I reply; “why, they receive a thousand dollars monthly if they choose; they can take as much as they desire; everything is their property.”

“Isn’t that grand? And what are they required to do, Huck?”

“They don’t do anything! Honestly, what are you saying? They simply lounge about.”

“No; is that the truth?”

“Certainly it is. They just sit around—except, perhaps, when there is a war; then they head off to battle. But otherwise they just idle away; or go falconry—just hunting and sp— Shh!—did you hear a sound?”

We hurried out to investigate; but it was nothing more than the splashing of a steamboat’s paddle wheel far down the river, rounding the bend; so we returned.

“Yes,” I continue, “and at other times, when things are boring, they argue with the parliament; and if everyone doesn’t act exactly as they wish, they chop their heads off. But mostly they spend their time in the harem.”

“In the what?”

“Harem.”

“What is a harem?”

“The location where he houses his wives. Don’t you know about a harem? Solomon possessed one; he had about a million wives.”

“Why, yes, that’s true; I—I had forgotten. A harem is a boarding-house, I suppose. Most likely they have noisy times in the nursery. And I imagine the wives argue quite a bit; and that increases the commotion. Yet they claim Solomon was the wisest man who ever lived. I don’t believe that. Because why: would a wise man choose to live in the middle of such a racket all the time? No—indeed he wouldn’t. A wise man would go and build a boiler factory; and then he could shut down the boiler factory whenever he wanted to rest.”

“Well, but he was the wisest man, regardless; because the widow told me so, herself.”

“I don’t care what the widow says, he wasn’t a wise man at all. He had some of the most ridiculous habits I’ve ever seen. Do you know about that child he was going to cut in two?”

“Yes, the widow told me the whole story.”

“Well, then! Wasn’t that the most absurd idea in the world? You just take and consider it for a moment. There is the stump, there—that is one of the women; here is you—that is the other one; I am Solomon; and this dollar bill is the child. Both of you claim it. What do I do? Do I go around among the neighbors and discover which one the bill belongs to, and hand it over to the rightful owner, safely, the way anyone with any sense would? No; I take and chop the bill in two, and give half of it to you, and the other half to the other woman. That is how Solomon intended to treat the child. Now I want to ask you: what is the use of that half a bill?—you can’t buy anything with it. And what use is half a child? I wouldn’t give a damn for a million of them.”

“But hang it, Jim, you’ve completely missed the point—darn it, you’ve missed it by a thousand miles.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_009",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“I am, am I? Well, tell me this: didn’t you carry the rope out in the canoe to tie it to the sandbar?”

“No, I didn’t. What sandbar? I haven’t seen any sandbar.”

“You haven’t seen any sandbar? Listen here, didn’t the line snap and the raft go rushing down the river, leaving you and the canoe behind in the mist?”

“What mist?”

“Why, the mist! The mist that has been around all night. And didn’t you shout, and didn’t I shout, until we got confused among the islands and one of us got lost, and the other was just as good as lost because he didn’t know where he was? And didn’t I crash into a bunch of those islands and have a terrible time and nearly drown? Now isn’t that true, boss—isn’t it? You answer me that.”

“Well, this is beyond me, Jim. I haven’t seen any mist, or any islands, or any troubles, or anything. I’ve been sitting here talking with you all night until you fell asleep about ten minutes ago, and I suppose I did the same. You couldn’t have gotten drunk in that time, so naturally you’ve been dreaming.”

“Confound it, how am I going to dream all that in ten minutes?”

“Well, hang it all, you did dream it, because none of it actually occurred.”

“But, Huck, it’s all just as clear to me as—”

“It doesn’t matter how clear it is; there’s nothing to it. I know, because I’ve been here the whole time.”

Jim didn’t say anything for about five minutes, but sat there pondering it. Then he says:

“Well, then, I suppose I did dream it, Huck; but I’ll be darned if it isn’t the most intense dream I’ve ever had. And I’ve never had a dream before that exhausted me like this one.”

“Oh, well, that’s fine, because a dream can tire a person out completely sometimes. But this was a remarkable dream; tell me the whole thing, Jim.”

So Jim began and told me the entire story exactly as it occurred, though he embellished it quite a bit. Then he said he had to begin to “interpret” it, because it was sent as a warning. He explained that the first sandbar represented a man who would try to help us, but the current was another man who would pull us away from him. The shouts were warnings that would come to us occasionally, and if we didn’t try hard to comprehend them, they would lead us into misfortune instead of keeping us safe. The group of sandbars were troubles we would encounter with argumentative people and all sorts of nasty folks, but if we kept to ourselves and didn’t talk back or provoke them, we would survive and emerge from the mist into the large, clear river, which was the free States, and wouldn’t have any more difficulties.

It had grown quite dark just after I boarded the raft, but it was brightening up again now.

“Oh, well, that’s all interpreted well enough as far as it goes, Jim,” I say; “but what do these things represent?”"
}

“Why, the fog! The fog that’s been around all night. “Well, hang it all, you did dream it, because none of it happened.”

“But, Huck, it’s all just as clear to me as—”

“It doesn’t make any difference how clear it is; there’s nothing to it. I know, because I’ve been here the whole time.”

It had clouded up pretty dark just after I got onto the raft, but it was clearing up again now.

“Oh, well, that’s all interpreted well enough as far as it goes, Jim,” I says; “but what do these things stand for?”"
}

"Well, hang it all, you did dream it, because none of it happened."

"But, Huck, it is all just as clear to me as—"

"It doesn't make any difference how clear it is; there is nothing to it. I know, because I have been here the whole time."

Jim didn't say anything for about five minutes, but sat there thinking it over. Then he said:

"Well, then, I suppose I did dream it, Huck; but I'll be surprised if it isn't the most powerful dream I ever had. And I have never had a dream before that tired me out like this one."

"Oh, well, that is all right, because a dream does tire a person out sometimes. But this one was a remarkable dream; tell me all about it, Jim."

So Jim went to work and told me the whole thing from start to finish, just as it happened, only he added quite a bit to it. Then he said he must start in and interpret it, because it was sent as a warning. He said the first sandbar stood for a man who would try to do us some good, but the current was another man who would get us away from him. The shouts were warnings that would come to us every now and then, and if we didn't try hard to understand them, they would just lead us into bad luck instead of keeping us out of it. The group of sandbars represented troubles we were going to get into with quarrelsome people and all kinds of mean folks, but if we minded our own business and didn't talk back or aggravate them, we would pull through and get out of the fog and into the big clear river, which was the free States, and wouldn't have any more trouble.

It had clouded up pretty dark just after I got onto the raft, but it was clearing up again now.

"Oh, well, that is all interpreted well enough as far as it goes, Jim," I said; "but what do these things stand for?""
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_010",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "We pushed off into the darkness on our raft. Anyone who still doubts the folly of handling a snakeskin, after all the trouble it caused us, will surely be convinced after reading what happened next. The best way to acquire a canoe is from rafts moored along the bank, but we found none, so we drifted for over three hours. The night turned gray and hazy, which is nearly as difficult as fog, making it impossible to discern the river's contours or see very far. It grew late and silent until a steamboat approached. We ignited our lantern, assuming they would spot us. Usually, upstream vessels avoid us by following the sandbars and seeking calmer water near the reefs, but on nights like this, they plow straight up the main channel.

We heard her heavy thumping before we could clearly see her. She headed directly toward us. Steamboats often do this to see how close they can get without making contact; sometimes the paddlewheel clips an oar, and the pilot just leans out to laugh, feeling quite clever. This one approached rapidly, and we realized she intended to graze us, yet she showed no sign of veering away. She was a massive vessel moving at high speed, resembling a dark cloud rimmed with glowing lights. Suddenly, she loomed large and terrifying, her furnace doors gaping like red-hot teeth, her immense bow and guards hanging directly above us. A shout rang out, followed by the jingling of engine-room bells, a flurry of swearing, and the hiss of escaping steam. As Jim leaped off one side and I off the other, she crashed directly through our raft.

I dove deep, intending to reach the riverbed, as I needed to ensure the thirty-foot paddlewheel had ample space to pass over me. I could typically hold my breath for a minute, but this time I suspect I stayed under for ninety seconds. Then, feeling as though I might burst, I shot toward the surface. I broke through up to my armpits, cleared the water from my nose, and gasped for air. Naturally, the current was powerful, and the steamboat resumed its engines only ten seconds after stopping, as they had little regard for those on rafts. She churned away upstream, vanishing into the gloom, though I could still hear her.

I called out for Jim repeatedly, but received no reply. I grabbed a passing plank while treading water and began pushing it toward the bank. Realizing the current was pulling toward the left, indicating I was in a river crossing, I adjusted my course accordingly."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_011",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "One depicted a girl at a window gazing at the moon, tears streaming down her face; she held an open letter marked with black wax, pressing a chained locket to her lips, while the caption read, “And Art Thou Gone Yes Thou Art Gone Alas.” These were fine drawings, I suppose, yet they never appealed to me, as they gave me the jitters whenever I felt low. Everyone mourned her passing, for she had planned many more such works, and one could tell by her existing efforts what had been lost. Still, I figured that with her temperament, she was likely better off in the cemetery. She was laboring on what was considered her masterpiece when she fell ill, praying daily and nightly to survive until its completion, but that opportunity never arrived. It showed a girl in a long white dress standing on a bridge railing, prepared to leap, her hair loose, looking moonward with tears on her cheeks; she had two arms crossed over her chest, two extended forward, and two more reaching toward the moon—the plan was to determine which pair looked best and erase the rest; but, as I mentioned, she passed away before deciding, and now they kept the painting above her bed, adorning it with flowers each birthday. Otherwise, it remained hidden behind a small curtain. The girl in the image had a pleasant, sweet face, but the abundance of arms made her appear too much like a spider, in my opinion.

This girl maintained a scrapbook during her life, pasting in obituaries, accounts of accidents, and tales of suffering clipped from the Presbyterian Observer, accompanying them with original poetry. It was quite good verse. Here is what she composed regarding a boy named Stephen Dowling Bots who tumbled into a well and drowned:

ODE TO STEPHEN DOWLING BOTS, DEC’D

Did young Stephen grow ill,
 And did young Stephen die?
Did sorrowful hearts fill,
 And did the mourners cry?

No; such was not the destiny of
 Young Stephen Dowling Bots;
Though sorrowful hearts filled around him,
 It was not from sickness’ shots.

No whooping-cough racked his body,
 Nor dreary measles with spots;
Not these tarnished the sacred name
 Of Stephen Dowling Bots.

Rejected love brought no misery
 To that head of curly knots,
Nor did stomach ailments lay him low,
 Young Stephen Dowling Bots.

O no. Then listen with tearful eye,
 While I recount his fate.
His soul departed this cold world
 By falling down a well.

They retrieved him and drained him;
 Alas, it was too late;
His spirit had gone to frolic above
 In the realms of the good and great."
}

parts.text:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_011",
  "condition": "simplify",
  "rewritten_text": "There was one picture of a young lady at a window looking up at the moon with tears running down her cheeks. She held an open letter with black sealing wax on the edge, and she was pressing a locket on a chain against her mouth. Underneath the picture, it said, “And Art Thou Gone? Yes, Thou Art Gone, Alas.” These were nice pictures, I suppose, but I did not really like them because they always gave me the jitters whenever I felt a little sad. Everyone was sorry she died because she had planned many more of these pictures, and you could see by her work what they had lost. However, I figured that with her personality, she was having a better time in the graveyard. She was working on what they called her greatest picture when she got sick. Every day and night, she prayed to live long enough to finish it, but she never got the chance. It was a picture of a young woman in a long white gown standing on a bridge railing, ready to jump. Her hair was hanging down her back, and she was looking up at the moon with tears on her face. She had two arms folded across her chest, two arms stretched out in front, and two more reaching up toward the moon. The idea was to see which pair looked best and then erase the others, but as I said, she died before she could decide. Now, they kept this picture over the head of her bed, and they hung flowers on it every birthday. At other times, it was hidden behind a small curtain. The young woman in the picture had a sweet face, but having so many arms made her look too much like a spider to me.

This young girl kept a scrapbook while she was alive. She used to paste obituaries, reports of accidents, and stories of people suffering from the Presbyterian Observer into it, and she would write her own poetry to go with them. It was very good poetry. This is what she wrote about a boy named Stephen Dowling Bots who fell down a well and drowned:

ODE TO STEPHEN DOWLING BOTS, DECEASED

And did young Stephen sicken,
And did young Stephen die?
And did the sad hearts thicken,
And did the mourners cry?

No; such was not the fate of
Young Stephen Dowling Bots;
Though sad hearts round him thickened,
’Twas not from sickness’ shots.

No whooping-cough did rack his frame,
Nor measles drear with spots;
Not these impaired the sacred name
Of Stephen Dowling Bots.

Despised love struck not with woe
That head of curly knots,
Nor stomach troubles laid him low,
Young Stephen Dowling Bots.

O no. Then listen with tearful eye,
Whilst I his fate do tell.
His soul did from this cold world fly
By falling down a well.

They got him out and emptied him;
Alas, it was too late;
His spirit had gone to play above
In the realms of the good and great."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_013",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Jim maintained that it was ghosts, but I countered, "No, ghosts wouldn't exclaim, 'Dern the dern fog.'"

As soon as darkness fell, we pushed off. Once we reached the center of the river, we left the raft to drift with the current. We ignited our pipes, hung our feet over the edge, and chatted about various topics. We remained unclothed day and night, provided the mosquitoes allowed it; the new garments provided by Buck’s family were too fine to be cozy, and I never cared much for wearing clothes anyway.

At times, we had the entire river to ourselves for extended periods. Across the water lay the banks and islands, marked occasionally by a faint glimmer—a candle in a cabin window. Sometimes, we spotted similar lights on the water from a raft or flatboat, and perhaps we could hear the faint sound of a fiddle or a song drifting from those vessels. Life on a raft is wonderful. Above us, the sky was covered in stars, and we would lie on our backs, gazing upward and debating whether they were created or simply came into existence. Jim insisted they were made, but I argued they just happened; I figured it would have taken too long to create so many. Jim suggested the moon might have laid them, which seemed plausible enough that I didn't object, having seen a frog lay nearly as many, so it was clearly possible. We also watched for falling stars, observing them streak across the sky. Jim claimed they had gone bad and were tossed out of the nest.

Once or twice each night, we would watch a steamboat glide through the darkness. Occasionally, it would spew a multitude of sparks from its chimneys, which would rain down into the river, creating a beautiful sight. Then, it would round a bend, its lights would vanish, its noise would cease, and the river would return to silence. Eventually, its wake would reach us, long after the boat had passed, rocking the raft slightly. Afterward, there would be nothing to hear for an indefinite time, save perhaps for the sound of frogs.

After midnight, the people on the banks retired, leaving the shores in darkness for two or three hours without a single light in any cabin window. These lights served as our clock; the first one to reappear signaled that morning was approaching, prompting us to immediately seek a spot to hide and moor the raft.

One morning near dawn, I discovered a canoe and crossed a narrow channel to the main bank—a distance of only two hundred yards—and paddled about a mile up a creek through the cypress trees, hoping to gather some berries. Just as I passed a spot where a cowpath intersected the creek, two men came rushing down the path as fast as they could run. I feared I was finished, for whenever someone was chasing someone else, I assumed it was me—or perhaps Jim."
}

We were always naked, day and night, whenever the mosquitoes allowed it; the new clothes Buck’s family made for me were too nice to be comfortable, and besides, I never really cared for clothes anyway.

Sometimes we had the entire river to ourselves for a long time. Over there were the banks and islands, and maybe a tiny spark—a candle in a cabin window. Sometimes on the water, you could see a spark or two from a raft or a flatboat, and you might hear a fiddle or a song drifting over from one of those boats. It’s lovely living on a raft. We had the sky above us, all speckled with stars, and we used to lie on our backs, look up at them, and argue about whether they were created or just happened. Jim said the moon could have laid them, which sounded reasonable enough, so I didn’t argue, since I’ve seen a frog lay just about that many, so it could certainly be done. We also watched the shooting stars streak across the sky. Jim thought they had gone bad and were thrown out of the nest.

Once or twice a night, we would see a steamboat slipping through the dark, occasionally belching a cloud of sparks from its chimneys that would rain down into the river, looking beautiful. Then it would turn a corner, its lights would wink out, the noise would stop, and the river would be still again. Eventually, its wake would reach us, long after the boat was gone, rocking the raft a little. After that, you wouldn’t hear anything for a long time, except maybe for frogs or similar sounds.

After midnight, the people on shore went to bed, and for two or three hours, the banks were pitch black with no more sparks in the cabin windows. These sparks were our clock; the first one to reappear meant morning was coming, so we would immediately look for a place to hide and tie up.

One morning around daybreak, I found a canoe and crossed a channel to the main shore—it was only two hundred yards—and paddled about a mile up a creek through the cypress woods to see if I could find some berries. Just as I was passing a spot where a cowpath crossed the creek, two men came tearing down the path as fast as they could run. I thought I was finished, because whenever anyone was chasing someone, I assumed it was me—or maybe Jim."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_014",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Jim was nearly overcome with laughter. He was always the most prone to laughter of any man I had ever known.

I retrieved the watch, and Jim settled down to sleep, snoring soundly. Eventually, the storm ceased entirely, and as soon as the first light appeared from a cabin, I woke him, and we moved the raft into a concealed spot to remain for the day.

Following breakfast, the king produced a worn-out pack of cards, and he and the duke played seven-up for a nickel a round. Growing bored, they decided to plan a scheme, as they termed it. The duke rummaged through his carpet-bag, pulled out several printed handbills, and read them aloud. One announced that the famous Dr. Armand de Montalban, from Paris, would lecture on phrenology at a specific location on a certain date for ten cents, offering character charts for an additional quarter. The duke claimed that was his identity. Other bills identified him as the world-famous Shakespearean actor, Garrick the Younger, from London’s Drury Lane. Additional notices listed various other aliases and impressive feats, such as locating water or gold using a divining rod and breaking witchcraft spells. Finally, he remarked:

“But the dramatic arts are the true passion. Have you ever performed on stage, your Majesty?”

“Never,” the king replied.

“You shall do so before three days pass, your fallen Highness,” the duke declared. “At the first suitable town, we will rent a hall and perform the sword fight from Richard III and the balcony scene from Romeo and Juliet. How does that sound to you?”

“I am fully committed to anything that brings in money, Bilgewater; however, I know nothing of acting and have seen very little of it. I was too young when my father hosted such performances at the palace. Do you think you can teach me?”

“It will be simple!”

“Very well. I am eager for something new regardless. Let us begin immediately.”

Consequently, the duke explained the roles of Romeo and Juliet, noting that he usually played Romeo, so the king would take the part of Juliet.

“But if Juliet is such a young girl, duke, my bald head and white beard might look rather strange for the role.”

“Do not concern yourself; these rural folks will never notice. Furthermore, you will be in costume, which changes everything. Juliet is on a balcony, taking in the moonlight before retiring, wearing her nightgown and a ruffled nightcap. Here are the outfits for the roles.”

He produced several suits made of curtain calico, which he described as medieval armor for Richard III and the other character, along with a long white cotton nightshirt and a matching ruffled cap. The king was content, so the duke retrieved his book and recited the lines with grand, dramatic flair, pacing and gesturing to demonstrate the proper technique. He then handed the book to the king, instructing him to memorize his lines.

Three miles down the river bend lay a small, insignificant town. After their meal, the duke mentioned he had devised a plan to travel during daylight hours without endangering Jim, so he intended to head into town to arrange the matter."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_014",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "It nearly killed Jim with laughter. He was the easiest person to make laugh that I had ever met, anyway.

I took the watch, and Jim lay down and snored away. Eventually, the storm stopped for good, and as soon as the first cabin light appeared, I woke him up and we slid the raft into a hiding spot for the day.

The king pulled out an old, tattered deck of cards after breakfast, and he and the duke played seven-up for a while, at five cents a game. Then they grew tired of it and decided they would “plan a campaign,” as they called it. The duke reached into his carpetbag, pulled out a stack of small printed flyers, and read them aloud. One flyer stated that “the celebrated Dr. Armand de Montalban, of Paris,” would “lecture on the Science of Phrenology” at a certain place on a certain date, with a ten-cent admission fee, and would “provide character charts for twenty-five cents each.” The duke claimed that was him. In another flyer, he was the “world-renowned Shakespearean tragedian, Garrick the Younger, of Drury Lane, London.” In other flyers, he used various names and claimed to do other amazing things, like finding water and gold with a “divining rod,” “breaking witch spells,” and so on. Eventually, he said:

“But the acting profession is the best. Have you ever performed on stage, Your Majesty?”

“No,” the king replied.

“You will, then, before three days have passed, your fallen highness,” the duke said. “The first decent town we reach, we will rent a hall and perform the sword fight from Richard III and the balcony scene from Romeo and Juliet. How does that sound to you?”

“I am all in for anything that pays, Bilgewater; but, you see, I don’t know anything about acting, and I haven’t seen much of it. I was too young when my father used to have them at the palace. Do you think you can teach me?”

“Easily!”

“All right. I am just dying for something new, anyway. Let’s start right now.”

So the duke explained everything about who Romeo and Juliet were, and said he was used to playing Romeo, so the king could be Juliet.

“But if Juliet is such a young girl, duke, my bald head and white whiskers are going to look unusually strange on her, perhaps.”

“No, don’t you worry; these country bumpkins won’t ever think of that. Besides, you know, you will be in costume, and that makes all the difference in the world. Juliet is on a balcony, enjoying the moonlight before she goes to bed, and she is wearing her nightgown and a ruffled nightcap. Here are the costumes for the parts.”

He pulled out two or three suits made of curtain calico, which he said were medieval armor for Richard III and the other character, along with a long white cotton nightshirt and a matching ruffled nightcap. The king was satisfied, so the duke took out his book and read the parts in a most dramatic, grandiloquent style, prancing around and acting at the same time to demonstrate how it had to be done. Then he gave the book to the king and told him to memorize his lines.

There was a small, insignificant town about three miles down the river bend, and after dinner, the duke said he had figured out his plan for how to travel in daylight without it being dangerous for Jim; so he decided he would go down to the town and arrange it."
}

There was a small, insignificant town about three miles down the river bend, and after dinner, the duke said he had figured out his plan for how to travel in daylight without it being dangerous for Jim; so he decided he would go down to the town and arrange it."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_015",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The inhabitants had vacated those structures. The riverbank had eroded beneath the corner of several others, leaving that section suspended in mid-air. People still occupied some, though it was perilous, as occasionally a section of earth as broad as a house would collapse at once. Sometimes a strip of land a quarter-mile wide begins to crumble, sliding bit by bit into the river throughout a single summer. Such a settlement must constantly retreat further inland, as the current is perpetually consuming it.

As the day approached noon, the streets grew increasingly crowded with horses and wagons, with more arriving continuously. Families brought their midday meals from the countryside, consuming them within their carriages. There was a fair amount of alcohol consumption, and I witnessed three separate brawls. Eventually, someone shouted:

“Look, here is old Boggs! He has arrived from the country for his customary monthly spree; here he comes, fellows!”

All the idlers appeared pleased; I assumed they were accustomed to deriving amusement from Boggs. One remarked:

“I wonder whom he intends to devour this time. If he had actually consumed every man he has threatened to eat over the past two decades, he would possess quite a reputation by now.”

Another added, “I wish old Boggs would threaten me, because then I would know I was not destined to perish for a thousand years.”

Boggs arrived galloping on his horse, hollering and shrieking like a savage, calling out:

“Clear the way, there. I am on the warpath, and the cost of burial caskets is about to increase.”

He was intoxicated, swaying in his saddle; he was past fifty, with a deeply flushed face. Everyone cheered, mocked, and taunted him, and he retorted, claiming he would deal with them and dispatch them in their proper order, but he could not delay because he had come to town to slay old Colonel Sherburn, and his philosophy was, “Main course first, and soft food for dessert.”

He spotted me, rode closer, and asked:

“Where did you arrive from, lad? Are you ready to meet your maker?”

Then he rode onward. I was frightened, but a man remarked:

“He signifies nothing; he always behaves in this manner when he is in his cups. He is the most good-natured old simpleton in Arkansas—he has never harmed a soul, whether intoxicated or sober.”

Boggs pulled up before the largest shop in the village, lowered his head to peer beneath the awning’s edge, and bellowed:

“Step outside, Sherburn! Come out and confront the man you have cheated. You are the cur I am hunting, and I intend to catch you, too!”

He continued in this fashion, hurling every insult he could muster at Sherburn, while the entire street was jammed with onlookers who were listening, chuckling, and carrying on. Presently, a dignified-looking gentleman of about fifty-five—who was also by far the most elegantly dressed person in the town—emerged from the shop, and the throng parted on either side to allow him passage. He addressed Boggs, very composed and deliberate—he stated:

“I am weary of this, but I shall tolerate it until one o’clock.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_015",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "The residents had moved out of those buildings. The riverbank had eroded beneath the corner of several others, leaving them hanging over the edge. People still lived in some, but it was hazardous, as a strip of land as wide as a house could collapse at any moment. Occasionally, a section of land a quarter-mile deep would begin to crumble, eroding steadily until the entire piece fell into the river over the course of a single summer. A town like that had to keep moving further inland because the river was constantly eating away at it.

As noon approached that day, the streets became increasingly crowded with wagons and horses, with more arriving constantly. Families brought their lunches from the countryside and ate them in their wagons. There was a fair amount of drinking, and I witnessed three fights. Eventually, someone shouted:

“Here comes old Boggs! He’s in from the country for his monthly bender; here he comes, boys!”

All the idlers looked pleased; I figured they were accustomed to getting entertainment out of Boggs. One of them remarked:

“I wonder who he’s going to tear into this time. If he’d actually fought all the men he’s threatened to fight over the last twenty years, he’d have quite a reputation by now.”

Another added, “I wish old Boggs would threaten me, because then I’d know I wasn’t going to die for a thousand years.”

Boggs came charging along on his horse, whooping and yelling like a wild man, shouting:

“Clear the way! I’m on the warpath, and the price of coffins is about to go up.”

He was intoxicated and swaying in his saddle; he was over fifty years old and had a very red face. Everyone yelled at him, laughed at him, and taunted him, and he taunted them back, claiming he would deal with them in due time, but he couldn’t wait now because he had come to town to kill Colonel Sherburn, and his motto was, “Meat first, and dessert to finish.”

He saw me, rode up, and asked:

“Where did you come from, boy? Are you prepared to die?”

Then he rode on. I was frightened, but a man said:

“He doesn’t mean anything by it; he always carries on like that when he’s drunk. He’s the best-natured old fool in Arkansas—he’s never hurt anyone, drunk or sober.”

Boggs rode up to the largest store in town, bent his head to see under the awning, and yelled:

“Come out here, Sherburn! Come out and face the man you’ve swindled. You’re the dog I’m after, and I’m going to get you, too!”

He continued, calling Sherburn every insult he could think of, while the entire street was packed with people listening, laughing, and carrying on. Eventually, a proud-looking man of about fifty-five—who was also by far the best-dressed man in town—stepped out of the store, and the crowd stepped back on both sides to let him through. He spoke to Boggs, very calmly and slowly:

“I’m tired of this, but I’ll put up with it until one o’clock.”"
}

{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_015",
