  "rewritten_text": "The people had moved out of those houses. The riverbank had eroded under the corner of some others, leaving that corner hanging over the water. People still lived in some of them, but it was dangerous because sometimes a strip of land as wide as a house would collapse at once. Occasionally, a section of land a quarter-mile deep would begin to crumble, caving into the river bit by bit throughout the summer. A town like that has to keep moving further back because the river is always gnawing away at it.

As it got closer to noon that day, the streets became increasingly crowded with wagons and horses, and more kept arriving. Families brought their lunches from the country and ate them in their wagons. There was quite a bit of whiskey drinking going on, and I saw three fights. Eventually, someone shouted:

“Here comes old Boggs! He’s in from the country for his usual monthly drunk; here he comes, boys!”

All the loafers looked happy; I figured they were used to having fun at Boggs’s expense. One of them said:

“I wonder who he’s going to chew up this time. If he had actually chewed up all the men he’s threatened to over the last twenty years, he’d have quite a reputation by now.”

Another one said, “I wish old Boggs would threaten me, because then I’d know I wasn’t going to die for a thousand years.”

Boggs came tearing along on his horse, whooping and yelling like an Indian, and shouting:

“Clear the track, there. I’m on the warpath, and the price of coffins is going to rise.”

He was drunk and swaying in his saddle; he was over fifty years old and had a very red face. Everyone yelled at him, laughed at him, and teased him, and he teased them back. He said he would take care of them and deal with them in turn, but he couldn’t wait now because he had come to town to kill old Colonel Sherburn, and his motto was, “Meat first, and soft food to finish off with.”

He saw me, rode up, and said:

“Where did you come from, boy? Are you prepared to die?”

Then he rode on. I was scared, but a man said:

“He doesn’t mean anything; he always carries on like that when he’s drunk. He’s the best-natured old fool in Arkansas—he never hurt anyone, drunk or sober.”

Boggs rode up in front of the biggest store in town, bent his head down so he could see under the awning, and yelled:

“Come out here, Sherburn! Come out and meet the man you’ve swindled. You’re the hound I’m after, and I’m going to get you, too!”

He kept going, calling Sherburn every name he could think of, while the whole street was packed with people listening, laughing, and carrying on. Eventually, a proud-looking man of about fifty-five—who was by far the best-dressed man in town—stepped out of the store, and the crowd stepped back on each side to let him through. He said to Boggs, very calmly and slowly:

“I’m tired of this, but I’ll put up with it until one o’clock.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_017",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Louis or Cincinnati, or some other major city. Head for the steamboat, Huckleberry; we shall arrive at the town on it.”

I did not need to be told twice to go for a steamboat trip. I reached the bank a half-mile above the village, then moved quickly along the bluff in the calm water. Soon, we encountered a pleasant, guileless-looking young country fellow sitting on a log, wiping perspiration from his forehead, as the weather was extremely hot; he had two large carpet-bags beside him.

“Run her nose into the bank,” the king commanded. I obeyed. “Where are you headed, young man?”

“To the steamboat; I am going to Orleans.”

“Come aboard,” the king said. “Wait a moment, my servant will assist you with those bags. Jump out and help the gentleman, Adolphus”—referring to me, I realized.

I did so, and the three of us continued on. The young man was very grateful; he remarked that it was difficult labor carrying his luggage in such heat. He inquired where the king was traveling, and the king replied that he had come down the river and arrived at the other village that morning, and was now heading a few miles up to visit an old acquaintance on a farm. The young man remarked:

“When I first saw you, I thought to myself, ‘That is surely Mr. Wilks, and he nearly arrived in time.’ But then I thought again, ‘No, I suppose it is not him, or he would not be traveling up the river.’ You are not him, are you?”

“No, my name is Blodgett—Alexander Blodgett—Reverend Alexander Blodgett, I suppose I must say, as I am one of the Lord’s humble servants. But I am just as capable of feeling sorry for Mr. Wilks for not arriving in time, if he has lost anything by it—which I hope he has not.”

“Well, he does not lose any property by it, because he will receive that just fine; but he missed seeing his brother Peter pass away—which he might not mind, no one can say—but his brother would have given anything in this world to see him before he died; he spoke of nothing else for these three weeks; he had not seen him since they were boys together—and had never seen his brother William at all—that is the deaf and mute one—William is no more than thirty or thirty-five. Peter and George were the only ones who came out here; George was the married brother; he and his wife both passed away last year. Harvey and William are the only ones remaining now; and, as I was saying, they did not arrive in time.”

“Did anyone notify them?”

“Oh, yes; a month or two ago, when Peter first fell ill; because Peter said then that he felt as though he might not recover this time. You see, he was quite elderly, and George’s girls were too young to be much company for him, except Mary Jane, the red-headed one; and so he was rather lonely after George and his wife died, and did not seem to care much about living. He desperately wanted to see Harvey—and William, too, for that matter—because he was the type of person who cannot bear to write a will.”"
}

  "rewritten_text": "Louis or Cincinnati, or some other big city. Go for the steamboat, Huckleberry; we will head down to the village on it.”

I didn’t need to be told twice to go for a steamboat ride. I reached the shore half a mile above the village and then went scooting along the bluff bank in the calm water. Pretty soon, we came to a nice, innocent-looking young country fellow sitting on a log, wiping the sweat off his face, for it was extremely hot weather; he had a couple of large carpet-bags beside him.

“Run her nose into the shore,” the king said. I did it. “Where are you headed, young man?”

“For the steamboat; I’m going to Orleans.”

“Get aboard,” the king said. “Hold on a minute, my servant will help you with those bags. Jump out and help the gentleman, Adolphus”—meaning me, I realized.

I did so, and then all three of us started off again. The young man was very thankful; he said it was hard work carrying his luggage in such weather. He asked the king where he was going, and the king told him he had come down the river and landed at the other village that morning, and now he was going up a few miles to see an old friend on a farm there. The young fellow said:

“When I first saw you, I said to myself, ‘That’s Mr. Wilks, for sure, and he came very close to getting here in time.’ But then I said again, ‘No, I don’t think it’s him, or he wouldn’t be paddling up the river.’ You aren’t him, are you?”

“No, my name is Blodgett—Alexander Blodgett—Reverend Alexander Blodgett, I suppose I should say, as I am one of the Lord’s humble servants. But still, I am just as capable of feeling sorry for Mr. Wilks for not arriving in time, all the same, if he has missed anything by it—which I hope he hasn’t.”

“Well, he doesn’t miss any property by it, because he will get that all right; but he missed seeing his brother Peter die—which he might not mind, nobody can tell about that—but his brother would have given anything in this world to see him before he died; he never talked about anything else all these three weeks; he hadn’t seen him since they were boys together—and hadn’t ever seen his brother William at all—that’s the deaf and dumb one—William isn’t more than thirty or thirty-five. Peter and George were the only ones that came out here; George was the married brother; he and his wife both died last year. Harvey and William are the only ones left now; and, as I was saying, they haven’t arrived in time.”

“Did anybody send them word?”

“Oh, yes; a month or two ago, when Peter first got sick; because Peter said then that he sort of felt like he wasn’t going to get well this time. You see, he was pretty old, and George’s girls were too young to be much company for him, except Mary Jane, the red-headed one; and so he was kind of lonely after George and his wife died, and didn’t seem to care much to live. He desperately wanted to see Harvey—and William, too, for that matter—because he was one of those people who can’t bear to make a will.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_018",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "He kept rambling on and on, clearly enjoying the sound of his own voice, and every so often he brought up his funeral orgies again, until the duke couldn't take it anymore. He wrote on a small scrap of paper, “obsequies, you old fool,” folded it up, and started making gestures to pass it over people’s heads to him. The king read it, put it in his pocket, and said:

“Poor William, afflicted as he is, his heart is always in the right place. He’s asking me to invite everyone to the funeral—he wants me to make them all welcome. But he didn’t need to worry—that’s exactly what I was doing.”

Then he continued his rambling, perfectly calm, and kept dropping in his funeral orgies every now and then, just as he had before. When he did it for the third time, he said:

“I say orgies, not because it’s the common term, because it isn’t—obsequies being the common term—but because orgies is the correct term. Obsequies isn’t used in England anymore; it’s gone out of style. We say orgies now in England. Orgies is better because it describes what you’re after more precisely. It’s a word made up of the Greek orgo, meaning outside, open, or abroad; and the Hebrew jeesum, meaning to plant or cover up; hence, inter. So, you see, funeral orgies is an open or public funeral.”

He was the worst I had ever encountered. Well, the iron-jawed man laughed right in his face. Everyone was shocked. Everyone said, “Why, doctor!” and Abner Shackleford said:

“Why, Robinson, haven’t you heard the news? This is Harvey Wilks.”

The king smiled eagerly, reached out his hand, and said:

“Is it my poor brother’s dear good friend and physician? I—”

“Keep your hands off me!” said the doctor. “You talk like an Englishman, don’t you? It’s the worst imitation I’ve ever heard. You, Peter Wilks’s brother! You’re a fraud, that’s what you are!”

Well, how they all reacted! They crowded around the doctor and tried to quiet him down, attempting to explain to him how Harvey had shown in forty ways that he was Harvey, knowing everyone by name, even the names of the dogs, and they begged and begged him not to hurt Harvey’s feelings or the poor girls’ feelings, and all that. But it was no use; he kept right on storming, saying any man who pretended to be an Englishman and couldn’t imitate the accent any better than he did was a fraud and a liar. The poor girls were clinging to the king and crying; and all of a sudden, the doctor turned on them. He said:

“I was your father’s friend, and I’m your friend; and I warn you as a friend, and an honest one who wants to protect you and keep you out of harm and trouble, to turn your backs on that scoundrel and have nothing to do with him, the ignorant tramp, with his idiotic Greek and Hebrew, as he calls it. He is the thinnest kind of impostor—he has come here with a lot of empty names and facts which he picked up somewhere, and you take them for proofs, and are helped to fool yourselves by these foolish friends here, who ought to know better.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_019",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I went to bed fully dressed, yet sleep was impossible because I was so anxious to finish my task. Eventually, I heard the king and the duke arrive, so I slid off my mattress and rested my chin on the ladder’s edge, watching for any activity. Nothing occurred.

I waited until the night noises ceased and the morning sounds had not yet started, then descended the ladder.

I crept toward their rooms and listened; they were snoring. I moved quietly downstairs without incident. Everything was silent. Peering through a gap in the dining-room door, I saw the men guarding the body fast asleep in their chairs. The door to the parlor where the corpse lay was open, and candles burned in both rooms. I walked past, noting the parlor was empty except for Peter’s remains, but the front door was locked and the key was missing. Suddenly, I heard footsteps behind me on the stairs. I dashed into the parlor and scanned the room, realizing the coffin was the only place to hide the bag. The lid was pushed aside by about a foot, revealing the dead man’s face beneath a damp cloth and his burial shroud. I shoved the money-bag under the lid, just past his crossed hands—which made me shudder, they were so cold—and then I hurried across the room to hide behind the door.

Mary Jane was the one approaching. She walked to the coffin, knelt down gently, and looked inside. She raised her handkerchief and began to weep, though I could not hear her, as her back was turned toward me. I slipped away, checking the dining-room crack as I passed to ensure the guards had not noticed me; they remained undisturbed.

I went back to bed, feeling rather discouraged that things had turned out this way after all my effort and risk. I reasoned that if the money stayed put, it might be fine; once we were a hundred or two miles downriver, I could write to Mary Jane so she could retrieve it. However, that was unlikely; the money would surely be discovered when they fastened the lid. Then the king would reclaim it, and it would be a long time before anyone else had a chance to steal it from him. Naturally, I wanted to go back and retrieve it, but I dared not. It was growing later by the minute, and the watchers would soon stir, risking my capture with six thousand dollars in my possession that I had no authority to hold."
}

Of course, I wanted to sneak back down and get it out of there, but I didn’t dare. model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_020",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I remarked. 

Goodness, it slipped out before I could stop myself! Before I could even move, she wrapped her arms around my neck and begged me to repeat it, over and over again.

I realized I had spoken too impulsively and revealed too much, landing myself in a difficult spot. I requested a moment to reflect; she sat there, restless and agitated yet beautiful, appearing relieved and content, much like someone who has finally had an aching tooth extracted. I began to ponder the situation. I reasoned that anyone who blurts out the truth while in a predicament is taking significant chances, though I lacked the experience to be sure; still, that was my impression. Yet, in this instance, it appeared that honesty was actually superior and safer than deception. I decided to store this thought away to contemplate later, as it felt quite peculiar and unconventional. I had never encountered anything like it. Eventually, I concluded that I would risk it; I would tell the truth this time, even though it felt like sitting on a keg of gunpowder and lighting the fuse just to see where I might land. Then I spoke:

“Miss Mary Jane, is there somewhere outside of town where you could stay for three or four days?”

“Yes, at Mr. Lothrop’s. Why?”

“Don’t worry about the reason yet. If I can explain how I know the enslaved people will reunite within two weeks—right here in this house—and provide proof, will you go to Mr. Lothrop’s and remain for four days?”

“Four days!” she exclaimed. “I would stay for a year!”

“Very well,” I replied. “I don’t need anything more from you than your promise; I would prefer it over any other man’s sworn oath.” She smiled and blushed charmingly, and I added, “If you don’t mind, I’m going to close the door and bolt it.”

I returned to my seat and continued:

“Don’t scream. Just sit quietly and take this like a soldier. I must be honest, and you need to steel yourself, Miss Mary, because it is unpleasant news and difficult to hear, but there is no way around it. These men claiming to be your uncles are not your relatives at all; they are complete frauds and total deadbeats. Now that the worst is over, you should be able to handle the rest.”

It shocked her deeply, naturally; but since I had navigated the most dangerous part, I pressed on. With her eyes flashing with increasing intensity, I recounted everything, from when we first encountered that young fool heading to the steamboat, all the way to when she threw herself onto the king’s chest at the front door and he kissed her repeatedly. She leaped up, her face glowing like a sunset, and cried:

“The beast! Come, we must not waste a single second—we will have them tarred and feathered and thrown into the river!”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_020",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "I said. 

Goodness, it slipped out before I could think! And before I could move, she threw her arms around my neck and told me to say it again, say it again, say it again!

I realized I had spoken too quickly and said too much, and I was in a difficult spot. I asked her to let me think for a minute; she sat there, very impatient and excited and beautiful, but looking happy and relieved, like someone who just had a tooth pulled. So I started thinking it through. I said to myself, I suppose a person who suddenly tells the truth when they are in a tight spot is taking quite a few risks, though I haven’t had any experience and can’t say for certain; but it looks that way to me, anyway; and yet here is a case where I’m blessed if it doesn’t look to me like the truth is better and actually safer than a lie. I must keep that in mind and think it over sometime, it’s so strange and unusual. I’ve never seen anything like it. Well, I said to myself at last, I’m going to risk it; I’ll go ahead and tell the truth this time, even though it feels like sitting on a keg of powder and lighting it just to see where you’ll land. Then I said:

“Miss Mary Jane, is there any place a little way out of town where you could go and stay for three or four days?”

“Yes; Mr. Lothrop’s. Why?”

“Never mind why yet. If I tell you how I know the enslaved people will see each other again within two weeks—here in this house—and prove how I know it—will you go to Mr. Lothrop’s and stay for four days?”

“Four days!” she said; “I’ll stay a year!”

“All right,” I said, “I don’t want anything more from you than just your word—I’d rather have it than another man’s sworn oath.” She smiled and blushed very sweetly, and I said, “If you don’t mind, I’ll shut the door—and bolt it.”

Then I came back and sat down again, and said:

“Don’t scream. Just sit still and take it like a man. I have to tell the truth, and you need to brace yourself, Miss Mary, because it’s bad news and it’s going to be hard to hear, but there’s no way around it. These uncles of yours aren’t uncles at all; they’re a couple of frauds—total deadbeats. There, now that we’re over the worst of it, you can handle the rest fairly easily.”

It shocked her, of course; but I was past the shallow water now, so I kept going, her eyes blazing brighter and brighter the whole time, and I told her every single thing, from where we first met that young fool heading to the steamboat, all the way through to where she threw herself onto the king’s chest at the front door and he kissed her sixteen or seventeen times—and then she jumped up, her face burning like a sunset, and said:

“The brute! Come on, don’t waste a minute—not a second—we’ll have them tarred and feathered, and thrown in the river!”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_021",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Don’t you realize they would go and report it? There is no other way except to tell absolutely nobody.”

“Well, perhaps you are correct—yes, I suppose you are right.”

“But I think we should inform Uncle Harvey that she has stepped out for a bit, just so he won’t worry about her?”

“Yes, Miss Mary Jane requested that you do that. She said, ‘Tell them to give Uncle Harvey and William my love and a kiss, and mention that I have crossed the river to visit Mr.’—Mr.—what is the name of that wealthy family your uncle Peter held in such high regard?—I mean the one that—”

“Why, you must be referring to the Apthorps, aren’t you?”

“Naturally; blast those sorts of names, a person can never seem to recall them, most of the time, somehow. Yes, she said, tell them she has gone over to ask the Apthorps to be certain to attend the auction and purchase this house, because she figured her uncle Peter would prefer they had it over anyone else; and she intends to stay with them until they promise to come, and then, if she isn’t too exhausted, she is returning home; and if she is, she will be back in the morning regardless. She said, don’t mention the Proctors, but only the Apthorps—which will be entirely accurate, because she is going there to speak about them buying the house; I am certain of it, because she told me so herself.”

“Very well,” they replied, and departed to wait for their uncles, to deliver the love and the kisses, and relay the message.

Everything was settled now. The girls would remain silent because they desired to travel to England; and the king and the duke preferred Mary Jane to be away working on the auction rather than within reach of Doctor Robinson. I felt quite satisfied; I figured I had managed it rather skillfully—I reckoned Tom Sawyer could not have done it any better himself. Naturally, he would have added more flair to it, but I cannot manage that very easily, not having been raised to it.

Well, they conducted the auction in the public square, toward the end of the afternoon, and it dragged on and on, and the old man was present and looking his most venomous, standing there beside the auctioneer, and interjecting a bit of Scripture now and then, or some pious remark of some sort, and the duke was nearby acting pathetic for sympathy as best he knew how, and generally making a spectacle of himself.

But eventually the affair concluded, and everything was sold—everything except a small, insignificant plot in the graveyard. So they had to dispose of that—I never saw such a glutton as the king was for wanting to swallow everything. Well, while they were at it, a steamboat arrived, and in about two minutes a crowd came up whooping and yelling and laughing and carrying on, and shouting out:

“Here is your opposition line! Here are your two sets of heirs to old Peter Wilks—and you pay your money and you take your choice!”

CHAPTER XXIX.

They were bringing along a very respectable-looking old gentleman, and a respectable-looking younger one, with his right arm in a sling."
}

The girls would remain silent because they desired to travel to England; and the king and the duke preferred Mary Jane to be away working on the auction rather than within reach of Doctor Robinson. I felt quite satisfied; I figured I had managed it rather skillfully—I reckoned Tom Sawyer could not have done it any better himself. Naturally, he would have added more flair to it, but I cannot manage that very easily, not having been raised to it.

Well, they conducted the auction in the public square, toward the end of the afternoon, and it dragged on and on, and the old man was present and looking his most venomous, standing there beside the auctioneer, and interjecting a bit of Scripture now and then, or some pious remark of some sort, and the duke was nearby acting pathetic for sympathy as best he knew how, and generally making a spectacle of himself.

But eventually the affair concluded, and everything was sold—everything except a small, insignificant plot in the graveyard. So they had to dispose of that—I never saw such a glutton as the king was for wanting to swallow everything. Well, while they were at it, a steamboat arrived, and in about two minutes a crowd came up whooping and yelling and laughing and carrying on, and shouting out:

“Here is your opposition line! Here are your two sets of heirs to old Peter Wilks—and you pay your money and you take your choice!”

CHAPTER XXIX.

They were bringing along a very respectable-looking old gentleman, and a respectable-looking younger one, with his right arm in a sling."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_022",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Upon reaching the town, I noticed the storm had cleared the streets, so I walked directly down the main thoroughfare instead of seeking side paths. As I neared our residence, I stared intently at it. The building was completely dark, which left me feeling unexpectedly sad and let down. However, just as I was passing, a light suddenly flickered in Mary Jane’s window. My heart surged with such intensity it felt as though it might burst, and in that same instant, the house vanished into the darkness behind me, never to be seen again. She was truly the finest and bravest girl I had ever known.

Once I had traveled far enough past the town to spot the tow-head, I began searching intently for a boat to take. When a flash of lightning revealed one that was not chained, I grabbed it and pushed off. It was merely a canoe tied with a simple rope. The tow-head was quite a distance away, situated in the middle of the river, but I hurried toward it. By the time I reached the raft, I was so exhausted that I would have collapsed to catch my breath if I had the time. Instead, I jumped aboard and shouted, “Get moving, Jim, and cast us off! Thank goodness, we are finally rid of them!”

Jim rushed toward me with his arms wide, overflowing with happiness. However, when I caught sight of him during a lightning flash, I was so startled by his appearance—dressed as King Lear and a drowned Arab—that I fell backward into the water. The sight nearly terrified me to death. Jim pulled me back onto the raft and tried to embrace me in his relief that I had returned and that we were free of the king and the duke, but I interrupted him: “Not now; save it for breakfast! Just cut the line and let us go!”

Within moments, we were drifting down the river, and it felt wonderful to be alone and liberated on the water once more. I felt so relieved that I began jumping around and clicking my heels together. Yet, after a few moments, I heard a familiar sound. I held my breath to listen, and when the next flash of lightning illuminated the river, I saw them approaching, rowing their skiff with great speed. It was the king and the duke. I collapsed onto the wooden planks, defeated, and struggled to hold back tears.

When they climbed onto the raft, the king grabbed me by the collar and demanded, “Trying to run away from us, were you, you little rascal? Tired of our company, are you?” I replied, “No, your majesty, we were not—please, your majesty, don’t!”"
}

  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "When I reached town, I saw no one out in the storm, so I didn't bother with back alleys but walked straight down the main street. As I neared our house, I stared at it. No light; the place was completely dark, which left me feeling strangely sad and disappointed. But just as I was passing by, a light flashed in Mary Jane’s window. My heart suddenly swelled, nearly bursting. A second later, the house was behind me in the dark, never to be seen by me again in this life. She was the finest girl I had ever met, and the bravest.

Once I was far enough past town to reach the sandbar, I started looking for a boat to borrow. As soon as the lightning revealed one that wasn't chained, I grabbed it and pushed off. It was a canoe, tied only with a rope. The sandbar was a long way off, out in the middle of the river, but I didn't waste any time. When I finally reached the raft, I was so exhausted I would have just collapsed to catch my breath if I could have afforded to. But I didn't. As I jumped aboard, I shouted, "Get out here, Jim, and cut her loose! Thank goodness, we’re finally rid of them!"

Jim rushed out, arms wide, so full of joy; but when I caught sight of him in the lightning, my heart jumped into my throat and I fell backward overboard. I had forgotten he was dressed as old King Lear and a drowned Arab, and it scared the life out of me. Jim pulled me out, ready to hug and bless me, so happy that I was back and that we were free of the king and the duke. But I said, "Not now; save it for breakfast! Cut the line and let her go!"

Within two seconds, we were sliding down the river. It felt so good to be free again, all by ourselves on the wide river with no one to bother us. I had to move around, jumping up and clicking my heels together—I couldn't help it. But around the third jump, I heard a sound I knew all too well. I held my breath, listening and waiting. Sure enough, when the next flash of lightning broke over the water, there they were, rowing hard and making their skiff fly. It was the king and the duke.

I collapsed onto the planks, defeated; it was all I could do to keep from crying.

CHAPTER XXX.

When they climbed aboard, the king grabbed me by the collar and said, "Trying to ditch us, were you, you pup? Tired of our company, eh?"

I replied, "No, your majesty, we weren't—please don't, your majesty!""
}

  "rewritten_text": "When I reached the town, I saw that nobody was out in the storm, so I did not look for any back streets but walked straight through the main one. As I approached our house, I stared at it. There was no light; the house was completely dark, which made me feel sad and disappointed, though I did not know why. But finally, just as I was passing by, a light flashed in Mary Jane’s window. My heart swelled up suddenly, as if it might burst. In that same second, the house and everything else were behind me in the dark, never to be in front of me again in this world. She was the best girl I had ever seen and had the most courage.

The minute I was far enough past the town to see that I could reach the sandbar, I began to look closely for a boat to borrow. The first time the lightning showed me one that was not chained, I grabbed it and pushed off. It was a canoe, and it was only tied with a rope. The sandbar was a long way off, way out there in the middle of the river, but I did not waste any time. When I finally reached the raft, I was so exhausted that I would have just laid down to rest and catch my breath if I could have afforded to. But I did not. As I jumped aboard, I shouted:

“Get out here, Jim, and set her loose! Thank goodness, we are rid of them!”

Jim came out and was running toward me with both arms spread, he was so full of joy. But when I saw him in the lightning, my heart jumped into my throat and I fell overboard backward. I had forgotten that he was dressed as old King Lear and a drowned Arab all in one, and it nearly scared the life out of me. But Jim pulled me out and was going to hug me and bless me, he was so glad I was back and that we were rid of the king and the duke, but I said:

“Not now; save it for breakfast, save it for breakfast! Cut the rope and let her go!”

So, in two seconds, we were sliding down the river. It felt so good to be free again and all by ourselves on the big river, with nobody to bother us. I had to move around a bit and jump up and click my heels together a few times—I could not help it. But around the third jump, I noticed a sound that I knew very well. I held my breath, listened, and waited. Sure enough, when the next flash of lightning broke over the water, there they came! They were rowing hard and making their small boat move fast. It was the king and the duke.

I collapsed right down onto the planks and gave up; it was all I could do to keep from crying.

CHAPTER XXX.

When they got aboard, the king came for me, shook me by the collar, and said:

“Trying to give us the slip, were you, you pup! Tired of our company, hey?”

I said:

“No, your majesty, we were not—please do not, your majesty!”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_024",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "However, their joy was nothing compared to mine; I felt as though I had been reborn, so relieved was I to discover my identity. They clung to me for two hours, and eventually, when my jaw was so exhausted it could barely move, I had recounted more tales about my family—the Sawyer family—than could have possibly occurred to any six such families combined. I described in detail how we had blown a cylinder-head near the mouth of the White River, requiring three days for repairs. This fabrication served me perfectly, as they had no reason to doubt that such a repair would take that long. I could have just as easily claimed it was a bolthead, and it would have been just as effective.

I felt quite at ease on one side, yet deeply uneasy on the other. Pretending to be Tom Sawyer was simple enough until I heard a steamboat chugging down the river. I worried that if Tom Sawyer arrived on that vessel and walked in, he might shout my name before I could signal him to remain silent. That scenario was impossible; I had to intercept him. I informed the family that I intended to head into town to retrieve my luggage. The old man offered to accompany me, but I declined, insisting I could manage the horse alone and preferred he not trouble himself.

I set off toward town in the wagon, and halfway there, I spotted another vehicle approaching. It was indeed Tom Sawyer, so I pulled over and waited. I called out for him to stop, and as he pulled alongside, his mouth dropped open in shock. He gulped several times, as if his throat were parched, and asked why I had returned to haunt him when he had never done me any wrong.

I assured him I had not returned because I had never left. Hearing my voice calmed him slightly, though he remained skeptical. He warned me not to trick him and asked, in all sincerity, if I was truly not a ghost. I gave him my word that I was not.

He admitted that should settle it, yet he still could not grasp the situation. He asked if I had ever been murdered at all. model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_025",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "If I owned a yellow dog as ignorant as a person’s conscience, I would poison it. It occupies more space than everything else inside a person, yet it serves no purpose whatsoever. Tom Sawyer holds the same opinion.

CHAPTER XXXIV.

We ceased our conversation and fell into deep thought. Eventually, Tom remarked:

“Listen here, Huck, what idiots we are for not realizing this sooner! I am certain I know where Jim is kept.”

“No! Where?”

“Inside that shed near the ash-hopper. Just consider this. When we were eating, didn’t you observe a black man enter with some food?”

“I did.”

“What did you assume the food was for?”

“For a dog.”

“I thought so too. Well, it was not for a dog.”

“Why not?”

“Because part of it was watermelon.”

“That is true—I noticed it. It is truly remarkable that I never considered a dog would not eat watermelon. It demonstrates how a person can look at something and yet fail to see it.”

“Well, the man unlocked the padlock upon entering, and he secured it again when he departed. He handed the uncle a key just as we finished our meal—the same key, I am sure. Watermelon indicates a man, the lock indicates a prisoner; and it is unlikely there are two prisoners on such a small farm, especially where the people are so kind and generous. Jim is the prisoner. Very well—I am pleased we discovered it using detective methods; I would not value any other approach. Now, you apply your mind to devise a scheme to liberate Jim, and I will formulate one as well; then we shall select the one we prefer.”

What an intellect for a mere boy to possess! If I possessed Tom Sawyer’s brain, I would not trade it to become a duke, a steamboat mate, a circus clown, or anything else I can imagine. I began to contemplate a strategy, though only to keep busy; I knew perfectly well where the superior plan would originate. Shortly, Tom asked:

“Are you ready?”

“Yes,” I replied.

“Very well—present it.”

“My plan is this,” I said. “We can easily verify if Jim is inside. Then, I will retrieve my canoe tomorrow night and bring my raft over from the island. Then, on the first dark night, we steal the key from the old man’s trousers after he retires, and depart down the river on the raft with Jim, hiding during the day and traveling at night, just as Jim and I used to do. Would that plan not succeed?”

“Succeed? Why, certainly it would succeed, like rats fighting. But it is far too simple; there is nothing to it. What is the value of a plan that requires no more effort than that? It is as weak as goose-milk. Why, Huck, it would cause no more excitement than breaking into a soap factory.”

I remained silent, as I expected nothing else; yet I knew quite well that whenever he revealed his own plan, it would be free of such flaws.

And it was. He explained his idea, and I immediately recognized it was fifteen times better than mine in terms of style, and it would make Jim just as free as my plan would, while perhaps getting us all killed in the process."
}

CHAPTER XXXIV.

We stopped talking and started thinking. After a while, Tom says:

“Look here, Huck, what fools we are for not thinking of this sooner! I bet I know where Jim is.”

“No! Where?”

“In that hut down by the ash hopper. Look here. When we were at dinner, didn’t you see a man go in there with some food?”

“Yes.”

“What did you think the food was for?”

“For a dog.”

“Me too. Well, it wasn’t for a dog.”

“Why?”

“Because part of it was watermelon.”

“So it was—I noticed it. Well, it’s amazing that I never thought about a dog not eating watermelon. It shows how a person can look at something and not really see it at the same time.”

“Well, the man unlocked the padlock when he went in, and he locked it again when he came out. He brought Uncle a key right around the time we got up from the table—the same key, I bet. Watermelon proves it’s a man, the lock proves he’s a prisoner; and it isn’t likely there are two prisoners on such a small plantation, especially where the people are all so kind and good. Jim is the prisoner. All right—I’m glad we figured it out detective-style; I wouldn’t care for any other way. Now you use your brain and come up with a plan to steal Jim, and I’ll come up with one too; then we’ll pick the one we like best.”

What a mind for a boy to have! If I had Tom Sawyer’s brain, I wouldn’t trade it to be a duke, or a steamboat mate, or a circus clown, or anything else I can think of. I started thinking of a plan, but only just to keep busy; I knew very well where the real plan was going to come from. Pretty soon, Tom says:

“Ready?”

“Yes,” I say.

“All right—let’s hear it.”

“My plan is this,” I say. “We can easily find out if it’s Jim in there. Then I’ll get my canoe tomorrow night and bring my raft over from the island. “Work? Why, certainly it would work, like rats fighting. But it’s too darn simple; there’s nothing to it. What’s the point of a plan that isn’t any more trouble than that? It’s as weak as water. Why, Huck, it wouldn’t cause any more excitement than breaking into a soap factory.”

I didn’t say anything, because I wasn’t expecting anything different; but I knew perfectly well that whenever he got his plan ready, it wouldn’t have any of those flaws.

And it didn’t. He told me what it was, and I saw in a minute it was worth fifteen of mine for style, and would make Jim just as free a man as mine would, and maybe get us all killed besides."
}

“In that hut down by the ash-hopper. Look here. When we were at dinner, didn’t you see a Black man go in there with some food?”

“Yes.”

“What did you think the food was for?”

“For a dog.”

“I thought so too. Well, it wasn’t for a dog.”

“Why?”

“Because part of it was watermelon.”

“So it was—I noticed it. It is amazing that I never thought about a dog not eating watermelon. It shows how a person can see something and not really see it at the same time.”

“Well, the man unlocked the padlock when he went in, and he locked it again when he came out. He brought Uncle a key around the time we got up from the table—the same key, I bet. Watermelon proves it is a man, and the lock proves he is a prisoner. It is unlikely there are two prisoners on such a small plantation, especially where the people are all so kind and good. Jim is the prisoner. All right—I am glad we figured it out like detectives; I wouldn’t care for any other way. Now, you use your mind and think of a plan to steal Jim, and I will think of one too; then we will choose the one we like best.”

What a mind for a boy to have! If I had Tom Sawyer’s brain, I wouldn’t trade it to be a duke, a steamboat captain, a circus clown, or anything else I can think of. I started thinking of a plan, but only just to have something to do; I knew very well where the real plan was going to come from. Pretty soon, Tom said:

“Ready?”

“Yes,” I said.

“All right—let’s hear it.”

“My plan is this,” I said. “We can easily find out if it is Jim in there. Then I can get my canoe tomorrow night and bring my raft over from the island. Then, on the first dark night, we steal the key out of the old man’s pants after he goes to bed, and we head down the river on the raft with Jim, hiding during the day and traveling at night, the way Jim and I used to do before. Wouldn’t that plan work?”

“Work? Why, certainly it would work, like rats fighting. But it is too simple; there is nothing to it. What is the point of a plan that isn’t any trouble at all? It is as mild as goose milk. Why, Huck, it wouldn’t cause any more excitement than breaking into a soap factory.”

I didn’t say anything, because I wasn’t expecting anything different; but I knew very well that whenever he got his plan ready, it wouldn’t have any of those problems.

And it didn’t. He told me what it was, and I saw in a minute it was fifteen times better than mine in terms of style, and it would make Jim just as free a man as mine would, and maybe get us all killed besides."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_026",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The Man in the Iron Mask always followed that practice, and it is a perfectly sound method, too.”

“Jim doesn’t possess any tin plates. They serve his meals in a pan.”

“That is irrelevant; we can acquire some for him.”

“No one can read his plates.”

“That has absolutely nothing to do with the matter, Huck Finn. All he must do is scratch words onto the plate and toss it away. You are not required to be able to decipher it. Why, half the time, you cannot read what a prisoner scrawls on a tin plate or anywhere else.”

“Well, then, what is the point of squandering the plates?”

“Good heavens, they aren’t the prisoner’s plates.”

“But they are someone’s plates, aren’t they?”

“Well, suppose they are? Why should the prisoner care whose—”

He stopped abruptly, as we heard the breakfast horn sounding. Consequently, we hurried toward the house.

During the morning, I took a sheet and a white shirt from the clothesline; I located an old sack, placed them inside, and we went to gather fox-fire, adding that to the bag as well. I referred to it as borrowing, since that was the term my father always used; however, Tom insisted it wasn’t borrowing, but stealing. He explained that we were acting as prisoners, and prisoners do not concern themselves with how they obtain items as long as they get them, and no one faults them for it. Tom claimed it is not a crime for a prisoner to steal what is necessary for an escape; it is his right. Therefore, as long as we were playing the part of prisoners, we had every right to steal anything on the property that we might find useful for our liberation. He noted that if we were not prisoners, the situation would be quite different, and only a low, despicable person would steal when not in captivity. Thus, we decided we would steal anything that was convenient. Yet, he made a great deal of trouble later when I stole a watermelon from the slave patch and ate it; he forced me to give the slaves a dime without explaining why. Tom argued that he meant we could only steal what we required. Well, I replied, I required the watermelon. But he insisted I did not need it to escape from prison; that was the distinction. He said if I had wanted it to conceal a knife and smuggle it to Jim to slay the jailer, it would have been acceptable. So I dropped the subject, although I could see no benefit in pretending to be a prisoner if I had to sit and debate such fine, gold-leaf distinctions every time I saw an opportunity to snatch a watermelon.

As I was saying, we waited that morning until everyone was occupied and no one was visible in the yard; then Tom carried the sack into the lean-to while I stood at a distance to keep watch. Eventually, he emerged, and we sat on the woodpile to converse. He said:

“Everything is prepared now, except for the tools; and that is easily resolved.”

“Tools?” I asked.

“Yes.”

“Tools for what?”"
}

{

  "rewritten_text": "The Iron Mask always did that, and it’s a darn good way, too.”

“Jim doesn’t have any tin plates. They feed him from a pan.”

“That’s nothing; we can get him some.”

“Nobody can read his plates.”

“That has nothing to do with it, Huck Finn. All he has to do is write on the plate and throw it out. You don’t have to be able to read it. Why, half the time you can’t read anything a prisoner writes on a tin plate, or anywhere else.”

“Well, then, what’s the point of wasting the plates?”

“Why, blast it all, they aren’t the prisoner’s plates.”

“But they’re somebody’s plates, aren’t they?”

“Well, suppose they are? What does the prisoner care whose—”

He stopped there, because we heard the breakfast horn blowing. So we headed for the house.

During the morning, I borrowed a sheet and a white shirt from the clothesline; I found an old sack and put them in it, and we went down and got the fox-fire, and put that in too. I called it borrowing, because that was what Dad always called it; but Tom said it wasn’t borrowing, it was stealing. He said we were acting as prisoners; and prisoners don’t care how they get a thing as long as they get it, and nobody blames them for it, either. It isn’t a crime for a prisoner to steal what he needs to escape, Tom said; it’s his right; and so, as long as we were acting as prisoners, we had a perfect right to steal anything on this property we had the slightest use for to help us escape. He said if we weren’t prisoners it would be a very different thing, and only a mean, low-down person would steal when he wasn’t a prisoner. So we decided we would steal everything that came in handy. And yet he made a huge fuss, one day after that, when I stole a watermelon from the slave patch and ate it; he made me go and give the workers a dime without telling them what it was for. Tom said that what he meant was, we could steal anything we needed. Well, I said, I needed the watermelon. But he said I didn’t need it to escape from prison; that was the difference. He said if I had wanted it to hide a knife in, and smuggle it to Jim to kill the jailer with, it would have been all right. So I let it go at that, though I couldn’t see any advantage in acting as a prisoner if I had to sit down and chew over a lot of fine-tuned distinctions like that every time I saw a chance to grab a watermelon.

Well, as I was saying, we waited that morning until everyone was busy and nobody was in sight around the yard; then Tom carried the sack into the lean-to while I stood off a distance to keep watch. Eventually he came out, and we went and sat down on the woodpile to talk. He said:

“Everything’s all right now except for tools; and that’s easily fixed.”

“Tools?” I said.

“Yes.”

“Tools for what?”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_027",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Once that passed, we were fine again; the sudden shock had simply rattled us for a moment. Uncle Silas remarked:

“It is truly strange, I cannot fathom it. I am certain I removed it, because—”

“Because you are only wearing one. Just hear the man! I am aware you took it off, and I have better proof than your wandering mind, since I saw it on the clothesline myself yesterday. Regardless, it has vanished, and you will simply have to wear a red flannel one until I find the time to sew another. That will be the third I have produced in two years. It keeps a person constantly busy providing you with shirts; where you manage to misplace them all is beyond my understanding. One would assume you might learn to be more careful with them at your age.”

“I realize that, Sally, and I truly try. But it should not be entirely blamed on me, as I never see them or handle them except when I am wearing them; I do not believe I have ever lost one while it was on my back.”

“Well, it is not your fault if you have not; you would have if you could, I suppose. And the shirt is not the only thing missing, either. A spoon is gone; and that is not the end of it. There were ten, and now there are only nine. The calf might have taken the shirt, I suppose, but the calf certainly did not take the spoon.”

“Why, what else is missing, Sally?”

“Six candles are gone—that is what. The rats could have taken the candles, and I suspect they did; I am surprised they do not carry off the entire house, given how you always promise to plug their holes but never do; if they were not foolish, they would nest in your hair, Silas—you would never notice; but you cannot blame the spoon on the rats, of that I am certain.”

“Well, Sally, I am to blame, and I admit it; I have been negligent; but I will not let tomorrow pass without sealing those holes.”

“Oh, do not rush; next year will be soon enough. Matilda Angelina Araminta Phelps!”

Her thimble strikes, and the child pulls her hand out of the sugar bowl without hesitation. Just then, the Black woman steps onto the porch and says:

“Missus, a sheet is missing.”

“A sheet missing! Well, for heaven’s sake!”

“I will plug those holes today,” Uncle Silas says, appearing dejected.

“Oh, be quiet! Do you suppose the rats took the sheet? Where has it gone, Lize?”

“I truly have no idea, Miss Sally. It was on the clothesline yesterday, but it is gone; it is not there anymore.”

“I believe the world is ending. I have never seen anything like it in my entire life. A shirt, and a sheet, and a spoon, and six can—”

“Missus,” a young light-skinned girl interrupts, “a brass candlestick is missing.”

“Get out of here, you hussy, or I will throw a skillet at you!”

She was absolutely furious."
}

“Well, it isn’t your fault if you haven’t, Silas; you would have if you could, I suppose. And the shirt isn’t all that’s gone, either. There’s a spoon missing; and that isn’t all. There were ten, and now there are only nine. The calf might have taken the shirt, I suppose, but the calf never took the spoon, that’s certain.”

“Why, what else is gone, Sally?”

“There are six candles gone—that’s what. The rats could have taken the candles, and I suppose they did; I’m surprised they don’t walk off with the whole house, the way you’re always promising to plug their holes and never do it; and if they weren’t fools they’d sleep in your hair, Silas—you’d never even notice; but you can’t blame the spoon on the rats, and that I know.”

“Well, Sally, I’m at fault, and I admit it; I’ve been negligent; but I won’t let tomorrow go by without blocking up those holes.”

“Oh, I wouldn’t rush; next year will do. Matilda Angelina Araminta Phelps!”

Whack comes the thimble, and the child snatches her hand out of the sugar bowl without wasting any time. Just then the Black woman steps onto the porch and says:

“Missus, there’s a sheet missing.”

“A sheet missing! Well, for heaven’s sake!”

“I’ll block up those holes today,” says Uncle Silas, looking sorrowful.

“Oh, just be quiet! Do you suppose the rats took the sheet? Where did it go, Lize?”

“I honestly have no idea, Miss Sally. It was on the clothesline yesterday, but it’s gone: it isn’t there anymore.”

“I think the world is coming to an end. I’ve never seen anything like it in all my life. A shirt, and a sheet, and a spoon, and six can—”

“Missus,” says a young woman, “there’s a brass candlestick missing.”

“Get out of here, you hussy, or I’ll throw a skillet at you!”

Well, she was absolutely furious."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_028",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“I beg you, Mars Tom—don’t speak that way! I cannot endure it! He would let me push his head into my mouth—that’s a fine favor, isn’t it? I bet he would wait a very long time before I asked him. And besides that, I have no desire for him to sleep in my bed.”

“Jim, stop acting so silly. A prisoner must possess some sort of simple pet, and if a rattlesnake has never been attempted, then there is more honor in you being the first to try it than any other method you could imagine to preserve your life.”

“Why, Mars Tom, I have no need for such honor. If a snake bites Jim’s chin off, then where is the honor? No, sir, I want no part of such business.”

“Confound it, can you not attempt it? I only ask that you try—you do not have to continue if it fails.”

“But the damage is already finished if the snake strikes me while I am testing him. Mars Tom, I am prepared to handle most things that are not unreasonable, but if you and Huck bring a rattlesnake in here for me to domesticate, I am going to depart, that is certain.”

“Very well, then, forget it, forget it, if you are so stubborn regarding the matter. We can obtain some garter snakes, and you can fasten some buttons to their tails, and pretend they are rattlesnakes, and I suppose that will have to suffice.”

“I can tolerate those, Mars Tom, but I swear I could manage without them, I tell you that. I never realized before that it was such a nuisance and hardship to be a prisoner.”

“Well, it is always that way when it is performed correctly. Do you have any rats in this vicinity?”

“No, sir, I have not observed any.”

“Well, we shall procure some rats for you.”

“Why, Mars Tom, I do not want any rats. They are the most confounded creatures for disturbing a person, and scurrying around over him, and biting his feet, while he is attempting to sleep, that I have ever seen. No, sir, give me garter snakes, if I must have them, but do not give me any rats; I have no use for them, hardly.”

“But, Jim, you must have them—they all do. So do not create any more trouble about it. Prisoners are never without rats. There is no example of it. And they train them, and pet them, and teach them tricks, and they become as friendly as flies. But you must play music for them. Do you possess anything to play music upon?”

“I have nothing but a coarse comb and a piece of paper, and a jews-harp; but I suspect they would not care for a jews-harp.”

“Yes, they would. They do not mind what variety of music it is. A jews-harp is perfectly adequate for a rat. All animals enjoy music—in a prison they adore it. Especially, mournful music; and you cannot produce any other kind from a jews-harp. It always captures their interest; they emerge to see what is wrong with you. Yes, you are prepared; you are situated very well. You should sit on your bed at night before you go to sleep, and early in the mornings, and play your jews-harp; play ‘The Last Link is Broken’—that is the selection that will attract a rat faster than anything else; and when you have played for about two minutes you will observe all the rats, and the snakes, and spiders, and creatures begin to feel concerned for you, and arrive.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_029",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Man the oars—man the oars!”

However, Jim and I were conferring and reflecting. After a moment of thought, I spoke.

“Tell me, Jim.”

He replied:

“Well, then, here is how it appears to me, Huck. If it were him being liberated, and one of the boys were to be shot, would he declare, ‘Proceed and rescue me, never mind a physician to save this one?’ Is that like Master Tom Sawyer? Would he utter that? You can bet he would not! Well, then, is Jim going to say it? No, sir—I am not moving an inch from this spot without a doctor; not even if it takes forty years!”

I understood he possessed a noble heart, and I figured he would speak exactly as he did—so everything was settled, and I informed Tom I was departing to fetch a physician. He made quite a fuss, but Jim and I remained firm and would not yield; he attempted to crawl out and release the raft himself, but we prevented him. He then scolded us, yet it accomplished nothing.

When he observed me preparing the canoe, he remarked:

“Well, then, if you are determined to go, I will instruct you on how to proceed once you reach the town. Close the door, bind the doctor’s eyes securely, and force him to swear to absolute silence. Place a purse of gold in his hand, lead him through the back alleys and everywhere in the darkness, then bring him here by canoe, taking a circuitous route through the islands. Search him and confiscate his chalk, and do not return it until you have brought him back to the village, or he will mark this raft so he can locate it again. That is the standard procedure.”

I agreed, then departed, with Jim instructed to conceal himself in the woods upon the doctor’s arrival until he had left.

CHAPTER XLI.

The physician was an elderly man; a very pleasant, kind-appearing gentleman once I roused him. I explained that my brother and I were hunting on Spanish Island yesterday afternoon and had camped on a section of a raft we discovered. Around midnight, he must have kicked his firearm while dreaming, causing it to discharge and wound his leg. We requested that he travel there to treat it without mentioning a word to anyone, as we intended to return home this evening to surprise our family.

“Who are your people?” he inquired.

“The Phelpses, down that way.”

“Oh,” he said. After a moment, he added:

“How did you say he was shot?”

“He had a dream,” I answered, “and it shot him.”

“A peculiar dream,” he remarked.

He ignited his lantern, retrieved his saddlebags, and we set off. Upon seeing the canoe, however, he appeared uneasy—stating it was large enough for one, but did not seem particularly safe for two. I replied:

“Oh, you need not be afraid, sir; it carried the three of us easily enough.”"
}

However, Jim and I were conferring and reflecting. After a moment of thought, I spoke.

“Tell me, Jim.”

He replied:

“Well, then, here is how it appears to me, Huck. If it were him being liberated, and one of the boys were to be shot, would he declare, ‘Proceed and rescue me, never mind a physician to save this one?’ Is that like Master Tom Sawyer? Would he utter that? You can bet he would not! Well, then, is Jim going to say it? No, sir—I am not moving an inch from this spot without a doctor; not even if it takes forty years!”

I understood he possessed a noble heart, and I figured he would speak exactly as he did—so everything was settled, and I informed Tom I was departing to fetch a physician. He made quite a fuss, but Jim and I remained firm and would not yield; he attempted to crawl out and release the raft himself, but we prevented him. He then scolded us, yet it accomplished nothing.

When he observed me preparing the canoe, he remarked:

“Well, then, if you are determined to go, I will instruct you on how to proceed once you reach the town. Close the door, bind the doctor’s eyes securely, and force him to swear to absolute silence. Place a purse of gold in his hand, lead him through the back alleys and everywhere in the darkness, then bring him here by canoe, taking a circuitous route through the islands. Search him and confiscate his chalk, and do not return it until you have brought him back to the village, or he will mark this raft so he can locate it again. That is the standard procedure.”

I agreed, then departed, with Jim instructed to conceal himself in the woods upon the doctor’s arrival until he had left.

CHAPTER XLI.

The physician was an elderly man; a very pleasant, kind-appearing gentleman once I roused him. I explained that my brother and I were hunting on Spanish Island yesterday afternoon and had camped on a section of a raft we discovered. Around midnight, he must have kicked his firearm while dreaming, causing it to discharge and wound his leg. We requested that he travel there to treat it without mentioning a word to anyone, as we intended to return home this evening to surprise our family.

“Oh, you need not be afraid, sir; it carried the three of us easily enough.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_HUCKLEBERRY_FINN_030",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "As she was leaving, she looked into my eyes, steady and gentle, and said:

“The door won’t be locked, Tom, and there’s the window and the lightning rod; but you’ll be good, won’t you? And you won’t leave? For my sake.”

Heaven knows I wanted to go badly enough to check on Tom, and I had fully intended to; but after she said that, I wouldn’t have left, not for all the kingdoms on earth.

But she was on my mind and Tom was on my mind, so I slept very restlessly. Twice during the night I climbed down the rod and slipped around to the front, seeing her sitting there by her candle in the window, her eyes fixed on the road with tears in them. I wished I could do something for her, but I couldn’t, except to swear that I would never do anything to grieve her again. The third time I woke up at dawn and slid down, she was still there, her candle nearly out, her old gray head resting on her hand as she slept.

CHAPTER XLII.

The old man was back in town before breakfast, but couldn’t find any trace of Tom. They both sat at the table lost in thought, saying nothing, looking mournful, their coffee growing cold as they left their food untouched. Eventually, the old man asked:

“Did I give you the letter?”

“What letter?”

“The one I got yesterday from the post office.”

“No, you didn’t give me any letter.”

“Well, I must have forgotten it.”

He rummaged through his pockets, then went off to where he had set it down, retrieved it, and handed it to her. She said:

“Why, it’s from St. Petersburg—it’s from Sis.”

I thought another walk would do me good, but I couldn’t move. Before she could open it, she dropped it and ran, for she saw something. I saw it too. It was Tom Sawyer on a mattress, accompanied by that old doctor, Jim wearing her calico dress with his hands tied behind him, and a crowd of people. I hid the letter behind the first thing I could find and rushed over. She threw herself at Tom, crying, “Oh, he’s dead, he’s dead, I know he’s dead!”

Tom turned his head slightly and muttered something, showing he wasn’t in his right mind. She threw up her hands and cried, “He’s alive, thank God! And that’s enough!” She snatched a kiss from him and raced toward the house to prepare the bed, shouting orders left and right to the servants and everyone else, her tongue moving as fast as her feet.

I followed the men to see what they intended to do with Jim, while the old doctor and Uncle Silas followed Tom into the house. The men were very angry, and some wanted to hang Jim as an example to all the other enslaved people in the area, so they wouldn’t try to run away like Jim had, causing such a mountain of trouble and keeping an entire family terrified for days and nights."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_001",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Ting-a-ling-ling! Chow-ch-chow-chow!”
 The left hand began tracing circles.

“Halt the starboard! Ting-a-ling-ling! Halt the larboard! Advance the starboard! Stop her! Let the outer wheel rotate slowly! Ting-a-ling-ling! Chow-ow-ow! Throw out that head-line! Move quickly! Come—uncoil your spring-line—what are you doing? Loop the bight around that stump! Prepare the gangplank—release it! Engines finished, sir! Ting-a-ling-ling! SH’T! S’H’T! SH’T!”
(testing the gauge-cocks).

Tom continued whitewashing, ignoring the steamboat. Ben gazed for a second and then remarked: “Hi-Yi! You’re stuck, aren’t you!”

No reply. Tom examined his latest stroke with an artist’s gaze, then brushed again lightly and inspected the outcome, just as before. Ben moved beside him. Tom’s mouth craved the apple, yet he remained focused on his task. Ben spoke:

“Hello, friend, you’re forced to work, huh?”

Tom spun around and replied:

“Oh, it’s you, Ben! I didn’t notice.”

“Listen—I’m heading off to swim. Don’t you wish you could? But naturally you’d prefer to work—wouldn’t you? Of course you would!”

Tom studied the boy for a moment and asked:

“What do you define as work?”

“Well, isn’t that work?”

Tom went back to his whitewashing, replying nonchalantly:

“Well, perhaps it is, and perhaps it isn’t. All I know is, it pleases Tom Sawyer.”

“Oh, come on, you don’t expect me to believe you enjoy it?”

The brush kept moving.

“Enjoy it? Well, I don’t see why I shouldn’t. Does a boy get the opportunity to whitewash a fence every day?”

That cast the situation in a different light. Ben ceased chewing his apple. Tom moved his brush gracefully back and forth—stepped away to observe the result—added a stroke here and there—assessed the effect again—Ben observing every motion and becoming increasingly intrigued and captivated. Soon he said:

“Say, Tom, let me whitewash a bit.”

Tom pondered, nearly agreeing, but changed his mind:

“No—no—I don’t think that would work, Ben. You see, Aunt Polly is extremely strict regarding this fence—right here on the street, you know—but if it were the back fence, I wouldn’t mind, and neither would she. Yes, she is very particular about this fence; it must be done with great precision; I suspect there isn’t one boy in a thousand, maybe two thousand, who can do it the way it requires.”

“No—is that so? Oh, come on—let me just try. Only a little—I’d let you, if you were me, Tom.”

“Ben, I’d like to, truly; but Aunt Polly—well, Jim wanted to do it, but she refused; Sid wanted to do it, and she wouldn’t allow Sid. Now don’t you see my predicament? If you were to attempt this fence and anything went wrong—”

“Oh, nonsense, I’ll be just as careful. Now let me try. Say—I’ll give you the core of my apple.”

“Well, here—No, Ben, don’t. I’m afraid—”

“I’ll give you the whole thing!”

Tom surrendered the brush with feigned reluctance but internal eagerness. And while the former steamer Big Missouri labored and perspired in the heat, the retired artist sat on a nearby barrel in the shade, swung his legs, ate his apple, and plotted the downfall of more unsuspecting victims."
}

parts.text:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_001",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Ting-a-ling-ling! Chow-ch-chow-chow!”
 The left hand began tracing circles.

“Halt the starboard! Ting-a-ling-ling! Halt the larboard! Advance the starboard! Stop her! Let the outer wheel rotate slowly! Ting-a-ling-ling! Chow-ow-ow! Throw out that head-line! Move quickly! Come—uncoil your spring-line—what are you doing? Loop the bight around that stump! Prepare the gangplank—release it! Engines finished, sir! Ting-a-ling-ling! SH’T! S’H’T! SH’T!”
(testing the gauge-cocks).

Tom continued whitewashing, ignoring the steamboat. Ben gazed for a second and then remarked: “Hi-Yi! You’re stuck, aren’t you!”

No reply. Tom examined his latest stroke with an artist’s gaze, then brushed again lightly and inspected the outcome, just as before. Ben moved beside him. Tom’s mouth craved the apple, yet he remained focused on his task. Ben spoke:

“Hello, friend, you’re forced to work, huh?”

Tom spun around and replied:

“Oh, it’s you, Ben! I didn’t notice.”

“Listen—I’m heading off to swim. Don’t you wish you could? But naturally you’d prefer to work—wouldn’t you? Of course you would!”

Tom studied the boy for a moment and asked:

“What do you define as work?”

“Well, isn’t that work?”

Tom went back to his whitewashing, replying nonchalantly:

“Well, perhaps it is, and perhaps it isn’t. All I know is, it pleases Tom Sawyer.”

“Oh, come on, you don’t expect me to believe you enjoy it?”

The brush kept moving.

“Enjoy it? Well, I don’t see why I shouldn’t. Does a boy get the opportunity to whitewash a fence every day?”

That cast the situation in a different light. Ben ceased chewing his apple. Tom moved his brush gracefully back and forth—stepped away to observe the result—added a stroke here and there—assessed the effect again—Ben observing every motion and becoming increasingly intrigued and captivated. Soon he said:

“Say, Tom, let me whitewash a bit.”

Tom pondered, nearly agreeing, but changed his mind:

“No—no—I don’t think that would work, Ben. You see, Aunt Polly is extremely strict regarding this fence—right here on the street, you know—but if it were the back fence, I wouldn’t mind, and neither would she. Yes, she is very particular about this fence; it must be done with great precision; I suspect there isn’t one boy in a thousand, maybe two thousand, who can do it the way it requires.”

“No—is that so? Oh, come on—let me just try. Only a little—I’d let you, if you were me, Tom.”

“Ben, I’d like to, truly; but Aunt Polly—well, Jim wanted to do it, but she refused; Sid wanted to do it, and she wouldn’t allow Sid. Now don’t you see my predicament? If you were to attempt this fence and anything went wrong—”

“Oh, nonsense, I’ll be just as careful. Now let me try. Say—I’ll give you the core of my apple.”

“Well, here—No, Ben, don’t. I’m afraid—”

“I’ll give you the whole thing!”

Tom surrendered the brush with feigned reluctance but internal eagerness. And while the former steamer Big Missouri labored and perspired in the heat, the retired artist sat on a nearby barrel in the shade, swung his legs, ate his apple, and plotted the downfall of more unsuspecting victims."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_001",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“Ding-a-ling-ling! Chug-ch-chug-chug!”
His left hand began to trace circles.

“Stop the starboard! Ding-a-ling-ling! Stop the port! Come ahead on the starboard! Stop her! Let your outside turn over slowly! Ding-a-ling-ling! Chug-ug-ug! Get out that head-line! Lively now! Come—out with your spring-line—what are you doing there! Take a turn around that stump with the loop of it! Stand by that gangplank, now—let her go! Finished with the engines, sir! Ding-a-ling-ling! SHH! SHH! SHH!” (testing the gauge-cocks).

Tom kept on whitewashing—paying no attention to the steamboat. Ben stared for a moment and then said: “Hi-yi! You’re in a tough spot, aren’t you!”

No answer. Tom examined his last stroke with an artist’s eye, then he gave his brush another gentle sweep and surveyed the result, just as before. Ben moved up alongside him. Tom’s mouth watered for the apple, but he stuck to his work. Ben said:

“Hello, old buddy, you’ve got to work, huh?”

Tom turned suddenly and said:

“Oh, it’s you, Ben! I didn’t notice you.”

“Say—I’m going swimming, I am. Don’t you wish you could? But of course you’d rather work—wouldn’t you? Of course you would!”

Tom looked at the boy for a moment and said:

“What do you call work?”

“Well, isn’t that work?”

Tom resumed his whitewashing and answered casually:

“Well, maybe it is, and maybe it isn’t. All I know is, it suits Tom Sawyer.”

“Oh come on, you don’t mean to tell me that you like it?”

The brush kept moving.

“Like it? Well, I don’t see why I shouldn’t like it. Does a boy get a chance to whitewash a fence every day?”

That put the situation in a new light. Ben stopped nibbling his apple. Tom swept his brush daintily back and forth—stepped back to note the effect—added a touch here and there—critiqued the result again—Ben watching every move and getting more and more interested, more and more absorbed. Finally, he said:

“Say, Tom, let me whitewash a little.”

Tom considered, and was about to agree, but he changed his mind:

“No—no—I don’t think that would really work, Ben. You see, Aunt Polly is extremely particular about this fence—right here on the street, you know—but if it were the back fence I wouldn’t mind and she wouldn’t either. Yes, she’s very particular about this fence; it has to be done very carefully; I don’t think there is one boy in a thousand, maybe two thousand, who can do it the way it has to be done.”

“No—is that so? Oh come on—let me just try. Only just a little—I’d let you, if you were me, Tom.”

“Ben, I’d like to, I promise; but Aunt Polly—well, Jim wanted to do it, but she wouldn’t let him; Sid wanted to do it, and she wouldn’t let Sid. Now don’t you see how I’m stuck? If you were to take on this fence and anything happened to it—”

“Oh, forget it, I’ll be just as careful. Now let me try. Say—I’ll give you the core of my apple.”

“Well, here—No, Ben, don’t. I’m afraid—”

“I’ll give you all of it!”

Tom handed over the brush with reluctance on his face, but eagerness in his heart. And while the former steamer Big Missouri worked and sweated in the sun, the retired artist sat on a barrel in the shade nearby, dangled his legs, munched his apple, and planned the downfall of more victims."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_002",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "It was true that the knife couldn't cut anything, but it was a genuine Barlow, and there was unimaginable prestige in that—though why the boys out West ever thought such a tool could be faked to its detriment is a profound mystery that will likely always remain one. Tom managed to scratch up the cupboard with it and was planning to start on the dresser when he was called away to get dressed for Sunday school.

Mary handed him a tin basin of water and a bar of soap. He went outside, set the basin on a small bench, dipped the soap in the water, and set it aside. He rolled up his sleeves, poured the water onto the ground, and walked back into the kitchen, where he began to vigorously wipe his face with the towel hanging behind the door. Mary took the towel away and said, "Aren't you ashamed, Tom? You shouldn't be so difficult. Water won't hurt you."

Tom felt a bit flustered. The basin was refilled, and this time he stood over it for a moment to gather his courage, took a deep breath, and started. When he walked into the kitchen a moment later, eyes squeezed shut and hands groping for the towel, his face was dripping with a respectable amount of soap and water. However, when he finished drying off, he still wasn't clean enough; the scrubbed area stopped abruptly at his chin and jawline like a mask. Below that line, a dark expanse of unwashed skin stretched down his chest and around his neck. Mary took over, and when she finished, he was a clean, presentable boy. His soaked hair was neatly brushed, with the short curls styled into a tidy, symmetrical look. He secretly smoothed out the curls with great effort, plastering his hair flat against his head, as he considered curls to be girly, and his own hair made him miserable. Mary then brought out his Sunday suit—the only other clothes he owned, which gives you an idea of the size of his wardrobe. She finished getting him ready after he dressed himself; she buttoned his neat jacket to his chin, folded his large shirt collar over his shoulders, brushed him off, and placed his speckled straw hat on his head. He looked much better, though he felt incredibly uncomfortable. He was just as miserable as he looked, as the restriction of clean, formal clothes irritated him. He hoped Mary would forget his shoes, but that hope was crushed; she greased them thoroughly with tallow, as was the custom, and brought them out. He lost his temper, complaining that he was always forced to do things he didn't want to do. But Mary said persuasively, "Please, Tom—be a good boy."

So, he put on the shoes while grumbling. Mary was soon ready, and the three children left for Sunday school—a place Tom hated with all his heart, though Sid and Mary enjoyed it. Sunday school lasted from nine until ten-thirty, followed by the church service."
}
