model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_003",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "And so it continued. There is no need to record the remainder of the address. It followed a standard, unchanging format, making it well-known to everyone present.

The final portion of the talk was disrupted by the return of scuffles and other diversions among the mischievous boys, along with fidgeting and murmurs that spread throughout the room, eventually reaching even the most steadfast and well-behaved students like Sid and Mary. However, every noise vanished abruptly when Mr. Walters stopped speaking, and the end of his address was greeted with a wave of quiet relief.

Much of the murmuring had been triggered by a somewhat unusual occurrence: the arrival of guests. Lawyer Thatcher entered with a frail, elderly man, a distinguished, stout gentleman of middle age with iron-gray hair, and a stately woman who was clearly the gentleman's wife. The woman was guiding a young girl. Tom had been agitated and filled with complaints; he also felt guilty, unable to face Amy Lawrence or endure her affectionate look. Yet, upon seeing this new girl, his spirit was instantly ignited with joy. Immediately, he began to display his prowess with great intensity—striking other boys, tugging hair, and pulling faces—employing every tactic he thought might charm a girl and gain her admiration. His delight was only slightly dampened by the memory of his embarrassment in this girl's garden, but that recollection was rapidly fading beneath the tides of happiness now washing over him.

The guests were seated in the most prominent place of honor, and once Mr. Walters concluded his speech, he presented them to the students. The middle-aged man proved to be a remarkable figure—none other than the county judge—the most imposing person these children had ever beheld. They marveled at his nature, half-hoping to hear him speak with authority while simultaneously fearing he might. He hailed from Constantinople, twelve miles distant, meaning he had journeyed and experienced the world; he had even gazed upon the county courthouse, which was rumored to have a roof made of tin. The reverence these thoughts provoked was evident in the profound silence and the rows of wide-eyed children. This was the renowned Judge Thatcher, the brother of their local lawyer. Jeff Thatcher promptly stepped forward to associate with the great man and draw the envy of his classmates. It would have delighted him to overhear the hushed comments:

“Observe him, Jim! He is heading up there. Look—he is going to shake his hand—he is actually shaking hands with him! Goodness, don't you wish you were Jeff?”

Mr. Walters began to perform for the audience, engaging in various official displays and busywork, issuing commands, making rulings, and giving instructions wherever he could find an opportunity. The librarian also put on a show, rushing back and forth with his arms burdened by books, creating the sort of frantic commotion that petty authority enjoys. The young female teachers likewise performed, leaning gracefully over students they had recently disciplined, raising delicate fingers to warn naughty boys while affectionately patting the well-behaved ones."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_004",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Amen.

Fabric rustled as the standing crowd took their seats. The lad whose story this narrative follows did not find pleasure in the prayer; he merely tolerated it, if he managed even that. He remained fidgety throughout; he tracked the prayer’s components without thinking, as he was not truly attending, yet he was familiar with the minister’s customary path. Whenever a small bit of novel content was inserted, his ears caught it and his entire being bristled; he viewed such additions as dishonest and villainous. During the prayer, a fly landed on the pew ahead of him, tormenting his spirit by serenely rubbing its hands, clasping its head, and scrubbing it so intensely that it seemed nearly detached from its frame, revealing its thin neck. It scraped its wings with its hind legs, smoothing them against its sides like coat-tails, performing its entire grooming routine as calmly as if it understood it was secure. It was indeed safe; though Tom’s hands ached to snatch it, he dared not, fearing his soul would be immediately ruined if he committed such an act during the prayer. Yet, as the final sentence concluded, his hand began to arch and creep forward, and the moment the word “Amen” was spoken, the fly became a captive. His aunt noticed the deed and forced him to release it.

The preacher announced his text and droned on in a dull tone through a discourse so tedious that many heads eventually began to droop, even though the argument concerned endless fire and brimstone, narrowing the predestined chosen few to a group so tiny it hardly seemed worth the effort of salvation. Tom tallied the sermon’s pages; after the service, he could always state how many pages there were, though he rarely recalled anything else regarding the speech. Still, he was briefly engaged this time. The minister painted a grand, stirring image of the world’s multitudes gathering at the millennium, where the lion and the lamb would rest together and a small child would guide them. Yet the emotion, the instruction, and the moral of the magnificent scene were wasted on the boy; he only considered how prominent the main figure would be before the watching nations. His face brightened at the notion, and he mused that he would like to be that child, provided the lion was tame.

He returned to his misery as the dry lecture resumed. Soon, he remembered a prize he possessed and retrieved it. It was a large, dark beetle with intimidating mandibles—a “pinchbug,” as he termed it. It was kept inside a percussion-cap container. The beetle immediately bit his finger. A reflexive flick followed, the insect tumbled into the aisle and landed on its back, and the wounded finger went into the boy’s mouth. The beetle remained there, struggling with its helpless legs, unable to right itself."
}

Tom counted the pages of the sermon; after church, he always knew how many pages there had been, though he rarely remembered anything else about the speech. However, this time he was genuinely interested for a short while. The minister painted a grand, moving picture of the world’s people gathering at the millennium, when the lion and the lamb would lie down together and a little child would lead them. But the emotion, the lesson, and the moral of the great scene were lost on the boy. He only thought about how much attention that main character would get in front of the watching nations. His face lit up at the thought, and he told himself he wished he could be that child, provided the lion was tame.

He soon returned to suffering as the dry argument resumed. Before long, he remembered a treasure he had and took it out. It was a large black beetle with powerful jaws—a “pinchbug,” as he called it. It was kept in a small percussion-cap box. The first thing the beetle did was bite his finger. Tom instinctively flicked it, and the beetle went tumbling into the aisle, landing on its back, while the boy put his hurt finger into his mouth. The beetle lay there, waving its helpless legs, unable to turn over."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_005",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "He drifted off, feeling like a hero stripped of his glory. Before long, Tom encountered the village’s young outcast, Huckleberry Finn, the offspring of the local drunk. Mothers throughout the town deeply loathed and feared Huckleberry, viewing him as lazy, rebellious, crude, and wicked. They also resented that their own children idolized him, relished his forbidden company, and secretly wished they had the courage to emulate him. Like the other well-behaved boys, Tom envied Huckleberry’s flashy, marginalized lifestyle and was strictly forbidden from associating with him. Consequently, he spent time with him whenever the opportunity arose. Huckleberry was perpetually clad in discarded adult garments that were constantly tattered and flapping with rags. His hat was a massive wreck with a large chunk missing from the brim; his coat, when he bothered to wear one, reached nearly to his ankles with buttons positioned low on the back; a single suspender held up his trousers, which sagged empty in the seat, their frayed hems trailing in the dust unless he rolled them up.

Huckleberry lived entirely on his own terms. He spent warm nights sleeping on porches and rainy ones inside empty barrels; he was exempt from school, church, and the authority of any master; he could swim or fish whenever and wherever he liked, for as long as he pleased; no one stopped him from fighting; he could stay awake as late as he wanted; he was invariably the first boy to go barefoot each spring and the last to wear shoes in the autumn; he never had to bathe or change into fresh clothing; and he possessed a remarkable talent for swearing. In short, that boy enjoyed everything that made existence worthwhile. That was the consensus among every burdened, restricted, respectable boy in St. Petersburg.

Tom greeted the adventurous pariah:

“Hi, Huckleberry!”

“Hi to you, and see how you enjoy it.”

“What are you carrying?”

“A dead cat.”

“Let me look at him, Huck. Wow, he’s quite rigid. Where did you find him?”

“I purchased him from a boy.”

“What did you trade?”

“I gave him a blue ticket and a bladder I picked up at the slaughterhouse.”

“Where did you get that blue ticket?”

“I bought it from Ben Rogers a fortnight ago for a hoop-stick.”

“Tell me—what are dead cats useful for, Huck?”

“Useful for? They cure warts.”

“Really! Is that true? I know of something even better.”

“I doubt it. What is it?”

“Well, spunk-water.”

“Spunk-water! I wouldn’t give a damn for spunk-water.”

“You wouldn’t, really? Have you ever tested it?”

“No, I haven’t. But Bob Tanner did.”

“Who told you that?”

“Well, he told Jeff Thatcher, and Jeff told Johnny Baker, and Johnny told Jim Hollis, and Jim told Ben Rogers, and Ben told a Black man, and the Black man told me. There!”

“So what? They all lie. At least, all of them except the Black man. I don’t know him. But I’ve never met a Black man who wouldn’t lie. Pshaw! Now explain how Bob Tanner did it, Huck.”

“Well, he dipped his hand into a decaying tree stump filled with rainwater.”

“During the day?”

“Of course.”

“Facing the stump?”

“Yes. At least, I assume so.”"
}

His hat was a massive wreck with a wide crescent cut out of the brim; his coat, when he wore one, hung nearly to his heels with the back buttons positioned far down; a single suspender held up his trousers, which sagged in the seat and were empty, their frayed legs dragging in the dirt unless rolled up.

Huckleberry came and went as he pleased. He slept on doorsteps in good weather and in empty barrels when it rained; he didn't have to attend school or church, answer to any master, or obey anyone. He could go fishing or swimming whenever and wherever he chose, staying as long as he liked; no one stopped him from fighting; he could stay up as late as he wanted; he was always the first boy to go barefoot in the spring and the last to wear shoes in the fall; he never had to wash or wear clean clothes; and he could swear impressively. In short, that boy had everything that makes life worth living. At least, that is what every stressed, restricted, respectable boy in St. Petersburg thought.

Tom called out to the romantic outcast:

“Hello, Huckleberry!”

“Hello yourself, and see how you like it.”

“What’s that you’ve got?”

“A dead cat.”

“Let me see him, Huck. Wow, he’s pretty stiff. Where did you get him?”

“Bought him from a boy.”

“What did you give for him?”

“I gave a blue ticket and a bladder I got at the slaughterhouse.”

“Where did you get the blue ticket?”

“Bought it from Ben Rogers two weeks ago for a hoop-stick.”

“Say—what are dead cats good for, Huck?”

“Good for? Curing warts.”

“No! Is that true? I know something better.”

“I bet you don’t. What is it?”

“Well, spunk-water.”

“Spunk-water! I wouldn’t give a damn for spunk-water.”

“You wouldn’t, really? Have you ever tried it?”

“No, I haven’t. But Bob Tanner did.”

“Who told you that?”

“Well, he told Jeff Thatcher, and Jeff told Johnny Baker, and Johnny told Jim Hollis, and Jim told Ben Rogers, and Ben told a Black man, and the man told me. There now!”

“Well, so what? They’ll all lie. At least all of them except the Black man. I don’t know him. But I’ve never seen a Black man who wouldn’t lie. Forget it! Now tell me how Bob Tanner did it, Huck.”

“Well, he dipped his hand into a rotten stump filled with rainwater.”

“In the daytime?”

“Certainly.”

“With his face toward the stump?”

“Yes. At least, I think so.”"

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_006",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Oh, will you? When?”

“At midday. Do you go home for your meal?”

“I’ll remain if you do.”

“Fine—that’s a deal. What is your name?”

“Becky Thatcher. What is yours? Oh, I know. It’s Thomas Sawyer.”

“That is the name they use when they whip me. I’m Tom when I behave. You call me Tom, will you?”

“Yes.”

Now Tom started to scribble something on the slate, concealing the writing from the girl. But she was not hesitant this time. She pleaded to look. Tom remarked:

“Oh, it isn’t anything.”

“Yes it is.”

“No it isn’t. You don’t want to see.”

“Yes I do, truly I do. Please let me.”

“You’ll tell.”

“No I won’t—truly and truly and double truly I won’t.”

“You won’t tell anyone at all? Ever, as long as you live?”

“No, I won’t ever tell anyone. Now let me.”

“Oh, you don’t want to see!”

“Now that you act this way, I will see.” And she placed her small hand upon his and a brief struggle followed, Tom feigning to resist seriously but allowing his hand to slip bit by bit until these words were shown: “I love you.”

“Oh, you naughty thing!” And she struck his hand a sharp blow, yet flushed and appeared delighted, nonetheless.

Just at this moment the boy felt a slow, inevitable grip tightening on his ear, and a constant lifting force. In that manner he was carried across the room and placed in his own chair, amidst a barrage of laughter from the entire class. Then the teacher stood above him for a few dreadful moments, and eventually walked away to his desk without uttering a word. But although Tom’s ear throbbed, his spirit was triumphant.

As the classroom grew quiet Tom made a sincere attempt to focus, but the agitation inside him was too intense. In turn he joined the reading group and performed poorly; then in the geography group and transformed lakes into mountains, mountains into rivers, and rivers into continents, until total confusion reigned; then in the spelling group, and was defeated by a series of simple words, until he ended up at the bottom and surrendered the metal badge which he had displayed with pride for months.


CHAPTER VII


The more Tom attempted to fix his attention on his textbook, the more his thoughts drifted. So finally, with a sigh and a yawn, he abandoned the effort. It felt to him that the noon break would never arrive. The atmosphere was completely stagnant. There was not a breeze moving. It was the drowsiest of drowsy days. The sleepy drone of the twenty-five studying students calmed the spirit like the charm found in the humming of bees. Far away in the blazing sunlight, Cardiff Hill raised its gentle green slopes through a shimmering haze of heat, colored with the purple of distance; a few birds drifted on idle wings high in the sky; no other living creature was seen except for some cows, and they were slumbering. Tom’s heart longed to be liberated, or else to have something engaging to do to occupy the tedious time. His hand reached into his pocket and his expression brightened with a warmth of thankfulness that was a prayer, though he did not realize it."
}

{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_006",

  "rewritten_text": "“Oh, will you? When?”

“At noon. Do you go home for lunch?”

“I’ll stay if you do.”

“Great—it’s a deal. What’s your name?”

“Becky Thatcher. What’s yours? Oh, I know. It’s Thomas Sawyer.”

“That’s the name they use when they punish me. I’m Tom when I’m behaving. You call me Tom, will you?”

“Yes.”

Now Tom began to scribble something on his slate, shielding the words from the girl. But she wasn’t shy this time. She begged to see. Tom said:

“Oh, it’s nothing.”

“Yes, it is.”

“No, it isn’t. You don’t want to see.”

“Yes I do, I really do. Please let me.”

“You’ll tell.”

“No I won’t—I promise, I promise, I promise.”

“You won’t tell anyone at all? Ever, as long as you live?”

“No, I won’t ever tell anyone. Now let me.”

“Oh, you don’t want to see!”

“Since you’re acting like that, I will see.” She placed her small hand over his and a brief struggle followed, Tom pretending to resist while gradually letting his hand slip until these words were revealed: “I love you.”

“Oh, you bad boy!” She gave his hand a sharp tap, but she blushed and looked pleased nonetheless.

Just then, the boy felt a slow, inevitable grip closing on his ear, followed by a steady upward pull. In this manner, he was marched across the room and deposited in his own seat, amidst a barrage of giggles from the entire class. The teacher stood over him for a few terrifying moments, then finally walked back to his desk without saying a word. Even though Tom’s ear stung, his heart was jubilant.

As the school settled down, Tom made a sincere effort to study, but his inner excitement was too intense. When he took his turn in the reading class, he stumbled through it; then, in the geography class, he turned lakes into mountains, mountains into rivers, and rivers into continents, until everything was in total disarray; finally, in the spelling class, he was “turned down” by a series of simple words until he ended up at the back of the line, forced to surrender the pewter medal he had worn so proudly for months.

CHAPTER VII

The harder Tom tried to focus on his book, the more his mind wandered. Finally, with a sigh and a yawn, he gave up. It felt as though the noon break would never arrive. The air was completely still. Not a breeze stirred. It was the sleepiest of days. The drowsy murmur of the twenty-five studying students soothed the soul like the hum of bees. Far off in the blazing sunshine, Cardiff Hill rose with its soft green slopes through a shimmering veil of heat, tinted with the purple of distance; a few birds drifted on lazy wings high above; no other living thing was visible except for some cows, and they were asleep. Tom longed to be free, or at least to have something interesting to do to pass the tedious time. His hand wandered into his pocket, and his face lit up with a glow of gratitude that was a prayer, though he didn’t realize it."
}

  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“Oh, will you? When?”

“At noon. Do you go home for lunch?”

“I’ll stay if you do.”

“Great—it’s a deal. What’s your name?”

“Becky Thatcher. What’s yours? Oh, I know. It’s Thomas Sawyer.”

“That’s the name they use when they punish me. I’m Tom when I’m behaving. You call me Tom, will you?”

“Yes.”

Now Tom began to scribble something on his slate, shielding the words from the girl. But she wasn’t shy this time. She begged to see. Tom said:

“Oh, it’s nothing.”

“Yes, it is.”

“No, it isn’t. You don’t want to see.”

“Yes I do, I really do. Please let me.”

“You’ll tell.”

“No I won’t—I promise, I promise, I promise.”

“You won’t tell anyone at all? Ever, as long as you live?”

“No, I won’t ever tell anyone. Now let me.”

“Oh, you don’t want to see!”

“Since you’re acting like that, I will see.” She placed her small hand over his and a brief struggle followed, Tom pretending to resist while gradually letting his hand slip until these words were revealed: “I love you.”

“Oh, you bad boy!” She gave his hand a sharp tap, but she blushed and looked pleased nonetheless.

Just then, the boy felt a slow, inevitable grip closing on his ear, followed by a steady upward pull. In this manner, he was marched across the room and deposited in his own seat, amidst a barrage of giggles from the entire class. The teacher stood over him for a few terrifying moments, then finally walked back to his desk without saying a word. Even though Tom’s ear stung, his heart was jubilant.

As the school settled down, Tom made a sincere effort to study, but his inner excitement was too intense. When he took his turn in the reading class, he stumbled through it; then, in the geography class, he turned lakes into mountains, mountains into rivers, and rivers into continents, until everything was in total disarray; finally, in the spelling class, he was “turned down” by a series of simple words until he ended up at the back of the line, forced to surrender the pewter medal he had worn so proudly for months.

CHAPTER VII

The harder Tom tried to focus on his book, the more his mind wandered. Finally, with a sigh and a yawn, he gave up. It felt as though the noon break would never arrive. The air was completely still. Not a breeze stirred. It was the sleepiest of days. The drowsy murmur of the twenty-five studying students soothed the soul like the hum of bees. Far off in the blazing sunshine, Cardiff Hill rose with its soft green slopes through a shimmering veil of heat, tinted with the purple of distance; a few birds drifted on lazy wings high above; no other living thing was visible except for some cows, and they were asleep. Tom longed to be free, or at least to have something interesting to do to pass the tedious time. His hand wandered into his pocket, and his face lit up with a glow of gratitude that was a prayer, though he didn’t realize it."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_007",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "She knocked it to the ground. Tom then strode out of the dwelling, crossing the hills and traveling far, with no intention of returning to his lessons that day. Soon, Becky grew suspicious. She hurried to the entrance; he was nowhere to be seen; she rushed to the playground; he was absent. Then she shouted, “Tom! Return, Tom!” She listened closely, but heard no reply. She was left only with quiet and solitude. Consequently, she sat down to weep once more and reproach herself; by then, the students were returning, forcing her to conceal her sorrow, quiet her aching heart, and endure the burden of a long, dismal, painful afternoon, surrounded by peers with whom she could share no grief.

CHAPTER VIII

Tom darted back and forth through alleys until he was safely away from the path of the returning pupils, then settled into a somber trot. He waded across a small stream several times, following a common childhood belief that crossing water would thwart anyone pursuing him. Thirty minutes later, he vanished behind the Douglas estate atop Cardiff Hill, the schoolhouse barely visible in the distant valley below. He pushed into a thick forest, navigated his way to its heart, and rested on a patch of moss beneath a wide-reaching oak. Not a breeze moved; the heavy midday warmth had silenced even the birds; the natural world remained in a stupor, interrupted only by the occasional distant tapping of a woodpecker, which only served to deepen the surrounding quiet and sense of isolation. The boy’s spirit was drenched in sadness; his mood matched his environment perfectly. He sat for a long time, elbows on his knees and chin in his palms, lost in thought. It appeared to him that existence was merely a burden, and he felt a degree of envy for Jimmy Hodges, who had recently passed away; it must be tranquil, he mused, to rest and sleep and dream eternally, with the breeze murmuring through the branches and brushing the grass and blossoms atop one’s grave, free from all worry and pain forever. If only his Sunday-school record were spotless, he would be ready to depart and finish it all. And then, this girl. What had he committed? Nothing. He had intended only the best, yet was treated like a cur—like a common dog. She would regret it one day—perhaps when it was too late. Oh, if only he could perish temporarily!

Yet the resilient heart of a youth cannot remain held in one restricted state for long. Tom soon began to wander unconsciously back toward the matters of this world. What if he simply turned away now and vanished without a trace? What if he traveled far off—to distant, mysterious lands across the oceans—and never returned? How would she feel then! The notion of becoming a circus clown returned to him, only to provoke his disgust. For levity, jests, and colorful costumes were offensive when they encroached upon a spirit elevated into the vague, noble sphere of the romantic."
}

{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_007",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "She knocked it to the floor. Then Tom marched out of the house, heading over the hills and far away, with no intention of returning to school that day. Soon, Becky grew suspicious. She ran to the door, but he was nowhere to be seen; she rushed to the playground, but he was not there either. Then she called out, “Tom! Come back, Tom!” She listened intently, but there was no answer. She was left with nothing but silence and loneliness. She sat down to cry again and blame herself. By then, the other students began to gather, forcing her to hide her grief, quiet her broken heart, and endure the burden of a long, dreary, painful afternoon, with no one among the strangers around her to share her sorrow.

CHAPTER VIII

Tom dodged back and forth through the lanes until he was well away from the path of the returning students, then fell into a moody jog. He crossed a small creek several times, following a common childhood superstition that crossing water would throw off anyone pursuing him. Half an hour later, he disappeared behind the Douglas mansion on the summit of Cardiff Hill, with the schoolhouse barely visible in the valley far behind him. He entered a dense wood, picked his way through the brush to the center, and sat down on a mossy patch under a spreading oak. There wasn't even a breeze stirring; the stagnant midday heat had even silenced the birds. Nature lay in a trance, broken by no sound except the occasional distant hammering of a woodpecker, which only made the surrounding silence and sense of loneliness feel more profound. The boy’s soul was steeped in melancholy, and his feelings were in perfect harmony with his surroundings. He sat for a long time with his elbows on his knees and his chin in his hands, meditating. It seemed to him that life was nothing but trouble, and he more than half-envied Jimmy Hodges, who had recently passed away; it must be very peaceful, he thought, to lie and sleep and dream forever, with the wind whispering through the trees and brushing against the grass and flowers over his grave, with nothing left to worry or grieve about ever again. If only he had a clean Sunday-school record, he would be willing to go and be done with it all. Now, about this girl. What had he done? Nothing. He had meant the best in the world, yet he had been treated like a dog—like a common dog. She would be sorry someday—maybe when it was too late. Ah, if only he could die temporarily!

But the resilient heart of youth cannot be held in one constrained shape for long. Tom soon began to drift back into the concerns of daily life. What if he just turned his back now and disappeared mysteriously? What if he went away—ever so far away, into unknown countries across the seas—and never came back? How would she feel then? The idea of becoming a circus clown crossed his mind, only to fill him with disgust. Frivolity, jokes, and spotted tights were offensive when they intruded upon a spirit that was elevated into the vague, grand realm of the romantic."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_008",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Hurry, Tom, hurry! Who is he talking about?”

“Huck, he must mean both of us—we are standing right here.”

“Oh, Tom, I think we are finished. I know exactly where I am headed. I have been so sinful.”

“Darn it! This is the result of skipping school and doing everything a boy is told not to do. I could have been well-behaved, like Sid, if I had tried—but no, I refused, of course. But if I ever escape this situation, I swear I will immerse myself in Sunday-schools!” And Tom started to sniffle slightly.

“You are bad!” and Huckleberry began to sniffle as well. “Confound it, Tom Sawyer, you are a saint compared to me. Oh, lordy, lordy, lordy, I wish I only had half your opportunity.”

Tom stopped and whispered:

“Look, Hucky, look! He has his back turned toward us!”

Huck looked, feeling joyful.

“Well, he does, by golly! Did he before?”

“Yes, he did. But I, like an idiot, never realized. Oh, this is great, you know. Now who could he be referring to?”

The howling ceased. Tom listened intently.

“Shh! What is that?” he whispered.

“It sounds like—like pigs grunting. No—it is someone snoring, Tom.”

“That is it! Where is it coming from, Huck?”

“I believe it is at the other end. It sounds like it, anyway. My father used to sleep there sometimes, alongside the hogs, but heavens, he really makes a racket when he snores. Besides, I do not think he is ever returning to this town again.”

The spirit of adventure returned to the boys’ hearts once more.

“Hucky, do you dare to go if I lead the way?”

“I do not like to, much. Tom, what if it is Injun Joe!”

Tom flinched. But soon the temptation grew powerful again and the boys decided to try, with the agreement that they would run away if the snoring stopped. So they crept stealthily down, one following the other. When they reached a point five steps from the sleeper, Tom stepped on a twig, and it snapped loudly. The man groaned, shifted slightly, and his face was revealed in the moonlight. It was Muff Potter. The boys’ hearts had frozen, along with their hopes, when the man moved, but their anxiety vanished now. They crept out through the damaged siding and paused a short distance away to share a final word. That long, mournful howl rose into the night air once more! They turned and saw the mysterious dog standing a few feet from where Potter lay, facing Potter, with his snout aimed toward the sky.

“Oh, goodness, it is him!” the boys exclaimed simultaneously.

“Say, Tom—they say a stray dog came howling around Johnny Miller’s house, around midnight, about two weeks ago; and a whippoorwill flew in and landed on the railing and sang, that very same evening; and nobody has died there yet.”

“Well, I know that. And suppose nobody has. Did Gracie Miller not fall into the kitchen fire and burn herself badly the very next Saturday?”

“Yes, but she is not dead. And furthermore, she is recovering, too.”

“Fine, you wait and see. She is doomed, just as certainly as Muff Potter is doomed. That is what the Black folks say, and they understand all about these sorts of things, Huck.”"
}

Tom flinched. But soon the temptation grew powerful again and the boys decided to try, with the agreement that they would run away if the snoring stopped. So they crept stealthily down, one following the other. When they reached a point five steps from the sleeper, Tom stepped on a twig, and it snapped loudly. The man groaned, shifted slightly, and his face was revealed in the moonlight. It was Muff Potter. The boys’ hearts had frozen, along with their hopes, when the man moved, but their anxiety vanished now. They crept out through the damaged siding and paused a short distance away to share a final word. That long, mournful howl rose into the night air once more! They turned and saw the mysterious dog standing a few feet from where Potter lay, facing Potter, with his snout aimed toward the sky.

“Oh, goodness, it is him!” the boys exclaimed simultaneously.

“Say, Tom—they say a stray dog came howling around Johnny Miller’s house, around midnight, about two weeks ago; and a whippoorwill flew in and landed on the railing and sang, that very same evening; and nobody has died there yet.”

“Well, I know that. And suppose nobody has. Did Gracie Miller not fall into the kitchen fire and burn herself badly the very next Saturday?”

“Yes, but she is not dead. And furthermore, she is recovering, too.”

“Fine, you wait and see. She is doomed, just as certainly as Muff Potter is doomed. That is what the Black folks say, and they understand all about these sorts of things, Huck.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_009",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“What incredible nerve!” remarked an observer; “I suppose he intended to inspect his handiwork in peace, never anticipating an audience.”

The onlookers parted as the Sheriff arrived, conspicuously escorting Potter by the arm. The wretched man appeared gaunt, his gaze reflecting the terror gripping him. Upon facing the corpse, he trembled violently, then buried his face in his palms and wept openly.

“I am innocent, neighbors,” he cried; “I swear on my life I did not do it.”

“Who has blamed you?” someone called out.

This remark struck a nerve. Potter raised his head, scanning the crowd with a look of miserable despair. Catching sight of Injun Joe, he cried out, “Oh, Injun Joe, you gave me your word you would never—”

“Is this your blade?” the Sheriff asked, holding it out to him.

Potter would have collapsed had he not been supported and lowered to the ground. He murmured, “A feeling told me that if I did not return to retrieve—” He shivered, then gestured weakly with a defeated hand, adding, “Explain it, Joe, tell them—it is pointless now.”

Huckleberry and Tom stood frozen in silence, listening as the cold-blooded deceiver spun his calm narrative. They waited for the heavens to strike him down with divine fire, marveling at the delay. When he concluded his tale unscathed, their wavering resolve to break their vow and rescue the victim dissolved; clearly, this villain had pledged his soul to the Devil, and interfering with such a dark entity would be suicidal.

“Why did you not flee? What brought you back here?” someone inquired.

“I was unable to stop myself,” Potter lamented. “I desired to escape, yet I felt drawn only to this spot.” He began to sob once more.

Injun Joe recounted his story with equal composure during the inquest shortly after, swearing to its truth. Observing that the lightning still failed to strike, the boys grew convinced that Joe had indeed bartered with Satan. He became, in their eyes, the most terrifyingly compelling figure they had ever beheld, and they could not tear their mesmerized gazes from him.

They silently vowed to keep watch over him at night, hoping to catch a glimpse of his sinister master.

Injun Joe assisted in lifting the victim’s body into a wagon for transport; a rumor rippled through the trembling crowd that the wound had bled slightly! The boys hoped this sign would shift the blame appropriately, but they were let down when several villagers noted, “It bled while it was within three feet of Muff Potter.”

Tom’s terrifying secret and troubled conscience haunted his rest for nearly a week afterward; then, one morning at breakfast, Sid commented, “Tom, you toss and talk in your sleep so much that you keep me awake half the night.”

Tom turned pale and looked away."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_009",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“What incredible nerve!” a bystander remarked. “I suppose he wanted to get a quiet look at his handiwork and didn’t expect to run into anyone.”

The crowd parted as the Sheriff walked through, conspicuously leading Potter by the arm. The poor man looked haggard, his eyes wide with terror. Standing before the corpse, he trembled violently, then covered his face with his hands and began to cry.

“I didn’t do it, everyone,” he sobbed. “I swear on my life, I never did it.”

“Who accused you?” someone shouted.

The question hit home. Potter looked up, his eyes filled with pathetic hopelessness. Seeing Injun Joe, he cried out, “Oh, Injun Joe, you promised me you’d never—”

“Is this your knife?” the Sheriff asked, thrusting it in front of him.

Potter would have collapsed if they hadn’t caught him and lowered him to the ground. He muttered, “Something told me that if I didn’t come back and get—” He shuddered, then waved a limp hand in defeat. “Tell them, Joe, tell them—it’s no use anymore.”

Tom and Huckleberry stood frozen, staring as the cold-blooded liar calmly spun his story. They expected the clear sky to strike him down with divine lightning at any second, and they were shocked by the delay. When he finished and remained standing, alive and unharmed, their wavering urge to break their oath and save the betrayed prisoner vanished. Clearly, this villain had sold his soul to Satan, and it would be dangerous to interfere with someone under such protection.

“Why didn’t you leave? Why did you come back here?” someone asked.

“I couldn’t help it—I couldn’t help it,” Potter moaned. “I wanted to run away, but I couldn’t seem to go anywhere but here.” He began sobbing again.

Injun Joe repeated his story just as calmly a few minutes later during the inquest, under oath. Seeing that the lightning still didn’t strike, the boys were convinced that Joe had indeed sold himself to the devil. He became, to them, the most terrifyingly fascinating person they had ever seen, and they couldn’t take their eyes off him.

They secretly resolved to watch him at night, whenever possible, hoping to catch a glimpse of his dark master.

Injun Joe helped lift the body into a wagon, and a rumor spread through the shuddering crowd that the wound had bled a little. The boys thought this would surely shift suspicion to the right person, but they were disappointed when several villagers noted, “It bled when Muff Potter was within three feet of it.”

Tom’s secret and his guilty conscience ruined his sleep for a week. One morning at breakfast, Sid said, “Tom, you toss and turn and talk in your sleep so much that you keep me awake half the night.”

Tom turned pale and looked down at his plate."
}

Injun Joe repeated his statement just as calmly a few minutes later during the inquest, while under oath. The boys, seeing that the lightning still did not strike, were convinced that Joe had indeed sold himself to the devil. He had become the most terrifyingly fascinating person they had ever seen, and they could not take their eyes off him.

They secretly decided to watch him at night whenever possible, hoping to catch a glimpse of his dark master.

Injun Joe helped lift the murdered man’s body into a wagon to be moved. A rumor spread through the shivering crowd that the wound had bled a little! The boys thought this sign would turn suspicion toward the real killer, but they were disappointed when several villagers remarked, “It was within three feet of Muff Potter when it happened.”

Tom’s secret and guilty conscience disturbed his sleep for about a week after this. Then, one morning at breakfast, Sid said, “Tom, you toss and turn and talk in your sleep so much that you keep me awake half the night.”

Tom turned pale and looked down at his plate."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_010",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The stem of the incriminating teaspoon could be seen beneath the bed skirt. Aunt Polly retrieved it and held it aloft. Tom flinched and lowered his gaze. Aunt Polly hoisted him by his customary grip—his ear—and rapped his skull firmly with her thimble.

“Now, young man, why did you feel the need to treat that unfortunate creature in such a way?”

“I did it out of sympathy for him—because he lacked an aunt.”

“Lacked an aunt! You simpleton. What does that have to do with anything?”

“Everything. Because if he had possessed one, she would have burned him out herself! She would have scorched his insides without any more concern than if he were a person!”

Aunt Polly experienced a sudden twinge of guilt. This presented the situation in a fresh perspective; what constituted cruelty toward a cat might also be cruelty toward a boy. Her resolve began to weaken; she felt regret. Her eyes grew slightly moist, and she placed a hand upon Tom’s head, speaking softly:

“I intended the best for you, Tom. And, Tom, it truly benefited you.”

Tom glanced up at her with a faint glimmer of amusement visible through his solemn expression.

“I know you meant well, Aunty, and I did the same with Peter. It benefited him, too. I have never seen him move with such energy since—”

“Oh, be off with you, Tom, before you irritate me once more. Try to see if you can behave like a good boy for once, and you will not need any further medicine.”

Tom arrived at school early. It had been observed that this unusual behavior had been happening daily of late. And now, as had become his habit, he lingered near the schoolyard gate rather than joining his peers in play. He claimed to be unwell, and his appearance supported this. He attempted to appear as though he were looking everywhere except where he actually was—down the road. Soon, Jeff Thatcher appeared, and Tom’s expression brightened; he watched for a moment, then turned away in disappointment. When Jeff arrived, Tom approached him and cautiously maneuvered toward topics concerning Becky, but the distracted boy failed to take the bait. Tom kept watch, feeling hopeful whenever a fluttering dress appeared, only to resent the wearer as soon as he realized it was not the right girl. Eventually, the dresses stopped appearing, and he sank into a state of hopeless dejection; he walked into the vacant schoolhouse and sat down to brood. Then, one final dress passed through the gate, and Tom’s heart leaped. The next moment, he was outside, behaving like a wild man; shouting, laughing, pursuing other boys, leaping over the fence at the risk of his own safety, performing cartwheels, and standing on his head—executing every daring feat he could imagine, all while keeping a watchful eye to see if Becky Thatcher was observing. Yet she seemed entirely oblivious; she never glanced his way. Was it possible she did not know he was present? He brought his antics into her immediate area; he charged past with a war cry, snatched a boy’s cap, flung it onto the schoolhouse roof, plowed through a cluster of boys, scattering them in every direction, and landed sprawling right before Becky, nearly knocking her over—and she turned, tilting her nose upward, and he heard her remark: “Hmph!”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_010",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "The handle of the incriminating teaspoon was visible beneath the bed skirt. Aunt Polly picked it up and held it aloft. Tom winced and looked down. Aunt Polly hoisted him by his usual handle—his ear—and rapped his head firmly with her thimble.

“Now, young man, why did you want to treat that poor animal that way?”

“I did it out of pity for him—because he didn’t have an aunt.”

“Didn’t have an aunt! You blockhead. What does that have to do with anything?”

“Everything. Because if he’d had one, she would have burned him out herself! She would have roasted his insides out without any more feeling than if he were a person!”

Aunt Polly felt a sudden pang of guilt. This put the matter in a new light; what was cruelty to a cat might also be cruelty to a boy. She began to soften; she felt sorry. Her eyes watered slightly, and she placed her hand on Tom’s head and said gently:

“I meant well, Tom. And, Tom, it really did you good.”

Tom looked up into her face with a faint twinkle peeking through his serious expression.

“I know you meant well, Aunty, and so did I with Peter. It did him good, too. I’ve never seen him move around like that since—”

“Oh, get along with you, Tom, before you annoy me again. Try to see if you can’t be a good boy for once, and you won’t have to take any more medicine.”

Tom reached school early. It had been noticed that this strange behavior had been happening every day lately. And now, as usual, he hung around the schoolyard gate instead of playing with his friends. He claimed to be sick, and he looked the part. He tried to appear as if he were looking everywhere except where he actually was—down the road. Soon, Jeff Thatcher appeared, and Tom’s face brightened; he watched for a moment, then turned away sadly. When Jeff arrived, Tom approached him and carefully tried to steer the conversation toward Becky, but the distracted boy never took the bait. Tom watched and watched, hoping whenever a fluttering dress came into view, and hating the girl as soon as he saw she wasn’t the right one. Finally, the dresses stopped appearing, and he sank hopelessly into a bad mood; he entered the empty schoolhouse and sat down to suffer. Then, one more dress passed through the gate, and Tom’s heart gave a great leap. The next instant, he was outside, acting like a wild man; yelling, laughing, chasing boys, jumping over the fence at the risk of life and limb, doing cartwheels, standing on his head—performing every heroic feat he could imagine, all while keeping a sneaky eye out to see if Becky Thatcher was noticing. But she seemed completely unaware of it all; she never looked. Could it be possible she didn’t know he was there? He took his stunts into her immediate vicinity; he came war-whooping around, snatched a boy’s cap, hurled it onto the schoolhouse roof, plowed through a group of boys, knocking them in every direction, and fell sprawling right under Becky’s nose, nearly knocking her over—and she turned, with her nose in the air, and he heard her say: “Hmph!”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_011",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Once the final crunchy piece of bacon had vanished and the last portion of corn bread was consumed, the boys reclined upon the grass, brimming with satisfaction. They might have sought a shadier spot, yet they refused to forgo the atmospheric charm of their glowing campfire.

“Isn’t it grand?” Joe remarked.

“It’s fantastic!” Tom replied. “How would the other boys react if they saw us now?”

“React? They would be absolutely desperate to join us—wouldn’t they, Hucky?”

“I suppose so,” Huckleberry agreed; “regardless, I am satisfied. I desire nothing superior to this. Usually, I never get my fill of food—and here, nobody can come around to nag or harass me.”

“This is the life for me,” Tom declared. “You aren’t forced to rise in the mornings, nor attend school, or wash, or endure any of that tiresome nonsense. You see, a pirate has no obligations, Joe, while on land, whereas a hermit must spend his time praying constantly, and he never experiences any joy, being entirely alone like that.”

“Oh yes, that is true,” Joe noted, “but I had not considered it much. I would much prefer being a pirate, now that I have experienced it.”

“You see,” Tom explained, “people do not value hermits much these days, unlike in ancient times, but a pirate is always held in high regard. Besides, a hermit is required to sleep on the most uncomfortable surface available, cover his head with sackcloth and ashes, stand out in the rain, and—”

“Why does he put sackcloth and ashes on his head?” Huck asked.

“I am not sure. But they are obligated to do it. Hermits always do. You would be required to do that if you were a hermit.”

“I’ll be darned if I would,” Huck stated.

“Well, what would you do instead?”

“I don’t know. But I would not do that.”

“Why, Huck, you would be forced to. How could you avoid it?”

“Well, I simply would not tolerate it. I would run away.”

“Run away! Well, you would make a pathetic excuse for a hermit. You would be a disgrace.”

The Red-Handed offered no reply, as he was better occupied. He had finished hollowing out a corncob, and now he inserted a weed stem, filled it with tobacco, pressed a glowing coal to the mixture, and exhaled a cloud of aromatic smoke—he was in the peak of luxurious comfort. The other pirates envied this grand vice and privately decided to master it soon. Shortly, Huck asked:

“What do pirates actually do?”

Tom replied:

“Oh, they have a marvelous time—they capture ships, burn them, seize the gold, and hide it in terrifying locations on their island where spirits and such guard it, and they kill everyone on the ships—making them walk the plank.”

“And they bring the women to the island,” Joe added; “they do not kill the women.”

“No,” Tom agreed, “they do not kill the women—they are too chivalrous. And the women are always beautiful, too.”

“And don’t they wear the most magnificent outfits! Oh, absolutely! All gold and silver and diamonds,” Joe said with excitement.

“Who?” Huck asked.

“Why, the pirates.”

Huck examined his own attire despondently.

“I suppose I am not dressed appropriately for a pirate,” he said, with a sorrowful tone in his voice; “but I have nothing else besides these.”"
}

You see, a pirate doesn’t have to do anything, Joe, when he’s on land, but a hermit has to spend a lot of time praying, and he doesn’t have any fun anyway, being all by himself like that.”

“Oh yes, that’s true,” Joe said, “but I hadn’t thought much about it. I’d much rather be a pirate, now that I’ve tried it.”

“You see,” Tom explained, “people don’t care much for hermits these days like they used to in the old times, but a pirate is always respected. And a hermit has to sleep on the hardest surface he can find, put sackcloth and ashes on his head, stand out in the rain, and—”

“Why does he put sackcloth and ashes on his head?” Huck asked.

“I don’t know. But they have to do it. Hermits always do. You’d have to do that if you were a hermit.”

“Darned if I would,” Huck said.

“Well, what would you do?”

“I don’t know. But I wouldn’t do that.”

“Why, Huck, you’d have to. How would you get around it?”

“Well, I just wouldn’t put up with it. I’d run away.”

“Run away! Well, you’d be a pathetic excuse for a hermit. You’d be a disgrace.”

The Red-Handed made no response, as he was better occupied. He had finished hollowing out a corncob, and now he fitted a weed stem into it, packed it with tobacco, and was pressing a coal to the charge, blowing out a cloud of fragrant smoke—he was in the peak of luxurious contentment. The other pirates envied him this majestic vice and secretly resolved to take it up soon. Presently, Huck asked:

“What do pirates do?”

Tom replied:

“Oh, they have a great time—they take ships and burn them, get the money, and bury it in scary places on their island where there are ghosts and things to guard it, and they kill everyone on the ships—make them walk the plank.”

“And they take the women to the island,” Joe added; “they don’t kill the women.”

“No,” Tom agreed, “they don’t kill the women—they’re too noble. And the women are always beautiful, too.”

“And don’t they wear the coolest clothes! Oh man! All gold and silver and diamonds,” Joe said with enthusiasm.

“Who?” Huck asked.

“Why, the pirates.”

Huck looked at his own clothing sadly.

“I guess I’m not dressed like a proper pirate,” he said, with a regretful sadness in his voice; “but I don’t have anything else but these.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_012",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Tom roused his fellow pirates, who dashed off with a cheer, quickly shedding their clothes to chase and wrestle in the clear, shallow water of the white sandbar. They felt no desire for the small town resting far away across the vast expanse of the river. Although a stray current or a slight swell had swept away their raft, they were pleased, viewing its departure as akin to destroying the connection to their former lives. 

Returning to camp invigorated, joyful, and starving, they soon rekindled the fire. Huck discovered a nearby spring of cold, clear water, and the boys fashioned cups from large oak or hickory leaves, deciding that water with such rustic appeal was a fine replacement for coffee. As Joe prepared to slice bacon for their meal, Tom and Huck requested a brief delay; they walked to a likely spot on the riverbank and cast their lines, finding success almost instantly. Before Joe could grow restless, they returned with several fine bass, a pair of sun-perch, and a small catfish—a bounty sufficient for a large family. They fried the fish alongside the bacon and were amazed, as no fish had ever tasted so exquisite. They were unaware that freshwater fish are best when cooked immediately after being caught, nor did they fully appreciate how much appetite, outdoor exercise, swimming, and sleeping under the sky enhanced their meal.

Following breakfast, they lounged in the shade while Huck smoked, then departed on a journey to explore the woods. They marched happily over rotting logs, pushed through thick brush, and walked among the forest’s towering trees, which were draped from top to bottom in hanging grapevines. Occasionally, they stumbled upon cozy clearings covered in grass and adorned with flowers.

They encountered many delightful sights, though nothing truly surprising. They determined the island spanned about three miles in length and a quarter-mile in width, separated from the nearest shore by a narrow channel barely two hundred yards across. They swam roughly every hour, so it was nearly mid-afternoon by the time they returned to camp. Too famished to fish, they feasted on cold ham before lying in the shade to converse. However, their talk soon faltered and ceased. The quiet, the heavy atmosphere of the woods, and the feeling of isolation began to weigh on their moods. They grew pensive, and a vague yearning emerged, eventually taking the form of budding homesickness. Even Finn the Red-Handed found himself picturing his doorstep and empty barrels. Yet, each felt embarrassed by this vulnerability, and none possessed the courage to voice his feelings.

For a while, the boys had been vaguely aware of a strange noise in the distance, much like the ticking of a clock that one hears without truly noticing."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_012",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Tom roused the other pirates, and they all scrambled away with a shout. Within a minute or two, they were stripped and chasing each other, tumbling through the shallow, clear water of the white sandbar. They felt no desire for the small village sleeping in the distance beyond the vast expanse of water. A stray current or a slight rise in the river had carried off their raft, but this only pleased them, as its disappearance felt like burning the bridge between them and civilization.

They returned to camp feeling wonderfully refreshed, lighthearted, and ravenous; soon, they had the campfire blazing again. Huck found a spring of clear, cold water nearby, and the boys fashioned cups out of broad oak or hickory leaves, deciding that water sweetened with such wildwood charm was a fine substitute for coffee. While Joe was slicing bacon for breakfast, Tom and Huck asked him to wait a moment; they stepped to a promising spot on the riverbank and cast their lines, receiving an almost immediate reward. Joe had barely grown impatient before they returned with some handsome bass, a couple of sunfish, and a small catfish—enough food for a whole family. They fried the fish with the bacon and were amazed, for no fish had ever tasted so delicious. They did not realize that the sooner a freshwater fish is cooked after being caught, the better it tastes; nor did they consider how much of a seasoning open-air sleeping, outdoor exercise, swimming, and intense hunger provide.

After breakfast, they lounged in the shade while Huck smoked, then headed into the woods on an exploring expedition. They tramped happily along, climbing over rotting logs, pushing through tangled brush, and walking among the solemn giants of the forest, which were draped from their crowns to the ground with hanging grapevines. Now and then, they discovered cozy nooks carpeted with grass and decorated with flowers.

They found plenty of things to enjoy, though nothing to truly surprise them. They discovered the island was about three miles long and a quarter-mile wide, and that the nearest shore was separated from it by a narrow channel barely two hundred yards across. They went for a swim about every hour, so it was nearly mid-afternoon when they returned to camp. They were too hungry to stop and fish, so they ate a feast of cold ham before throwing themselves into the shade to talk. However, the conversation soon dragged and then died out. The stillness, the solemnity brooding in the woods, and the sense of isolation began to weigh on the boys' spirits. They fell into deep thought. A vague longing crept over them, which soon took a clearer shape: it was budding homesickness. Even Finn the Red-Handed was dreaming of his front steps and empty barrels. Yet, they were all ashamed of their weakness, and none was brave enough to voice his thoughts.

For some time now, the boys had been vaguely aware of a peculiar sound in the distance, much like how one sometimes notices the ticking of a clock without paying it any real attention."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_015",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "—and every person indicated the precise location where the missing boys were positioned at that moment, then added remarks like, “and I was standing exactly like this—just as I am now, and if you were him—I was that close—and he grinned, just in this manner—and then a strange sensation washed over me, like—dreadful, you understand—and I did not realize what it signified, naturally, but I perceive it clearly now!”

Then a disagreement arose regarding who had seen the deceased boys last while they were alive, and many asserted that gloomy honor, providing evidence that was more or less embellished by the witness; and when it was finally determined who truly saw the departed last and spoke the final words to them, the fortunate individuals assumed a kind of holy significance, and were stared at and envied by everyone else. One unfortunate fellow, who possessed no other greatness to present, remarked with clearly visible pride in the recollection:

“Well, Tom Sawyer thrashed me once.”

But that attempt at fame was unsuccessful. Most of the boys could claim that, and so it diminished the distinction too much. The crowd drifted away, continuing to recount memories of the lost heroes in hushed tones.

When the Sunday-school session concluded the following morning, the bell started to toll, rather than ringing in the customary fashion. It was an exceptionally quiet Sabbath, and the somber tolling felt appropriate for the contemplative stillness that rested upon the landscape. The townspeople began to assemble, lingering briefly in the entryway to speak in hushed tones about the tragic occurrence. Yet there was no whispering inside the building; only the funereal swishing of garments as the women moved to their pews disturbed the quiet there. No one could recall when the small church had been so crowded previously. There was eventually a period of waiting, a tense silence, and then Aunt Polly walked in, accompanied by Sid and Mary, followed by the Harper family, all dressed in somber black, and the entire assembly, including the elderly pastor, rose respectfully and remained standing until the grieving families were settled in the front pew. There was another shared silence, interrupted periodically by stifled weeping, and then the minister extended his hands and offered a prayer. A stirring hymn was performed, and the sermon commenced: “I am the Resurrection and the Life.”

As the service continued, the preacher painted such portraits of the virtues, the charming manners, and the exceptional potential of the lost boys that every person present, believing they recognized these descriptions, felt a sharp pain in recalling that they had consistently ignored these qualities before, and had just as consistently perceived only errors and shortcomings in the unfortunate youths. The minister recounted many a poignant event from the lives of the departed, which demonstrated their kind, magnanimous spirits, and the attendees could easily perceive, now, how noble and lovely those moments were, and recalled with sorrow that at the time they happened they had appeared to be mere mischief, fully deserving of the whip. The congregation grew increasingly emotional as the sorrowful narrative continued, until finally the entire assembly collapsed and joined the grieving mourners in a collective outburst of pained sobbing, with the preacher himself succumbing to his emotions and weeping at the pulpit."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_016",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Harper and defeat her skepticism with Tom’s incredible dream. Sid possessed enough sense to keep his private thoughts to himself as he departed the residence. His internal reflection was: “Quite flimsy—a dream of that duration, without a single inconsistency!”

What a celebrity Tom had turned into! He refrained from skipping or leaping, instead adopting a stately strut appropriate for a pirate who sensed the public gaze upon him. Indeed, the attention was constant; he attempted to appear oblivious to the stares and comments as he walked, yet they were sustenance to him. Younger children trailed behind him, as honored to be in his company and permitted by him to follow as if he were the lead drummer in a parade or the elephant heading a traveling circus. Peers his own age acted as though they were unaware he had ever been gone; yet, they were burning with jealousy nonetheless. They would have traded anything for his dark, sun-baked complexion and his shining fame; and Tom would not have surrendered either for a ticket to the circus.

In the classroom, the students showered him and Joe with such intense, wordless admiration that the pair of heroes quickly grew insufferably arrogant. They started recounting their exploits to eager audiences—but they only started; it was not a narrative likely to conclude, given the creative power of their imaginations. Finally, when they produced their pipes and strolled about puffing contentedly, they reached the absolute pinnacle of fame.

Tom concluded that he could manage without Becky Thatcher now. Renown was enough. He would dedicate his life to glory. Now that he was famous, perhaps she would desire a reconciliation. Fine, let her—she would discover that he could be just as detached as others. Soon, she appeared. Tom acted as if he did not notice her. He walked off to join a cluster of children and began chatting. Before long, he noted that she was skipping happily to and fro with a glowing face and bright eyes, feigning interest in chasing her classmates and shrieking with joy whenever she caught one; yet he observed that she always made her catches near him, and that she seemed to glance his way during those moments as well. It satisfied all the spiteful pride within him; consequently, rather than drawing him in, it only bolstered his ego further and made him more determined to hide the fact that he knew she was nearby. Eventually, she stopped her playful antics and wandered aimlessly, sighing occasionally and casting secretive, longing looks toward Tom. Then she noticed that Tom was currently speaking more intently to Amy Lawrence than to anyone else. She felt a sudden sting and became immediately agitated and restless. She attempted to leave, but her feet betrayed her, leading her to the group instead. She remarked to a girl standing nearly at Tom’s side—with artificial enthusiasm:

“Oh, Mary Austin! You naughty girl, why didn’t you attend Sunday-school?”

“I did attend—didn’t you notice me?”

“Why, no! Did you? Where were you sitting?”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_017",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“So I simply returned the bark to my pocket and stayed silent.”

“What bark?”

“The piece I wrote on to inform you we had gone off to be pirates. I truly wish you had awakened when I kissed you—I mean that sincerely.”

The stern features of his aunt softened, and a sudden warmth appeared in her gaze.

“Did you really kiss me, Tom?”

“Why, yes, I certainly did.”

“Are you positive, Tom?”

“Yes, auntie—absolutely positive.”

“Why did you kiss me, Tom?”

“Because I cared for you so much, and you were lying there moaning, which made me feel so sorry.”

His words carried the ring of sincerity. The elderly woman could not conceal a quiver in her tone as she replied:

“Kiss me once more, Tom! Now, hurry off to school and stop pestering me.”

As soon as he departed, she rushed to a cupboard and retrieved the tattered jacket Tom had worn during his pirate adventure. She paused, holding it, and murmured to herself:

“No, I shouldn’t. Poor child, I suspect he fabricated the story—but it is a wonderful, blessed fabrication, and it brings me such solace. I trust the Lord—I am certain the Lord will pardon him, for it was such a kind-hearted impulse that led him to say it. Yet, I do not wish to confirm it is a falsehood. I shall not look.”

She tucked the jacket away and stood lost in thought for a moment. Twice she reached out to grab the garment again, and twice she pulled back. Finally, she tried once more, steeling herself with the thought: “It is a kind lie—a kind lie—I will not let it trouble me.” She reached into the jacket pocket. Moments later, she was reading Tom’s scrap of bark through streaming tears, declaring: “I could pardon the boy now, even if he had committed a million transgressions!”

CHAPTER XX

There was a quality to Aunt Polly’s behavior when she kissed Tom that banished his gloom and restored his cheerful spirits. He headed toward school and happened to encounter Becky Thatcher near the start of Meadow Lane. His temperament always dictated his behavior. Without pausing, he hurried to her and said:

“I behaved quite poorly today, Becky, and I am truly sorry. I will never, ever act that way again as long as I live—please forgive me, won’t you?”

The girl halted and stared at him with disdain:

“I would appreciate it if you kept to yourself, Mr. Thomas Sawyer. I shall never speak to you again.”

She turned away and walked on. Tom was so shocked that he lacked the presence of mind to retort “Who cares, Miss Smarty?” until the opportunity had passed. Consequently, he remained silent, though he was filled with intense fury. He shuffled into the schoolyard, wishing she were a boy so he could imagine how he might thrash her. He soon crossed her path and delivered a biting comment as he walked by. She fired a sharp reply back, and the hostile rift was finalized. In her heated indignation, Becky felt she could barely wait for classes to begin, so eager was she to witness Tom being whipped for the damaged spelling book."
}

As soon as he departed, she rushed to a cupboard and retrieved the tattered jacket Tom had worn during his pirate adventure. She paused, holding it, and murmured to herself:

“No, I shouldn’t. Poor child, I suspect he fabricated the story—but it is a wonderful, blessed fabrication, and it brings me such solace. I trust the Lord—I am certain the Lord will pardon him, for it was such a kind-hearted impulse that led him to say it. Yet, I do not wish to confirm it is a falsehood. I shall not look.”

She tucked the jacket away and stood lost in thought for a moment. Twice she reached out to grab the garment again, and twice she pulled back. Finally, she tried once more, steeling herself with the thought: “It is a kind lie—a kind lie—I will not let it trouble me.” She reached into the jacket pocket. Moments later, she was reading Tom’s scrap of bark through streaming tears, declaring: “I could pardon the boy now, even if he had committed a million transgressions!”

CHAPTER XX

There was a quality to Aunt Polly’s behavior when she kissed Tom that banished his gloom and restored his cheerful spirits. He headed toward school and happened to encounter Becky Thatcher near the start of Meadow Lane. His temperament always dictated his behavior. Without pausing, he hurried to her and said:

“I behaved quite poorly today, Becky, and I am truly sorry. I will never, ever act that way again as long as I live—please forgive me, won’t you?”

The girl halted and stared at him with disdain:

“I would appreciate it if you kept to yourself, Mr. Thomas Sawyer. I shall never speak to you again.”

She turned away and walked on. Tom was so shocked that he lacked the presence of mind to retort “Who cares, Miss Smarty?” until the opportunity had passed. Consequently, he remained silent, though he was filled with intense fury. He shuffled into the schoolyard, wishing she were a boy so he could imagine how he might thrash her. He soon crossed her path and delivered a biting comment as he walked by. She fired a sharp reply back, and the hostile rift was finalized. In her heated indignation, Becky felt she could barely wait for classes to begin, so eager was she to witness Tom being whipped for the damaged spelling book."
}

“Are you sure you did, Tom?”

“Why did you kiss me, Tom?”

The words sounded like the truth. The old lady could not hide a tremor in her voice when she said:

The moment he left, she ran to a closet and pulled out the ruined jacket Tom had worn while pirating. She paused with it in her hand and said to herself:

The girl stopped and looked him scornfully in the face:

“I’ll thank you to keep to yourself, Mr. Thomas Sawyer. I’ll never speak to you again.”

}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_018",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Eventually, Mr. Dobbins sat upright, stretched with a yawn, and opened his desk. He reached for his book, appearing hesitant about whether to remove it. While most students looked up listlessly, two pupils observed his actions with keen attention. Mr. Dobbins toyed with the volume for a moment before pulling it out and settling into his seat to read. Tom looked toward Becky and saw the expression of a trapped, terrified rabbit facing a leveled weapon. His previous argument with her vanished instantly. He needed to act, and quickly, yet the urgency of the situation stifled his ability to think. Suddenly, he had an idea: he would dash forward, grab the book, and bolt through the doorway. His resolve wavered for a second, and the opportunity vanished as the teacher opened the pages. Tom wished he could reclaim that lost moment, but it was too late; Becky was beyond his help. The master turned to face the room, his gaze causing every student to look down, as even the guiltless felt a sense of dread. After a ten-second silence while the teacher collected his temper, he demanded, “Who tore this book?”

No one made a sound; the room was so quiet a pin drop would have been audible. The master studied each face for evidence of wrongdoing.

“Benjamin Rogers, did you tear this book?”

A denial followed. After another pause, he asked, “Joseph Harper, did you?”

Again, a denial. Tom grew increasingly anxious during the slow, agonizing process. The teacher surveyed the boys, pondered, and then addressed the girls: “Amy Lawrence?”

She shook her head. “Gracie Miller?”

She gave the same response. “Susan Harper, did you do this?”

Another negative answer. The next student was Becky Thatcher. Tom shook with agitation, feeling the situation was hopeless.

“Rebecca Thatcher,” the master said—Tom saw her face had turned pale with fear—“did you tear—no, look me in the eye,” he commanded as she raised her hands in a plea, “did you tear this book?”

A sudden realization struck Tom. He jumped up and yelled, “I did it!”

The students gazed at him in bewildered shock. Tom paused to collect his thoughts, and as he walked forward to accept his penalty, the look of surprise, thankfulness, and admiration in Becky’s eyes felt like sufficient reward for any number of beatings. Fueled by the nobility of his sacrifice, he endured the most brutal lashing Mr. Dobbins had ever delivered without a sound. He also accepted the additional punishment of staying two hours after class with indifference, knowing who would be waiting for him outside, happy to pass the time until his release.

That night, Tom went to bed plotting revenge against Alfred Temple, as Becky had tearfully confessed everything, including her own betrayal. Yet, his desire for retribution soon faded, replaced by happier thoughts as he drifted off to sleep with Becky’s final words echoing in his mind: “Tom, how could you be so noble!”"
}

  "rewritten_text": "Eventually, Mr. Dobbins straightened up, yawned, and unlocked his desk. He reached for his book, though he seemed unsure whether to take it out or leave it. Most of the students looked up listlessly, but two of them watched his every move with intense focus. Mr. Dobbins toyed with the book absentmindedly for a moment, then pulled it out and settled into his chair to read. Tom glanced at Becky. She looked like a trapped, helpless rabbit staring down the barrel of a gun. Instantly, he forgot his argument with her. He had to act, and he had to do it immediately. But the urgency of the situation paralyzed his ability to think. Then, he had an idea. He would run up, grab the book, bolt through the door, and escape. His resolve wavered for a split second, and the opportunity vanished—the teacher opened the book. Tom wished he could have that moment back, but it was too late. There was no way to save Becky now. A moment later, the teacher turned to face the class. Every student looked down to avoid his gaze; there was something in his expression that frightened even the innocent. The room was silent for ten seconds while the teacher gathered his anger. Then he spoke: “Who tore this book?”

There was no sound. You could have heard a pin drop. The silence dragged on as the teacher searched every face for signs of guilt.

“Benjamin Rogers, did you tear this book?”

A denial. Another pause.

“Joseph Harper, did you?”

Another denial. Tom’s anxiety grew as the slow, agonizing process continued. The teacher scanned the boys, thought for a moment, and then turned to the girls.

“Amy Lawrence?”

A shake of the head.

“Gracie Miller?”

The same gesture.

“Susan Harper, did you do this?”

Another negative response. The next girl was Becky Thatcher. Tom was trembling from head to toe with nerves and a sense of how hopeless the situation had become.

“Rebecca Thatcher” [Tom glanced at her face—it was pale with terror]—“did you tear—no, look me in the face” [her hands rose in a plea]—“did you tear this book?”

A thought struck Tom like lightning. He jumped to his feet and shouted, “I did it!”

The school stared in confusion at this unbelievable stupidity. Tom stood for a moment to collect his scattered thoughts, and as he walked forward to accept his punishment, the surprise, gratitude, and adoration shining from Becky’s eyes felt like payment enough for a hundred floggings. Inspired by his own heroic act, he endured the most brutal beating Mr. Dobbins had ever given without a sound. He also accepted the additional cruelty of being kept two hours after school with indifference, knowing who would be waiting for him outside until his detention ended, and that she wouldn't mind the long wait at all.

Tom went to bed that night planning revenge against Alfred Temple, for Becky had tearfully confessed everything, including her own betrayal. But even the desire for revenge soon gave way to more pleasant thoughts, and he finally fell asleep with Becky’s last words echoing in his mind: “Tom, how could you be so noble!”"
}

“Rebecca Thatcher,” the teacher said—Tom looked at her face, which was pale with terror—“did you tear—no, look me in the face,” he commanded as her hands rose in a plea, “did you tear this book?”

A thought struck Tom like lightning. He jumped to his feet and shouted, “I did it!”

The school stared in confusion at this unbelievable foolishness. Tom stood for a moment to collect his thoughts. As he stepped forward to receive his punishment, the surprise, gratitude, and adoration shining in Becky’s eyes felt like enough reward for a hundred floggings. Inspired by his own heroic act, he endured the most brutal beating Mr. Dobbins had ever given without a sound. He also accepted the additional punishment of staying two hours after school without complaint, knowing who would be waiting for him outside and would not mind the long wait.

Tom went to bed that night planning revenge against Alfred Temple, as Becky had tearfully confessed everything, including her own betrayal. However, even his desire for revenge soon gave way to happier thoughts, and he finally fell asleep with Becky’s last words echoing in his mind: “Tom, how could you be so noble!”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_019",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Her footsteps were so hushed they made no noise, and had it not been for the enchanting sensation caused by her pleasant touch, she might have slipped away unnoticed, much like other quiet beauties. A peculiar melancholy lingered on her face, resembling frozen tears on a winter garment, as she gestured toward the storm outside and instructed me to observe the two figures displayed there.

This unsettling dream spanned roughly ten pages of writing and concluded with a sermon so devastating to the hopes of anyone who was not a Presbyterian that it secured the top award. This piece was deemed the most impressive performance of the night. The town mayor, while presenting the prize to the writer, delivered an enthusiastic address, declaring it the most eloquent work he had ever heard and suggesting that even Daniel Webster would have felt honored by it.

It is worth noting that the frequency of essays that excessively used the term “beauteous” and described human existence as “life’s page” remained consistent with the typical standard.

Then the schoolmaster, softened nearly to the point of friendliness, pushed his chair away, turned his back to the crowd, and started sketching a map of America on the blackboard to test the geography students. However, his shaky hand made a mess of the task, and a suppressed giggle spread through the room. Aware of the problem, he tried to fix it. He wiped away lines and redrew them, but only made them more crooked, causing the laughter to grow louder. He focused all his energy on the task, seemingly resolved not to be defeated by the amusement. He sensed every eye was on him; he believed he was doing well, yet the giggling persisted and clearly intensified. And with good reason. There was an attic above, featuring a hatch directly over his head; through this opening descended a cat, held by a string tied around its hips. It had a cloth wrapped around its head and mouth to prevent it from meowing. As it slowly lowered, it arched its back and swiped at the string, then swung downward, clawing at empty air. The laughter swelled—the cat was within six inches of the focused teacher’s head—down it went, and it snatched his wig with its frantic claws, held tight, and was instantly pulled back into the attic with its prize! And how the light shone off the master’s bare scalp—for the sign-painter’s apprentice had covered it in gold leaf!

That ended the gathering. The boys had their revenge. School was out for the summer.

}
