model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_020",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The confusion and discontent within the room manifested as low murmurs, drawing a reprimand from the judge. The prosecutor then declared:

“Through the sworn testimony of citizens whose integrity is beyond reproach, we have definitively linked this horrific crime to the unfortunate man standing before the court. We conclude our case.”

A moan broke from Potter, who buried his face in his hands and swayed gently, while a heavy silence settled over the courtroom. Many men were stirred, and many women showed their sympathy through tears. The defense attorney stood and stated:

“Your honor, in our opening remarks, we indicated our intention to demonstrate that our client committed this terrible act while suffering from a state of irrational delirium brought on by intoxication. We have altered our strategy. We will not pursue that defense.” [Turning to the clerk:] “Summon Thomas Sawyer!”

Bewildered surprise appeared on every face in the room, Potter’s included. Every gaze turned with curious intensity toward Tom as he stood and moved to the witness stand. The boy appeared quite frantic, as he was deeply frightened. He was sworn in.

“Thomas Sawyer, where were you on the night of June seventeenth, around midnight?”

Tom looked at Injun Joe’s stony expression, and his voice failed him. The crowd listened in anticipation, but he could not speak. After a few moments, the boy regained some composure and managed to project his voice enough for part of the room to hear:

“In the graveyard!”

“A bit louder, please. Do not be nervous. You were—”

“In the graveyard.”

A scornful grin crossed Injun Joe’s features.

“Were you near the grave of Horse Williams?”

“Yes, sir.”

“Speak up—just a little louder. How close were you?”

“As close as I am to you.”

“Were you concealed, or not?”

“I was hidden.”

“Where?”

“Behind the elm trees at the edge of the grave.”

Injun Joe flinched almost imperceptibly.

“Was anyone with you?”

“Yes, sir. I went there with—”

“Wait—hold on. Do not mention your companion’s name. We will call him at the appropriate time. Did you bring anything with you?”

Tom paused, looking flustered.

“Speak up, my boy—do not be shy. The truth is always honorable. What did you bring?”

“Only a—a—dead cat.”

A wave of laughter broke out, which the court suppressed.

“We will present the remains of that cat. Now, my boy, recount everything that happened—tell it in your own words—do not omit anything, and do not be afraid.”

Tom started—hesitating at first, but as he became absorbed in his story, his words flowed more freely; soon, all other sounds faded, leaving only his voice; every eye remained locked on him; with parted lips and held breath, the audience listened, losing track of time, captivated by the grim details of the narrative. The tension reached its peak when the boy said:

“—and as the doctor swung the board around and Muff Potter collapsed, Injun Joe lunged with the knife and—”

Crash! Swift as a flash, the half-breed leaped toward a window, forced his way past those in his path, and vanished!"
}

“Thomas Sawyer, where were you on the seventeenth of June, around midnight?”

Tom glanced at Injun Joe’s stoic face, and his voice failed him. The audience listened breathlessly, but the words would not come. After a few moments, however, the boy regained some composure and managed to speak loudly enough for part of the room to hear:

“In the graveyard!”

“A little louder, please. Don’t be afraid. You were—”

“In the graveyard.”

A mocking smile crossed Injun Joe’s face.

“Were you anywhere near Horse Williams’ grave?”

“Yes, sir.”

“Speak up—just a bit louder. How close were you?”

“As close as I am to you.”

“Were you hidden, or not?”

“I was hidden.”

“Where?”

“Behind the elm trees at the edge of the grave.”

Injun Joe flinched almost imperceptibly.

“Was anyone with you?”

“Yes, sir. I went there with—”

“Wait—wait a moment. Never mind mentioning your companion’s name. We will produce him at the right time. Did you bring anything with you?”

Tom hesitated and looked confused.

“Speak up, my boy—don’t be shy. The truth is always respectable. What did you take there?”

“Only a—a—dead cat.”

A ripple of laughter broke out, which the court quickly silenced.

“We will produce the remains of that cat. Now, my boy, tell us everything that happened—tell it in your own words—don’t leave anything out, and don’t be afraid.”

Tom began—hesitantly at first, but as he got into the story, his words flowed more easily. Soon, every sound ceased except for his voice; every eye was fixed on him. With parted lips and bated breath, the audience hung on his words, losing track of time, captivated by the gruesome details. The tension reached its peak when the boy said:

“—and as the doctor swung the board around and Muff Potter fell, Injun Joe jumped with the knife and—”

Crash! Quick as lightning, the half-breed lunged for a window, shoved his way through everyone in his path, and was gone!"

A groan escaped from poor Potter. He covered his face with his hands and rocked back and forth while a painful silence filled the courtroom. Many men were moved, and many women showed their compassion through tears. The defense attorney stood up and said:

“Your honor, in our opening remarks, we mentioned our plan to prove that our client committed this act while in a state of temporary insanity caused by alcohol. We have changed our minds. We will not use that defense.” He then turned to the clerk and said, “Call Thomas Sawyer!”

Confused amazement appeared on every face in the room, including Potter’s. Every eye turned toward Tom with curious interest as he stood up and took his place on the witness stand. The boy looked frantic because he was terrified. He was sworn in.

“Thomas Sawyer, where were you on the night of June seventeenth, around midnight?”

Tom glanced at Injun Joe’s stone-cold face, and he could not speak. The audience listened breathlessly, but the words would not come. After a few moments, however, the boy regained some strength and managed to speak loudly enough for part of the room to hear:

“In the graveyard!”

“A little louder, please. Don’t be afraid. You were—”

“In the graveyard.”

A mocking smile crossed Injun Joe’s face.

“Were you anywhere near Horse Williams’ grave?”

“Yes, sir.”

“Speak up—just a little louder. How close were you?”

“As close as I am to you.”

“Were you hiding, or not?”

“I was hiding.”

“Where?”

“Behind the elm trees at the edge of the grave.”

Injun Joe flinched almost imperceptibly.

“Was anyone with you?”

“Yes, sir. I went there with—”

“Wait—wait a moment. Never mind mentioning your companion’s name. We will bring him forward at the right time. Did you bring anything with you?”

Tom hesitated and looked confused.

“Speak up, my boy—don’t be shy. The truth is always respectable. What did you take there?”

“Only a—a—dead cat.”

A ripple of laughter went through the room, which the court quickly silenced.

“We will produce the skeleton of that cat. Now, my boy, tell us everything that happened—tell it in your own way—don’t skip anything, and don’t be afraid.”

Tom began hesitantly at first, but as he got into the story, his words flowed more easily. Soon, every sound stopped except for his voice. Every eye was fixed on him. With parted lips and bated breath, the audience hung on his words, losing track of time, captivated by the gruesome details. The emotional tension reached its peak when the boy said:

“—and as the doctor swung the board around and Muff Potter fell, Injun Joe jumped with the knife and—”

Crash! As quick as lightning, the half-breed leaped toward a window, fought his way through everyone in his path, and was gone!"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_021",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": ""What is your opinion?"

"It is very strange, Huck. I am baffled. Sometimes sorcerers meddle. I suppose that might be the issue here."

"Nonsense! Witches possess no influence during the daylight."

"True, that is correct. I overlooked that. Oh, I realize what the problem is! What a pair of idiots we are! You must locate where the branch's shadow lands at midnight, and that is the spot to excavate!"

"Then blast it, we have squandered all this effort for nothing. Now confound it, we must return after dark. It is a terribly long distance. Can you sneak out?"

"I certainly will. We must accomplish it tonight as well, because if anyone discovers these pits, they will immediately realize what is buried here and claim it."

"Fine, I will arrive and meow tonight."

"Agreed. Let us conceal the implements in the shrubbery."

The boys arrived that evening, near the scheduled hour. They waited within the darkness. It was a desolate location, and a time rendered grave by ancient legends. Phantoms murmured in the shivering foliage, spirits hid in the shadowy corners, the mournful howling of a dog drifted from afar, and an owl responded with a funereal cry. The boys felt dampened by these grim surroundings and spoke rarely. Eventually, they estimated that midnight had arrived; they noted the shadow's position and commenced digging. Their expectations began to climb. Their enthusiasm intensified, and their labor increased accordingly. The pit grew deeper and deeper, yet every time their pulses raced hearing the pick hit an object, they only endured fresh frustration. It was merely a rock or a fragment. Finally, Tom remarked:

"It is pointless, Huck, we are mistaken once more."

"But we cannot be mistaken. We marked the shadow precisely."

"I realize that, but there is another factor."

"What is that?"

"Well, we merely estimated the hour. Likely it was either too late or too early."

Huck let his spade fall.

"That is it," he declared. "That is the exact difficulty. We must abandon this attempt. We are unable to determine the accurate time, and furthermore, this sort of activity is too terrifying, being here at this hour with witches and specters hovering about. I feel as though something is trailing me constantly; and I am terrified to look behind, because perhaps there are others in front waiting for an opportunity. I have been shivering all over since I arrived."

"Well, I have felt much the same, Huck. They almost invariably station a corpse when they inter treasure beneath a tree, to guard it."

"Goodness!"

"Indeed, they do. I have always heard that."

"Tom, I dislike messing around where there are deceased individuals. A person is certain to encounter trouble with them."

"I dislike disturbing them either. Suppose this one were to thrust his skull out and speak!"

"Do not, Tom! It is dreadful."

"Well, it truly is. Huck, I do not feel at ease at all."

"Listen, Tom, let us abandon this site and attempt somewhere else."

"Very well, I suppose we should."

"Where shall it be?"

Tom pondered for a moment, then replied:

"The haunted house. That is the place!"

"Confound it, I dislike haunted houses, Tom.""
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_021",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“What do you think?”

“It’s really strange, Huck. I don’t get it. Sometimes witches interfere. I suppose that might be the problem now.”

“Nonsense! Witches don’t have any power during the day.”

“Well, that’s true. I didn’t think of that. Oh, I know what the problem is! What a bunch of idiots we are! You have to find where the shadow of the branch falls at midnight, and that’s where you dig!”

“Then damn it, we’ve wasted all this work for nothing. Now, blast it, we have to come back at night. It’s a long way. Can you sneak out?”

“I bet I can. We have to do it tonight, too, because if someone sees these holes, they’ll know immediately what’s here and they’ll go for it.”

“Fine, I’ll come by and meow tonight.”

“All right. Let’s hide the tools in the bushes.”

The boys were there that night, around the appointed time. They sat in the shadow waiting. It was a lonely place, and an hour made solemn by old legends. Spirits whispered in the rustling leaves, ghosts lurked in the dark corners, the deep baying of a hound drifted up from the distance, and an owl answered with its mournful call. The boys were subdued by these solemnities and talked little. Eventually, they judged that midnight had arrived; they marked where the shadow fell and began to dig. Their hopes started to rise. Their interest grew stronger, and their effort kept pace with it. The hole grew deeper and deeper, but every time their hearts jumped to hear the pick strike something, they only suffered a new disappointment. It was just a stone or a chunk of dirt. Finally, Tom said:

“It’s no use, Huck, we’re wrong again.”

“Well, but we can’t be wrong. We marked the shadow perfectly.”

“I know, but there’s another thing.”

“What’s that?”

“Well, we only guessed at the time. It’s likely it was too late or too early.”

Huck dropped his shovel.

“That’s it,” he said. “That’s the exact problem. We have to give this one up. We can’t ever tell the right time, and besides, this kind of thing is too terrifying, being here this time of night with witches and ghosts fluttering around. I feel like something is behind me all the time; and I’m afraid to turn around, because maybe there are others in front waiting for a chance. I’ve been shivering ever since I got here.”

“Well, I’ve felt pretty much the same, Huck. They almost always put a dead man in when they bury treasure under a tree, to watch over it.”

“Good grief!”

“Yes, they do. I’ve always heard that.”

“Tom, I don’t like messing around where there are dead people. You’re bound to get into trouble with them, for sure.”

“I don’t like to stir them up, either. Suppose this one here were to stick his skull out and say something!”

“Don’t, Tom! It’s awful.”

“Well, it really is. Huck, I don’t feel comfortable at all.”

“Say, Tom, let’s give this place up and try somewhere else.”

“All right, I think we’d better.”

“Where to?”

Tom considered for a while, and then said:

“The haunted house. That’s it!”

“Damn it, I don’t like haunted houses, Tom.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_022",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "His companion nudged him a couple of times, and he settled down. Soon, the man keeping watch started nodding; his chin sank lower and lower, and before long, both men were snoring.

The boys exhaled a long, relieved breath. Tom whispered:

“This is our moment—let’s go!”

Huck replied:

“I can’t—I’d be terrified if they woke up.”

Tom pressed him, but Huck resisted. Finally, Tom stood up slowly and quietly, intending to go by himself. However, his first step caused the unstable floorboards to emit such a harsh creak that he collapsed, paralyzed with fear. He did not try again. The boys remained there, tracking the agonizingly slow passage of time until it felt as though the world had ended and eternity was fading; then, they were relieved to see the sun finally dipping below the horizon.

Suddenly, one snore stopped. Injun Joe sat upright, looked about, and gave a harsh smile at his partner, whose head was resting on his knees. He kicked him awake and remarked:

“Hey! You’re a fine sentry, aren’t you! It’s fine, though—nothing occurred.”

“Goodness! Did I doze off?”

“Oh, a little bit. It’s almost time for us to head out, partner. What should we do with the remaining loot?”

“I’m not sure—leave it here as we’ve been doing, I suppose. No point in hauling it around until we head south. Six hundred and fifty in silver is a heavy load.”

“Fine—that works—it won’t hurt to return here one more time.”

“True—but I’d suggest coming at night like we used to—it’s safer.”

“Agreed: but listen; it might be a while before I get the right opening for that task; things could go wrong; this isn’t the most secure spot; we should just bury it properly—and bury it deep.”

“Smart thinking,” said the partner, who crossed the room, knelt, lifted one of the back hearthstones, and pulled out a bag that clinked musically. He removed twenty or thirty dollars for himself and an equal amount for Injun Joe, handing the bag to the latter, who was now kneeling in the corner, excavating the dirt with his bowie-knife.

The boys instantly forgot their terror and their discomfort. With wide, eager eyes, they observed every action. Fortune! The sheer brilliance of it was beyond belief! Six hundred dollars was enough wealth to make six boys rich! This was treasure hunting under the most perfect conditions—there would be no frustrating guesswork about where to dig. They poked each other constantly—expressive pokes that were easily interpreted, as they simply signaled, “Oh, aren’t you thrilled we came here now!”

Joe’s blade hit something solid.

“Look here!” he exclaimed.

“What is it?” his partner asked.

“A rotting board—no, it’s a box, I think. Here—give me a hand and we’ll see what it’s doing here. Never mind, I’ve punched a hole in it.”

He reached inside and pulled his hand out—

“Man, it’s gold!”

The two men inspected the handful of coins. They were gold pieces. The boys hidden above were just as thrilled and delighted as the men below.

Joe’s partner said:

“We’ll finish this quickly. There’s an old, corroded pickaxe near the weeds in the corner by the fireplace—I spotted it a moment ago.”

He hurried over and retrieved the boys’ pick and shovel."
}

“True—but I’d suggest coming at night like we used to—it’s safer.”

“Agreed: but listen; it might be a while before I get the right opening for that task; things could go wrong; this isn’t the most secure spot; we should just bury it properly—and bury it deep.”

“Smart thinking,” said the partner, who crossed the room, knelt, lifted one of the back hearthstones, and pulled out a bag that clinked musically. He removed twenty or thirty dollars for himself and an equal amount for Injun Joe, handing the bag to the latter, who was now kneeling in the corner, excavating the dirt with his bowie-knife.

The boys instantly forgot their terror and their discomfort. With wide, eager eyes, they observed every action. Fortune! The sheer brilliance of it was beyond belief! Six hundred dollars was enough wealth to make six boys rich! This was treasure hunting under the most perfect conditions—there would be no frustrating guesswork about where to dig. They poked each other constantly—expressive pokes that were easily interpreted, as they simply signaled, “Oh, aren’t you thrilled we came here now!”

Joe’s blade hit something solid.

“Look here!” he exclaimed.

“What is it?” his partner asked.

“A rotting board—no, it’s a box, I think. Here—give me a hand and we’ll see what it’s doing here. Never mind, I’ve punched a hole in it.”

He reached inside and pulled his hand out—

“Man, it’s gold!”

The two men inspected the handful of coins. They were gold pieces. The boys hidden above were just as thrilled and delighted as the men below.

Joe’s partner said:

“We’ll finish this quickly. There’s an old, corroded pickaxe near the weeds in the corner by the fireplace—I spotted it a moment ago.”

He hurried over and retrieved the boys’ pick and shovel."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_023",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Collect every key you can locate, and I will swipe all of auntie’s; then, on the first pitch-black night, we shall head over and test them. Remember to keep a sharp eye out for Injun Joe, as he mentioned he intended to sneak into town to scout for an opportunity to exact his vengeance. If you spot him, just shadow him; if he doesn’t head toward that Number Two, then that isn’t the right spot.

“Goodness, I don’t want to trail him all by myself!”

“Well, it will be nighttime, obviously. He might never notice you—and even if he did, he probably wouldn’t suspect a thing.”

“Well, if it is sufficiently dark, I suppose I will track him. I don’t know—I don’t know. I will give it a shot.”

“You can be certain I will follow him if it is dark, Huck. After all, he might have realized he couldn’t get his revenge and be heading straight for that money.”

“That’s true, Tom, that’s true. I will follow him; I will, by jingoes!”

“Now you’re talking! Don’t you ever lose your nerve, Huck, and I won’t either.”

CHAPTER XXVIII

That evening, Tom and Huck prepared for their escapade. They loitered near the tavern until past nine, one monitoring the alley from afar while the other watched the tavern entrance. No one went into or out of the alley; no one looking like the Spaniard passed through the tavern door. The night looked like it would be clear, so Tom headed home with the plan that if it grew sufficiently dark, Huck would come and “meow,” at which point he would sneak out to test the keys. However, the night stayed bright, and Huck finished his vigil and went to sleep in an abandoned sugar hogshead around midnight.

Tuesday brought the boys the same poor fortune. Wednesday was the same. But Thursday night looked more promising. Tom snuck out early with his aunt’s antique tin lantern and a large towel to shroud it. He concealed the lantern inside Huck’s sugar hogshead, and their watch commenced. An hour before midnight, the tavern shut down, and its lights—the only ones in the vicinity—were extinguished. No Spaniard had appeared. No one had entered or exited the alley. Everything seemed favorable. Total darkness prevailed, and the absolute silence was broken only by the occasional rumble of far-off thunder.

Tom retrieved his lantern, ignited it inside the hogshead, swaddled it tightly in the towel, and the two explorers crept through the shadows toward the tavern. Huck acted as guard while Tom felt his way into the alley. Then followed a period of anxious waiting that pressed upon Huck’s nerves like a heavy weight. He began wishing he could see a glimmer from the lantern—it would terrify him, but it would at least confirm that Tom was still alive. It felt like hours since Tom had vanished. Surely he must have fainted; perhaps he was dead; maybe his heart had given out from the terror and excitement. In his agitation, Huck found himself creeping nearer and nearer to the alley, dreading all manner of horrible outcomes and expecting some disaster to occur at any moment that would steal his breath. There wasn’t much to steal, as he seemed only able to draw in tiny gasps, and his heart felt as though it would soon exhaust itself from the way it was pounding."
}

That night, Tom and Huck were prepared for their adventure. {
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_023",
  "condition": "simplify",
  "rewritten_text": "Now you get hold of every doorkey you can find, and I will grab all of auntie’s. The first dark night, we will go there and try them. And remember, keep a lookout for Injun Joe, because he said he was going to come into town and spy around again for a chance to get his revenge. If you see him, just follow him; if he does not go to that Number Two, then that is not the right place.

“Lord, I do not want to follow him by myself!”

“Why, it will be night, for sure. He might never see you—and if he did, maybe he would never suspect anything.”

“Well, if it is pretty dark, I suppose I will track him. I do not know—I do not know. I will try.”

“You can bet I will follow him if it is dark, Huck. Why, he might have realized he could not get his revenge and is going right after that money.”

“That is true, Tom, that is true. I will follow him; I will, by jingoes!”

“Now you are talking! Do not ever lose your nerve, Huck, and I will not either.”

CHAPTER XXVIII

That night, Tom and Huck were ready for their adventure. They hung around the neighborhood of the tavern until after nine, one watching the alley from a distance and the other watching the tavern door. Nobody entered the alley or left it; nobody resembling the Spaniard entered or left the tavern door. The night promised to be clear, so Tom went home with the understanding that if it got dark enough, Huck would come and “meow,” and then Tom would slip out to try the keys. However, the night remained clear, and Huck ended his watch and went to bed in an empty sugar barrel around midnight.

Tuesday, the boys had the same bad luck. Wednesday was the same. But Thursday night looked more promising. Tom slipped out early with his aunt’s old tin lantern and a large towel to cover it. He hid the lantern in Huck’s sugar barrel, and the watch began. An hour before midnight, the tavern closed, and its lights—the only ones nearby—were put out. No Spaniard had been seen. Nobody had entered or left the alley. Everything looked perfect. Total darkness reigned, and the perfect stillness was broken only by the occasional rumble of distant thunder.

Tom got his lantern, lit it inside the barrel, wrapped it tightly in the towel, and the two adventurers crept through the gloom toward the tavern. Huck stood guard while Tom felt his way into the alley. Then followed a period of anxious waiting that weighed on Huck’s spirits like a mountain. He began to wish he could see a flash from the lantern—it would frighten him, but it would at least tell him that Tom was still alive. It seemed like hours since Tom had disappeared. Surely he must have fainted; maybe he was dead; maybe his heart had burst from terror and excitement. In his nervousness, Huck found himself drawing closer and closer to the alley, fearing all sorts of dreadful things and expecting some disaster to happen at any moment that would take his breath away. There was not much breath to take, as he seemed only able to inhale in tiny amounts, and his heart would soon wear itself out from the way it was beating."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_024",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "No sign arrived that night.

Daylight eventually broke, and by mid-morning a lively and boisterous group had assembled at Judge Thatcher’s residence, prepared for departure. It was not customary for the older generation to spoil these outings with their attendance. The youngsters were deemed sufficiently secure under the supervision of a few young women of eighteen and some young men around twenty-three. The aged steam ferry-boat was hired for the event; soon, the cheerful crowd marched up the main thoroughfare, carrying hampers of food. Sid was unwell and forced to skip the festivities; Mary stayed behind to look after him. The final instruction Mrs. Thatcher gave to Becky was:

“You will not return until late. Perhaps it would be best to spend the night with one of the girls residing near the ferry-dock, dear.”

“Then I shall stay with Susy Harper, mother.”

“Very well. And remember to conduct yourself properly and do not be a nuisance.”

Shortly after, as they walked along, Tom remarked to Becky:

“Listen—I have an idea. Instead of heading to Joe Harper’s, we will hike up the hill and visit the Widow Douglas. She will have ice-cream! She serves it nearly every day—heaps of it. And she will be delighted to host us.”

“Oh, that sounds delightful!”

Then Becky pondered for a moment and asked:

“But what will mother say?”

“How would she ever find out?”

The girl considered the suggestion, then replied hesitantly:

“I suppose it is improper—but—”

“Oh, nonsense! Your mother will never know, so where is the harm? All she cares about is your safety; and I wager she would have told you to go there if she had thought of it. I am certain she would!”

The Widow Douglas’s generous hospitality was an alluring prospect. It, combined with Tom’s urging, eventually won the argument. Thus, they resolved to tell no one about their plans for the night. Suddenly, it struck Tom that Huck might arrive that very evening to provide the signal. This thought dampened his excitement significantly. Yet, he could not bring himself to abandon the fun at the Widow Douglas’. And why should he, he reasoned—the signal did not appear the previous night, so why would it be any more probable tonight? The certain enjoyment of the evening outweighed the doubtful treasure; and, like a boy, he chose to follow his stronger desire and refused to let himself dwell on the chest of money for the remainder of the day.

Three miles downstream, the ferry-boat halted at the entrance of a wooded ravine and moored. The group poured onto the shore, and soon the forest depths and rocky cliffs rang out with shouts and laughter. Every possible method of becoming overheated and exhausted was attempted, and eventually, the wanderers returned to camp with hearty appetites, at which point the consumption of the provisions commenced. Following the meal, there was a pleasant period of relaxation and conversation beneath the sprawling oaks. Before long, someone called out:

“Who is prepared for the cave?”

Everyone was. Supplies of candles were gathered, and immediately there was a collective rush up the hillside. The entrance to the cave was located on the slope—a portal shaped like the letter A."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_024",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "No signal arrived that night.

Morning came eventually, and by ten or eleven o’clock a giddy and boisterous group had gathered at Judge Thatcher’s, and everything was ready to depart. It was not customary for older people to spoil the picnics with their presence. The children were considered safe enough under the supervision of a few young women of eighteen and a few young men of about twenty-three. The old steam ferryboat was chartered for the occasion; soon the cheerful crowd filed up the main street, carrying baskets of food. Sid was sick and had to miss the fun; Mary stayed home to look after him. The last thing Mrs. Thatcher said to Becky was:

“You won’t be back until late. Perhaps you had better stay the night with one of the girls who lives near the ferry landing, dear.”

“Then I’ll stay with Susy Harper, Mom.”

“Very well. And remember to behave yourself and don’t be any trouble.”

Soon, as they walked along, Tom said to Becky:

“Hey—I’ll tell you what we’ll do. Instead of going to Joe Harper’s, we’ll climb right up the hill and stop at the Widow Douglas’. She’ll have ice cream! She has it almost every day—tons of it. And she’ll be thrilled to have us.”

“Oh, that will be fun!”

Then Becky thought for a moment and said:

“But what will Mom say?”

“How would she ever know?”

The girl considered the idea, and said reluctantly:

“I suppose it’s wrong—but—”

“Oh, forget it! Your mother won’t know, so what’s the harm? All she wants is for you to be safe; and I bet she would have said to go there if she had thought of it. I know she would!”

The Widow Douglas’ generous hospitality was a tempting offer. It, combined with Tom’s persuasion, eventually won the day. So it was decided to say nothing to anyone about the plans for the night. Soon it occurred to Tom that perhaps Huck might come that very night and give the signal. The thought took a lot of the excitement out of his anticipation. Still, he could not bear to give up the fun at the Widow Douglas’. And why should he give it up, he reasoned—the signal didn’t come the night before, so why would it be any more likely to come tonight? The certain fun of the evening outweighed the uncertain treasure; and, like a boy, he decided to follow his stronger inclination and not allow himself to think of the box of money for the rest of the day.

Three miles below town, the ferryboat stopped at the mouth of a wooded hollow and tied up. The crowd swarmed ashore, and soon the forest and rocky heights echoed with shouting and laughter. They tried every way to get hot and tired, and eventually the wanderers straggled back to camp with healthy appetites, and then the feast began. After eating, there was a refreshing period of rest and conversation in the shade of spreading oaks. Before long, someone shouted:

“Who’s ready for the cave?”

Everyone was. Bundles of candles were gathered, and immediately there was a general scramble up the hill. The mouth of the cave was on the hillside—an opening shaped like the letter A."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_025",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "My sons will join them shortly. I wish we had a description of those villains; it would be quite useful. I assume you could not make out their appearances in the darkness, lad?”

“Oh, I certainly could; I spotted them in town and trailed them.”

“Excellent! Describe them—give me a description, my boy!”

“One is that elderly deaf and dumb Spaniard who has visited here a few times, and the other is a nasty-looking, tattered—”

“That suffices, lad, we recognize the men! We encountered them in the woods behind the widow’s property once, and they retreated. Go now, boys, and notify the sheriff—you can eat your breakfast tomorrow morning!”

The Welshman’s sons left immediately. As they exited, Huck jumped up and cried:

“Oh, please do not inform anyone that I was the one who exposed them! Please!”

“Very well, if that is your wish, Huck, though you deserve the recognition for your actions.”

“Oh no, no! Please keep it quiet!”

Once the young men had departed, the elderly Welshman remarked:

“They will not speak of it—and neither shall I. But why do you wish to remain anonymous?”

Huck refused to elaborate, stating only that he already possessed too much knowledge regarding one of those individuals and would not have that man realize he knew anything against him for anything in the world—he would certainly be murdered for knowing it.

The old man pledged his silence again and asked:

“How did you happen to trail these men, lad? Did they appear suspicious?”

Huck paused to construct a sufficiently careful response. Then he replied:

“Well, you see, I am considered a bit of a troublemaker—at least that is what everyone says, and I cannot argue with it—and sometimes I cannot sleep, because I am thinking about it and trying to find a better way to live. That is how it was last night. I could not sleep, so I walked up the street around midnight, pondering things, and when I reached that dilapidated brick store near the Temperance Tavern, I leaned against the wall to think some more. Just then, these two fellows came creeping past me, carrying something under their arms, and I figured they had stolen it. One was smoking, and the other requested a light; they stopped right in front of me, and the cigars illuminated their faces, allowing me to see that the large one was the deaf and dumb Spaniard, with his white whiskers and eye patch, and the other was a grimy, ragged-looking wretch.”

“Could you discern the rags by the glow of the cigars?”

This caught Huck off guard for a second. Then he answered:

“Well, I am not certain—but somehow it feels as though I did.”

“Then they continued on, and you—”

“Followed them—yes. That is right. I wanted to see what was happening—they were sneaking along so. I tracked them to the widow’s stile, stood in the shadows, and heard the ragged one plead for the widow, and the Spaniard swear he would ruin her appearance, just as I told you and your two—”

“What! The deaf and dumb man said all that!”

Huck had committed another grave error! He was attempting his best to prevent the old man from gaining the slightest clue as to the Spaniard’s identity, yet his own tongue seemed bent on causing him trouble despite his best efforts."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_025",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "My boys will be with them shortly. I wish we had some kind of description of those scoundrels—it would help a great deal. But I suppose you couldn’t see what they looked like in the dark, could you, lad?”

“Oh yes; I saw them downtown and followed them.”

“Splendid! Describe them—describe them, my boy!”

“One is the old deaf and dumb Spaniard who has been around here once or twice, and the other is a mean-looking, ragged—”

“That’s enough, lad, we know the men! We ran into them in the woods behind the widow’s place one day, and they slunk away. Off with you, boys, and tell the sheriff—get your breakfast tomorrow morning!”

The Welshman’s sons left immediately. As they were exiting the room, Huck jumped up and exclaimed:

“Oh, please don’t tell anyone it was me who tipped you off! Oh, please!”

“That’s fine if you say so, Huck, but you really should get the credit for what you did.”

“Oh no, no! Please don’t tell!”

When the young men were gone, the old Welshman said:

“They won’t tell—and I won’t. But why don’t you want it known?”

Huck would not explain, other than to say that he already knew too much about one of those men and would not have that man know he was aware of anything against him for anything in the world—he would be killed for knowing it, for sure.

The old man promised secrecy once more, and asked:

“How did you come to follow these fellows, lad? Did they look suspicious?”

Huck was silent while he framed a sufficiently cautious reply. Then he said:

“Well, you see, I’m a bit of a troublemaker—at least everyone says so, and I don’t see anything wrong with it—and sometimes I can’t sleep much, because I’m thinking about it and trying to figure out a new way of doing things. That’s how it was last night. I couldn’t sleep, so I came up the street around midnight, thinking it all over, and when I got to that old, shaky brick store by the Temperance Tavern, I leaned against the wall to think some more. Well, just then these two guys came slipping along right by me, with something under their arms, and I figured they had stolen it. One was smoking, and the other wanted a light; so they stopped right in front of me and the cigars lit up their faces, and I saw that the big one was the deaf and dumb Spaniard, with his white whiskers and the patch on his eye, and the other one was a rusty, ragged-looking devil.”

“Could you see the rags by the light of the cigars?”

This caught Huck off guard for a moment. Then he said:

“Well, I don’t know—but somehow it seems like I did.”

“Then they went on, and you—”

“Followed them—yes. That was it. I wanted to see what was going on—they were sneaking along so. I trailed them to the widow’s stile, and stood in the dark and heard the ragged one beg for the widow, and the Spaniard swear he’d ruin her looks just as I told you and your two—”

{

  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "My boys will be with them shortly. I wish we had some kind of description of those scoundrels—it would help a great deal. But I suppose you couldn’t see what they looked like in the dark, could you, lad?”

“Oh yes; I saw them downtown and followed them.”

“Splendid! Describe them—describe them, my boy!”

“One is the old deaf and dumb Spaniard who has been around here once or twice, and the other is a mean-looking, ragged—”

“That’s enough, lad, we know the men! We ran into them in the woods behind the widow’s place one day, and they slunk away. Off with you, boys, and tell the sheriff—get your breakfast tomorrow morning!”

The Welshman’s sons left immediately. As they were exiting the room, Huck jumped up and exclaimed:

“Oh, please don’t tell anyone it was me who tipped you off! Oh, please!”

“That’s fine if you say so, Huck, but you really should get the credit for what you did.”

“Oh no, no! Please don’t tell!”

When the young men were gone, the old Welshman said:

“They won’t tell—and I won’t. But why don’t you want it known?”

Huck would not explain, other than to say that he already knew too much about one of those men and would not have that man know he was aware of anything against him for anything in the world—he would be killed for knowing it, for sure.

The old man promised secrecy once more, and asked:

“How did you come to follow these fellows, lad? Did they look suspicious?”

Huck was silent while he framed a sufficiently cautious reply. Then he said:

“Well, you see, I’m a bit of a troublemaker—at least everyone says so, and I don’t see anything wrong with it—and sometimes I can’t sleep much, because I’m thinking about it and trying to figure out a new way of doing things. That’s how it was last night. I couldn’t sleep, so I came up the street around midnight, thinking it all over, and when I got to that old, shaky brick store by the Temperance Tavern, I leaned against the wall to think some more. Well, just then these two guys came slipping along right by me, with something under their arms, and I figured they had stolen it. One was smoking, and the other wanted a light; so they stopped right in front of me and the cigars lit up their faces, and I saw that the big one was the deaf and dumb Spaniard, with his white whiskers and the patch on his eye, and the other one was a rusty, ragged-looking devil.”

“Could you see the rags by the light of the cigars?”

This caught Huck off guard for a moment. Then he said:

“Well, I don’t know—but somehow it seems like I did.”

“Then they went on, and you—”

“Followed them—yes. That was it. I wanted to see what was going on—they were sneaking along so. I trailed them to the widow’s stile, and stood in the dark and heard the ragged one beg for the widow, and the Spaniard swear he’d ruin her looks just as I told you and your two—”

“What! The deaf and dumb man said all that!”

Huck had made another terrible mistake! He was trying his best to keep the old man from getting the faintest hint of who the Spaniard might be, and yet his tongue seemed determined to get him into trouble despite everything he could do."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_026",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "In a remote area, away from the typical tourist paths, the names “BECKY & TOM” were discovered written on the stone wall using candle soot, accompanied by a ribbon stained with grease. Mrs. Thatcher identified the ribbon and wept over it. She declared it the final memento of her daughter, more valuable than any other because it was the last item to touch her living form before the tragic end. Occasionally, observers reported seeing a distant flicker of light within the cave, prompting a joyful cry and a group of men to rush down the echoing passages, only to be met with crushing disappointment; the children were never found, as it was merely another searcher’s lantern.

Three agonizing days and nights passed slowly, leaving the village in a state of despair. Nobody had the spirit for anything. Even the sudden revelation that the Temperance Tavern owner was hiding alcohol on the premises barely caused a stir, despite its significance. During a moment of clarity, Huck weakly brought up the topic of taverns, eventually asking—with a vague fear of the answer—if anything had been found at the Temperance Tavern during his illness.

“Yes,” the widow replied.

Huck sat up in bed, his eyes wide:

“What? What was it?”

“Liquor!—and the establishment has been closed. Lie down, dear—you startled me!”

“Just tell me one thing—only one—please! Was it Tom Sawyer who found it?”

The widow began to cry. “Hush, hush, child! I have told you before, you must not talk. You are extremely ill!”

So, only liquor had been discovered; there would have been a massive celebration if it had been the gold. The treasure was lost for good—gone forever! Yet, why would she be weeping? It was strange that she should cry.

These ideas drifted through Huck’s mind, and under the weight of his exhaustion, he drifted off to sleep. The widow whispered to herself:

“There—he is asleep, poor thing. Tom Sawyer find it! If only someone could find Tom Sawyer! Alas, there are few remaining now who possess enough hope or strength to continue the search.”

CHAPTER XXXI

Returning to Tom and Becky’s experience at the picnic, they walked through the dark corridors with the others, exploring the cave’s well-known sights—wonders given rather descriptive titles, such as “The Drawing-Room,” “The Cathedral,” “Aladdin’s Palace,” and similar names. Soon, the game of hide-and-seek commenced, and Tom and Becky participated enthusiastically until the activity became somewhat tiring; they then wandered down a winding path, holding their candles high to read the intricate collection of names, dates, addresses, and sayings that had been marked onto the rock walls with candle smoke. As they continued drifting and chatting, they barely realized they had entered a section of the cave where the walls were bare. They burned their own names beneath a protruding ledge and proceeded. Before long, they arrived at a spot where a small stream, dripping over a ledge and depositing limestone, had over countless ages created a delicate, ruffled Niagara made of shimmering, permanent stone."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_026",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "In one location, far from the areas usually visited by tourists, the names “BECKY & TOM” were found traced onto the rocky wall with candle soot, and nearby lay a grease-stained piece of ribbon. Mrs. Thatcher recognized the ribbon and wept over it. She said it was the final relic she would ever have of her child, and that no other memorial could be as precious, because this item had been in contact with her living body just before the terrible death occurred. Some reported that occasionally, deep in the cave, a distant speck of light would glimmer, followed by a triumphant shout as a group of men rushed down the echoing passage—only for a sickening disappointment to follow; the children were not there; it was merely another searcher’s light.

Three dreadful days and nights dragged on, and the village fell into a hopeless stupor. No one had the energy for anything. The recent accidental discovery that the owner of the Temperance Tavern kept alcohol on the premises barely caused a stir, despite how significant that fact usually would have been. During a moment of clarity, Huck weakly brought up the subject of taverns, and finally asked—fearing the worst—if anything had been discovered at the Temperance Tavern since he had been ill.

“Yes,” said the widow.

Huck sat up in bed, eyes wide:

“What? What was it?”

“Liquor!—and the place has been shut down. Lie down, child—you really startled me!”

“Just tell me one thing—only one—please! Was it Tom Sawyer who found it?”

The widow burst into tears. “Hush, hush, child, hush! I’ve told you before, you must not talk. You are very, very sick!”

So only liquor had been found; there would have been a huge celebration if it had been the gold. The treasure was gone forever—gone forever! But why would she be crying? It was strange that she should cry.

These thoughts drifted through Huck’s mind, and under the weight of his exhaustion, he fell asleep. The widow said to herself:

“There—he’s asleep, poor wreck. Tom Sawyer find it! If only someone could find Tom Sawyer! Ah, there aren’t many left now who have enough hope, or strength, to keep searching.”

CHAPTER XXXI

Now to return to Tom and Becky’s experience at the picnic. They walked along the dark aisles with the rest of the group, visiting the familiar wonders of the cave—wonders given rather descriptive names, such as “The Drawing-Room,” “The Cathedral,” “Aladdin’s Palace,” and so on. Soon, the hide-and-seek games began, and Tom and Becky joined in with enthusiasm until the effort became a bit tiring; then they wandered down a winding path, holding their candles high and reading the tangled web of names, dates, addresses, and mottos with which the rocky walls had been decorated in candle soot. Still drifting along and talking, they barely noticed that they were now in a part of the cave whose walls were not marked. They smoked their own names under an overhanging ledge and moved on. Soon they reached a spot where a small stream of water, trickling over a ledge and carrying limestone sediment, had, over the slow passage of ages, formed a laced and ruffled Niagara in gleaming, permanent stone."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_027",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "He sat beside her and wrapped his arms around her; she pressed her face against his chest, holding onto him tightly while she voiced her fears and useless regrets, which the distant echoes mocked with derisive laughter. Tom urged her to regain her courage, but she insisted she could not. He began to criticize and berate himself for leading her into this wretched predicament; this approach proved more effective. She promised to attempt to hope once more, saying she would rise and follow wherever he guided her if only he would cease speaking in that manner. She maintained that he was no more at fault than she was.

Thus, they continued on—without purpose—simply wandering—all they could manage was to keep moving. For a brief period, hope appeared to stir—not because of any logical justification, but simply because it is the nature of hope to return when its spirit has not been crushed by age or repeated disappointment.

Eventually, Tom took Becky’s candle and extinguished it. This act of conservation spoke volumes; no words were necessary. Becky grasped the implication, and her hope vanished once more. She realized that although Tom possessed a full candle and several remnants in his pockets, he felt compelled to save them.

Before long, exhaustion began to take its toll; the children struggled to remain alert, as the thought of stopping was terrifying when every moment had become so valuable. Moving in any direction represented progress and potential success, whereas sitting down meant welcoming death and hastening its arrival.

Finally, Becky’s weak legs could carry her no further. She sat down. Tom rested beside her, and they spoke of home, their friends there, their cozy beds, and, most of all, the light. Becky wept, and Tom attempted to find ways to console her, but his reassurances had become worn out from repetition and sounded like bitter irony. Weariness weighed so heavily on Becky that she drifted into sleep. Tom felt thankful. He watched her strained face and observed it becoming relaxed and natural as she experienced pleasant dreams; soon, a smile appeared and lingered there. Her tranquil expression brought a measure of calm and restoration to his own mind, and his thoughts drifted toward past days and hazy memories. While he was lost in reflection, Becky awakened with a light, airy laugh—but it was cut short on her lips, followed by a moan.

“Oh, how could I have slept! I wish I had never, ever awakened! No! No, I don’t, Tom! Don’t look at me like that! I won’t say it again.”

“I am happy you slept, Becky; you will feel refreshed now, and we will discover the path out.”

“We can attempt it, Tom; but I saw such a lovely land in my dream. I suspect we are going there.”

“Perhaps not, perhaps not. Cheer up, Becky, and let us continue to try.”

They stood up and walked along, holding hands and feeling hopeless. They attempted to calculate how long they had been inside the cave, but all they understood was that it felt like days and weeks, even though it was obvious that could not be true, as their candles had not yet burned out."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_028",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“For what reason?”

“Because I had the main entrance reinforced with boiler iron a fortnight ago and secured with three locks—and I possess the keys.”

Tom’s face drained of all color.

“What is wrong, lad! Someone hurry! Bring a glass of water!”

The water was fetched and splashed onto Tom’s face.

“There, you are recovered. What troubled you, Tom?”

“Oh, Judge, Injun Joe is inside the cave!”

CHAPTER XXXIII

In moments the report had traveled, and several small boats carrying men departed for McDougal’s cave, followed shortly by the ferryboat packed with people. Tom Sawyer traveled in the boat carrying Judge Thatcher.

Upon unlocking the cave entrance, a grim scene appeared in the faint light. Injun Joe lay prone on the floor, deceased, his face pressed near the door’s gap, as though his desperate gaze had remained fixed, until his final breath, upon the brightness and warmth of the world beyond. Tom felt moved, understanding from his own ordeal the agony this man had endured. Compassion stirred within him, yet he simultaneously experienced a profound sense of safety and liberation, realizing for the first time the immense burden of fear he had carried since the moment he testified against the violent fugitive.

Injun Joe’s bowie-knife rested nearby, the blade snapped in two. The heavy support beam of the door had been laboriously chipped and cut; it was futile work, as the natural rock created a threshold outside, and the knife had made no impression on that unyielding surface; the only result was the destruction of the blade. Even without the stone barrier, the effort would have been in vain, for had the beam been entirely removed, Injun Joe could not have fit his frame beneath the door, a fact he understood. He had merely hacked at the wood to occupy himself—to endure the agonizing passage of time—to keep his tormented mind active. Usually, one might discover several candle stubs tucked into the vestibule’s cracks, abandoned by visitors; none remained now. The captive had hunted them down and consumed them. He had also managed to trap a few bats, which he had likewise eaten, leaving only the claws. The wretched man had perished from starvation. Nearby, a stalagmite had been rising from the floor for centuries, formed by water dripping from a stalactite above. The prisoner had snapped off the stalagmite and set a stone upon the base, having carved a small depression into it to collect the vital drop that fell every three minutes with the monotonous rhythm of a clock—a single spoonful every twenty-four hours. That drop was falling when the Pyramids were young; when Troy collapsed; when Rome was founded; when Christ was crucified; when the Conqueror established the British empire; when Columbus voyaged; when the Lexington massacre was fresh news.

It continues to fall now; it will persist long after these events have faded into the late afternoon of history and the dusk of legend, eventually consumed by the deep darkness of total forgetfulness."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_028",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“Why?”

“Because I had its heavy door reinforced with boiler iron two weeks ago, and triple-locked it—and I have the keys.”

Tom turned as pale as a sheet.

“What’s wrong, boy! Someone, run! Get a glass of water!”

The water was brought and splashed onto Tom’s face.

“There, you’re fine now. What was wrong with you, Tom?”

“Oh, Judge, Injun Joe is in the cave!”

CHAPTER XXXIII

Within minutes the news had spread, and a dozen small boats filled with men were heading toward McDougal’s cave, followed quickly by the ferryboat packed with passengers. Tom Sawyer was in the boat carrying Judge Thatcher.

When the cave door was unlocked, a tragic sight appeared in the dim light. Injun Joe lay stretched out on the ground, dead, his face pressed against the crack in the door, as if his longing eyes had been fixed, until his final moment, on the light and warmth of the free world outside. Tom was moved, for he knew from his own experience how much this wretch had suffered. He felt pity, yet he also experienced an overwhelming sense of relief and security, which revealed to him more clearly than before just how heavy a burden of fear he had carried since the day he testified against this murderous outcast.

Injun Joe’s bowie knife lay nearby, its blade snapped in two. The heavy support beam of the door had been chipped and hacked through with exhausting effort; it was futile work, too, because the solid rock formed a sill outside it, and the knife had made no impression on that stubborn material; the only damage was to the knife itself. Even without the stone obstruction, the labor would have been pointless, for even if the beam had been cut away, Injun Joe could not have squeezed his body under the door, and he knew it. He had only hacked at that spot to keep busy—to pass the agonizing time—to occupy his tortured mind. Usually, one could find several candle stubs left by tourists in the crevices of this entrance, but there were none now. The prisoner had hunted them down and eaten them. He had also managed to catch a few bats, which he had also eaten, leaving only their claws. The poor man had starved to death. Nearby, a stalagmite had been slowly growing from the floor for ages, formed by water dripping from a stalactite above. The captive had broken off the stalagmite and placed a stone on the stump, having carved a shallow hollow into it to catch the precious drop that fell once every three minutes with the dreary regularity of a clock tick—a dessert spoonful every twenty-four hours. That drop was falling when the Pyramids were new; when Troy fell; when the foundations of Rome were laid; when Christ was crucified; when the Conqueror created the British Empire; when Columbus sailed; when the massacre at Lexington was “news.”

It is falling now; it will still be falling when all these events have faded into the afternoon of history and the twilight of tradition, and been swallowed up in the deep night of oblivion."
}

Injun Joe’s bowie knife lay nearby, its blade broken in two. The heavy support beam of the door had been chipped and hacked away with exhausting effort. It was useless work, however, because the solid rock formed a ledge outside the door, and the knife had no effect on that stubborn material; the only thing damaged was the knife itself. Even if there had been no stone in the way, the work would still have been pointless, for even if the beam had been cut away, Injun Joe could not have squeezed his body under the door, and he knew it. He had only hacked at that spot to keep busy, to pass the long, weary hours, and to occupy his tortured mind. Usually, one could find several candle stubs left by tourists in the cracks of this entrance, but there were none now. The prisoner had searched them out and eaten them. He had also managed to catch a few bats, which he had eaten as well, leaving only their claws. The poor man had starved to death. Nearby, a stalagmite had been slowly growing from the ground for ages, built by water dripping from a stalactite above. The captive had broken off the stalagmite and placed a stone on the stump. He had carved a shallow hollow in the stone to catch the precious drop that fell every three minutes with the steady rhythm of a clock—a dessert spoonful every twenty-four hours. That drop was falling when the Pyramids were new, when Troy fell, when Rome was founded, when Christ was crucified, when the Conqueror created the British Empire, when Columbus sailed, and when the massacre at Lexington was news.

It is falling now; it will still be falling when all these events have faded into the distant past, into the twilight of tradition, and been swallowed up by the darkness of oblivion."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_029",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Jones intends to reveal something to the guests this evening, though I happened to hear him mention it to Auntie earlier as a confidential matter; I suspect it is hardly a secret anymore. Everyone is aware—including the widow, despite her attempts to act otherwise. Mr. Jones insisted that Huck be present, as he apparently could not manage his big surprise without him.

“What is the secret about, Sid?”

“About Huck following those robbers to the widow’s place. I suppose Mr. Jones planned to make a grand spectacle of his announcement, but I imagine it will fall quite flat.”

Sid laughed in a smug, self-satisfied manner.

“Sid, were you the one who leaked it?”

“Oh, it makes no difference who did it. Someone told—that is sufficient.”

“Sid, there is only one person in this village spiteful enough for that, and it is you. If you had been in Huck’s position, you would have crept away and never reported the thieves to anyone. You are incapable of anything but malice, and you cannot stand to see others commended for virtuous acts. There—do not thank me, as the widow says”—and Tom boxed Sid’s ears and shoved him toward the exit with a few kicks. “Now go and inform Auntie if you have the nerve—and you will suffer for it tomorrow!”

Shortly afterward, the widow’s visitors were seated for supper, with a dozen youngsters arranged at smaller tables nearby, following the customs of that region and era. At the appropriate moment, Mr. Jones delivered a brief address, thanking the widow for the honor she bestowed upon him and his sons, but noting that there was another individual whose humility—

And so the speech continued. He unveiled his secret regarding Huck’s role in the event with the most theatrical flair he could muster, yet the resulting surprise was mostly feigned and lacked the boisterous enthusiasm that might have occurred under better conditions. Still, the widow performed a decent display of shock and showered Huck with so much praise and appreciation that he nearly forgot the unbearable irritation of his stiff new suit in the even worse agony of being the focus of everyone’s attention and acclaim.

The widow announced her intention to provide Huck with a home and an education, and stated that once she had sufficient funds, she would help him establish a modest business. Tom saw his opening. He declared:

“Huck does not require that. Huck is wealthy.”

Only a significant effort to maintain social decorum prevented the guests from offering the expected polite laughter at this humorous remark. The silence grew somewhat strained. Tom interrupted it:

“Huck possesses money. You may doubt it, but he has a great deal. Oh, there is no need to smirk—I believe I can prove it. Just give me a moment.”

Tom dashed outside. The guests exchanged looks of confused curiosity—and glanced inquiringly at Huck, who remained speechless.

“Sid, what is wrong with Tom?” Aunt Polly asked. “He—well, there is simply no understanding that boy. I never—”

Tom returned, laboring under the burden of his heavy bags, and Aunt Polly left her remark unfinished."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_029",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Jones is planning to reveal something to everyone here tonight. I overheard him telling Auntie about it earlier today as a secret, but I don’t think it’s much of a secret anymore. Everyone knows—even the Widow, despite her pretending she doesn’t. Mr. Jones insisted that Huck be here; he couldn’t manage his big surprise without Huck, you know!”

“Secret about what, Sid?”

“About Huck tracking the robbers to the Widow’s house. I suppose Mr. Jones intended to make a big deal out of his surprise, but I bet it falls pretty flat.”

Sid chuckled in a very smug and satisfied way.

“Sid, were you the one who told?”

“Oh, never mind who it was. Someone told—that’s enough.”

“Sid, there’s only one person in this town mean enough to do that, and that’s you. If you had been in Huck’s place, you would have sneaked down the hill and never told anyone about the robbers. You can’t do anything but mean things, and you can’t stand to see anyone praised for doing good ones. There—no thanks, as the Widow says”—and Tom cuffed Sid’s ears and helped him to the door with several kicks. “Now go and tell Auntie if you dare—and tomorrow you’ll get what’s coming to you!”

Some minutes later, the Widow’s guests were at the dinner table, and a dozen children were propped up at little side tables in the same room, following the custom of that region and era. At the appropriate moment, Mr. Jones made his short speech, in which he thanked the Widow for the honor she was showing him and his sons, but noted that there was another person whose modesty—

And so on. He revealed his secret about Huck’s role in the adventure with the most dramatic flair he could muster, but the surprise it caused was largely feigned and not as loud or enthusiastic as it might have been under better circumstances. Still, the Widow put on a decent show of astonishment and heaped so many compliments and so much gratitude upon Huck that he almost forgot the nearly intolerable discomfort of his new clothes in the entirely intolerable discomfort of being a target for everyone’s gaze and praise.

The Widow said she intended to give Huck a home under her roof and have him educated; and that when she could spare the money, she would help him start a business in a modest way. Tom’s chance had arrived. He said:

“Huck doesn’t need it. Huck’s rich.”

Only a heavy strain on the guests' good manners kept back the expected polite laugh at this joke. But the silence was a little awkward. Tom broke it:

“Huck has money. Maybe you don’t believe it, but he has plenty of it. Oh, you don’t need to smile—I think I can prove it to you. Just wait a minute.”

Tom ran outside. The company looked at each other with perplexed interest—and inquiringly at Huck, who was speechless.

“Sid, what is wrong with Tom?” said Aunt Polly. “He—well, there is just no understanding that boy. I never—”

Tom entered, struggling with the weight of his sacks, and Aunt Polly did not finish her sentence."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "TWAIN_TOM_SAWYER_030",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "He appeared disheveled, unkempt, and wore the same tattered remnants of clothing that had once rendered him a charming figure during his days of liberty and joy. Tom discovered him, explained the distress his absence had caused, and pressed him to return home. Huck’s expression shifted from peaceful satisfaction to a somber mood. He remarked:

“Please don’t mention it, Tom. I attempted it, but it simply fails; it doesn’t work, Tom. It isn’t suited for me; I am not accustomed to such a life. The widow is kind and welcoming, but I cannot endure those habits. She insists I rise at the identical hour each morning; she forces me to bathe, they comb my hair until it hurts; she forbids me from sleeping in the woodshed; I am compelled to wear those cursed garments that stifle me, Tom; they seem to block all airflow; and they are so excessively fine that I cannot sit, lie down, or tumble about anywhere; I haven’t slid down a cellar-door in—well, it feels like years; I must attend church and perspire endlessly—I despise those wretched sermons! I cannot catch a fly in there, I cannot chew. I am required to wear shoes every Sunday. The widow eats by a bell; she retires by a bell; she rises by a bell—everything is so dreadfully structured that a person cannot bear it.”

“Well, everyone lives that way, Huck.”

“Tom, that makes no difference. I am not everyone, and I cannot tolerate it. It is miserable to be so restricted. And food arrives too effortlessly—I take no pleasure in meals served that way. I must request permission to go fishing; I must ask to go swimming—darn it, I have to ask to do everything. Well, I had to speak so politely that it provided no comfort—I had to retreat to the attic and curse for a while each day, just to feel like myself, or I would have perished, Tom. The widow would not permit me to smoke; she would not allow me to shout, yawn, stretch, or scratch in front of others—” [Then with a sudden burst of particular annoyance and resentment]—“And blast it, she prayed constantly! I have never encountered such a woman! I had to leave, Tom—I simply had to. Furthermore, that school is about to start, and I would have been forced to attend—well, I would not endure that, Tom. Listen, Tom, being wealthy is not as wonderful as people claim. It is nothing but constant worry, constant sweating, and wishing you were dead the whole time. Now these clothes suit me, and this barrel suits me, and I am never going to abandon them again. Tom, I never would have gotten into all this trouble if it hadn’t been for that money; now you just take my portion of it along with yours, and give me a dime occasionally—not often, because I don’t care for anything unless it is reasonably difficult to obtain—and you go and plead my case to the widow.”

“Oh, Huck, you know I cannot do that. It isn’t right; and besides, if you will attempt this lifestyle just a little longer, you will grow to enjoy it.”"
}

The widow wouldn’t let me smoke; she wouldn’t let me yell, yawn, stretch, or scratch in front of people—” [Then, with a spasm of specific irritation and injury]—“And damn it, she prayed all the time! I’ve never seen such a woman! I had to leave, Tom—I just had to. Besides, school is about to start, and I would have had to go to that—well, I wouldn’t stand for that, Tom. Look here, Tom, being rich isn’t what it’s cracked up to be. It’s just worry after worry, sweating constantly, and wishing you were dead the whole time. Now, these clothes suit me, this barrel suits me, and I’m never going to give them up again. Tom, I never would have gotten into all this trouble if it hadn’t been for that money. model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_001",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“I believe you are mistaken, Basil, though I shall not debate it. Only those who have lost their intellectual way ever engage in arguments. Tell me, does Dorian Gray hold you in high regard?”

The artist pondered for a brief interval. “He is fond of me,” he replied eventually; “I am certain of it. Naturally, I offer him excessive flattery. I derive a peculiar satisfaction from uttering remarks to him that I know I will later regret. Generally, he is delightful toward me, and we remain in the studio discussing countless topics. Occasionally, however, he is dreadfully inconsiderate and appears to find genuine joy in causing me distress. At those moments, Harry, I feel as though I have surrendered my entire spirit to someone who views it merely as a blossom for his lapel, a trivial adornment to flatter his ego, or a seasonal decoration.”

“Summer days have a tendency to drag on,” Lord Henry whispered. “Perhaps you will grow weary before he does. It is a melancholy thought, yet there is no question that talent outlives physical beauty. That explains why we exert such effort to over-educate ourselves. In the fierce competition for survival, we desire something permanent, so we clutter our intellects with useless trivia and data, foolishly hoping to maintain our standing. The perfectly informed individual is the modern ideal. Yet, the mind of such a person is a terrifying thing. It resembles a shop filled with curiosities, cluttered with dust and grotesque items, each marked with an inflated price. Regardless, I suspect you will be the first to tire. One day you will observe your friend and find him slightly distorted, or perhaps you will dislike his complexion or some other detail. You will harbor bitter resentment toward him and conclude that he has treated you poorly. When he visits next, you will be entirely detached and aloof. It would be a great shame, as it would change you. Your account is quite a romance—a romance of art, perhaps—and the misfortune of any romance is that it leaves one feeling so unromantic.”

“Harry, cease speaking in that manner. As long as I draw breath, Dorian Gray’s personality will hold sway over me. You cannot comprehend my feelings. You shift your allegiances too frequently.”

“Ah, my dear Basil, that is precisely why I understand it so well. Those who remain loyal perceive only the superficial aspects of love; it is the unfaithful who truly grasp love’s tragedies.” Lord Henry ignited a match against a delicate silver container and commenced smoking a cigarette with a self-assured, content expression, as if he had encapsulated the entire world in a single statement. A rustling sound came from sparrows chirping within the ivy’s green, lacquered foliage, and blue shadows from the clouds raced across the lawn like swallows. The garden was truly pleasant. And how charming the emotions of others were—far more captivating than their opinions, he reflected. One’s own soul and the passions of one’s companions—these were the truly enthralling elements of existence."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_001",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“I think you’re wrong, Basil, but I won’t argue. Only those who are intellectually lost ever argue. Tell me, is Dorian Gray very fond of you?”

The painter thought for a few moments. “He likes me,” he answered after a pause. “I know he likes me. Of course, I flatter him dreadfully. I find a strange pleasure in saying things to him that I know I’ll regret later. Usually, he’s charming to me, and we sit in the studio and talk about a thousand things. Occasionally, however, he’s horribly thoughtless and seems to take real delight in hurting me. Then I feel, Harry, that I’ve given my entire soul to someone who treats it like a flower for his lapel, a bit of decoration to feed his vanity, an ornament for a summer day.”

“Summer days tend to drag on,” Lord Henry murmured. “Perhaps you’ll tire of him before he tires of you. It’s a sad thought, but there’s no doubt that genius lasts longer than beauty. That explains why we all take such pains to over-educate ourselves. In the brutal struggle for existence, we want something that lasts, so we fill our minds with useless facts and junk, in the foolish hope of maintaining our status. The thoroughly well-informed person—that’s the modern ideal. And the mind of a well-informed person is a dreadful thing. It’s like a thrift shop, full of monsters and dust, with everything priced far above its actual value. Regardless, I think you’ll tire first. One day you’ll look at your friend, and he’ll seem a little out of proportion to you, or you won’t like his tone, or something similar. You’ll bitterly reproach him in your heart and truly believe he’s treated you badly. The next time he visits, you’ll be perfectly cold and indifferent. It will be a great pity, because it will change you. What you’ve told me is quite a romance—a romance of art, one might call it—and the worst part of any romance is that it leaves one feeling so unromantic.”

“Harry, don’t talk like that. As long as I live, Dorian Gray’s personality will dominate me. You can’t understand what I feel. You change too often.”

“Ah, my dear Basil, that is exactly why I can understand it. Those who are faithful know only the trivial side of love: it is the faithless who know love’s tragedies.” Lord Henry struck a light on a dainty silver case and began to smoke a cigarette with a self-conscious, satisfied air, as if he had summed up the world in a single phrase. There was a rustle of chirping sparrows in the ivy, and blue cloud-shadows chased each other across the grass like swallows. How pleasant the garden was! And how delightful other people’s emotions were—much more delightful than their ideas, it seemed to him. One’s own soul and the passions of one’s friends—those were the truly fascinating things in life."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_002",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "He remained unaware of the quiet.

“Basil, I am weary of posing,” Dorian Gray exclaimed abruptly. “I need to step outside and sit in the garden. The atmosphere in here is suffocating.”

“My dear friend, please forgive me. When I am at my easel, I lose track of everything else. Yet you have never posed better. You remained completely motionless. I have successfully captured the impression I desired—the slightly parted lips and the luminous quality in your eyes. I am unsure what Harry has been telling you, but he has surely given you a most remarkable look. I assume he has been flattering you. You must not trust a single thing he says.”

“He has certainly not been offering me compliments. Perhaps that is why I do not credit anything he has told me.”

“You are aware that you believe every word,” Lord Henry remarked, observing him with his dreamy, lethargic gaze. “I shall accompany you to the garden. It is dreadfully warm in this studio. Basil, let us have some iced refreshments, perhaps something containing strawberries.”

“Of course, Harry. Simply ring the bell, and when Parker arrives, I will relay your request. I must finish this background, so I will meet you shortly. Do not keep Dorian away for too long. I have never felt more capable of painting than I do today. This will be my greatest work. It is already a masterpiece as it stands.”

Lord Henry walked into the garden and discovered Dorian Gray pressing his face into the large, refreshing lilac blossoms, greedily inhaling their scent as though it were wine. He approached him and rested a hand on his shoulder. “You are entirely correct to do so,” he whispered. “Nothing can heal the soul except the senses, just as nothing can heal the senses except the soul.”

The young man jumped and retreated. He was hatless, and the foliage had ruffled his unruly curls, tangling their golden strands. A look of alarm appeared in his eyes, the kind seen in those suddenly roused from sleep. His finely sculpted nostrils flared, and an unseen tension caused his scarlet lips to quiver.

“Indeed,” Lord Henry persisted, “that is a primary secret of existence—to remedy the soul through the senses, and the senses through the soul. You are a marvelous being. You possess more knowledge than you realize, just as you possess less than you desire.”

Dorian Gray scowled and averted his gaze. He could not help but feel drawn to the tall, elegant man standing beside him. His romantic, olive-toned face and weary demeanor intrigued him. There was a quality in his soft, languid voice that was utterly captivating. Even his cool, pale, flower-like hands held a strange allure. They gestured as he spoke, moving like music, appearing to possess a language of their own. Yet he felt a sense of dread toward him, and felt ashamed of that fear. Why had it required a stranger to unveil his own nature to him? He had known Basil Hallward for months, yet their friendship had never changed him. Suddenly, someone had entered his life who seemed to have unmasked the mystery of existence."
}
