“Basil, I’m tired of standing,” Dorian Gray suddenly exclaimed. “I need to go out and sit in the garden. The air in here is stifling.”

“My dear fellow, I’m so sorry. When I’m painting, I can’t think of anything else. But you’ve never sat better. You were perfectly still. I’ve captured the exact effect I wanted—the slightly parted lips and that bright look in your eyes. I don’t know what Harry has been telling you, but he’s certainly given you a wonderful expression. I assume he’s been flattering you. You shouldn’t believe a word he says.”

“He certainly hasn’t been flattering me. Perhaps that’s why I don’t believe anything he’s told me.”

“You know you believe every bit of it,” Lord Henry said, watching him with his dreamy, languid eyes. “I’ll join you in the garden. It’s dreadfully hot in this studio. Basil, let’s have something iced to drink, maybe something with strawberries.”

“Certainly, Harry. Just ring the bell, and when Parker arrives, I’ll tell him what you want. I need to finish this background, so I’ll join you later. Don’t keep Dorian too long. I’ve never been in better form for painting than I am today. This is going to be my masterpiece. It’s my masterpiece just as it is.”

Lord Henry went out to the garden and found Dorian Gray burying his face in the large, cool lilac blossoms, feverishly drinking in their scent as if it were wine. He stepped close and placed a hand on his shoulder. “You’re quite right to do that,” he murmured. “Nothing can heal the soul but the senses, just as nothing can heal the senses but the soul.”

The young man started and pulled back. He was bareheaded, and the leaves had tossed his unruly curls, tangling their golden threads. There was a look of fear in his eyes, the kind people have when they are suddenly awakened. His finely chiseled nostrils quivered, and a hidden nerve made his scarlet lips tremble.

“Yes,” Lord Henry continued, “that is one of the great secrets of life—to heal the soul through the senses, and the senses through the soul. You are a wonderful creation. You know more than you realize, just as you know less than you wish to know.”

Dorian Gray frowned and turned his head away. He couldn’t help but like the tall, graceful man standing beside him. His romantic, olive-toned face and weary expression intrigued him. There was something about his low, languid voice that was absolutely fascinating. Even his cool, white, flower-like hands had a strange charm. They moved as he spoke, like music, and seemed to have a language of their own. Yet, he felt afraid of him, and ashamed of that fear. Why had it taken a stranger to reveal him to himself? He had known Basil Hallward for months, but their friendship had never changed him. Suddenly, someone had entered his life who seemed to have unveiled the mystery of existence."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_003",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Avoid the theatre this evening, Dorian,” Hallward requested. “Stay and have dinner with me.”

“That is impossible, Basil.”

“For what reason?”

“I have given my word to Lord Henry Wotton that I would accompany him.”

“He will not value you more for being reliable. He is constantly unfaithful to his own commitments. I urge you to stay away.”

Dorian Gray chuckled and moved his head in refusal.

“I implore you.”

The young man paused, glancing toward Lord Henry, who observed them from the tea-table with a grin of amusement.

“I am obliged to go, Basil,” he replied.

“As you wish,” Hallward stated, walking over to place his teacup upon the tray. “The hour is late, and since you must change your attire, you should not delay. Farewell, Harry. Farewell, Dorian. Visit me soon. Come tomorrow.”

“Certainly.”

“You will keep that in mind?”

“Naturally, I shall,” Dorian exclaimed.

“And ... Harry!”

“Yes, Basil?”

“Keep in mind the request I made of you while we were in the garden earlier today.”

“That has slipped my mind.”

“I have faith in you.”

“I wish I possessed such faith in myself,” Lord Henry remarked with a laugh. “Let us go, Mr. Gray; my carriage waits outside, and I can leave you at your residence. Farewell, Basil. It has been a truly fascinating afternoon.”

Once the door had shut behind them, the artist collapsed onto a sofa, his expression twisting with distress.

CHAPTER III.

At half-past twelve the following day, Lord Henry Wotton walked from Curzon Street to the Albany to visit his uncle, Lord Fermor. Fermor was a pleasant yet blunt-spoken elderly bachelor; the general public labeled him selfish because they gained nothing from him, though high society viewed him as generous since he entertained those who provided him amusement. His father had served as ambassador in Madrid during the youth of Isabella, long before Prim was a factor, but had quit the diplomatic corps in a fit of pique after being denied the Paris Embassy—a position he felt he deserved due to his noble lineage, his laziness, the quality of his writing, and his intense love of luxury. The son, having served as his father’s assistant, had resigned alongside him, a move many considered unwise at the time. Upon inheriting the title months later, he dedicated himself to the noble pursuit of total idleness. He owned two expansive town residences but chose to reside in apartments to avoid bother, taking most meals at his club. He occasionally oversaw his coal mines in the Midlands, justifying this minor exertion by claiming the sole benefit of coal was that it allowed a gentleman the luxury of burning timber in his own fireplace. Politically, he was a Tory, except when that party held power, at which point he loudly criticized them as a group of Radicals. He was a hero to his personal servant, who intimidated him, and a menace to his kin, whom he intimidated in return. Only England could have fostered such a man, and he frequently declared that the nation was headed toward ruin. His beliefs were archaic, yet there was significant merit to his biases."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_003",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“Don’t go to the theater tonight, Dorian,” Hallward said. “Stay and have dinner with me.”

“I can’t, Basil.”

“Why?”

“Because I promised Lord Henry Wotton I’d go with him.”

“He won’t respect you any more for keeping your promises. He always breaks his own. I’m asking you not to go.”

Dorian Gray laughed and shook his head.

“I’m begging you.”

The young man hesitated and looked over at Lord Henry, who was watching them from the tea table with an amused smile.

“I have to go, Basil,” he replied.

“Very well,” Hallward said, walking over to set his cup on the tray. “It’s getting late, and since you need to get dressed, you shouldn’t waste any time. Goodbye, Harry. Goodbye, Dorian. Come see me soon. Come tomorrow.”

“Certainly.”

“You won’t forget?”

“No, of course not,” Dorian exclaimed.

“And... Harry!”

“Yes, Basil?”

“Remember what I asked you when we were in the garden this morning.”

“I’ve forgotten it.”

“I trust you.”

“I wish I could trust myself,” Lord Henry said, laughing. “Come, Mr. Gray, my cab is outside, and I can drop you off at your place. Goodbye, Basil. It’s been a very interesting afternoon.”

As the door closed behind them, the painter threw himself onto a sofa, a look of pain crossing his face.

CHAPTER III.

At half-past twelve the next day, Lord Henry Wotton strolled from Curzon Street over to the Albany to visit his uncle, Lord Fermor, a genial if somewhat blunt-mannered old bachelor. The outside world called him selfish because they gained nothing from him, but society considered him generous since he fed the people who entertained him. His father had been an ambassador in Madrid during Isabella’s youth, long before Prim was a factor, but had retired from the diplomatic service in a fit of pique after being denied the Paris Embassy—a post he felt he deserved due to his birth, his laziness, the quality of his dispatches, and his intense love of pleasure. The son, who had served as his father’s secretary, resigned alongside him—a foolish move, as people thought at the time—and upon inheriting the title months later, dedicated himself to the serious aristocratic art of doing absolutely nothing. He owned two large townhouses but preferred living in apartments because it was less trouble, and he ate most of his meals at his club. He paid some attention to managing his coal mines in the Midlands, excusing this hint of industry by claiming the only advantage of owning coal was that it allowed a gentleman the luxury of burning wood in his own fireplace. Politically, he was a Tory, except when the Tories were in power, at which point he loudly criticized them for being a pack of radicals. He was a hero to his valet, who bullied him, and a terror to most of his relatives, whom he bullied in return. Only England could have produced him, and he always insisted the country was going to the dogs. His principles were outdated, but there was a lot to be said for his prejudices."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_004",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Indeed! And where do wicked Americans end up after death?” the duchess asked.

“They go to America,” Lord Henry whispered.

Sir Thomas scowled. “I fear your nephew holds a bias against that magnificent nation,” he remarked to Lady Agatha. “I have toured the entire country in vehicles supplied by the directors, who are remarkably courteous in such affairs. I promise you, visiting it is a true education.”

“But is a trip to Chicago truly necessary for one’s education?” Mr. Erskine inquired mournfully. “I lack the energy for such a voyage.”

Sir Thomas gestured dismissively. “Mr. Erskine of Treadley possesses the world within his library. We practical men prefer witnessing things firsthand rather than reading about them. Americans are a fascinating group. They are entirely rational. I believe that is their defining trait. Yes, Mr. Erskine, a completely rational people. I guarantee there is no nonsense regarding the Americans.”

“How appalling!” Lord Henry exclaimed. “I can endure brute force, but brute reason is utterly intolerable. There is an injustice in its application. It is an assault upon the intellect.”

“I fail to follow you,” Sir Thomas said, his face flushing.

“I follow you, Lord Henry,” Mr. Erskine murmured, smiling.

“Paradoxes are fine in their own right...” the baronet replied.

“Was that a paradox?” Mr. Erskine asked. “I did not think so. Perhaps it was. Regardless, the path of paradoxes is the path of truth. To verify reality, we must observe it upon a tightrope. When truths turn into acrobats, we are able to evaluate them.”

“Goodness!” Lady Agatha remarked, “how you gentlemen debate! I am certain I can never discern your subject. Oh! Harry, I am truly annoyed with you. Why do you attempt to convince our charming Mr. Dorian Gray to abandon the East End? I assure you he would be quite essential. They would adore his music.”

“I prefer that he plays for me,” Lord Henry declared, smiling, as he glanced down the table and received a bright look in return.

“But they are so miserable in Whitechapel,” Lady Agatha persisted.

“I can feel sympathy for everything aside from suffering,” Lord Henry stated, lifting his shoulders. “I cannot sympathize with that. It is too unsightly, too ghastly, too upsetting. There is something deeply unhealthy about the modern obsession with pain. One ought to sympathize with the vibrancy, the beauty, and the joy of existence. The less discussed regarding life’s wounds, the better.”

“Nevertheless, the East End remains a significant issue,” Sir Thomas noted, shaking his head solemnly.

“Precisely,” the young lord replied. “It is the issue of slavery, and we attempt to resolve it by entertaining the slaves.”

The politician scrutinized him. “What reform do you suggest, then?” he asked.

Lord Henry chuckled. “I have no desire to alter anything in England besides the climate,” he replied. “I am perfectly satisfied with philosophical reflection. However, since the nineteenth century has become insolvent through an excess of sympathy, I would propose that we turn to science to rectify our course. The benefit of emotions is that they lead us off course, while the benefit of science is its lack of emotion.”

“But we carry such heavy obligations,” Mrs. Vandeleur suggested shyly.

“Dreadfully heavy,” Lady Agatha repeated."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_005",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Dorian offered a smile and a shake of his head: “I suspect not, Lady Henry. I refrain from speaking while music plays—at least, when it is quality music. Should the music be poor, it is a necessity to suppress it with chatter.”

“Oh! That sounds like one of Harry’s notions, doesn’t it, Mr. Gray? I usually learn Harry’s opinions through his acquaintances. It is the sole method I have of discovering them. However, do not assume I dislike fine music. I love it, yet it frightens me. It renders me excessively romantic. I have simply idolized pianists—sometimes a pair at once, according to Harry. I cannot explain the appeal. Perhaps it is because they are foreign. They all are, aren’t they? Even those native to England seem to turn into foreigners eventually, don’t they? It is quite brilliant of them, and a tribute to art. It renders it entirely international, doesn’t it? You have never attended my gatherings, have you, Mr. Gray? You really must. I cannot manage orchids, but I never skimp on foreigners. They make my drawing rooms appear so artistic. But here is Harry! Harry, I entered seeking you, to inquire about something—I cannot recall what—and discovered Mr. Gray here. We enjoyed a delightful conversation regarding music. We share identical views. No; I believe our views are entirely distinct. Still, he has been perfectly charming. I am delighted to have met him.”

“I am enchanted, my dear, truly enchanted,” Lord Henry remarked, raising his dark, arched brows and observing them both with a playful grin. “My apologies for my tardiness, Dorian. I went to hunt for a length of antique brocade on Wardour Street and spent hours haggling over it. People today understand the cost of everything but the worth of nothing.”

“I fear I must depart,” Lady Henry declared, interrupting a tense pause with her light, abrupt giggle. “I have an engagement to drive with the duchess. Farewell, Mr. Gray. Farewell, Harry. You are dining out, I assume? As am I. Perhaps we shall cross paths at Lady Thornbury’s.”

“I expect so, my dear,” Lord Henry replied, closing the door after her as she fluttered from the room, resembling a bird of paradise that had spent the night in a downpour, leaving behind a subtle scent of frangipanni. He then ignited a cigarette and collapsed onto the sofa.

“Never wed a woman with hair the color of straw, Dorian,” he remarked after a few inhalations.

“Why is that, Harry?”

“Because they are so prone to sentimentality.”

“But I enjoy sentimental individuals.”

“Never wed at all, Dorian. Men wed out of fatigue; women, out of curiosity: both end in disappointment.”

“I doubt I shall marry, Harry. I am too deeply in love. That is one of your maxims. I am applying it, just as I do with all your advice.”

“Who is the object of your affection?” Lord Henry inquired after a moment.

“An actress,” Dorian Gray replied, his face flushing.

Lord Henry gave a shrug. “That is a somewhat ordinary start.”

“You would not say so if you beheld her, Harry.”

“Who is she?”

“Her name is Sibyl Vane.”

“I have never heard of her.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_005",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Dorian smiled and shook his head. "I’m afraid I don’t think so, Lady Henry. I never talk during music—at least, not during good music. If one hears bad music, it’s one’s duty to drown it out with conversation."

"Ah! That’s one of Harry’s opinions, isn’t it, Mr. Gray? I always hear Harry’s views from his friends. It’s the only way I ever learn what they are. But you mustn’t think I don’t like good music. I adore it, but it frightens me. It makes me too romantic. I’ve simply worshipped pianists—sometimes two at once, Harry tells me. I don’t know what it is about them. Perhaps it’s that they’re foreigners. They all are, aren’t they? Even those born in England become foreigners after a while, don’t they? It’s so clever of them, and such a compliment to art. It makes it quite cosmopolitan, doesn’t it? You’ve never been to any of my parties, have you, Mr. Gray? You must come. I can’t afford orchids, but I spare no expense on foreigners. They make one’s rooms look so picturesque. But here is Harry! Harry, I came in to look for you, to ask you something—I forget what it was—and I found Mr. Gray here. We’ve had such a pleasant chat about music. We have exactly the same ideas. No; I think our ideas are quite different. But he has been most pleasant. I’m so glad I’ve seen him."

"I am charmed, my love, quite charmed," said Lord Henry, raising his dark, crescent-shaped eyebrows and looking at them both with an amused smile. "So sorry I’m late, Dorian. I went to look for a piece of old brocade in Wardour Street and had to bargain for hours for it. Nowadays, people know the price of everything and the value of nothing."

"I’m afraid I must be going," exclaimed Lady Henry, breaking an awkward silence with her silly, sudden laugh. "I’ve promised to drive with the duchess. Goodbye, Mr. Gray. Goodbye, Harry. You’re dining out, I suppose? So am I. Perhaps I’ll see you at Lady Thornbury’s."

"I dare say, my dear," said Lord Henry, shutting the door behind her as she flitted out of the room, looking like a bird of paradise that had been out all night in the rain, leaving behind a faint scent of frangipani. Then he lit a cigarette and threw himself down on the sofa.

"Never marry a woman with straw-colored hair, Dorian," he said after a few puffs.

"Why, Harry?"

"Because they are so sentimental."

"But I like sentimental people."

"Never marry at all, Dorian. Men marry because they are tired; women, because they are curious: both are disappointed."

"I don’t think I’m likely to marry, Harry. I’m too much in love. That is one of your aphorisms. I’m putting it into practice, just as I do everything you say."

"Who are you in love with?" asked Lord Henry after a pause.

"An actress," said Dorian Gray, blushing.

Lord Henry shrugged his shoulders. "That is a rather commonplace debut."

"You wouldn’t say so if you saw her, Harry."

"Who is she?"

"Her name is Sibyl Vane."

"Never heard of her.""
}

"Who is she?"

"Her name is Sibyl Vane."

"Never heard of her.""

{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_005",
  "condition": "simplify",
  "rewritten_text": "Dorian smiled and shook his head. "I am afraid I don't think so, Lady Henry. I never talk during music—at least, not during good music. If one hears bad music, it is one's duty to drown it out with conversation."

"Ah! That is one of Harry's opinions, isn't it, Mr. Gray? I always hear Harry's views from his friends. It is the only way I ever learn what they are. But you must not think I don't like good music. I adore it, but I am afraid of it. It makes me too romantic. I have simply worshipped pianists—sometimes two at a time, Harry tells me. I don't know what it is about them. Perhaps it is that they are foreigners. They all are, aren't they? Even those born in England become foreigners after a time, don't they? It is so clever of them, and such a compliment to art. It makes it quite international, doesn't it? You have never been to any of my parties, have you, Mr. Gray? You must come. I cannot afford orchids, but I spare no expense on foreigners. They make one's rooms look so picturesque. But here is Harry! Harry, I came in to look for you to ask you something—I forget what it was—and I found Mr. Gray here. We have had such a pleasant chat about music. We have exactly the same ideas. No, I think our ideas are quite different. But he has been most pleasant. I am so glad I have seen him."

"I am charmed, my love, quite charmed," said Lord Henry, raising his dark, crescent-shaped eyebrows and looking at them both with an amused smile. "I am so sorry I am late, Dorian. I went to look for a piece of old brocade in Wardour Street and had to bargain for hours for it. Nowadays, people know the price of everything and the value of nothing."

"I am afraid I must be going," exclaimed Lady Henry, breaking an awkward silence with her silly, sudden laugh. "I have promised to drive with the duchess. Goodbye, Mr. Gray. Goodbye, Harry. You are dining out, I suppose? So am I. Perhaps I shall see you at Lady Thornbury's."

"I expect so, my dear," said Lord Henry, shutting the door behind her as she flitted out of the room, looking like a bird of paradise that had been out all night in the rain, leaving behind a faint scent of frangipani. Then he lit a cigarette and threw himself down on the sofa.

"Never marry a woman with straw-colored hair, Dorian," he said after a few puffs.

"Why, Harry?"

"Because they are so sentimental."

"But I like sentimental people."

"Never marry at all, Dorian. Men marry because they are tired; women, because they are curious: both are disappointed."

"I don't think I am likely to marry, Harry. I am too much in love. That is one of your aphorisms. I am putting it into practice, just as I do everything you say."

"Who are you in love with?" asked Lord Henry after a pause.

"With an actress," said Dorian Gray, blushing.

Lord Henry shrugged his shoulders. "That is a rather commonplace beginning."

"You would not say so if you saw her, Harry."

"Who is she?"

"Her name is Sibyl Vane."

"Never heard of her.""
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_006",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "They are easily discovered. There is nothing enigmatic about them. They go riding in the park during the morning hours and gossip at afternoon tea gatherings. They possess their predictable smiles and trendy behaviors. They are entirely transparent. Yet, an actress! How distinct an actress is! Harry, why did you fail to inform me that an actress is the sole object worth loving?”

“Because I have been enamored with so many of them, Dorian.”

“Oh, certainly, dreadful individuals with artificial hair and cosmetic-laden faces.”

“Do not disparage artificial hair and painted faces. There is a remarkable allure to them, at times,” Lord Henry remarked.

“I regret now that I spoke to you regarding Sibyl Vane.”

“You could not have avoided telling me, Dorian. Throughout your entire existence, you will disclose everything you do to me.”

“Yes, Harry, I suspect that is accurate. I am unable to refrain from sharing things with you. You exert a strange power over me. If I were to commit a crime, I would arrive and confess it to you. You would comprehend me.”

“Individuals such as you—the willful rays of sunshine in this world—do not perpetrate crimes, Dorian. Regardless, I am grateful for the sentiment. Now, tell me—hand me the matches, like a good fellow—thanks—what is the true nature of your connection with Sibyl Vane?”

Dorian Gray sprang up, his face reddened and his eyes glowing. “Harry! Sibyl Vane is holy!”

“Only holy things are worth handling, Dorian,” Lord Henry stated, his voice carrying a peculiar hint of sorrow. “But why are you irritated? I assume she will be yours one day. When someone is in love, they invariably start by fooling themselves, and they invariably finish by fooling everyone else. That is what society labels a romance. You are acquainted with her, at least, I presume?”

“Naturally I am acquainted with her. During my initial night at the theater, that ghastly old Jew approached my box once the show concluded and proposed taking me backstage to present me to her. I was incensed with him, and informed him that Juliet had been deceased for centuries and that her remains rested within a marble sepulcher in Verona. I suspect, judging by his vacant expression of bewilderment, that he believed I had consumed too much champagne, or something similar.”

“I am not astonished.”

“Then he inquired if I contributed to any newspapers. I replied that I never even peruse them. He appeared deeply let down by that, and confessed to me that every dramatic critic was involved in a plot against him, and that each one of them could be purchased.”

“I would not be surprised if he were entirely correct regarding that. Conversely, judging by their outward appearance, most of them cannot be very costly.”

“Well, he seemed to believe they were beyond his budget,” Dorian chuckled. “By that point, however, the theater lights were being extinguished, and I was forced to leave. He wanted me to sample some cigars he highly suggested. I refused. The following evening, naturally, I returned to the venue. Upon seeing me, he offered a deep bow and insisted that I was a generous benefactor of the arts.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_006",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "You can always find them. There is no mystery to any of them. They ride in the park in the morning and gossip at tea parties in the afternoon. They have their predictable smiles and their trendy manners. They are completely transparent. But an actress! How different an actress is! Harry, why didn’t you tell me that the only thing worth loving is an actress?”

“Because I have loved so many of them, Dorian.”

“Oh, yes, awful people with dyed hair and painted faces.”

“Don’t criticize dyed hair and painted faces. There is an extraordinary charm in them, sometimes,” said Lord Henry.

“I wish now I hadn’t told you about Sibyl Vane.”

“You couldn’t have helped telling me, Dorian. Throughout your life, you will tell me everything you do.”

“Yes, Harry, I believe that is true. I can’t help telling you things. You have a strange influence over me. If I ever committed a crime, I would come and confess it to you. You would understand me.”

“People like you—the willful sunbeams of life—don’t commit crimes, Dorian. But I am much obliged for the compliment, all the same. And now tell me—hand me the matches, like a good boy—thanks—what is your actual relationship with Sibyl Vane?”

Dorian Gray jumped to his feet, his cheeks flushed and his eyes burning. “Harry! Sibyl Vane is sacred!”

“It is only the sacred things that are worth touching, Dorian,” said Lord Henry, with a strange touch of pathos in his voice. “But why should you be annoyed? I suppose she will belong to you someday. When one is in love, one always begins by deceiving oneself, and one always ends by deceiving others. That is what the world calls a romance. You know her, at any rate, I suppose?”

“Of course I know her. On the first night I was at the theater, the awful old man came around to the box after the performance was over and offered to take me behind the scenes and introduce me to her. I was furious with him and told him that Juliet had been dead for hundreds of years and that her body was lying in a marble tomb in Verona. I think, from his blank look of amazement, that he was under the impression that I had had too much champagne, or something.”

“I am not surprised.”

“Then he asked me if I wrote for any of the newspapers. I told him I never even read them. He seemed terribly disappointed at that and confided to me that all the dramatic critics were in a conspiracy against him and that every one of them could be bought.”

“I wouldn’t wonder if he was quite right there. But, on the other hand, judging from their appearance, most of them cannot be at all expensive.”

“Well, he seemed to think they were beyond his means,” laughed Dorian. “By this time, however, the lights were being turned off in the theater, and I had to go. He wanted me to try some cigars that he strongly recommended. I declined. The next night, of course, I arrived at the place again. When he saw me, he gave me a low bow and assured me that I was a generous patron of art.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_007",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "He rose and gazed toward the street. The setting sun had transformed the upper windows of the buildings across the way into scarlet gold. The glass shimmered like sheets of glowing metal. The sky overhead resembled a withered rose. He reflected upon his friend’s vibrant, youthful existence and pondered how it might conclude.

Upon reaching his residence around half-past twelve, he noticed a telegram resting on the hall table. He broke the seal and discovered it was from Dorian Gray, informing him of his engagement to marry Sibyl Vane.

CHAPTER V.

“Mother, Mother, I am so joyful!” the girl murmured, hiding her face in the lap of the weary, worn-out woman who sat with her back to the harsh, prying light in the only armchair their shabby living room provided. “I am so joyful!” she reiterated, “and you must share in this joy!”

Mrs. Vane flinched, resting her slender, pale hands upon her daughter’s hair. “Joyful!” she repeated, “I only feel joy, Sibyl, when I watch you perform. You must focus solely on your acting. Mr. Isaacs has been very kind to us, and we are in his debt.”

The girl glanced up and pouted. “Money, Mother?” she exclaimed, “what significance does money hold? Love surpasses wealth.”

“Mr. Isaacs provided us fifty pounds to settle our accounts and secure a suitable wardrobe for James. You must remember that, Sibyl. Fifty pounds is a significant amount. Mr. Isaacs has been extremely thoughtful.”

“He is no gentleman, Mother, and I despise the manner in which he speaks to me,” the girl declared, standing up and walking toward the window.

“I fail to see how we could survive without his assistance,” the older woman replied peevishly.

Sibyl Vane tossed her head and chuckled. “We have no further need of him, Mother. Prince Charming governs our lives now.” She paused. A flush of color rose to her cheeks. Her lips parted with quick, trembling breaths. A sudden wave of passion washed over her, rustling the delicate fabric of her gown. “I love him,” she stated plainly.

“Silly girl! Silly girl!” came the repetitive, hollow response. The gesturing of her crooked, ring-adorned fingers added a grotesque quality to the remark.

The girl laughed once more. Her voice held the delight of a bird released from a cage. Her eyes mirrored this melody with brilliance, then shut briefly, as if to conceal their mystery. When they reopened, the haze of a reverie had drifted across them.

Cold, pragmatic advice drifted from the worn chair, suggesting caution and echoing the philosophy of those who masquerade as common sense. She ignored it. She was liberated within her own intense devotion. Her prince, Prince Charming, remained by her side. She had summoned memory to reconstruct him. She had dispatched her spirit to seek him out, and it had returned with him. His kiss scorched her lips once more. Her eyelids felt warm from his breath.

Then, the advice shifted, touching upon investigation and discovery. Perhaps this young man possessed wealth. If that were the case, matrimony should be considered. The tide of worldly calculation crashed against her ears."
}

The sky above resembled a faded rose. He thought about his friend’s vibrant, intense life and wondered how it would all conclude.

When he reached home around twelve-thirty, he noticed a telegram on the hall table. He opened it and saw it was from Dorian Gray. It informed him that he was engaged to be married to Sibyl Vane.

CHAPTER V.

“Mother, Mother, I am so happy!” the girl whispered, burying her face in the lap of the faded, weary-looking woman who sat in the only armchair in their dingy living room, her back turned to the harsh, intrusive light. “I am so happy!” she repeated. “And you must be happy, too!”

Mrs. Vane flinched and placed her thin, pale hands on her daughter’s head. “Happy!” she echoed. “I am only happy, Sibyl, when I see you perform. You must not think of anything but your acting. Mr. Isaacs has been very good to us, and we owe him money.”

The girl looked up and pouted. “Money, Mother?” she cried. “What does money matter? Love is more important than money.”

“Mr. Isaacs has advanced us fifty pounds to pay off our debts and to buy a proper outfit for James. You must not forget that, Sibyl. Fifty pounds is a very large sum. Mr. Isaacs has been most considerate.”

“He is not a gentleman, Mother, and I hate the way he speaks to me,” the girl said, standing up and walking over to the window.

“I don’t know how we could manage without him,” the older woman answered fretfully.

Sibyl Vane tossed her head and laughed. “We don’t need him anymore, Mother. Prince Charming rules our lives now.” Then she paused. A flush rose in her blood and colored her cheeks. Her lips parted with quick breaths. They trembled. A sudden wave of passion swept over her, stirring the delicate folds of her dress. “I love him,” she said simply.

“Foolish child! Foolish child!” was the automatic response. The waving of her crooked, ring-adorned fingers made the words seem grotesque.

The girl laughed again. Her voice held the joy of a caged bird. Her eyes caught the melody and reflected it with radiance, then closed for a moment, as if to hide their secret. When they opened, the haze of a dream had passed over them.

Cold, practical wisdom spoke to her from the worn chair, hinting at caution and quoting from that book of cowardice whose author mimics the name of common sense. She did not listen. She was free within her prison of passion. Her prince, Prince Charming, was with her. She had called on memory to recreate him. She had sent her soul to search for him, and it had brought him back. His kiss burned on her mouth again. Her eyelids were warm with his breath.

Then, wisdom changed its approach and spoke of investigation and discovery. This young man might be wealthy. If so, marriage should be considered. The waves of worldly cunning broke against the shell of her ear."
}

When he reached home around half-past twelve, he saw a telegram on the hall table. He opened it and saw it was from Dorian Gray. It informed him that Dorian was engaged to marry Sibyl Vane.

CHAPTER V.

“Mother, Mother, I am so happy!” the girl whispered, burying her face in the lap of the tired, faded woman who sat in the only armchair in their dingy living room, her back turned to the harsh, intrusive light. “I am so happy!” she repeated. “And you must be happy, too!”

Mrs. Vane flinched and placed her thin, pale hands on her daughter’s head. “Happy!” she echoed. “Sibyl, I am only happy when I see you act. You must not think of anything but your acting. Mr. Isaacs has been very good to us, and we owe him money.”

The girl looked up and pouted. “Money, Mother?” she cried. “What does money matter? Love is more important than money.”

“Mr. Isaacs has lent us fifty pounds to pay our debts and buy a proper outfit for James. You must not forget that, Sibyl. Fifty pounds is a very large sum. Mr. Isaacs has been very considerate.”

“He is not a gentleman, Mother, and I hate the way he talks to me,” the girl said, standing up and walking to the window.

“I don’t know how we could manage without him,” the older woman replied in a complaining tone.

Sibyl Vane tossed her head and laughed. “We don’t need him anymore, Mother. Prince Charming rules our lives now.” She paused. A flush of color rose to her cheeks. Her lips parted as she breathed quickly, and they trembled. A sudden wave of passion swept over her, stirring the folds of her dress. “I love him,” she said simply.

“Foolish child! Foolish child!” her mother repeated like a parrot. The waving of her crooked, ring-adorned fingers made the words seem strange.

The girl laughed again. Her voice held the joy of a caged bird. Her eyes sparkled with that same joy, then closed for a moment as if to hide a secret. When she opened them, they looked dreamy.

Her mother continued to speak from the worn chair, offering cautious advice and practical warnings. Sibyl did not listen. She was lost in her own feelings. Her prince, Prince Charming, was with her. She remembered him clearly; she had searched for him in her mind, and he had returned. She could still feel his kiss on her mouth and his breath on her eyelids.

Then her mother changed her tone, speaking of spying and discovery. She suggested that if this young man were rich, they should consider marriage. The girl heard her mother’s worldly, calculating words, but they did not reach her."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_008",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "He was scheduled to disembark at Melbourne, offer a courteous farewell to the captain, and head immediately for the gold mines. Within seven days, he would unearth a massive piece of solid gold, the biggest ever found, and transport it to the shore in a wagon escorted by six armed officers. The outlaws would assault them on three occasions, only to be repelled with heavy casualties. Or perhaps not. He would avoid the gold mines entirely. They were dreadful spots where men became drunk, shot one another in taverns, and spoke foully. Instead, he would become a pleasant sheep-rancher; one evening, while returning home on horseback, he would witness a lovely heiress being abducted by a bandit on a dark steed, pursue them, and save her. Naturally, they would fall in love, wed, return home, and reside in a grand London mansion. Truly, wonderful prospects awaited him. However, he had to remain virtuous, avoid losing his temper, and refrain from wasting his wealth. She was merely a year his senior, yet she possessed far greater worldly wisdom. He must also remember to send her letters with every post and recite his nightly prayers before retiring. God was benevolent and would guard him. She would offer prayers for his safety as well, and after several years, he would return home wealthy and content.

The boy heard her with a sullen expression and offered no reply. He felt miserable about departing from his home.

Nevertheless, this was not the sole cause of his somber and irritable mood. Despite his lack of experience, he possessed a keen awareness of the risks surrounding Sibyl. This young gentleman courting her could intend her no benefit. He was a man of status, and he loathed him for it, feeling a strange, instinctive animosity he could not explain, which only made it more powerful. He was also aware of his mother’s superficial and vain character, perceiving in it a vast threat to Sibyl and her well-being. Children start by adoring their parents; as they mature, they evaluate them; eventually, they may pardon them.

His mother! He had a question to pose to her, something he had pondered in silence for many months. A casual remark overheard at the theater, a quiet insult that reached him while he stood by the stage entrance, had triggered a series of dreadful ideas. He recalled the moment as if a whip had struck his face. His forehead creased into a sharp line, and he bit his lip in distress.

“You aren’t paying attention to me, Jim,” Sibyl exclaimed, “and I am creating such wonderful plans for your future. Please, say something.”

“What would you like me to say?”

“Oh! Just that you will be a good boy and keep us in your thoughts,” she replied, beaming at him.

He gave a shrug. “It is far more probable that you will forget me than that I will forget you, Sibyl.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_008",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "He was to get off the ship at Melbourne, say a polite goodbye to the captain, and head straight for the gold mines. Within a week, he would find a massive nugget of pure gold—the largest ever discovered—and transport it to the coast in a wagon escorted by six armed police officers. The outlaws would attack them three times, only to be defeated with heavy casualties. Or, no. He wouldn't go to the gold mines at all. They were awful places where men got drunk, shot each other in bars, and used foul language. Instead, he would become a respectable sheep farmer. One evening, while riding home, he would see a beautiful heiress being kidnapped by a bandit on a black horse, give chase, and rescue her. Naturally, they would fall in love, get married, return home, and live in a massive house in London. Yes, wonderful things were waiting for him. But he had to be very good, keep his temper, and not waste his money. She was only a year older than him, but she understood so much more about life. He also had to make sure to write to her with every mail delivery and say his prayers every night before bed. God was very good and would watch over him. She would pray for him, too, and in a few years, he would return quite wealthy and happy.

The boy listened sullenly to her and didn't reply. He was heartbroken about leaving home.

Yet it wasn't just this that made him gloomy and morose. Inexperienced as he was, he still had a strong sense of the danger surrounding Sibyl’s situation. This young dandy courting her could mean her no good. He was a gentleman, and he hated him for that—hated him through some strange, instinctive prejudice he couldn't explain, which made it all the more powerful within him. He was also aware of his mother’s shallow and vain nature, and in that, he saw infinite peril for Sibyl and her happiness. Children start by loving their parents; as they grow older, they judge them; sometimes, they forgive them.

His mother! He had something on his mind to ask her, something he had brooded over for many silent months. A chance phrase he had heard at the theater, a whispered sneer that reached his ears one night while he waited at the stage door, had triggered a train of horrible thoughts. He remembered it as if it had been the lash of a whip across his face. His brows knit together into a sharp furrow, and with a twitch of pain, he bit his lower lip.

“You aren't listening to a word I’m saying, Jim,” Sibyl cried, “and I’m making the most delightful plans for your future. Do say something.”

“What do you want me to say?”

“Oh! Just that you’ll be a good boy and not forget us,” she answered, smiling at him.

He shrugged his shoulders. “You’re more likely to forget me than I am to forget you, Sibyl.”"
}

{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_009",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I must depart. Farewell. Remember that you have only one child left to care for, and trust me when I say that if this man harms my sister, I shall discover his identity, hunt him down, and slay him like a cur. I vow it.”

The absurd excess of the vow, the intense motion that went with it, and the frantic, theatrical language made existence feel more intense to her. She knew this environment well. She drew breath more easily, and for the first time in many months, she truly felt pride in her son. She wished to sustain the encounter at that same level of emotion, but he interrupted her. Trunks required moving and scarves needed searching for. The servant of the boarding house hurried back and forth. There was haggling with the carriage driver. The moment vanished into common trivialities. It was with a fresh sense of letdown that she fluttered her frayed lace cloth from the casement as her son departed. She sensed that a significant chance had been squandered. She comforted herself by informing Sibyl how lonely she imagined her existence would be, now that she possessed only one child to watch over. She recalled the wording. It had satisfied her. Regarding the vow, she remained silent. It was expressed with such striking, theatrical flair. She felt certain they would all find humor in it eventually.

CHAPTER VI.

“I assume you have received the intelligence, Basil?” remarked Lord Henry that evening as Hallward was ushered into a small, secluded chamber at the Bristol where a meal had been prepared for three.

“No, Harry,” replied the painter, handing his headgear and outer garment to the deferential server. “What is it? I trust it concerns nothing political! Those matters do not engage me. There is scarcely a soul in the House of Commons worth capturing on canvas, though many would benefit from a bit of moral cleansing.”

“Dorian Gray is betrothed,” stated Lord Henry, observing him closely as he spoke.

Hallward recoiled and then scowled. “Dorian betrothed!” he exclaimed. “That cannot be!”

“It is entirely accurate.”

“To whom?”

“To some minor performer or other.”

“I cannot credit it. Dorian is far too prudent.”

“Dorian is far too intelligent not to commit foolish acts occasionally, my dear Basil.”

“Matrimony is hardly an act one performs occasionally, Harry.”

“Unless one is in America,” replied Lord Henry lazily. “But I did not claim he was wed. I claimed he was betrothed. There is a vast distinction. I possess a clear memory of being wed, yet I have no recollection of being betrothed. I suspect I was never betrothed at all.”

“But consider Dorian’s lineage, status, and fortune. It would be ridiculous for him to wed so far below his station.”

“If you desire to ensure he weds this young woman, tell him that, Basil. He will certainly do it then. Whenever a man commits a truly idiotic act, it is invariably for the most honorable reasons.”

“I trust the girl is virtuous, Harry. I have no desire to see Dorian bound to some wretched person who might corrupt his character and destroy his mind.”

“Oh, she is more than virtuous—she is lovely,” whispered Lord Henry, while sipping a drink of vermouth and orange bitters."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_010",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Enjoyment is the sole subject worth theorizing about,” he replied in his languid, musical tone. “Yet I fear I cannot take credit for this idea. It belongs to Nature, not to me. Pleasure serves as Nature’s litmus test, her mark of validation. When we experience happiness, we are invariably virtuous, yet when we are virtuous, we are not always happy.”

“Ah! But how do you define goodness?” Basil Hallward exclaimed.

“Indeed,” Dorian added, reclining in his seat and gazing at Lord Henry past the dense bunches of purple-petaled irises centered on the table, “what is your definition of goodness, Harry?”

“To be good is to exist in accord with oneself,” he answered, tapping the slender stem of his wine glass with his pale, tapered fingers. “Discord arises from being compelled to align with others. One’s personal existence is what truly matters. Regarding the lives of one’s peers, if one chooses to be a moralist or a Puritan, one may parade one’s ethical judgments, but they are not one’s business. Furthermore, individualism possesses a loftier purpose. Contemporary morality relies on adopting the standards of one’s era. I believe that for any refined individual to conform to the standards of his time is a form of profound immorality.”

“But surely, Harry, if one lives solely for oneself, one pays a heavy cost?” the artist proposed.

“Indeed, we are overtaxed for everything in this day and age. I suspect the true misfortune of the impoverished is that they can afford nothing beyond self-denial. Exquisite sins, much like exquisite objects, are the prerogative of the wealthy.”

“One pays in ways other than currency.”

“What sort of ways, Basil?”

“Oh! I imagine through regret, through agony, or... well, through the awareness of one’s own decline.”

Lord Henry lifted his shoulders in a shrug. “My dear friend, medieval art is delightful, but medieval sentiments are obsolete. One might employ them in literature, naturally. Yet the only elements suitable for fiction are those we have abandoned in reality. Trust me, no civilized person ever laments a pleasure, and no uncivilized person ever understands what pleasure is.”

“I understand what pleasure is,” Dorian Gray declared. “It is to worship someone.”

“That is certainly superior to being worshipped,” he replied, fiddling with some fruit. “Being worshipped is a burden. Women treat us much as humanity treats its deities. They adore us, and are constantly pestering us to perform favors for them.”

“I would argue that whatever they request, they have already bestowed upon us,” the young man remarked solemnly. “They awaken love within our souls. They have a right to reclaim it.”

“That is entirely accurate, Dorian,” Hallward insisted.

“Nothing is ever entirely accurate,” Lord Henry remarked.

“This is,” Dorian interjected. “You must concede, Harry, that women offer men the very gold of their existence.”

“Perhaps,” he sighed, “but they invariably demand it back in such trivial denominations. That is the vexation. Women, as a clever Frenchman once remarked, inspire us to create masterpieces and then perpetually prevent us from finishing them.”

“Harry, you are awful! I cannot fathom why I am so fond of you.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_010",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“Pleasure is the only thing worth theorizing about,” he answered in his slow, melodic voice. “But I’m afraid I can’t claim this theory as my own. It belongs to Nature, not me. Pleasure is Nature’s test, her sign of approval. When we are happy, we are always good, but when we are good, we aren’t always happy.”

“Ah! But what do you mean by good?” Basil Hallward cried.

“Yes,” Dorian echoed, leaning back in his chair and looking at Lord Henry over the heavy clusters of purple-lipped irises in the center of the table, “what do you mean by good, Harry?”

“To be good is to be in harmony with oneself,” he replied, touching the thin stem of his glass with his pale, slender fingers. “Discord is being forced to be in harmony with others. One’s own life—that is what matters. As for the lives of one’s neighbors, if you want to be a prig or a Puritan, you can flaunt your moral views about them, but they aren’t your concern. Besides, individualism has a higher aim. Modern morality consists of accepting the standards of one’s age. I consider it a form of the grossest immorality for any cultured person to accept the standards of their time.”

“But surely, if one lives merely for oneself, Harry, one pays a terrible price?” the painter suggested.

“Yes, we are overcharged for everything these days. I imagine the real tragedy of the poor is that they can afford nothing but self-denial. Beautiful sins, like beautiful things, are the privilege of the rich.”

“One has to pay in ways other than money.”

“What sort of ways, Basil?”

“Oh! I imagine in remorse, in suffering, in... well, in the consciousness of degradation.”

Lord Henry shrugged. “My dear fellow, medieval art is charming, but medieval emotions are outdated. You can use them in fiction, of course. But then, the only things one can use in fiction are the things one has stopped using in reality. Believe me, no civilized man ever regrets a pleasure, and no uncivilized man ever knows what a pleasure is.”

“I know what pleasure is,” Dorian Gray cried. “It is to adore someone.”

“That is certainly better than being adored,” he answered, toying with some fruit. “Being adored is a nuisance. Women treat us just as humanity treats its gods. They worship us and are always bothering us to do something for them.”

“I would have said that whatever they ask for, they have first given to us,” the young man murmured gravely. “They create love in our natures. They have a right to demand it back.”

“That is quite true, Dorian,” Hallward cried.

“Nothing is ever quite true,” Lord Henry said.

“This is,” Dorian interrupted. “You must admit, Harry, that women give men the very gold of their lives.”

“Possibly,” he sighed, “but they invariably want it back in such small change. That is the worry. Women, as some witty Frenchman once put it, inspire us with the desire to create masterpieces and always prevent us from carrying them out.”

“Harry, you are dreadful! I don’t know why I like you so much.”"
}

“That is quite true, Dorian,” cried Hallward.

“Nothing is ever quite true,” said Lord Henry.

“This is,” interrupted Dorian. “You must admit, Harry, that women give to men the very gold of their lives.”

“Possibly,” he sighed, “but they invariably want it back in such very small change. That is the worry. Women, as some witty Frenchman once put it, inspire us with the desire to do masterpieces and always prevent us from carrying them out.”

“Harry, you are dreadful! I don’t know why I like you so much.”"
}

  "rewritten_text": "“Pleasure is the only thing worth having a theory about,” he answered in his slow, melodic voice. “But I am afraid I cannot claim this theory as my own. It belongs to Nature, not to me. Pleasure is Nature’s test, her sign of approval. When we are happy, we are always good, but when we are good, we are not always happy.”

“Ah! But what do you mean by good?” cried Basil Hallward.

“Yes,” echoed Dorian, leaning back in his chair and looking at Lord Henry over the heavy clusters of purple-lipped irises that stood in the center of the table, “what do you mean by good, Harry?”

“To be good is to be in harmony with oneself,” he replied, touching the thin stem of his glass with his pale, slender fingers. “Discord is being forced to be in harmony with others. One’s own life—that is the important thing. As for the lives of one’s neighbors, if one wishes to be a prig or a Puritan, one can flaunt moral views about them, but they are not one’s concern. Besides, individualism has a higher aim. Modern morality consists in accepting the standards of one’s age. I consider it a form of the grossest immorality for any man of culture to accept the standards of his age.”

“But surely, if one lives merely for oneself, Harry, one pays a terrible price for doing so?” suggested the painter.

“Yes, we are overcharged for everything nowadays. I suspect the real tragedy of the poor is that they can afford nothing but self-denial. Beautiful sins, like beautiful things, are the privilege of the rich.”

“One has to pay in other ways besides money.”

“What sort of ways, Basil?”

“Oh! I should imagine in remorse, in suffering, in... well, in the awareness of degradation.”

Lord Henry shrugged his shoulders. “My dear fellow, medieval art is charming, but medieval emotions are out of date. One can use them in fiction, of course. But then, the only things one can use in fiction are the things one has stopped using in reality. Believe me, no civilized man ever regrets a pleasure, and no uncivilized man ever knows what a pleasure is.”

“I know what pleasure is,” cried Dorian Gray. “It is to adore someone.”

“That is certainly better than being adored,” he answered, toying with some fruit. “Being adored is a nuisance. Women treat us just as humanity treats its gods. They worship us and are always bothering us to do something for them.”

“Harry, you are dreadful! I don’t know why I like you so much.”"

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_011",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“I am terribly sorry that I caused you to lose an evening, Harry. My apologies to you both.”

“My dear Dorian, I suspect Miss Vane must be unwell,” Hallward interjected. “We shall return another night.”

“I wish she were ill,” he replied. “Instead, she appears merely indifferent and cold. She has completely changed. Yesterday she was a brilliant artist. Tonight, she is nothing more than a dull, average actress.”

“Do not speak that way of someone you love, Dorian. Love is far more magnificent than art.”

“Both are merely types of imitation,” Lord Henry observed. “But let us depart. Dorian, you must not remain here any longer. Witnessing poor acting is detrimental to one’s character. Furthermore, I doubt you intend for your wife to perform, so what does it signify if she portrays Juliet like a stiff mannequin? She is quite beautiful, and if she understands as little of life as she does of acting, she will be a charming discovery. There are only two types of people who are truly captivating—those who possess total knowledge, and those who possess none at all. Good heavens, my dear boy, do not look so miserable! The key to staying young is to never experience an unflattering emotion. Join Basil and me at the club. We shall smoke cigarettes and toast to Sibyl Vane’s beauty. She is lovely. What more could you desire?”

“Leave, Harry,” the young man shouted. “I wish to be by myself. Basil, you must depart. Oh! Can you not see that my heart is shattering?” Hot tears welled in his eyes. His lips quivered, and he hurried to the rear of the box, leaning against the wall while burying his face in his hands.

“Let us leave, Basil,” Lord Henry said, his voice filled with an odd gentleness, and the two men exited together.

Shortly thereafter, the footlights brightened and the curtain ascended for the third act. Dorian Gray returned to his chair. He appeared pale, haughty, and detached. The performance dragged on, feeling endless. Half the crowd departed, stomping in heavy boots and chuckling. The entire event was a disaster. The final act was performed before nearly vacant seats. The curtain fell amidst giggles and groans.

Once it concluded, Dorian Gray hurried backstage to the greenroom. The girl stood there by herself, a triumphant expression on her face. Her eyes glowed with a beautiful intensity. She seemed radiant. Her parted lips smiled over some private secret.

Upon his arrival, she looked at him, and a look of boundless happiness filled her. “How poorly I performed tonight, Dorian!” she exclaimed.

“Terribly!” he replied, staring at her in shock. “Terribly! It was awful. Are you sick? You have no concept of what it was like. You have no idea how much I endured.”

The girl smiled. “Dorian,” she replied, savoring his name with melodic resonance, as if it were sweeter than honey to her lips. “Dorian, you should have grasped it. But you understand now, don’t you?”

“Understand what?” he demanded, with irritation.

“Why I performed so poorly tonight.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_011",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“I am incredibly sorry that I have wasted your evening, Harry. I apologize to you both.”

“My dear Dorian, I assume Miss Vane must be ill,” Hallward interrupted. “We can come back another night.”

“I wish she were ill,” he replied. “But she seems simply callous and cold to me. She has completely changed. Last night she was a great artist. This evening she is just a commonplace, mediocre actress.”

“Don’t speak like that about someone you love, Dorian. Love is a more wonderful thing than art.”

“They are both just forms of imitation,” Lord Henry remarked. “But let’s go. Dorian, you shouldn’t stay here any longer. It isn’t good for one’s morals to watch bad acting. Besides, I don’t suppose you will want your wife to act, so what does it matter if she plays Juliet like a wooden doll? She is very lovely, and if she knows as little about life as she does about acting, she will be a delightful experience. There are only two kinds of people who are truly fascinating—people who know absolutely everything, and people who know absolutely nothing. Good heavens, my dear boy, don’t look so tragic! The secret to staying young is never to have an emotion that is unbecoming. Come to the club with Basil and me. We will smoke cigarettes and drink to the beauty of Sibyl Vane. She is beautiful. What more could you want?”

“Go away, Harry,” the young man cried. “I want to be alone. Basil, you must go. Ah! Can’t you see that my heart is breaking?” Hot tears filled his eyes. His lips trembled, and rushing to the back of the box, he leaned against the wall, hiding his face in his hands.

“Let’s go, Basil,” Lord Henry said with a strange tenderness in his voice, and the two men left together.

A few moments later, the footlights flared up and the curtain rose on the third act. Dorian Gray returned to his seat. He looked pale, proud, and indifferent. The play dragged on and seemed endless. Half the audience left, tramping in heavy boots and laughing. The whole thing was a disaster. The final act was performed to almost empty benches. The curtain fell to a smattering of giggles and groans.

As soon as it ended, Dorian Gray rushed behind the scenes into the greenroom. The girl was standing there alone, a look of triumph on her face. Her eyes were lit with an exquisite fire. There was a radiance about her. Her parted lips were smiling over some secret of their own.

When he entered, she looked at him, and an expression of infinite joy crossed her face. “How badly I acted tonight, Dorian!” she cried.

“Horribly!” he answered, staring at her in amazement. “Horribly! It was dreadful. Are you ill? You have no idea what it was like. You have no idea what I suffered.”

The girl smiled. “Dorian,” she answered, lingering over his name with a musical tone in her voice, as though it were sweeter than honey to her lips. “Dorian, you should have understood. But you understand now, don’t you?”

“Understand what?” he asked, angrily.

“Why I was so bad tonight.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_012",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The portrait remained exactly as it had been. It was absurd to imagine otherwise. 

Nevertheless, it seemed to observe him, displaying its lovely yet corrupted features and a malicious grin. Its vibrant hair shone in the morning light, and its azure eyes locked with his. A profound feeling of compassion, directed not toward himself but toward the depicted likeness, washed over him. It had already shifted and would continue to do so. Its golden tones would fade to ash, and its rosy complexion would perish. Every transgression he committed would leave a blemish to mar its beauty. Yet, he would remain virtuous. The painting, regardless of its state, would serve as his tangible moral compass. He would withstand temptation. He would avoid Lord Henry—or at the very least, refuse to heed those insidious, corrupting ideas that had first ignited his longing for unattainable things while in Basil Hallward’s garden. He would return to Sibyl Vane, seek her forgiveness, wed her, and attempt to rekindle his affection. Indeed, it was his obligation. She must have endured more than he had. Poor girl! He had acted with cruelty and selfishness toward her. The enchantment she once held over him would surely revive. They would find happiness together. His existence alongside her would be virtuous and lovely.

He rose from his seat and positioned a tall screen directly before the painting, shivering as he cast a glance at it. “How dreadful!” he whispered, then crossed to the window to throw it open. Stepping onto the lawn, he inhaled deeply. The crisp morning breeze appeared to dispel his dark impulses. His thoughts centered entirely on Sibyl. A subtle resonance of his former love stirred within him. He whispered her name repeatedly. The birds chirping in the damp garden appeared to be whispering of her to the blossoms.

CHAPTER VIII.

It was well past midday when he roused himself. His servant had tiptoed into the chamber repeatedly to check if he had stirred, puzzled by his master’s late slumber. At last, the bell rang, and Victor entered quietly, bearing a cup of tea and a stack of correspondence on a tray of antique Sevres porcelain. He pulled back the olive-satin drapes, which featured shimmering blue linings and covered the three grand windows.

“Monsieur has enjoyed a long rest,” he remarked with a smile.

“What time is it, Victor?” Dorian Gray inquired sleepily.

“It is a quarter past one, Monsieur.”

How late the hour was! He sat upright, sipped his tea, and examined his mail. One envelope was from Lord Henry, delivered by hand that morning. He paused briefly before setting it aside. He opened the remaining items with indifference. They consisted of the typical assortment of calling cards, dinner invitations, gallery passes, and charity concert schedules that are routinely sent to stylish young gentlemen throughout the social season. There was also a substantial invoice for a decorative silver Louis-Quinze vanity set; he had not yet dared to forward it to his guardians, who were quite traditional and failed to grasp that we reside in an era where superfluous items are our sole requirements. Additionally, there were several polite notes from Jermyn Street creditors, proposing to provide loans of any amount instantly at highly competitive interest rates."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_012",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "The painting had not changed. It was foolish to think so.

Yet it was watching him, with its beautiful, marred face and its cruel smile. Its bright hair shone in the early sunlight. Its blue eyes met his own. A sense of infinite pity, not for himself, but for the painted image of himself, washed over him. It had already altered, and would alter more. Its gold would wither into grey. Its red and white roses would die. For every sin he committed, a stain would mark and ruin its beauty. But he would not sin. The picture, changed or unchanged, would be to him the visible symbol of his conscience. He would resist temptation. He would not see Lord Henry anymore—or, at any rate, he would not listen to those subtle, poisonous theories that had first stirred within him the passion for impossible things in Basil Hallward’s garden. He would go back to Sibyl Vane, make amends, marry her, and try to love her again. Yes, it was his duty. She must have suffered more than he had. Poor girl! He had been selfish and cruel to her. The fascination she had once held over him would return. They would be happy together. His life with her would be beautiful and pure.

He rose from his chair and pulled a large screen directly in front of the portrait, shuddering as he glanced at it. “How horrible!” he whispered to himself, then walked to the window and opened it. Stepping out onto the grass, he took a deep breath. The fresh morning air seemed to drive away all his dark passions. He thought only of Sibyl. A faint echo of his love returned to him. He repeated her name over and over. The birds singing in the dew-drenched garden seemed to be telling the flowers about her.

CHAPTER VIII.

It was long past noon when he woke. His valet had crept into the room on tiptoe several times to see if he was stirring, wondering what had made his young master sleep so late. Finally, his bell rang, and Victor entered softly with a cup of tea and a stack of letters on a small tray of antique Sèvres china. He drew back the olive-satin curtains, with their shimmering blue lining, that hung before the three tall windows.

“Monsieur has slept well this morning,” he said, smiling.

“What time is it, Victor?” asked Dorian Gray sleepily.

“It is one-fifteen, Monsieur.”

How late it was! He sat up, sipped some tea, and looked through his letters. One was from Lord Henry, delivered by hand that morning. He hesitated for a moment, then set it aside. He opened the others listlessly. They contained the usual collection of cards, dinner invitations, tickets for private gallery viewings, programs for charity concerts, and the like that are showered on fashionable young men every morning during the social season. There was a rather heavy bill for an engraved silver Louis-Quinze vanity set that he had not yet had the courage to send to his guardians, who were extremely old-fashioned people and did not realize that we live in an age when unnecessary things are our only necessities. parts.text:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_012",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "The painting had not changed. It was foolish to think so.

Yet it was watching him, with its beautiful, marred face and its cruel smile. Its bright hair shone in the early sunlight. Its blue eyes met his own. A sense of infinite pity, not for himself, but for the painted image of himself, washed over him. It had already altered, and would alter more. Its gold would wither into grey. Its red and white roses would die. For every sin he committed, a stain would mark and ruin its beauty. But he would not sin. The picture, changed or unchanged, would be to him the visible symbol of his conscience. He would resist temptation. He would not see Lord Henry anymore—or, at any rate, he would not listen to those subtle, poisonous theories that had first stirred within him the passion for impossible things in Basil Hallward’s garden. He would go back to Sibyl Vane, make amends, marry her, and try to love her again. Yes, it was his duty. She must have suffered more than he had. Poor girl! He had been selfish and cruel to her. The fascination she had once held over him would return. They would be happy together. His life with her would be beautiful and pure.

He rose from his chair and pulled a large screen directly in front of the portrait, shuddering as he glanced at it. “How horrible!” he whispered to himself, then walked to the window and opened it. Stepping out onto the grass, he took a deep breath. The fresh morning air seemed to drive away all his dark passions. He thought only of Sibyl. A faint echo of his love returned to him. He repeated her name over and over. The birds singing in the dew-drenched garden seemed to be telling the flowers about her.

CHAPTER VIII.

It was long past noon when he woke. His valet had crept into the room on tiptoe several times to see if he was stirring, wondering what had made his young master sleep so late. Finally, his bell rang, and Victor entered softly with a cup of tea and a stack of letters on a small tray of antique Sèvres china. He drew back the olive-satin curtains, with their shimmering blue lining, that hung before the three tall windows.

“Monsieur has slept well this morning,” he said, smiling.

“What time is it, Victor?” asked Dorian Gray sleepily.

“It is one-fifteen, Monsieur.”

How late it was! He sat up, sipped some tea, and looked through his letters. One was from Lord Henry, delivered by hand that morning. He hesitated for a moment, then set it aside. He opened the others listlessly. They contained the usual collection of cards, dinner invitations, tickets for private gallery viewings, programs for charity concerts, and the like that are showered on fashionable young men every morning during the social season. There was a rather heavy bill for an engraved silver Louis-Quinze vanity set that he had not yet had the courage to send to his guardians, who were extremely old-fashioned people and did not realize that we live in an age when unnecessary things are our only necessities. There were also several politely worded notes from Jermyn Street money-lenders offering to advance any sum of money at a moment’s notice and at the most reasonable interest rates."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_013",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Such occurrences might grant a man status in Paris. However, London society is incredibly narrow-minded. In this city, one ought to avoid starting out with a scandal; that should be saved to add flavor to one’s later years. I assume they are unaware of your identity at the playhouse? If that is the case, you are safe. Did anyone observe you entering her private quarters? That is a significant detail.

Dorian remained silent for a short time, stunned by shock. At last, he muttered in a choked tone, “Harry, did you mention an inquest? What was your meaning? Did Sibyl—? Oh, Harry, this is unbearable! Please, be brief. Tell me the whole truth immediately.”

“I am certain it was not a mere accident, Dorian, though it must be presented as such to the public. Apparently, while departing the theatre with her mother around half-past twelve, she claimed she had left an item behind upstairs. They waited for a while, but she failed to return. Eventually, they discovered her lifeless on the floor of her dressing-room. She had ingested something by mistake, some awful substance used in theatres. I am unsure of the exact nature, but it contained either prussic acid or white lead. I suspect it was prussic acid, given that her death appears to have been immediate.”

“Harry, Harry, this is horrific!” the young man exclaimed.

“Indeed; it is quite tragic, naturally, but you must avoid becoming entangled in the matter. I noticed in The Standard that she was seventeen. I would have guessed she was even younger. She appeared such a child, and seemed to possess so little knowledge of her craft. Dorian, you must not allow this to disturb your composure. You shall join me for dinner, and later we will attend the opera. It is a Patti performance, and everyone will be in attendance. You may sit in my sister’s box. She is hosting some fashionable ladies.”

“Then I have killed Sibyl Vane,” Dorian Gray murmured to himself, “killed her just as surely as if I had slit her throat with a blade. Yet, the roses remain just as beautiful. The birds continue to sing in my garden with the same joy. And tonight, I am to dine with you, then proceed to the opera, and likely have supper afterward. How remarkably theatrical life is! Had I encountered this in a novel, Harry, I believe I would have wept. Yet, now that it has occurred in reality, and to me, it feels far too extraordinary for tears. Here is the initial passionate love-letter I have ever composed. It is strange that my first such letter should be sent to a girl who is dead. I wonder, can those pale, quiet figures we label the dead truly feel? Sibyl! Can she sense, or understand, or hear? Oh, Harry, how I once adored her! It feels like years have passed since then. She was my entire world. Then came that awful evening—was it truly only last night?—when her performance was so poor, and my heart nearly shattered. She provided an explanation. It was deeply moving. Yet, I felt nothing at all.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_013",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Things like that make a man fashionable in Paris. But in London, people are so narrow-minded. Here, you should never make your debut with a scandal. You should save that to add interest to your old age. I assume they don’t know your name at the theater? If they don’t, it’s fine. Did anyone see you going around to her dressing room? That is an important point.”

Dorian did not answer for a few moments. He was dazed with horror. Finally, he stammered in a stifled voice, “Harry, did you say an inquest? What did you mean by that? Did Sibyl—? Oh, Harry, I can’t bear it! But be quick. Tell me everything at once.”

“I have no doubt it wasn’t an accident, Dorian, though it must be presented that way to the public. It seems that as she was leaving the theater with her mother, around twelve-thirty, she said she had forgotten something upstairs. They waited for her, but she didn’t come back down. They eventually found her lying dead on the floor of her dressing room. She had swallowed something by mistake, some terrible substance they use at theaters. I don’t know what it was, but it contained either prussic acid or white lead. I suspect it was prussic acid, as she seems to have died instantly.”

“Harry, Harry, it is terrible!” cried the young man.

“Yes; it is very tragic, of course, but you must not get yourself involved in it. I see by The Standard that she was seventeen. I would have thought she was even younger. She looked like such a child and seemed to know so little about acting. Dorian, you mustn’t let this get to you. You must come and have dinner with me, and afterward we will stop by the opera. It is a Patti night, and everyone will be there. You can join my sister’s box. She has some fashionable women with her.”

“So I have murdered Sibyl Vane,” said Dorian Gray, half to himself, “murdered her as surely as if I had cut her little throat with a knife. Yet the roses are no less lovely for all that. The birds sing just as happily in my garden. And tonight I am to dine with you, then go to the opera, and have supper somewhere afterward, I suppose. How extraordinarily dramatic life is! If I had read all this in a book, Harry, I think I would have wept over it. Somehow, now that it has actually happened, and to me, it seems far too surreal for tears. Here is the first passionate love letter I have ever written in my life. It is strange that my first passionate love letter should have been addressed to a dead girl. I wonder, can they feel, those white, silent people we call the dead? Sibyl! Can she feel, or know, or listen? Oh, Harry, how I loved her once! It feels like years ago to me now. She was everything to me. Then came that dreadful night—was it really only last night?—when she played so badly, and my heart almost broke. She explained it all to me. It was terribly pathetic. But I wasn’t moved at all."
}
