model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_014",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The instant she encountered genuine existence, she ruined it, and it ruined her, leading to her end. Weep for Ophelia, if you choose. Cover your head in ashes because Cordelia was choked. Protest against the heavens because Brabantio’s daughter perished. Yet, do not squander your sorrow on Sibyl Vane. She possessed less reality than those figures.

A quiet settled in. The room grew dim as evening arrived. Silently, with silver-toned steps, the darkness stole in from the garden. The hues drained wearily from the objects in the room.

Eventually, Dorian Gray glanced upward. “You have clarified my own nature to me, Harry,” he whispered, exhaling with a hint of ease. “I sensed everything you described, yet I was somehow intimidated by it and unable to articulate it to myself. You understand me so thoroughly! Still, we shall not discuss these events again. It was a remarkable occurrence. That is all. I question whether life holds anything equally remarkable in reserve for me.”

“Life holds everything in reserve for you, Dorian. There is nothing you, with your exceptional beauty, will be unable to accomplish.”

“But imagine, Harry, if I grew gaunt, aged, and creased? What then?”

“Ah, in that case,” Lord Henry remarked, standing to depart, “then, my dear Dorian, you would be forced to struggle for your triumphs. Currently, they are delivered to you. No, you must preserve your appearance. We inhabit an era that reads too extensively to possess wisdom, and thinks too deeply to possess beauty. We cannot afford to lose you. Now, you ought to dress and head to the club. We are already somewhat behind schedule.”

“I believe I will meet you at the opera, Harry. I feel too exhausted to dine. What is the number of your sister’s box?”

“Twenty-seven, I think. It is located on the grand tier. You will find her name on the door. I regret that you will not join me for dinner.”

“I do not have the energy,” Dorian replied with apathy. “However, I am deeply grateful for all you have told me. You are truly my finest friend. No one has ever grasped my character as you have.”

“Our friendship is merely commencing, Dorian,” Lord Henry replied, gripping his hand. “Farewell. I trust I will see you before nine-thirty. Do not forget, Patti is performing.”

Once he shut the door, Dorian Gray rang the bell, and shortly after, Victor arrived with the lamps and lowered the shades. He waited with impatience for the servant to depart. The man appeared to take an endless amount of time with every task.

Once he was alone, he hurried to the screen and pulled it aside. No; the portrait showed no additional alterations. It had received word of Sibyl Vane’s passing before he had learned of it himself. It remained aware of life’s events as they unfolded. The wicked cruelty that disfigured the delicate lines of the mouth had, surely, emerged the very moment the girl consumed the poison, whatever it might have been. Or was it unconcerned with outcomes? Did it merely observe what transpired within the spirit? He pondered this, hoping that one day he might witness the transformation occurring right before his eyes, trembling as he harbored the wish."
}

Eventually, Dorian Gray glanced upward. “You have clarified my own nature to me, Harry,” he whispered, exhaling with a hint of ease. “I sensed everything you described, yet I was somehow intimidated by it and unable to articulate it to myself. You understand me so thoroughly! Still, we shall not discuss these events again. It was a remarkable occurrence. That is all. I question whether life holds anything equally remarkable in reserve for me.”

“Life holds everything in reserve for you, Dorian. There is nothing you, with your exceptional beauty, will be unable to accomplish.”

“But imagine, Harry, if I grew gaunt, aged, and creased? What then?”

“Ah, in that case,” Lord Henry remarked, standing to depart, “then, my dear Dorian, you would be forced to struggle for your triumphs. Currently, they are delivered to you. No, you must preserve your appearance. We inhabit an era that reads too extensively to possess wisdom, and thinks too deeply to possess beauty. We cannot afford to lose you. Now, you ought to dress and head to the club. We are already somewhat behind schedule.”

“I believe I will meet you at the opera, Harry. I feel too exhausted to dine. What is the number of your sister’s box?”

“Twenty-seven, I think. It is located on the grand tier. You will find her name on the door. I regret that you will not join me for dinner.”

“I do not have the energy,” Dorian replied with apathy. “However, I am deeply grateful for all you have told me. You are truly my finest friend. No one has ever grasped my character as you have.”

“Our friendship is merely commencing, Dorian,” Lord Henry replied, gripping his hand. “Farewell. I trust I will see you before nine-thirty. Do not forget, Patti is performing.”

Once he shut the door, Dorian Gray rang the bell, and shortly after, Victor arrived with the lamps and lowered the shades. He waited with impatience for the servant to depart. The man appeared to take an endless amount of time with every task.

Once he was alone, he hurried to the screen and pulled it aside. No; the portrait showed no additional alterations. It had received word of Sibyl Vane’s passing before he had learned of it himself. It remained aware of life’s events as they unfolded. The wicked cruelty that disfigured the delicate lines of the mouth had, surely, emerged the very moment the girl consumed the poison, whatever it might have been. Or was it unconcerned with outcomes? Did it merely observe what transpired within the spirit? He pondered this, hoping that one day he might witness the transformation occurring right before his eyes, trembling as he harbored the wish."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_016",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "And yet the thing would still live on. It would be always alive.

He shuddered, and for a moment he regretted that he had not told Basil the true reason why he had wanted to hide the painting away. Basil would have helped him resist Lord Henry’s influence, and the even more poisonous influences that stemmed from his own temperament. The love he felt for him—for it was truly love—contained nothing that was not noble and intellectual. It was not that mere physical admiration of beauty born of the senses, which dies when the senses grow weary. It was the kind of love that Michelangelo, Montaigne, Winckelmann, and even Shakespeare had known. Yes, Basil could have saved him. But it was too late now. The past could always be erased. Regret, denial, or forgetfulness could accomplish that. But the future was inevitable. There were passions within him that would find their terrible outlet, dreams that would make the shadow of their evil real.

He picked up the large purple-and-gold fabric that covered the couch and, holding it in his hands, stepped behind the screen. Was the face on the canvas viler than before? It seemed to him that it was unchanged, yet his loathing of it had intensified. Gold hair, blue eyes, and rose-red lips—they were all there. It was simply the expression that had altered. That was horrible in its cruelty. Compared to the censure or rebuke he saw in it, how shallow Basil’s reproaches about Sibyl Vane had been!—how shallow, and of what little consequence! His own soul was looking out at him from the canvas, calling him to judgment. A look of pain crossed his face, and he flung the rich cloth over the picture. As he did so, a knock came at the door. He walked out as his servant entered.

“The men are here, sir.”

He felt that the man had to be gotten rid of at once. He must not be allowed to know where the picture was being taken. There was something sly about him, and he had thoughtful, treacherous eyes. Sitting down at the writing desk, he scribbled a note to Lord Henry, asking him to send over something to read and reminding him that they were to meet at eight-fifteen that evening.

“Wait for an answer,” he said, handing it to him, “and show the men in here.”

In two or three minutes there was another knock, and Mr. Hubbard himself, the celebrated frame-maker from South Audley Street, came in with a somewhat rough-looking young assistant. Mr. Hubbard was a florid, red-whiskered little man whose admiration for art was considerably tempered by the constant poverty of most of the artists who dealt with him. As a rule, he never left his shop. He waited for people to come to him. But he always made an exception for Dorian Gray. There was something about Dorian that charmed everyone. It was a pleasure even to see him.

“What can I do for you, Mr. Gray?” he said, rubbing his fat, freckled hands. “I thought I would do myself the honor of coming over in person. I have just gotten a beautiful frame, sir. I picked it up at an auction.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_017",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "What connection could Dorian Gray possibly have to the passing of Sibyl Vane? There was no reason for alarm. Dorian Gray had not caused her death.

His gaze landed upon the yellow-covered volume Lord Henry had provided. He pondered its nature. Moving toward the small, pearl-hued octagonal pedestal that always reminded him of the handiwork of exotic Egyptian bees crafting in silver, he picked up the book, collapsed into a chair, and started flipping through the pages. Within moments, he was captivated. It was the most peculiar work he had ever encountered. It appeared to him that the transgressions of humanity were parading before him in silent pantomime, dressed in magnificent attire and accompanied by the soft melodies of flutes. Concepts he had only vaguely imagined suddenly became tangible. Ideas he had never conceived were slowly unveiled.

It was a story devoid of a traditional plot, featuring only a single protagonist; essentially, it was a psychological examination of a young man from Paris who dedicated his existence to manifesting, within the nineteenth century, every emotion and philosophical outlook belonging to eras other than his own. He sought to embody the diverse temperaments the world-spirit had experienced, cherishing for their very artifice those acts of self-denial that people foolishly labeled virtue, just as much as those inherent rebellions that the wise still termed sin. The prose was crafted in that distinctive, ornate style—simultaneously vivid and cryptic—brimming with slang, archaic terms, specialized jargon, and intricate phrasing, which marks the output of the most skilled French Symbolist writers. The metaphors within were as grotesque as orchids and as nuanced in their hues. The experiences of the senses were depicted through the language of mystical philosophy. At times, it was difficult to discern if one was perusing the divine raptures of a medieval saint or the diseased admissions of a contemporary transgressor. It was a corrupting volume. A dense scent of incense appeared to linger upon the leaves, clouding the intellect. The very rhythm of the prose, with its delicate, hypnotic repetition and complex, recurring motifs, induced in the young man, as he progressed through the chapters, a state of trance—a sickness of fantasy—that rendered him oblivious to the fading daylight and the encroaching gloom.

A clear, copper-green sky, punctured by a single lonely star, shone through the glass. He continued reading by that pale illumination until he could see no longer. Eventually, after his servant had repeatedly alerted him to the lateness of the hour, he rose, walked into the adjacent chamber, set the book upon the small Florentine stand that sat by his bed, and commenced dressing for his evening meal.

It was nearly nine o'clock when he arrived at the club, where he discovered Lord Henry waiting by himself in the morning-room, appearing quite weary. 

"I apologize sincerely, Harry," he exclaimed, "but it is truly your doing. That volume you gave me held me so spellbound that I lost track of the time."

"Indeed, I suspected you would enjoy it," his host answered, standing up from his seat."
}

The prose was crafted in that distinctive, ornate style—simultaneously vivid and cryptic—brimming with slang, archaic terms, specialized jargon, and intricate phrasing, which marks the output of the most skilled French Symbolist writers. The metaphors within were as grotesque as orchids and as nuanced in their hues. The experiences of the senses were depicted through the language of mystical philosophy. At times, it was difficult to discern if one was perusing the divine raptures of a medieval saint or the diseased admissions of a contemporary transgressor. It was a corrupting volume. A dense scent of incense appeared to linger upon the leaves, clouding the intellect. The very rhythm of the prose, with its delicate, hypnotic repetition and complex, recurring motifs, induced in the young man, as he progressed through the chapters, a state of trance—a sickness of fantasy—that rendered him oblivious to the fading daylight and the encroaching gloom.

A clear, copper-green sky, punctured by a single lonely star, shone through the glass. He continued reading by that pale illumination until he could see no longer. Eventually, after his servant had repeatedly alerted him to the lateness of the hour, he rose, walked into the adjacent chamber, set the book upon the small Florentine stand that sat by his bed, and commenced dressing for his evening meal.

It was nearly nine o'clock when he arrived at the club, where he discovered Lord Henry waiting by himself in the morning-room, appearing quite weary. 

"I apologize sincerely, Harry," he exclaimed, "but it is truly your doing. That volume you gave me held me so spellbound that I lost track of the time."

"Indeed, I suspected you would enjoy it," his host answered, standing up from his seat."
}

They all possessed a ghastly allure. He envisioned them during the night, and they haunted his thoughts throughout the day. The Renaissance era understood peculiar methods of assassination—lethal contact via a helmet or a flaming torch, an ornate glove or a decorated fan, a gilded scent-box or a necklace of amber. Dorian Gray had been corrupted by a volume. There were times when he viewed wickedness merely as a medium through which he might achieve his vision of beauty.

CHAPTER XII.

It occurred on the ninth of November, the night preceding his thirty-eighth birthday, as he frequently recalled in later years.

He was returning home around eleven o’clock after dining at Lord Henry’s, bundled in thick furs against the chilly, mist-filled night. Near the intersection of Grosvenor Square and South Audley Street, a figure hurried past him through the fog, wearing a grey overcoat with the collar raised. The man carried a satchel. Dorian identified him. It was Basil Hallward. An inexplicable feeling of dread washed over him. He offered no greeting and continued rapidly toward his residence.

However, Hallward had spotted him. Dorian heard him pause on the sidewalk before hastening to catch up. Moments later, a hand gripped his arm.

“Dorian! What a remarkable stroke of fortune! I have been lingering in your library since nine o’clock. Eventually, I took pity on your exhausted servant and instructed him to retire as he showed me out.”"
}

At the corner of Grosvenor Square and South Audley Street, a man passed him in the mist, walking very fast with the collar of his grey overcoat turned up. He carried a bag. Dorian recognized him. It was Basil Hallward. A strange, inexplicable sense of fear washed over him. He gave no sign of recognition and hurried on toward his house.

“Dorian! What an extraordinary piece of luck! I have been waiting for you in your library since nine o’clock. Finally, I took pity on your tired servant and told him to go to bed when he let me out.”"

“I am waiting, Basil,” the young man stated in a sharp, distinct tone. 

Basil turned. “This is what I must say,” he exclaimed. “You are required to provide a response to these dreadful accusations leveled against you. Should you assure me they are entirely false, I will accept your word. Refute them, Dorian, refute them! Do you not perceive my suffering? Good heavens! Do not tell me that you are wicked, depraved, and disgraceful.” 

Dorian Gray offered a smile, his lips curling with disdain. “Follow me upstairs, Basil,” he replied calmly. “I maintain a daily record of my existence, which never leaves the chamber where it is composed. I will permit you to see it if you accompany me.” 

“I will go with you, Dorian, if that is your wish. I realize I have missed my train, but that is of no consequence. I can depart tomorrow. However, do not expect me to read anything tonight. I merely desire a straightforward answer to my inquiry.” 

“That you shall receive upstairs. I could not provide it here. You will not be required to read for long.” 

CHAPTER XIII. 

He exited the room and commenced the climb, with Basil Hallward trailing immediately behind. They moved quietly, as individuals naturally do during the night. The lamp projected strange, shifting shadows upon the walls and the staircase. A strengthening breeze caused the windows to vibrate. 

Upon reaching the upper landing, Dorian placed the lamp upon the floor, withdrew the key, and rotated it within the lock. “You are determined to know, Basil?” he inquired in a hushed tone. 

“I am.” 

“I am pleased,” he replied with a smile. He then added, with a touch of harshness, “You are the sole individual on earth with the right to know everything regarding me. You have influenced my life more than you realize.” Taking up the lamp, he unlocked the door and entered. A chilly draft brushed past them, causing the flame to flare with a dull, orange hue. He shivered. “Close the door behind you,” he murmured, setting the lamp upon the table. 

Hallward surveyed the surroundings with a look of bewilderment. The chamber appeared as though it had been abandoned for years. A worn Flemish tapestry, a draped painting, an antique Italian chest, and a nearly vacant bookshelf—these were its only contents, alongside a table and chair. As Dorian Gray ignited a partially consumed candle resting on the mantel, he observed that the entire space was coated in dust and the rug was tattered. A mouse scurried behind the wall paneling. The air held a damp, musty scent. 

“So, you believe that only God observes the soul, Basil? Pull back that curtain, and you shall behold mine.” 

The voice that delivered these words was frigid and heartless. “You are either insane, Dorian, or performing a role,” Hallward muttered, his brow furrowed. 

“You refuse? Then I shall do it myself,” the young man declared, ripping the curtain from its rod and casting it to the floor."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_021",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "He straightened up, walked over to the fireplace, and stood there, watching the burning logs with their frost-like ashes and pulsing cores of flame.

“I’m waiting, Basil,” the young man said in a hard, clear voice.

He turned around. “This is what I have to say,” he shouted. “You must give me an answer to these horrible accusations made against you. If you tell me they are completely false from start to finish, I will believe you. Deny them, Dorian, deny them! Can’t you see what I’m going through? My God! Don’t tell me that you are wicked, corrupt, and shameful.”

Dorian Gray smiled. A curl of contempt touched his lips. “Come upstairs, Basil,” he said quietly. “I keep a daily diary of my life, and it never leaves the room where it’s written. I’ll show it to you if you come with me.”

“I’ll come with you, Dorian, if you wish. I see I’ve missed my train. That doesn’t matter. I can go tomorrow. But don’t ask me to read anything tonight. All I want is a straight answer to my question.”

“You’ll get that upstairs. I couldn’t give it here. You won’t have to read for long.”

CHAPTER XIII.

He left the room and began to climb the stairs, with Basil Hallward following close behind. They walked softly, as people instinctively do at night. The lamp cast strange shadows on the wall and staircase. A rising wind made some of the windows rattle.

When they reached the top landing, Dorian set the lamp on the floor, took out the key, and turned it in the lock. “You insist on knowing, Basil?” he asked in a low voice.

“Yes.”

“I’m delighted,” he answered, smiling. Then he added, somewhat harshly, “You are the one person in the world entitled to know everything about me. You’ve had more to do with my life than you realize.” Taking up the lamp, he opened the door and went inside. A cold draft brushed past them, and the light flared up for a moment in a murky orange flame. He shuddered. “Shut the door behind you,” he whispered as he placed the lamp on the table.

Hallward looked around with a puzzled expression. The room looked as if it hadn’t been lived in for years. A faded Flemish tapestry, a curtained picture, an old Italian chest, and an almost empty bookcase—that was all it seemed to contain, besides a chair and a table. As Dorian Gray lit a half-burned candle standing on the mantel, he saw that the whole place was covered in dust and the carpet was full of holes. A mouse scurried behind the wall paneling. There was a damp smell of mildew.

“So you think it’s only God who sees the soul, Basil? Pull that curtain back, and you will see mine.”

The voice that spoke was cold and cruel. “You’re insane, Dorian, or you’re acting,” Hallward muttered, frowning.

“You won’t? Then I must do it myself,” the young man said, and he tore the curtain from its rod and flung it to the ground."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_022",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The heavens were clear, and the atmosphere held a pleasant warmth. It felt nearly like a day in May.

Little by little, the occurrences of the previous night stole into his mind with quiet, blood-spattered steps, rebuilding themselves with frightening clarity. He recoiled at the recollection of his ordeal, and for an instant, that same odd sensation of hatred for Basil Hallward that had driven him to murder him while he occupied the chair returned, leaving him chilled with rage. The corpse remained seated there, too, now exposed to the sunlight. How ghastly that was! Such repulsive sights belonged to the shadows, not to the daylight.

He sensed that if he dwelled upon his experiences, he would fall ill or lose his sanity. There existed transgressions whose allure resided more in the recollection than in the act itself, peculiar victories that satisfied vanity more than desire, providing the mind with an intensified feeling of delight, superior to any pleasure they offered, or could ever offer, to the physical senses. Yet this was not among them. It was a matter to be banished from his thoughts, to be numbed with opiates, to be stifled before it could stifle him in return.

When the half-hour chimed, he brushed his hand over his brow, then rose quickly and attired himself with even greater precision than usual, focusing intently on selecting his necktie and scarf-pin and swapping his rings multiple times. He also lingered over breakfast, sampling the various courses, chatting with his valet regarding some fresh liveries he considered commissioning for the staff at Selby, and reviewing his mail. He smirked at some of the notes. Three of them proved tedious. One he perused repeatedly before shredding it with a faint expression of irritation. “That dreadful thing, a woman’s memory!” as Lord Henry had once remarked.

Once he had finished his black coffee, he wiped his mouth deliberately with a cloth, signaled for his servant to remain, and walking to the desk, sat down to compose two letters. He placed one in his pocket and handed the other to the valet.

“Deliver this to 152, Hertford Street, Francis, and if Mr. Campbell is away, obtain his whereabouts.”

Once he was solitary, he ignited a cigarette and began to doodle on a sheet of paper, sketching initially flowers and architectural details, then human countenances. Abruptly, he noticed that every face he rendered bore a strange resemblance to Basil Hallward. He scowled, stood up, walked to the bookshelf, and pulled out a book at random. He resolved to avoid contemplating the recent events until it became strictly unavoidable.

After reclining on the couch, he examined the book’s title page. It was Gautier’s “Émaux et Camées,” the Charpentier Japanese-paper printing, featuring the Jacquemart etching. The cover was crafted from citron-green leather, decorated with a gold trellis pattern and dotted pomegranates. Adrian Singleton had gifted it to him. As he flipped through the leaves, his gaze landed on the verse concerning the hand of Lacenaire, that frigid yellow hand “du supplice encore mal lavée,” with its soft red fuzz and its “doigts de faune.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_022",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "The sky was bright, and the air felt pleasantly warm. It felt almost like a morning in May.

Slowly, the events of the previous night crept into his mind with silent, blood-stained steps, reconstructing themselves with terrifying clarity. He winced at the memory of his suffering, and for a moment, the same strange feeling of loathing for Basil Hallward that had driven him to kill him in his chair returned, leaving him cold with rage. The dead man was still sitting there, too, now exposed in the sunlight. How horrific that was! Such hideous things belonged in the darkness, not the daylight.

He felt that if he dwelled on what he had endured, he would fall ill or lose his mind. There were sins whose fascination lay more in the memory than in the act itself—strange triumphs that gratified pride more than passion, offering the intellect a heightened sense of joy greater than any pleasure they provided to the senses. But this was not one of them. It was something to be banished from his mind, to be numbed with sedatives, to be strangled before it could strangle him.

When the half-hour struck, he wiped his forehead, stood up quickly, and dressed with even more than his usual care. He paid close attention to his choice of necktie and scarf-pin, changing his rings more than once. He also spent a long time over breakfast, sampling various dishes, discussing new liveries for his servants at Selby with his valet, and reviewing his mail. He smiled at some of the letters. Three bored him. One he read several times before tearing it up with a look of slight annoyance. “That awful thing, a woman’s memory!” as Lord Henry had once remarked.

After finishing his black coffee, he wiped his lips slowly with a napkin, signaled for his servant to wait, and went to the table to write two letters. He put one in his pocket and handed the other to the valet.

“Take this to 152, Hertford Street, Francis, and if Mr. Campbell is out of town, find out his address.”

Once alone, he lit a cigarette and began sketching on a piece of paper, drawing flowers and architectural details, then human faces. Suddenly, he noticed that every face he drew seemed to bear a bizarre resemblance to Basil Hallward. He frowned, stood up, walked to the bookcase, and pulled out a volume at random. He was determined not to think about what had happened until it became absolutely necessary.

Stretching out on the sofa, he looked at the title page of the book. It was Gautier’s “Émaux et Camées,” the Charpentier Japanese-paper edition with the Jacquemart etching. The binding was citron-green leather, featuring a design of gold trellis-work and dotted pomegranates. Adrian Singleton had given it to him. As he flipped through the pages, his eyes landed on the poem about the hand of Lacenaire, the cold yellow hand “still poorly washed from the execution,” with its downy red hairs and its “faun-like fingers.”"
}

After drinking his black coffee, he wiped his lips slowly with a napkin, signaled for his servant to wait, and sat at the table to write two letters. He put one in his pocket and handed the other to the valet.

“Take this to 152, Hertford Street, Francis, and if Mr. Campbell is out of town, find out his address.”

Once alone, he lit a cigarette and began sketching on a piece of paper, drawing flowers, architectural details, and then human faces. Suddenly, he noticed that every face he drew seemed to bear a strange resemblance to Basil Hallward. He frowned, stood up, walked to the bookcase, and pulled out a book at random. He was determined not to think about what had happened until it became absolutely necessary.

After stretching out on the sofa, he looked at the title page of the book. It was Gautier’s “Émaux et Camées,” the Japanese-paper edition from Charpentier, featuring a Jacquemart etching. The binding was citron-green leather, decorated with a gold trellis design and dotted pomegranates. Adrian Singleton had given it to him. As he turned the pages, his eyes landed on the poem about the hand of Lacenaire—the cold, yellow hand “still poorly washed from the execution,” with its downy red hairs and its “faun-like fingers.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_023",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "He shall not be noticed as absent for many months. Once his disappearance is noted, there must be no evidence of him remaining here. You, Alan, must transform him, along with all his possessions, into a pile of cinders that I can cast into the wind.”

“You have lost your senses, Dorian.”

“Ah! I was anticipating you addressing me as Dorian.”

“You are insane, I repeat—insane to think I would lift a hand to assist you, insane to offer this hideous admission. I will have no part in this affair, whatever it entails. Do you believe I intend to jeopardize my standing for your sake? What concern is it of mine what wicked deeds you are committing?”

“It was a case of suicide, Alan.”

“I am relieved to hear that. But who pushed him to it? You, I would imagine.”

“Do you persist in refusing to perform this for me?”

“Naturally I refuse. I will have absolutely no involvement in it. I am indifferent to whatever disgrace befalls you. You have earned it all. I would not regret seeing you shamed, publicly shamed. How dare you request that I, of all people, involve myself in this nightmare? I assumed you possessed a better understanding of human nature. Your associate Lord Henry Wotton cannot have instructed you much regarding psychology, regardless of what else he has taught you. Nothing will persuade me to move a muscle to aid you. You have approached the incorrect person. Seek out some of your other companions. Do not come to me.”

“Alan, it was homicide. I took his life. You cannot fathom the suffering he caused me. Whatever my existence has become, he had a greater hand in shaping or ruining it than poor Harry ever did. He may not have intended it, yet the outcome remained identical.”

“Murder! Good heavens, Dorian, is this what you have descended to? I shall not report you. It is not my concern. Furthermore, even without my intervention, you are bound to be apprehended. No one ever perpetrates a crime without committing some blunder. But I will have no part in this.”

“You must have a part in this. Wait, pause for a moment; hear me out. Just listen, Alan. All I request is that you conduct a specific scientific procedure. You frequent hospitals and morgues, and the gruesome tasks you perform there do not disturb you. If in some ghastly dissecting-room or foul laboratory you encountered this man resting on a leaden table with crimson channels carved out for blood to drain, you would merely view him as a superb specimen. You would not be troubled. You would not believe you were committing any transgression. Conversely, you would likely feel you were serving humanity, or expanding the total of human knowledge, or satisfying intellectual curiosity, or something similar. What I require you to do is simply what you have frequently done in the past. In truth, destroying a corpse must be far less repulsive than the work you are accustomed to. And, bear in mind, it is the sole piece of proof against me. If it is found, I am ruined; and it is certain to be found unless you assist me.”"
}

“You’re insane, I tell you—insane to imagine I would lift a finger to help you, insane to make this monstrous confession. I will have nothing to do with this, whatever it is. Do you think I’m going to risk my reputation for you? What do I care about the devil’s work you’re involved in?”

“It was suicide, Alan.”

“I’m glad of that. But who drove him to it? You, I imagine.”

“Do you still refuse to do this for me?”

“Of course I refuse. I will have absolutely nothing to do with it. I don’t care what shame comes to you. You deserve it all. I wouldn’t be sorry to see you disgraced, publicly disgraced. How dare you ask me, of all people, to get involved in this horror? I would have thought you understood people better. Your friend Lord Henry Wotton can’t have taught you much about psychology, whatever else he’s taught you. Nothing will convince me to lift a finger to help you. You’ve come to the wrong person. Go to some of your other friends. Don’t come to me.”

“Alan, it was murder. I killed him. You don’t know what he made me suffer. Whatever my life is, he had more to do with making or ruining it than poor Harry ever did. He may not have intended it, but the result was the same.”

“You must have something to do with it. Wait, wait a moment; listen to me. Just listen, Alan. All I’m asking is for you to perform a certain scientific experiment. You go to hospitals and morgues, and the horrors you see there don’t affect you. If in some hideous dissecting room or foul laboratory you found this man lying on a lead table with grooves carved out for the blood to drain, you would simply view him as an excellent subject. What I want you to do is merely what you’ve often done before. In fact, destroying a body must be far less horrible than the work you’re accustomed to. }

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_024",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“I have fulfilled your request,” he murmured. “And now, farewell. Let us never encounter one another again.”

“You have rescued me from destruction, Alan. I cannot disregard that,” Dorian stated plainly.

Once Campbell had departed, he ascended the stairs. A pungent odor of nitric acid permeated the chamber. However, the object that had occupied the chair at the table had vanished.

CHAPTER XV.

That evening, at half-past eight, impeccably attired and sporting a large Parma violet boutonniere, Dorian Gray was escorted into Lady Narborough’s parlor by deferential attendants. His brow pulsed with frantic nerves, and he experienced intense agitation, yet his demeanor as he inclined over his hostess’s hand remained as fluid and elegant as usual. Perhaps one never appears so comfortable as when one is performing a role. Certainly, no observer of Dorian Gray that evening could have imagined he had endured a tragedy as ghastly as any in our era. Those slender, sculpted fingers could never have gripped a blade for wickedness, nor those grinning lips have invoked God and virtue. He found himself marveling at his own composure, and for an instant, he felt acutely the dark thrill of a dual existence.

It was an intimate gathering, arranged somewhat hastily by Lady Narborough, a highly intelligent woman possessing what Lord Henry frequently characterized as the remnants of truly striking ugliness. She had been a superb wife to one of our most tiresome ambassadors, and having interred her spouse appropriately in a marble tomb of her own design, and wed her daughters to wealthy, somewhat aged men, she now dedicated her time to the delights of French literature, French cuisine, and French wit whenever available.

Dorian was among her particular favorites, and she frequently remarked that she was immensely grateful she had not encountered him in her youth. “I am certain, my dear, I would have become infatuated with you,” she would declare, “and cast all caution to the winds for your benefit. It is quite fortunate you were not yet a consideration at that time. As it was, our headwear was so unflattering, and the mills were so busy attempting to generate profit, that I never even managed a flirtation with anyone. Still, that was entirely Narborough’s doing. He was terribly nearsighted, and there is no satisfaction in deceiving a husband who notices nothing.”

Her attendees that night were somewhat dull. The reality was, as she confided to Dorian from behind a rather worn fan, one of her married daughters had arrived quite unexpectedly to visit, and, to compound the issue, had brought her husband along. “I find it quite inconsiderate of her, my dear,” she whispered. “Naturally, I visit and stay with them each summer following my return from Homburg, but an elderly woman like myself requires fresh air occasionally, and besides, I truly enliven them. You cannot imagine the life they lead in the country. It is entirely unadulterated rural existence. They rise early, as they have so much to accomplish, and retire early, as they have so little to contemplate.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_024",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“I have done what you asked,” he muttered. “And now, goodbye. Let us never see each other again.”

“You have saved me from ruin, Alan. I cannot forget that,” Dorian said simply.

As soon as Campbell had left, he went upstairs. There was a pungent smell of nitric acid in the room, but the thing that had been sitting at the table was gone.

CHAPTER XV.

That evening, at eight-thirty, dressed impeccably and wearing a large Parma violet boutonniere, Dorian Gray was ushered into Lady Narborough’s drawing-room by bowing servants. His forehead throbbed with frayed nerves, and he felt intensely excited, yet his manner as he bent over his hostess’s hand was as relaxed and graceful as ever. Perhaps one never seems so at ease as when one is playing a part. Certainly, no one looking at Dorian Gray that night could have believed he had endured a tragedy as horrific as any in our age. Those finely shaped fingers could never have gripped a knife for sin, nor those smiling lips have cried out against God and goodness. He could not help but marvel at his own calm demeanor, and for a moment, he felt keenly the dark thrill of a double life.

It was a small party, organized rather hastily by Lady Narborough, a very clever woman with what Lord Henry used to describe as the remains of truly remarkable ugliness. She had been an excellent wife to one of our most tedious ambassadors, and having buried her husband properly in a marble mausoleum she had designed herself, and married off her daughters to wealthy, rather elderly men, she now devoted herself to the pleasures of French novels, French cooking, and French wit whenever she could find it.

Dorian was one of her favorites, and she always told him she was extremely glad she had not met him in her youth. “I know, my dear, I would have fallen madly in love with you,” she used to say, “and thrown all caution to the wind for your sake. It is most fortunate you were not around at the time. As it was, our styles were so unflattering, and our lives so preoccupied with basic survival, that I never even had a flirtation with anyone. However, that was all Narborough’s fault. He was dreadfully short-sighted, and there is no pleasure in deceiving a husband who never notices anything.”

Her guests that evening were rather dull. The fact was, as she explained to Dorian behind a very shabby fan, one of her married daughters had arrived quite suddenly to stay with her, and, to make matters worse, had actually brought her husband along. “I think it is most unkind of her, my dear,” she whispered. “Of course, I go and stay with them every summer after I return from Homburg, but an old woman like me needs fresh air sometimes, and besides, I really liven them up. You have no idea what an existence they lead down there. It is pure, unadulterated country life. They get up early because they have so much to do, and go to bed early because they have so little to think about.”"
}

It was a small party, organized rather hastily by Lady Narborough, a very clever woman with what Lord Henry used to describe as the remains of truly remarkable ugliness. She had been an excellent wife to one of our most tedious ambassadors, and having buried her husband properly in a marble mausoleum she had designed herself, and married off her daughters to wealthy, rather elderly men, she now devoted herself to the pleasures of French novels, French cooking, and French wit whenever she could find it.

Dorian was one of her favorites, and she always told him she was extremely glad she had not met him in her youth. “I know, my dear, I would have fallen madly in love with you,” she used to say, “and thrown all caution to the wind for your sake. It is most fortunate you were not around at the time. As it was, our styles were so unflattering, and our lives so preoccupied with basic survival, that I never even had a flirtation with anyone. However, that was all Narborough’s fault. He was dreadfully short-sighted, and there is no pleasure in deceiving a husband who never notices anything.”

Her guests that evening were rather dull. The fact was, as she explained to Dorian behind a very shabby fan, one of her married daughters had arrived quite suddenly to stay with her, and, to make matters worse, had actually brought her husband along. “I think it is most unkind of her, my dear,” she whispered. “Of course, I go and stay with them every summer after I return from Homburg, but an old woman like me needs fresh air sometimes, and besides, I really liven them up. You have no idea what an existence they lead down there. It is pure, unadulterated country life. They get up early because they have so much to do, and go to bed early because they have so little to think about.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_025",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "He observed it as if it were an object capable of both enchanting and terrifying him, as if it contained something he desired yet nearly detested. His breathing accelerated. An irrational yearning seized him. He ignited a cigarette, only to discard it immediately. His eyelids lowered until his long, fringed lashes nearly brushed his cheeks, yet he continued to gaze at the cabinet. Finally, he rose from the couch where he had been reclining, approached the piece of furniture, unlocked it, and pressed a concealed spring. A triangular compartment slid out gradually. His hand moved by instinct, reaching inside to grasp an object. It was a diminutive Chinese box crafted from black and gold-dust lacquer, intricately detailed with patterns of undulating waves on its sides, featuring silken cords adorned with spherical crystals and tassels of woven metal. He lifted the lid. Within lay a green paste with a waxy sheen, possessing a scent that was remarkably heavy and enduring.

He paused for several moments, a peculiar, frozen smile fixed upon his features. Then, trembling despite the intense heat of the room, he stood tall and checked the time. It was twenty minutes before midnight. He returned the box to its place, closed the cabinet doors, and retreated to his bedroom.

As midnight struck with bronze tolls against the dark atmosphere, Dorian Gray, wearing plain attire and a muffler wound about his neck, slipped silently from his residence. On Bond Street, he located a hansom cab pulled by a sturdy horse. He signaled the driver and whispered a destination.

The driver shook his head. "That is too great a distance for me," he grumbled.

"I will give you a sovereign," Dorian offered. "You shall receive another if you travel with haste."

"Very well, sir," the man replied, "you will arrive within the hour." Once his passenger had boarded, he turned the horse and sped toward the river.

CHAPTER XVI.

A chilly rain commenced, and the indistinct streetlights appeared spectral within the sodden fog. The taverns were in the process of shutting, and shadowy figures of men and women gathered in fragmented clusters near the entrances. From certain establishments, the noise of grotesque laughter emerged. In others, intoxicated patrons shouted and fought.

Reclining within the hansom with his hat brim pulled low, Dorian Gray observed the wretched disgrace of the sprawling city with apathetic eyes, occasionally whispering to himself the phrase Lord Henry had spoken upon their initial meeting: "To heal the soul through the senses, and the senses through the soul." Indeed, that was the mystery. He had frequently attempted it, and he would attempt it once more. There existed opium dens where one might purchase forgetfulness, chambers of dread where the recollection of past transgressions could be obliterated by the frenzy of fresh ones.

The moon remained low, resembling a yellow skull. Periodically, a massive, distorted cloud extended a lengthy limb to obscure it. The gaslights became sparse, and the thoroughfares grew narrower and more ominous. At one point, the driver lost his direction and was forced to backtrack for half a mile. Steam billowed from the horse as it splashed through the puddles."
}

His fingers moved instinctively toward it, reached inside, and gripped something. It was a small Chinese box made of black and gold-dusted lacquer, intricately crafted, its sides decorated with curved waves, the silk cords adorned with round crystals and tassels of braided metal thread. He opened it. Inside lay a green paste with a waxy sheen and a scent that was strangely heavy and lingering.

He hesitated for a few moments, a strangely frozen smile on his face. Then, shivering despite the intense heat of the room, he stood up straight and glanced at the clock. It was twenty minutes to twelve. He returned the box, closed the cabinet doors, and went into his bedroom.

As midnight struck with bronze echoes against the dark air, Dorian Gray, dressed in plain clothes with a scarf wrapped around his neck, slipped quietly out of his house. On Bond Street, he found a hansom cab with a strong horse. He hailed it and gave the driver an address in a low voice.

The man shook his head. "That's too far for me," he muttered.

"Here is a sovereign for you," Dorian said. "You'll get another if you drive fast."

"Alright, sir," the man replied. "You'll be there in an hour." After his passenger climbed in, he turned the horse around and drove quickly toward the river.

CHAPTER XVI.

A cold rain began to fall, and the blurred streetlights looked ghastly in the dripping mist. The pubs were just closing, and shadowy men and women gathered in broken groups around their entrances. From some of the bars came the sound of hideous laughter. In others, drunks brawled and screamed.

Leaning back in the cab with his hat pulled low over his forehead, Dorian Gray watched the sordid shame of the great city with listless eyes. Now and then, he repeated to himself the words Lord Henry had spoken the day they first met: "To cure the soul by means of the senses, and the senses by means of the soul." Yes, that was the secret. He had often tried it, and he would try it again now. There were opium dens where one could buy oblivion, dens of horror where the memory of old sins could be erased by the madness of new ones.

The moon hung low in the sky like a yellow skull. From time to time, a huge, misshapen cloud stretched a long arm across the sky to hide it. The gas lamps became fewer, and the streets grew narrower and gloomier. Once, the driver lost his way and had to turn back for half a mile. Steam rose from the horse as it splashed through the puddles."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_026",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Why do you inquire? Of what consequence are years?”

“Eighteen years,” Dorian Gray chuckled, his tone tinged with victory. “Eighteen years! Bring me beneath the lantern and examine my features!”

James Vane paused briefly, confused by the request. Then he grabbed Dorian Gray and pulled him out from the shadows of the arch.

Though the flickering, wind-tossed light was faint, it was sufficient to reveal the dreadful mistake he had apparently made, for the countenance of the man he had intended to slay possessed all the freshness of youth and the untarnished innocence of a boy. He appeared to be barely twenty years old, scarcely older, if at all, than his sister had been when they parted so long ago. It was clear this was not the person who had ruined her.

He released his grip and stumbled backward. “My God! My God!” he exclaimed, “and I nearly murdered you!”

Dorian Gray inhaled deeply. “You were standing on the edge of a monstrous sin, my friend,” he remarked, gazing at him with severity. “Take this as a lesson not to pursue retribution yourself.”

“Pardon me, sir,” James Vane whispered. “I was misled. A stray comment I overheard in that wretched place led me astray.”

“You ought to return home and stow that firearm away, lest you find yourself in difficulty,” Dorian stated, pivoting to walk leisurely down the road.

James Vane remained on the sidewalk, horrified. He shook violently. Shortly thereafter, a dark figure that had been sliding along the wet wall emerged into the illumination and approached him with quiet strides. He felt a hand touch his sleeve and turned around, startled. It was one of the women who had been consuming liquor at the tavern.

“Why did you spare him?” she whispered sharply, bringing her gaunt face near his. “I realized you were trailing him when you bolted from Daly’s. You idiot! You should have finished him. He possesses great wealth, and he is wicked through and through.”

“He is not the individual I seek,” he replied, “and I desire no man’s gold. I seek a man’s existence. The person I hunt must be nearly forty by now. This fellow is little more than a youth. Thank heaven I do not have his blood on my conscience.”

The woman let out a harsh laugh. “Little more than a youth!” she mocked. “Why, man, it has been nearly eighteen years since Prince Charming turned me into what I am today.”

“You are lying!” James Vane shouted.

She lifted her hand toward the sky. “I swear before God I speak the truth,” she cried.

“Before God?”

“May I be struck silent if it is not so. He is the most depraved one who visits here. People claim he bartered his soul to the devil for a handsome face. It has been nearly eighteen years since I first encountered him. He has not altered much since then. I, however, certainly have,” she added, with a ghastly grin.

“You vow this is true?”

“I vow it,” she echoed in a raspy voice from her thin lips. “But do not betray me to him,” she pleaded; “I am terrified of him.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_026",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“Why are you asking me? What do the years matter?”

“Eighteen years,” Dorian Gray laughed, his voice tinged with triumph. “Eighteen years! Pull me under the light and look at my face!”

James Vane hesitated for a moment, confused by the request. Then, he grabbed Dorian Gray and pulled him out from the archway.

Though the wind-blown light was dim and flickering, it was enough to reveal the terrible mistake he had seemingly made. The face of the man he had intended to kill possessed all the freshness of boyhood and the untarnished purity of youth. He looked like a young man of twenty, barely older—if older at all—than his sister had been when they parted all those years ago. It was clear this was not the man who had ruined her life.

He let go and stumbled back. “My God! My God!” he cried. “And I was about to murder you!”

Dorian Gray took a deep breath. “You were on the verge of committing a horrific crime, my man,” he said, looking at him sternly. “Let this be a warning to you not to take vengeance into your own hands.”

“Forgive me, sir,” James Vane muttered. “I was misled. A random comment I overheard in that wretched place put me on the wrong path.”

“You should go home and put that pistol away, or you might get into trouble,” Dorian said, turning around and walking slowly down the street.

James Vane stood on the sidewalk in horror, trembling from head to foot. A moment later, a dark shadow that had been creeping along the wet wall emerged into the light, approaching him with stealthy steps. He felt a hand touch his arm and spun around, startled. It was one of the women who had been drinking at the bar.

“Why didn’t you kill him?” she hissed, bringing her haggard face close to his. “I knew you were following him when you rushed out of Daly’s. You fool! You should have killed him. He has plenty of money, and he is as wicked as they come.”

“He isn’t the man I’m looking for,” he replied, “and I don’t want anyone’s money. I want a man’s life. The man I’m after must be nearly forty now. This one is barely more than a boy. Thank God I don’t have his blood on my hands.”

The woman let out a bitter laugh. “Barely more than a boy!” she sneered. “Why, man, it’s been nearly eighteen years since Prince Charming made me what I am.”

“You’re lying!” James Vane cried.

She raised her hand toward the sky. “I’m telling you the truth before God,” she insisted.

“Before God?”

“May I be struck dumb if it isn’t true. He’s the worst one who comes here. They say he sold his soul to the devil for a pretty face. It’s been nearly eighteen years since I met him. He hasn’t changed much since then. I have, though,” she added with a sickly leer.

“You swear this?”

“I swear it,” she echoed in a hoarse voice from her thin lips. “But don’t tell him I said anything,” she whined. “I’m afraid of him.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_027",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The crisp, pine-fragrant atmosphere of that winter morning appeared to restore his vitality and zest for existence. Yet, it was not solely the external surroundings that prompted this shift. His internal disposition had rebelled against the overwhelming misery that threatened to damage his tranquil state. This is a common occurrence for those with delicate and complex sensibilities; their intense emotions must either break or yield, ultimately either destroying the individual or perishing themselves. While minor griefs and affections endure, those of great magnitude are consumed by their own intensity. Furthermore, he had persuaded himself that his previous state was merely the product of a fearful mind, and he now viewed those anxieties with a mixture of sympathy and disdain.

Following his morning meal, he spent an hour strolling in the garden with the duchess before riding across the park to meet the hunting group. A layer of frost coated the lawn like salt, and the sky resembled a bowl of blue steel. A delicate sheet of ice edged the marshy, reed-filled lake.

Near the edge of the pine forest, he spotted Sir Geoffrey Clouston, the duchess’s brother, ejecting two empty shells from his firearm. He alighted from the carriage, instructed the groom to return the horse to the stables, and walked toward his guest through the dry ferns and tangled brush.

“Has the hunting been successful, Geoffrey?” he inquired.

“Not particularly, Dorian. I suspect the majority of the game has moved to the open fields. I expect things will improve after we eat, once we reach a different area.”

Dorian walked beside him. The sharp, scented air, the shimmering bronze and crimson hues within the woods, the occasional rough shouts of the beaters, and the sudden reports of the rifles captivated him, imbuing him with a feeling of pleasant liberty. He was governed by a lighthearted sense of bliss and a profound detachment born of joy.

Without warning, a hare emerged from a clump of overgrown grass about twenty yards ahead, its black-tipped ears alert and its powerful hind legs propelling it forward. It dashed toward a grove of alders. Sir Geoffrey raised his weapon, but the creature’s elegant movement held a strange fascination for Dorian Gray, who immediately shouted, “Don’t fire, Geoffrey. Let it go.”

“Don’t be ridiculous, Dorian!” his friend chuckled, and as the hare leaped into the brush, he pulled the trigger. Two sounds followed: the harrowing shriek of a wounded hare, and the even more terrible cry of a man in pain.

“Good heavens! I’ve struck a beater!” Sir Geoffrey cried out. “What a fool the man was to step into the line of fire! Cease shooting!” he bellowed. “Someone is injured.”

The head-keeper hurried over, clutching a walking stick.

“Where, sir? Where is he?” he yelled. Simultaneously, the gunfire along the perimeter stopped.

“Over here,” Sir Geoffrey replied irritably, rushing toward the thicket. “Why on earth can’t you keep your people back? You’ve ruined my sport for the day.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_027",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "There was something in the crisp, pine-scented air of that winter morning that seemed to restore his joy and passion for life. Yet it was not just the physical environment that caused this shift. His own nature had rebelled against the intense anguish that had threatened to damage his inner calm. This is always the case with sensitive, complex temperaments. Their powerful passions must either bruise or break; they either destroy the person or die out themselves. Shallow sorrows and loves endure, but great ones are consumed by their own intensity. Furthermore, he had convinced himself that he had been a victim of a panicked imagination, and he now looked back on his fears with a sense of pity and a fair amount of contempt.

After breakfast, he walked with the duchess for an hour in the garden and then drove across the park to join the shooting party. The crisp frost lay like salt on the grass. The sky was like an inverted bowl of blue metal. A thin layer of ice edged the flat, reed-filled lake.

At the corner of the pine woods, he spotted Sir Geoffrey Clouston, the duchess’s brother, ejecting two spent cartridges from his gun. He jumped from the cart, told the groom to take the mare home, and made his way toward his guest through the withered ferns and rough undergrowth.

“Have you had good sport, Geoffrey?” he asked.

“Not very good, Dorian. I think most of the birds have moved to the open. I expect it will be better after lunch when we reach new ground.”

Dorian strolled along beside him. The sharp, aromatic air, the brown and red light glimmering in the woods, the hoarse shouts of the beaters ringing out occasionally, and the sharp cracks of the guns that followed fascinated him and filled him with a sense of delightful freedom. He was overcome by the carelessness of happiness and the high indifference of joy.

Suddenly, from a clump of old grass about twenty yards ahead, a hare bolted, its black-tipped ears erect and its long hind legs propelling it forward. It dashed toward a thicket of alders. Sir Geoffrey raised his gun, but there was something in the animal’s graceful movement that strangely charmed Dorian Gray, and he shouted, “Don’t shoot it, Geoffrey. Let it live.”

“What nonsense, Dorian!” his companion laughed, and as the hare bounded into the thicket, he fired. Two cries were heard: the cry of a hare in pain, which is dreadful, and the cry of a man in agony, which is worse.

“Good heavens! I’ve hit a beater!” Sir Geoffrey exclaimed. “What an idiot the man was to get in front of the guns! Stop shooting!” he shouted at the top of his voice. “A man is hurt.”

The head keeper came running up with a stick in his hand.

“Where, sir? Where is he?” he shouted. At the same time, the firing stopped along the line.

“Here,” Sir Geoffrey answered angrily, hurrying toward the thicket. “Why on earth don’t you keep your men back? You’ve ruined my shooting for the day.”"
}

{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_028",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Death moved through the sunlight. The forest floor was stained with patches of blood. 

He then composed a letter to Lord Henry, explaining that he needed to travel to the city to visit his physician and requesting that he host the visitors while he was away. While sealing the envelope, a knock sounded at the door, and his servant reported that the head gamekeeper requested an audience. He scowled and chewed his lip. "Show him in," he murmured, after a brief pause.

Once the man stepped inside, Dorian retrieved his checkbook from a desk and laid it open before him.

"I assume you are here regarding the tragic mishap from earlier today, Thornton?" he inquired, picking up a pen.

"Indeed, sir," the gamekeeper replied.

"Did the unfortunate man have a wife? Was anyone reliant on him for support?" Dorian asked, appearing indifferent. "If so, I would not wish for them to suffer, and I am prepared to provide whatever funds you deem appropriate."

"We are uncertain of his identity, sir. That is the reason I took the liberty of approaching you."

"You do not know who he is?" Dorian asked, with a lack of interest. "What are you implying? Was he not one of your staff?"

"No, sir. I have never seen him before. He appears to be a sailor, sir."

The pen slipped from Dorian Gray’s fingers, and he felt as though his pulse had abruptly ceased. "A sailor?" he shouted. "Did you say a sailor?"

"Yes, sir. He seems to be some kind of seaman; he has tattoos on both arms, and things of that nature."

"Was anything discovered on his person?" Dorian asked, leaning in and staring at the man with wide, alarmed eyes. "Anything that might reveal his identity?"

"A small amount of cash, sir—not much, and a revolver. There was no identification of any sort. He is a decent-looking man, sir, though rugged. We believe he is a sailor."

Dorian jumped to his feet. A desperate hope flickered within him. He grasped at it frantically. "Where is the corpse?" he demanded. "Hurry! I must view it immediately."

"It is in a vacant stable at the Home Farm, sir. The locals dislike keeping such things in their homes. They believe a dead body invites misfortune."

"The Home Farm! Go there immediately and wait for me. Tell one of the stable hands to bring my horse. No. Never mind. I will head to the stables myself. It will be faster."

Within fifteen minutes, Dorian Gray was racing down the lengthy driveway at full speed. The trees appeared to rush by like a ghostly parade, and dark shadows cast themselves across his route. At one point, the mare veered toward a white gatepost, nearly unseating him. He struck her neck with his riding crop. She cut through the dim air like a projectile. Gravel sprayed from her hooves.

Finally, he arrived at the Home Farm. A pair of men were idling in the courtyard. He dismounted and tossed the reins to one of them. A faint light glowed from the most distant stable. An instinct told him the body lay within, so he rushed to the entrance and gripped the latch.

He hesitated for a heartbeat, sensing he stood at the edge of a revelation that would either secure or destroy his existence."
}

Then he wrote a note to Lord Henry, explaining that he was heading into town to see his doctor and asking him to host the guests in his absence. As he sealed the envelope, there was a knock at the door, and his valet told him the head gamekeeper wanted to see him. He frowned and bit his lip. “Send him in,” he muttered after a brief hesitation.

As soon as the man entered, Dorian pulled his checkbook from a drawer and laid it out before him.

“I assume you’re here about this morning’s unfortunate accident, Thornton?” he said, picking up a pen.

“Yes, sir,” the gamekeeper replied.

“Was the poor man married? Did he have anyone dependent on him?” Dorian asked, looking bored. “If so, I wouldn’t want them left in need, and I’ll send whatever amount you think is necessary.”

“We don’t know who he is, sir. That’s why I took the liberty of coming to you.”

“You don’t know who he is?” Dorian said listlessly. “What do you mean? Wasn’t he one of your men?”

“No, sir. I’ve never seen him before. He looks like a sailor, sir.”

The pen dropped from Dorian Gray’s hand, and he felt as if his heart had suddenly stopped. “A sailor?” he cried. “Did you say a sailor?”

“Yes, sir. He looks like he was some kind of sailor; he has tattoos on both arms, that sort of thing.”

“Was anything found on him?” Dorian asked, leaning forward and staring at the man with startled eyes. “Anything that would identify him?”

“Some money, sir—not much, and a revolver. There was no name of any kind. He’s a decent-looking man, sir, but rough. We think he’s a sailor.”

Dorian jumped to his feet. A terrible hope fluttered past him, and he grabbed at it frantically. “Where is the body?” he exclaimed. “Quick! I must see it immediately.”

“It’s in an empty stable at the Home Farm, sir. The locals don’t like having that sort of thing in their homes. They say a corpse brings bad luck.”

“The Home Farm! Go there right now and meet me. Tell one of the grooms to bring my horse around. No, never mind. I’ll go to the stables myself. It will be faster.”

In less than fifteen minutes, Dorian Gray was galloping down the long avenue as fast as he could. The trees seemed to sweep past him like ghosts, and wild shadows stretched across his path. Once, his mare swerved at a white gatepost and nearly threw him. He lashed her across the neck with his crop, and she cut through the dim air like an arrow, stones flying from her hooves.

Finally, he reached the Home Farm. Two men were loitering in the yard. He leaped from the saddle and threw the reins to one of them. A light flickered in the farthest stable. Something told him the body was there, so he hurried to the door and placed his hand on the latch.

He paused for a moment, feeling that he stood on the brink of a discovery that would either make or ruin his life."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_029",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "It appeared to me rather theatrical. London abounds in such odd spectacles. A rainy Sunday, a crude believer in a raincoat, a circle of pale, sickly faces beneath a canopy of leaking umbrellas, and a striking remark tossed into the atmosphere by piercing, frantic voices—it was truly effective in its own fashion, a genuine inspiration. I considered informing the preacher that while art possesses a soul, humanity does not. I suspect, however, he would have failed to grasp my meaning.

“Do not, Harry. The soul is a frightening reality. It can be purchased, traded, and exchanged. It can be corrupted or perfected. Every one of us possesses a soul. I am certain of it.”

“Are you entirely convinced of that, Dorian?”

“Entirely convinced.”

“Ah! Then it must be a delusion. The matters one feels completely positive about are never factual. That is the curse of belief and the moral of romance. How solemn you are! Do not be so earnest. What concern have you or I with the myths of our time? No: we have abandoned our faith in the soul. Perform something for me. Play a nocturne, Dorian, and while you play, whisper to me how you have preserved your youth. You must possess some mystery. I am merely ten years your senior, yet I am lined, exhausted, and sallow. You are truly remarkable, Dorian. You have never appeared more delightful than you do this evening. You bring to mind the day we first met. You were somewhat impudent, quite timid, and entirely exceptional. You have altered, certainly, but not in your looks. I wish you would share your secret. To regain my youth, I would attempt anything, provided it did not involve physical exertion, rising early, or living respectably. Youth! There is nothing comparable. It is ridiculous to speak of the naivety of youth. The only individuals whose views I currently value are those much younger than myself. They appear ahead of me. Life has unveiled its newest marvels to them. As for the elderly, I habitually disagree with them. I do so as a matter of principle. If you inquire about their perspective on a recent event, they gravely offer the sentiments prevalent in 1820, when men wore high cravats, believed in everything, and understood absolutely nothing. How beautiful that piece you are performing is! I wonder, did Chopin compose it in Majorca, with the ocean sobbing around the villa and the salty mist striking the windows? It is wonderfully romantic. What a gift it is that one art remains to us that is not merely imitative! Do not cease. I desire music tonight. It strikes me that you are the youthful Apollo and I am Marsyas listening to you. I harbor my own griefs, Dorian, of which even you are unaware. The tragedy of aging is not that one becomes old, but that one remains young inside. I am occasionally astonished by my own candor. Ah, Dorian, how fortunate you are! What a magnificent life you have led! You have tasted everything fully. You have pressed the grapes against your tongue. Nothing has been concealed from you."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_029",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "It struck me as rather dramatic. London is full of curious effects like that. A rainy Sunday, a rough-looking religious man in a raincoat, a circle of pale, sickly faces under a canopy of dripping umbrellas, and a powerful phrase shouted into the air by shrill, hysterical lips—it was quite impressive in its own way, a real inspiration. I considered telling the preacher that art has a soul, but that man does not. I suspect, however, he wouldn't have understood me.

“Don’t, Harry. The soul is a terrifying reality. It can be bought, sold, and traded away. It can be poisoned or perfected. There is a soul in each of us. I know it.”

“Are you quite sure of that, Dorian?”

“Quite sure.”

“Ah! Then it must be an illusion. The things one feels absolutely certain about are never true. That is the tragedy of faith and the lesson of romance. How grave you are! Don’t be so serious. What do you or I have to do with the superstitions of our time? No, we have abandoned our belief in the soul. Play something for me. Play a nocturne, Dorian, and as you play, tell me in a low voice how you have kept your youth. You must have a secret. I am only ten years older than you, yet I am wrinkled, worn, and sallow. You are truly wonderful, Dorian. You have never looked more charming than you do tonight. You remind me of the day I first saw you. You were a bit cheeky, very shy, and absolutely extraordinary. You have changed, of course, but not in appearance. I wish you would tell me your secret. To regain my youth, I would do anything in the world, except exercise, wake up early, or be respectable. Youth! There is nothing like it. It is absurd to talk about the ignorance of youth. The only people whose opinions I respect now are those much younger than myself. They seem ahead of me. Life has revealed its latest wonders to them. As for the elderly, I always contradict them. I do it on principle. If you ask their opinion on something that happened yesterday, they solemnly offer you the views current in 1820, when people wore high collars, believed in everything, and knew absolutely nothing. How lovely that piece you are playing is! I wonder, did Chopin write it in Majorca, with the sea weeping around the villa and salt spray dashing against the windows? It is marvelously romantic. What a blessing that there is one art left to us that is not imitative! Don’t stop. I want music tonight. It seems to me that you are the young Apollo and I am Marsyas listening to you. I have sorrows of my own, Dorian, that even you know nothing about. The tragedy of old age is not that one is old, but that one is still young inside. I am amazed sometimes at my own sincerity. Ah, Dorian, how happy you are! What an exquisite life you have had! You have drunk deeply of everything. You have crushed the grapes against your palate. Nothing has been hidden from you."
}

How lovely that piece you are playing is! I wonder, did Chopin write it in Majorca, with the sea weeping around the villa and salt spray hitting the windows? It is wonderfully romantic. What a blessing that we still have one art form that is not merely imitative! Don’t stop. I want music tonight. It seems to me that you are the young Apollo and I am Marsyas listening to you. I have my own sorrows, Dorian, that even you know nothing about. The tragedy of old age is not that one is old, but that one is still young inside. I am sometimes amazed at my own sincerity. Ah, Dorian, how happy you are! What an exquisite life you have led! You have tasted everything deeply. You have crushed the grapes against your palate. Nothing has been hidden from you."
}

I considered telling the preacher that art has a soul, but that man does not. I suspect, however, he would not have understood me.

“Don’t, Harry. The soul is a terrifying reality. It can be bought, sold, and traded. It can be poisoned or perfected. There is a soul in each of us. I know it.”

“Are you quite sure of that, Dorian?”

“Quite sure.”

“Ah! Then it must be an illusion. The things one feels absolutely certain about are never true. That is the tragedy of faith and the lesson of romance. How grave you are! Don’t be so serious. What do you or I have to do with the superstitions of our time? No, we have abandoned our belief in the soul. Play something for me. Play a nocturne, Dorian, and as you play, tell me in a low voice how you have kept your youth. You must have a secret. I am only ten years older than you, yet I am wrinkled, worn, and yellow. You are truly wonderful, Dorian. You have never looked more charming than you do tonight. You remind me of the day I first saw you. You were a bit cheeky, very shy, and absolutely extraordinary. You have changed, of course, but not in your appearance. I wish you would tell me your secret. To regain my youth, I would do anything in the world, except exercise, wake up early, or be respectable. Youth! There is nothing like it. It is absurd to talk about the ignorance of youth. The only people whose opinions I respect now are those much younger than myself. They seem ahead of me. model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_030",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The agonizing decay of his own spirit was what disturbed him. Basil had created the painting that had ruined his existence, and he could not pardon him for it. The portrait was the source of all his troubles. Basil had uttered intolerable remarks to him, which he had nevertheless endured with restraint. The killing had been merely a fleeting fit of insanity. Regarding Alan Campbell, his self-destruction was his own decision; he had elected to take that path. It meant nothing to him.

A fresh start! That was his desire, the thing he awaited. Surely, he had already commenced it. He had protected one blameless creature, at the very least. He would never again lead innocence astray. He would lead a virtuous life.

Reflecting on Hetty Merton, he started to question if the painting in the secured chamber had altered. Surely, it could not remain as ghastly as it once was? Perhaps, if his existence turned righteous, he might purge every trace of wicked desire from the visage. Perhaps the marks of depravity had already vanished. He would go and inspect it.

He lifted the lamp from the stand and stole upstairs. As he unfastened the door, a gleam of happiness crossed his remarkably youthful countenance and rested briefly upon his mouth. Indeed, he would be virtuous, and the grotesque object he had concealed would no longer haunt him. He felt as though the burden had already been removed.

He entered silently, securing the door behind him as he usually did, and pulled the violet curtain away from the canvas. A shout of agony and resentment escaped him. He observed no transformation, except for a glint of craftiness in the eyes and the arched crease of a deceiver about the lips. The object remained repulsive—even more so, if that were possible—and the crimson moisture staining the hand appeared more vivid, resembling freshly shed blood. Then he shook. Was it merely conceit that had prompted his single virtuous act? Or was it the craving for a novel experience, as Lord Henry had suggested with his derisive chuckle? Or perhaps that urge to perform a role that occasionally drives us to actions nobler than our true selves? Or maybe a combination of these? And why had the crimson mark expanded? It seemed to have spread like a vile contagion across the shriveled digits. There was blood upon the painted feet, as if the figure had leaked—blood even upon the hand that had not wielded the blade. Confess? Did that imply he must admit his guilt? To surrender himself and face execution? He chuckled. He considered the notion absurd. Furthermore, even if he were to confess, who would credit him? No evidence of the victim remained. Everything associated with the man had been obliterated. He had personally incinerated the items from the lower floor. The public would simply deem him insane. They would confine him if he insisted on his tale.... Yet, he felt an obligation to confess, to endure public disgrace, and to provide public restitution. There existed a God who summoned men to reveal their transgressions to the world as well as to heaven."
}
