model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_DORIAN_GRAY_030",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "It was the living death of his own soul that haunted him. Basil had painted the portrait that had ruined his life, and he could not forgive him for it. The portrait was the root of everything. Basil had said things to him that were intolerable, yet he had endured them with patience. The murder had been nothing more than a momentary fit of madness. As for Alan Campbell, his suicide was his own choice; he had decided to do it, and it meant nothing to Dorian.

A new life! That was what he craved and what he awaited. Surely he had already begun it. He had spared one innocent person, at least. He would never again lead innocence into temptation. He would be good.

As he thought of Hetty Merton, he wondered if the portrait in the locked room had changed. Surely it was not still as horrific as it had been? Perhaps if his life became pure, he could erase every sign of evil passion from the face. Perhaps the marks of wickedness had already faded. He would go and see.

He took the lamp from the table and crept upstairs. As he unbolted the door, a joyful smile flickered across his strangely youthful face and lingered on his lips. Yes, he would be good, and the hideous thing he had hidden away would no longer terrify him. He felt as if a weight had already been lifted from his shoulders.

He entered quietly, locking the door behind him as usual, and pulled the purple curtain from the portrait. A cry of pain and outrage escaped him. He saw no change, except that the eyes held a look of cunning and the mouth displayed the curved wrinkle of a hypocrite. The image was still loathsome—perhaps even more so than before—and the scarlet dew spotting the hand seemed brighter, like freshly spilled blood. Then he trembled. Had it been mere vanity that drove his one good deed? Or the desire for a new sensation, as Lord Henry had suggested with his mocking laugh? Or perhaps the urge to play a role that sometimes leads us to act better than we truly are? Or maybe all of these? And why was the red stain larger than it had been? It seemed to have spread like a grotesque disease over the wrinkled fingers. There was blood on the painted feet, as if the figure had been dripping—blood even on the hand that had not held the knife. Confess? Did that mean he was supposed to confess? To turn himself in and face execution? He laughed. The idea felt monstrous. Besides, even if he did confess, who would believe him? There was no trace of the murdered man left. Everything belonging to him had been destroyed; he had burned everything that had been downstairs. The world would simply call him insane. They would lock him away if he persisted in his story. Yet, he felt a duty to confess, to endure public shame, and to make public atonement. There was a God who demanded that men confess their sins to the earth as well as to heaven."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_001",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Mr. Podgers cast a nervous look about the room and pulled the thick curtain over the entrance. 

‘This will require a moment, Lord Arthur; you ought to take a seat.’

‘Make haste, sir,’ Lord Arthur exclaimed once more, striking his foot impatiently against the gleaming floorboards.

Mr. Podgers gave a smile, pulled a tiny magnifying lens from his coat, and polished it meticulously with a cloth.

‘I am prepared,’ he stated.

CHAPTER II

Ten minutes afterward, his complexion drained of color and his gaze frantic with sorrow, Lord Arthur Savile bolted from Bentinck House, pushing past the cluster of liveried servants gathered beneath the expansive striped canopy, seemingly blind and deaf to his surroundings. The evening air was biting, and the streetlights circling the square danced and wavered in the sharp breeze; yet his palms felt feverish, and his brow scorched. He pressed forward, moving with the unsteady rhythm of a man intoxicated. A constable observed him with suspicion, and a vagrant, emerging from a passage to beg, recoiled in alarm at the sight of suffering more profound than his own. At one point, he halted beneath a streetlamp to examine his hands. He imagined he could perceive the mark of blood upon them, and a soft moan escaped his quivering mouth.

Murder! That was the vision the palmist had discovered there. Murder! The night itself appeared conscious of the fact, and the lonely wind seemed to shriek it into his hearing. The shadows of the lanes were saturated with it. It leered at him from the gables of the buildings.

Initially, he arrived at the Park, whose dark groves appeared to draw him in. He rested heavily against the iron fence, pressing his forehead against the damp metal to cool it, while he listened to the shivering quiet of the woods. ‘Murder! Murder!’ he murmured repeatedly, as if the repetition might soften the terror of the term. The sound of his own speech caused him to tremble, though he half-wished that an echo might catch his words and rouse the sleeping metropolis from its slumber. He felt a frantic urge to halt a stranger and confess the entire truth.

He then drifted past Oxford Street into dismal, disreputable lanes. A pair of women with made-up faces jeered at him as he passed. From a gloomy courtyard drifted the noise of curses and violence, followed by piercing cries, and, curled upon a sodden threshold, he observed the twisted figures of destitution and old age. A peculiar compassion stirred within him. Were these souls born of vice and woe fated to their conclusion, just as he was to his? Were they, like him, merely marionettes in a grotesque performance?

Yet it was not the enigma, but the farce of agony that impacted him; its total futility, its absurd lack of purpose. How disjointed everything appeared! How devoid of any unity! He was astonished by the clash between the superficial cheerfulness of the era and the actual realities of life. He remained quite youthful.

Eventually, he arrived before Marylebone Church. The quiet street resembled a lengthy ribbon of gleaming silver, marked here and there by the dark, intricate patterns of swaying shadows. The row of wavering gaslights curved far into the distance, and parked outside a small enclosed dwelling sat a lone hansom cab, its coachman slumbering within."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_001",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Mr. Podgers looked around nervously and pulled the heavy curtain across the door.

‘It will take a little time, Lord Arthur; you had better sit down.’

‘Be quick, sir,’ Lord Arthur shouted again, stamping his foot angrily on the polished floor.

Mr. Podgers smiled, took a small magnifying glass from his breast pocket, and wiped it carefully with his handkerchief.

‘I am quite ready,’ he said.

CHAPTER II

Ten minutes later, his face pale with terror and his eyes wild with grief, Lord Arthur Savile rushed out of Bentinck House, pushing through the crowd of fur-coated footmen standing around the large striped awning, seemingly unable to see or hear anything. The night was bitterly cold, and the gas lamps around the square flared and flickered in the sharp wind; yet his hands were hot with fever, and his forehead burned like fire. He kept moving, walking almost like a drunkard. A police officer watched him curiously as he passed, and a beggar, who slouched out from an archway to ask for money, became frightened, seeing misery greater than his own. Once, he stopped under a lamp and looked at his hands. He thought he could already detect the stain of blood on them, and a faint cry escaped his trembling lips.

Murder! That was what the palm reader had seen there. Murder! The very night seemed to know it, and the desolate wind seemed to howl it into his ear. The dark corners of the streets were full of it. It grinned at him from the rooftops. 

First, he reached the Park, whose somber woodland seemed to fascinate him. He leaned wearily against the railings, cooling his brow against the wet metal and listening to the tremulous silence of the trees. ‘Murder! Murder!’ he kept repeating, as if repetition could dull the horror of the word. The sound of his own voice made him shudder, yet he almost hoped that an echo might hear him and wake the slumbering city from its dreams. He felt a mad desire to stop a passerby and tell them everything.

Then he wandered across Oxford Street into narrow, shameful alleys. Two women with painted faces mocked him as he went by. From a dark courtyard came the sound of swearing and blows, followed by shrill screams, and, huddled on a damp doorstep, he saw the hunched forms of poverty and old age. A strange pity washed over him. Were these children of sin and misery predestined to their end, just as he was to his? Were they, like him, merely puppets in a monstrous show?

And yet it was not the mystery, but the comedy of suffering that struck him; its absolute uselessness, its grotesque lack of meaning. How incoherent everything seemed! How lacking in all harmony! He was amazed at the discord between the shallow optimism of the day and the real facts of existence. He was still very young.

After a while, he found himself in front of Marylebone Church. The silent road looked like a long ribbon of polished silver, flecked here and there by the dark patterns of waving shadows. Far into the distance curved the line of flickering gas lamps, and outside a small walled-in house stood a solitary hansom cab, the driver asleep inside."
}

Murder! That was what the palm reader had seen there. Murder! The very night seemed to know it, and the desolate wind seemed to howl it into his ear. The dark corners of the streets were full of it. It grinned at him from the rooftops. 

First, he reached the Park, whose somber woodland seemed to fascinate him. He leaned wearily against the railings, cooling his brow against the wet metal and listening to the tremulous silence of the trees. ‘Murder! Murder!’ he kept repeating, as if repetition could dull the horror of the word. The sound of his own voice made him shudder, yet he almost hoped that an echo might hear him and wake the slumbering city from its dreams. He felt a mad desire to stop a passerby and tell them everything.

Then he wandered across Oxford Street into narrow, shameful alleys. Two women with painted faces mocked him as he went by. From a dark courtyard came the sound of swearing and blows, followed by shrill screams, and, huddled on a damp doorstep, he saw the hunched forms of poverty and old age. A strange pity washed over him. Were these children of sin and misery predestined to their end, just as he was to his? Were they, like him, merely puppets in a monstrous show?

And yet it was not the mystery, but the comedy of suffering that struck him; its absolute uselessness, its grotesque lack of meaning. How incoherent everything seemed! How lacking in all harmony! He was amazed at the discord between the shallow optimism of the day and the real facts of existence. He was still very young.

After a while, he found himself in front of Marylebone Church. The silent road looked like a long ribbon of polished silver, flecked here and there by the dark patterns of waving shadows. Far into the distance curved the line of flickering gas lamps, and outside a small walled-in house stood a solitary hansom cab, the driver asleep inside."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_002",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Dressed in a flowing gown of crêpe-de-chine and holding a broad, leaf-shaped fan, she resembled those fragile statuettes discovered in the olive groves surrounding Tanagra; her posture possessed a distinctly Hellenic elegance. She was not small, however, but rather possessed of ideal proportions—a rarity in an era when many women are either excessively large or remarkably slight.

As Lord Arthur observed her, he was overcome by the profound compassion that stems from affection. He believed that wedding her while the sentence of homicide loomed over him would constitute a betrayal akin to that of Judas, a transgression more grievous than any imagined by the Borgias. What joy could they possibly share if, at any instant, he might be summoned to fulfill the grim prediction etched upon his palm? What existence could they lead while Destiny kept this dreadful outcome in the balance? The nuptials had to be delayed at any price. He was firmly decided on this point. Despite his intense love for the girl—and the simple contact of her hand, when they sat together, caused his every nerve to vibrate with intense delight—he recognized his obligation clearly and understood that he possessed no right to wed until he had performed the killing. Once accomplished, he could approach the altar with Sybil Merton and entrust his life to her without fear of moral failure. Once finished, he could embrace her, certain that she would never need to blush for his sake or lower her head in disgrace. Yet it had to be completed first, and the sooner, the better for both.

Numerous men in his circumstances might have chosen the easy path of indulgence over the arduous climb of duty, but Lord Arthur was too scrupulous to prioritize pleasure over integrity. His devotion contained more than mere desire; to him, Sybil represented everything virtuous and honorable. Though he felt a brief, instinctive aversion to his required task, it quickly vanished. His heart assured him it was a sacrifice rather than a sin, while his intellect confirmed that no alternative existed. He faced a choice between self-interest and the welfare of others, and although the burden placed upon him was undeniably grim, he knew he must not allow selfishness to prevail over love. Sooner or later, we all face this same decision; the same inquiry is put to us all. It arrived for Lord Arthur early in his life—before his character was tainted by the calculating cynicism of later years or his spirit eroded by the superficial vanity of the age—and he felt no reluctance in fulfilling his duty. Fortunately, he was no mere visionary or aimless amateur. Had he been, he might have wavered like Hamlet, allowing indecision to ruin his resolve. Instead, he was fundamentally pragmatic. To him, existence signified action rather than contemplation. He possessed that most uncommon quality: common sense.

The turbulent, chaotic emotions of the previous evening had by now entirely dissipated, and he reflected upon his frantic roaming through the streets and his intense emotional suffering with a feeling bordering on embarrassment."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_003",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "He had long held a deep affection for Lady Clem, as she was universally known, and since he possessed a vast fortune inherited from Lord Rugby upon reaching his majority, there was no chance of him gaining any crude financial benefit from her passing. Indeed, the more he pondered the situation, the more she appeared to be the ideal candidate, and believing that any hesitation would be unjust to Sybil, he resolved to finalize his plans immediately.

The initial step was to compensate the palmist; he sat at a Sheraton desk by the window, wrote a cheque for 105 pounds made out to Mr. Septimus Podgers, placed it in an envelope, and instructed his valet to deliver it to West Moon Street. He then requested his hansom via telephone and prepared to depart. Before exiting, he glanced at Sybil Merton’s portrait, vowing that regardless of the outcome, he would never reveal his actions for her benefit, choosing instead to guard the secret of his sacrifice within his own heart.

En route to the Buckingham, he paused at a flower shop to send Sybil a stunning basket of narcissus, featuring delicate white petals and striking pheasant-eye centers. Upon reaching the club, he proceeded directly to the library, summoned the waiter, and requested a lemon-and-soda along with a volume on toxicology. He had firmly concluded that poison was the most appropriate method for this difficult task. Physical violence was deeply repulsive to him, and furthermore, he was keen to avoid killing Lady Clementina in a manner that might draw public scrutiny, as he loathed the prospect of being a celebrity at Lady Windermere’s or seeing his name splashed across the columns of common society journals. He also considered Sybil’s parents, who were somewhat traditional and might oppose the union if a scandal arose, though he felt certain that if he explained the full circumstances, they would be the first to understand the motives driving him. Thus, he had every reason to favor poison. It was secure, reliable, and discreet, eliminating the need for distressing confrontations, to which he, like most Englishmen, held a profound aversion.

He possessed no knowledge of the science of poisons, however, and since the waiter could locate nothing in the library beyond Ruff’s Guide and Bailey’s Magazine, he searched the shelves himself. Eventually, he discovered a finely bound edition of the Pharmacopoeia and a copy of Erskine’s Toxicology, edited by Sir Mathew Reid, the President of the Royal College of Physicians and one of the Buckingham’s oldest members, who had been admitted by mistake—a blunder that so infuriated the Committee that they blackballed the actual man when he applied. Lord Arthur found the technical language in both texts quite confusing and began to wish he had studied his classics more diligently at Oxford, until he discovered a fascinating and thorough description of the properties of aconitine in the second volume of Erskine, composed in relatively accessible English."
}

He sat down at a small Sheraton writing desk near the window, wrote a check for £105 made out to Mr. Septimus Podgers, and, placing it in an envelope, instructed his valet to deliver it to West Moon Street. He then called the stables for his hansom cab and got dressed to go out. As he left the room, he glanced back at Sybil Merton’s photograph and swore that, no matter what happened, he would never let her know what he was doing for her sake, but would keep the secret of his self-sacrifice hidden in his heart forever.

On his way to the Buckingham Club, he stopped at a florist and sent Sybil a beautiful basket of narcissus, with lovely white petals and striking centers, and upon arriving at the club, he went straight to the library, rang the bell, and ordered the waiter to bring him a lemon-and-soda and a book on toxicology. He had fully decided that poison was the best method to adopt for this troublesome business. Anything involving physical violence was extremely distasteful to him, and besides, he was very anxious not to murder Lady Clementina in a way that might attract public attention, as he hated the idea of being a celebrity at Lady Windermere’s parties or seeing his name featured in the columns of trashy society newspapers. He also had to consider Sybil’s parents, who were rather old-fashioned and might object to the marriage if there were any scandal, though he felt certain that if he explained the full facts of the case, they would be the first to appreciate the motives that had driven him. He had every reason, then, to choose poison. It was safe, sure, and quiet, and it eliminated the need for painful scenes, to which, like most Englishmen, he had a deep-seated aversion.

He knew absolutely nothing about the science of poisons, however, and since the waiter seemed unable to find anything in the library other than a racing guide and a magazine, he searched the bookshelves himself. He finally came across a handsomely bound edition of the Pharmacopoeia and a copy of Erskine’s Toxicology, edited by Sir Mathew Reid, the President of the Royal College of Physicians and one of the oldest members of the Buckingham, having been elected by mistake for someone else—a blunder that so enraged the committee that when the real man applied, they blackballed him unanimously. Lord Arthur was quite puzzled by the technical terms used in both books and began to regret that he had not paid more attention to his classics at Oxford, when, in the second volume of Erskine, he found a very interesting and complete account of the properties of aconitine, written in fairly clear English."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_004",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "All would eventually resolve, provided he exercised patience.

This occurred within the conservatory at Mr. Merton’s Park Lane residence, where Lord Arthur had been a dinner guest as per his custom. Sybil appeared more joyful than ever, and for an instant, Lord Arthur felt the urge to act like a coward, request the poison from Lady Clementina, and proceed with the wedding as if Mr. Podgers did not exist. His moral sense soon prevailed, and even as Sybil sobbed in his embrace, he remained resolute. The loveliness that captivated his heart also moved his sense of duty. He believed that ruining such a beautiful existence for the sake of a few months of happiness would be immoral.

He remained with Sybil until nearly midnight, offering and receiving comfort, and departed for Venice the following morning after sending a firm, honorable letter to Mr. Merton regarding the required delay of their nuptials.

CHAPTER IV

In Venice, he encountered his brother, Lord Surbiton, who had arrived from Corfu aboard his yacht. The pair enjoyed a pleasant two weeks. They spent mornings riding on the Lido or drifting through the emerald canals in their elongated black gondola; afternoons were typically spent hosting guests on the vessel; and evenings involved dining at Florian’s and smoking endless cigarettes in the Piazza. Nevertheless, Lord Arthur felt uneasy. He checked the death notices in the Times daily, hoping to find news of Lady Clementina’s passing, yet he was met with disappointment each time. He grew concerned that some mishap had befallen her, frequently wishing he had not stopped her from consuming the aconitine when she had been so eager to test it. Sybil’s correspondence, while brimming with affection, faith, and warmth, often carried a melancholy tone, leading him to occasionally fear they were separated permanently.

After two weeks, Lord Surbiton grew weary of Venice and decided to sail down the coast to Ravenna, having heard of the superb woodcock hunting in the Pinetum. Lord Arthur initially declined, but Surbiton, whom he held in high regard, eventually convinced him that remaining alone at the Danieli would be dreadfully dull. On the morning of the 15th, they departed amidst a stiff northeasterly breeze and somewhat turbulent waters. The hunting proved superb, and the unconstrained, outdoor lifestyle restored the color to Lord Arthur’s face, but by the 22nd, his worry regarding Lady Clementina returned, and he ignored Surbiton’s protests to travel back to Venice by rail.

As he exited his gondola onto the hotel landing, the owner approached him holding a bundle of telegrams. Lord Arthur grabbed them and ripped them open. Everything had gone as planned. Lady Clementina had passed away quite unexpectedly on the night of the 17th!

His initial concern was for Sybil, so he dispatched a telegram informing her of his imminent return to London. He then instructed his servant to prepare his luggage for the night train, tipped his gondoliers five times the standard rate, and hurried to his suite with a nimble step and a joyful spirit."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_004",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Everything would work out, but it required patience.

The scene unfolded in the conservatory at Mr. Merton’s Park Lane residence, where Lord Arthur had dined as he often did. Sybil had never appeared happier, and for a fleeting moment, Lord Arthur felt tempted to act like a coward, to write to Lady Clementina for the pill, and to proceed with the wedding as if Mr. Podgers did not exist. His better judgment soon took hold, however, and even when Sybil threw herself into his arms in tears, he did not waver. The beauty that captivated his senses had also stirred his conscience. He felt that ruining such a beautiful life for the sake of a few months of happiness would be wrong.

He remained with Sybil until nearly midnight, offering comfort and receiving it in return, and early the next morning he departed for Venice, having written a firm, honorable letter to Mr. Merton regarding the necessary delay of the wedding.

CHAPTER IV

In Venice, he encountered his brother, Lord Surbiton, who happened to be visiting from Corfu on his yacht. The two young men enjoyed a wonderful two weeks together. In the mornings, they rode on the Lido or glided along the green canals in their long black gondola; in the afternoons, they typically hosted guests on the yacht; and in the evenings, they dined at Florian’s and smoked countless cigarettes in the Piazza. Yet, somehow, Lord Arthur remained unhappy. Each day, he checked the obituary section of the Times, hoping to see a notice of Lady Clementina’s death, but each day he was disappointed. He began to fear that something had happened to her, and he often regretted that he had stopped her from taking the aconitine when she had been so eager to test its effects. Sybil’s letters, too, while filled with love, trust, and tenderness, were often quite sad in tone, and at times he worried that he was separated from her forever.

After two weeks, Lord Surbiton grew bored with Venice and decided to sail down the coast to Ravenna, having heard there was excellent woodcock hunting in the Pinetum. Lord Arthur initially refused to go, but Surbiton, whom he was very fond of, eventually convinced him that staying at the Danieli alone would be miserable. On the morning of the 15th, they departed with a strong northeast wind blowing and somewhat choppy seas. The hunting was superb, and the free, outdoor lifestyle brought the color back to Lord Arthur’s cheeks, but around the 22nd, he grew anxious about Lady Clementina and, despite Surbiton’s protests, returned to Venice by train.

As he stepped from his gondola onto the hotel steps, the proprietor approached him with a stack of telegrams. Lord Arthur grabbed them and tore them open. Everything had been a success. Lady Clementina had passed away quite suddenly on the night of the 17th!

His first thought was of Sybil, and he sent her a telegram announcing his immediate return to London. He then instructed his valet to pack his belongings for the night train, tipped his gondoliers five times their usual fare, and hurried to his suite with a light step and a buoyant heart."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_005",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Abruptly, he recalled his acquaintance Rouvaloff, a youthful Russian with radical leanings, whom he had encountered at Lady Windermere’s during the winter. Count Rouvaloff was ostensibly composing a biography of Peter the Great, having traveled to England to examine records concerning that Tsar’s time spent working as a shipwright; however, it was widely believed he functioned as a Nihilist operative, and the Russian Embassy clearly viewed his London residency with disapproval. Lord Arthur concluded he was the ideal individual for his needs, so he traveled one morning to his Bloomsbury quarters to request his counsel and aid.

‘So you are engaging in politics with sincerity?’ Count Rouvaloff inquired once Lord Arthur had explained his errand; yet Lord Arthur, who detested any form of pretension, felt compelled to confess that he possessed no interest in social issues, desiring the explosive device solely for a private family affair involving only himself.

Count Rouvaloff stared at him in astonishment for a time, then, realizing he was entirely earnest, scribbled an address on a slip of paper, marked it with his initials, and passed it over the table.

‘Scotland Yard would pay handsomely to possess this location, my dear friend.’

‘They shall not have it,’ Lord Arthur exclaimed, laughing; and after shaking the young Russian’s hand cordially, he hurried down the stairs, inspected the note, and instructed his driver to proceed to Soho Square.

There he let the carriage go and wandered down Greek Street until reaching a location known as Bayle’s Court. He walked beneath the archway, entering a strange dead-end street that seemed to house a French laundry, as a complex web of laundry lines spanned the buildings, with white linens fluttering in the morning breeze. He proceeded to the far end and rapped on a small green dwelling. Following a pause, during which every window in the court filled with curious, blurred faces, the door was opened by a coarse-featured foreigner who inquired in broken English regarding his purpose. Lord Arthur presented the paper provided by Count Rouvaloff. Upon seeing it, the man bowed and ushered Lord Arthur into a dilapidated front parlor on the ground floor. Shortly thereafter, Herr Winckelkopf, as he was known in England, bustled into the room wearing a wine-spotted napkin around his neck and holding a fork in his left hand.

‘Count Rouvaloff provided me with an introduction to you,’ Lord Arthur stated, bowing, ‘and I wish to have a brief consultation regarding a business matter. My name is Smith, Mr. Robert Smith, and I require you to provide me with an explosive clock.’

‘Delighted to make your acquaintance, Lord Arthur,’ the cheerful little German replied, laughing. ‘Do not appear so startled; it is my responsibility to know everyone, and I recall seeing you one evening at Lady Windermere’s. I trust her ladyship is in good health. Would you mind joining me while I conclude my breakfast? There is a superb pâté, and my associates are kind enough to claim that my Rhine wine surpasses anything served at the German Embassy.’ Before Lord Arthur had recovered from the shock of being identified, he found himself seated in the rear room, drinking exquisite Marcobrünner from a pale yellow hock-glass bearing the Imperial monogram, and conversing in the most amiable fashion with the renowned conspirator."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_006",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "It was indeed accurate that the device, upon detonating, merely obliterated a housemaid, as the Governor had departed the city a month and a half prior; nevertheless, it demonstrated that dynamite, utilized through mechanical means, served as a potent, if somewhat tardy, instrument of destruction. Lord Arthur found a measure of comfort in this thought, yet he was soon to be disappointed, for two days later, while he ascended the stairs, the Duchess summoned him to her private sitting room to display a missive she had just obtained from the Deanery.

“Jane composes delightful correspondence,” the Duchess remarked; “you truly must peruse her latest. It rivals the fiction Mudie provides us.”

Lord Arthur snatched the note from her grasp. It read thus:

THE DEANERY, CHICHESTER, 27th May.

My Dearest Aunt,

Many thanks for the flannel for the Dorcas Society, as well as the gingham. I concur entirely that it is absurd for them to desire attractive garments, but everyone is so radical and godless these days that it is hard to convince them they ought not attempt to dress like the aristocracy. I am certain I do not know what our future holds. As father has frequently stated in his homilies, we inhabit an era of skepticism.

We have been quite amused by a timepiece an anonymous benefactor forwarded to father last Thursday. It reached us in a crate from London, shipping prepaid, and father believes it was dispatched by someone who had perused his notable sermon, ‘Is Licence Liberty?’, for atop the clock sat a statue of a woman wearing what father identified as the cap of Liberty. I did not consider it particularly flattering myself, but father insisted it was historical, so I presume it is acceptable. Parker unboxed it, and father placed it upon the library mantel; we were all gathered there Friday morning when, exactly as the clock chimed twelve, we heard a buzzing sound, a small cloud of smoke emerged from the statue’s base, and the goddess of Liberty tumbled off, shattering her nose against the fireplace fender! Maria was quite frightened, but it appeared so absurd that James and I burst into laughter, and even father was entertained. Upon inspection, we discovered it was a type of alarm clock, and that if one adjusted it to a specific hour and placed gunpowder and a percussion cap beneath a small hammer, it would detonate whenever desired. Father declared it could not stay in the library due to the racket, so Reggie transported it to the schoolroom, where he does nothing but trigger minor explosions all day. Do you believe Arthur would appreciate one as a wedding gift? I assume they are quite the trend in London. Father claims they should accomplish much good, as they illustrate that Liberty cannot endure, but must inevitably collapse. Father says Liberty was conceived during the French Revolution. How dreadful it seems!

I must now depart for the Dorcas, where I shall read them your most enlightening letter. How accurate, dear aunt, is your notion that in their station of life they ought to wear what is unflattering."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_006",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "It was true that when it finally detonated, it only succeeded in blowing a housemaid to pieces, as the Governor had left town six weeks earlier. Still, it proved that dynamite, as a destructive force, was a powerful, if somewhat unreliable, agent when managed by machinery. Lord Arthur felt slightly comforted by this thought, but he was destined for further disappointment. Two days later, while he was heading upstairs, the Duchess summoned him into her boudoir and showed him a letter she had just received from the Deanery.

“Jane writes charming letters,” the Duchess remarked. “You really must read her latest. It is every bit as good as the novels Mudie sends us.”

Lord Arthur took the letter from her hand. It read as follows:

THE DEANERY, CHICHESTER, May 27th.

My Dearest Aunt,

Thank you so much for the flannel for the Dorcas Society, and for the gingham as well. I completely agree with you that it is ridiculous for them to want to wear pretty things, but everyone is so radical and irreligious these days that it is hard to make them understand they shouldn’t try to dress like the upper classes. I am sure I don’t know what we are coming to. As Papa has often said in his sermons, we live in an age of unbelief.

We have had great fun with a clock that an unknown admirer sent Papa last Thursday. It arrived in a wooden box from London, carriage paid, and Papa feels it must have been sent by someone who had read his remarkable sermon, ‘Is Licence Liberty?’ because on top of the clock was a figure of a woman wearing what Papa identified as the cap of Liberty. I didn’t think it was very flattering myself, but Papa said it was historical, so I suppose it is fine. Parker unpacked it, and Papa placed it on the library mantelpiece. We were all sitting there on Friday morning when, just as the clock struck twelve, we heard a whirring noise. A small puff of smoke emerged from the figure’s pedestal, and the goddess of Liberty fell off, breaking her nose on the fender! Maria was quite alarmed, but it looked so ridiculous that James and I burst into fits of laughter, and even Papa was amused. Upon inspection, we found it was a type of alarm clock; if you set it to a specific hour and placed some gunpowder and a cap under a small hammer, it would go off whenever you wanted. Papa said it could not stay in the library because of the noise, so Reggie took it to the schoolroom, where he does nothing but trigger small explosions all day long. Do you think Arthur would like one as a wedding present? I assume they are quite fashionable in London. Papa says they should do a great deal of good, as they demonstrate that Liberty cannot last, but must eventually fall. Papa says Liberty was invented during the French Revolution. How awful that seems!

I must go to the Dorcas meeting now, where I will read them your most instructive letter. How true your idea is, dear Aunt, that people in their station of life should wear what is unbecoming."
}

Thank you so much for the flannel for the Dorcas Society, and for the gingham as well. I completely agree with you that it is ridiculous for them to want to wear pretty things. Everyone is so radical and irreligious these days that it is hard to make them understand they should not try to dress like the upper classes. I am sure I do not know what the world is coming to. As Papa has often said in his sermons, we live in an age of unbelief.

We have had great fun with a clock that an unknown admirer sent Papa last Thursday. It arrived in a wooden box from London, with shipping paid, and Papa feels it must have been sent by someone who read his remarkable sermon, ‘Is Licence Liberty?’ On top of the clock was a figure of a woman wearing what Papa called the cap of Liberty. I did not think it looked very flattering myself, but Papa said it was historical, so I suppose it is fine. Parker unpacked it, and Papa placed it on the library mantelpiece. We were all sitting there on Friday morning when, just as the clock struck twelve, we heard a whirring noise. A little puff of smoke came from the base of the figure, and the goddess of Liberty fell off and broke her nose on the fireplace fender! Maria was quite frightened, but it looked so ridiculous that James and I burst into fits of laughter, and even Papa was amused. When we examined it, we found it was a type of alarm clock. If you set it to a specific hour and put some gunpowder and a percussion cap under a small hammer, it goes off whenever you want. Papa said it could not stay in the library because it made too much noise, so Reggie took it to the schoolroom, where he does nothing but set off small explosions all day long. Do you think Arthur would like one as a wedding present? I assume they are quite fashionable in London. Papa says they should do a great deal of good, as they show that Liberty cannot last and must eventually fall. Papa says Liberty was invented at the time of the French Revolution. How awful that seems!

I must go to the Dorcas meeting now, where I will read them your most instructive letter. How true your idea is, dear Aunt, that people in their social rank should wear clothing that is not flattering."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_007",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "P.S.—Do inform me regarding the bows. Jennings maintains they are currently in vogue.

Lord Arthur appeared so grave and miserable regarding the correspondence that the Duchess erupted into peals of laughter.

“My dear Arthur,” she exclaimed, “I shall never permit you to see a young lady’s note again! However, what shall I remark about the clock? I consider it an excellent device, and I would enjoy possessing one myself.”

“I do not think highly of them,” Lord Arthur replied with a melancholy grin, and, having kissed his mother, he exited the chamber.

Upon reaching the upper floor, he collapsed onto a couch, his eyes brimming with tears. He had exerted his utmost effort to execute this homicide, yet on both attempts he had miscarried, through no error of his own. He had endeavored to fulfill his obligation, but it appeared as though Fate had betrayed him. He felt burdened by the realization of how fruitless good intentions are, and the pointlessness of striving for excellence. Perhaps it would be wiser to terminate the engagement entirely. Sybil would experience pain, certainly, yet such suffering could never truly damage a spirit as virtuous as hers. As for his own fate, what did it signify? There is perpetually some conflict where a man might perish, some objective to which a man might dedicate his existence, and since life offered him no joy, death held no dread. Let Fate determine his end. He would not move to assist her.

At seven-thirty he attired himself and proceeded to the club. Surbiton was present with a group of young men, and he was compelled to eat with them. Their superficial chatter and aimless jokes failed to engage him, and as soon as coffee was served he departed, fabricating a prior commitment to facilitate his exit. As he was leaving the club, the porter delivered a letter to him. It was from Herr Winckelkopf, requesting that he visit the following evening to examine an explosive umbrella that detonated the moment it was unfurled. It was the most recent innovation, having just arrived from Geneva. He shredded the letter into pieces. He had resolved to cease all further trials. He then drifted toward the Thames Embankment and remained by the river for hours. The moon gazed through a thicket of amber clouds, resembling a lion’s eye, and countless stars decorated the vast expanse, like gold dust scattered across a violet ceiling. Occasionally a barge drifted into the murky current and floated away with the tide, while the railway signals shifted from green to crimson as the trains rushed screaming across the bridge. Eventually, twelve o’clock tolled from the high tower at Westminster, and with every strike of the resonant bell the night seemed to quiver. Then the railway lamps extinguished, leaving one solitary light glowing like a massive ruby on a colossal mast, and the city’s clamor grew more distant.

At two o’clock he rose and wandered toward Blackfriars. How illusory everything appeared! How much like a bizarre vision! The structures on the opposite bank seemed constructed from gloom. One might have claimed that silver and shadow had recreated the world."
}

‘I don’t think much of them,’ Lord Arthur said with a sad smile, and after kissing his mother, he left the room.

Once upstairs, he threw himself onto a sofa, his eyes filling with tears. He had done his best to commit this murder, but both times he had failed, and through no fault of his own. He had tried to do his duty, but it felt as if Destiny herself had betrayed him. He was weighed down by the pointlessness of good intentions and the futility of trying to be noble. At seven-thirty he dressed and went down to the club. Surbiton was there with a group of young men, and he was forced to dine with them. Their trivial conversation and idle jokes did not interest him, and as soon as coffee was served, he left them, making up an excuse to get away. As he was leaving the club, the hall-porter handed him a letter. It was from Herr Winckelkopf, asking him to stop by the next evening to look at an explosive umbrella that went off as soon as it was opened. It was the latest invention, having just arrived from Geneva. He tore the letter into pieces. He had decided not to attempt any more experiments. Then he wandered down to the Thames Embankment and sat by the river for hours. The moon peered through a mane of tawny clouds like a lion’s eye, and countless stars spangled the hollow vault like gold dust scattered on a purple dome. Now and then a barge swung out into the murky stream and floated away with the tide, and the railway signals changed from green to red as the trains ran shrieking across the bridge. After some time, twelve o’clock boomed from the tall tower at Westminster, and at each stroke of the deep bell, the night seemed to tremble. Then the railway lights went out, one solitary lamp left gleaming like a large ruby on a giant mast, and the roar of the city grew fainter.

At two o’clock he got up and strolled toward Blackfriars. How unreal everything looked! How like a strange dream! The houses on the other side of the river seemed built out of darkness. One would have said that silver and shadow had fashioned the world anew."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_008",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Furthermore, they act quite poorly if you treat them with genuine kindness. Do you recall that awful Mr. Podgers? He was a terrible fraud. Naturally, that did not bother me, and I even pardoned him when he attempted to solicit a loan, but I could not tolerate his romantic advances. He has truly caused me to loathe palmistry. I have taken up telepathy instead; it is far more entertaining.'

'You must not speak ill of palmistry in this company, Lady Windermere; it is the sole topic Arthur dislikes having mocked. I promise you he is entirely earnest regarding it.'

'You cannot possibly mean that he puts faith in it, Sybil?'

'Inquire of him yourself, Lady Windermere, for here he comes'; and Lord Arthur approached through the garden carrying a generous bouquet of yellow roses, with his two children skipping about him.

'Lord Arthur?'

'Yes, Lady Windermere.'

'You surely do not mean to suggest that you believe in palmistry?'

'I certainly do,' the young man replied with a smile.

'But for what reason?'

'Because I attribute every happiness in my existence to it,' he whispered, settling into a wicker seat.

'My dear Lord Arthur, what exactly do you owe to it?'

'Sybil,' he replied, presenting the flowers to his wife while gazing into her violet eyes.

'What utter rubbish!' Lady Windermere exclaimed. 'I have never heard such nonsense in my entire life.'

THE CANTERVILLE GHOST
A HYLO-IDEALISTIC ROMANCE

CHAPTER I

WHEN Mr. Hiram B. Otis, the American Minister, acquired Canterville Chase, everyone warned him he was acting quite foolishly, as there was no question that the estate was haunted. In fact, Lord Canterville himself, a man of the most scrupulous integrity, felt obligated to inform Mr. Otis of this circumstance while they negotiated the agreement.

'We have not cared to reside there ourselves,' Lord Canterville remarked, 'ever since my grandaunt, the Dowager Duchess of Bolton, was frightened into a seizure—from which she never truly regained her health—by a pair of skeletal hands resting upon her shoulders while she prepared for dinner. I feel compelled to inform you, Mr. Otis, that the apparition has been witnessed by various living relatives of mine, as well as by the parish rector, the Reverend Augustus Dampier, who is a Fellow of King’s College, Cambridge. Following the Duchess's unfortunate incident, none of our younger domestic staff would remain with us, and Lady Canterville frequently suffered from a lack of sleep due to the inexplicable sounds emanating from the hallway and the library.'

'My Lord,' the Minister replied, 'I shall accept the furnishings and the phantom at an appraised price. I hail from a modern nation where we possess everything wealth can procure; and with our energetic young men painting the Old World red and whisking away your finest actresses and prima donnas, I suspect that if a ghost truly existed in Europe, we would have it back home quite soon, either in one of our public museums or touring as a spectacle.'

'I am afraid the ghost is real,' Lord Canterville said with a smile, 'even if it has resisted the proposals of your ambitious promoters. It has been documented for three hundred years, since 1584 in fact, and invariably manifests prior to the passing of any member of our family.'"
}

I’m into telepathy now. It is much more entertaining.’

‘You mustn’t speak against palmistry here, Lady Windermere; it is the only topic Arthur dislikes people joking about. I assure you he is quite serious regarding it.’

‘You don’t mean to say he believes in it, Sybil?’

‘Ask him, Lady Windermere, here he comes’; and Lord Arthur approached through the garden with a large bouquet of yellow roses in his hand, and his two children dancing around him.

‘Lord Arthur?’

‘Yes, Lady Windermere.’

‘You don’t mean to say that you believe in palmistry?’

‘Of course I do,’ the young man said, smiling.

‘But why?’

‘Because I owe all the happiness of my life to it,’ he murmured, sinking into a wicker chair.

‘My dear Lord Arthur, what do you owe to it?’

‘Sybil,’ he replied, handing his wife the roses and gazing into her violet eyes.

‘What nonsense!’ Lady Windermere exclaimed. ‘I have never heard such nonsense in my entire life.’

THE CANTERVILLE GHOST
A HYLO-IDEALISTIC ROMANCE

CHAPTER I

WHEN Mr. Hiram B. Otis, the American Minister, purchased Canterville Chase, everyone told him he was making a very foolish mistake, as there was no doubt at all that the property was haunted. In fact, Lord Canterville himself, a man of the most scrupulous honor, had felt it his duty to mention this to Mr. Otis when they met to discuss the terms.

‘We haven’t cared to live there ourselves,’ Lord Canterville said, ‘since my grandaunt, the Dowager Duchess of Bolton, was frightened into a fit—from which she never truly recovered—by two skeleton hands being placed on her shoulders while she was dressing for dinner. I feel obligated to tell you, Mr. Otis, that the ghost has been seen by several living members of my family, as well as by the parish rector, the Rev. Augustus Dampier, who is a Fellow of King’s College, Cambridge. After the Duchess’s unfortunate accident, none of our younger servants would stay with us, and Lady Canterville often got very little sleep at night due to the mysterious noises coming from the hallway and the library.’

‘My Lord,’ the Minister replied, ‘I will take the furniture and the ghost at a valuation. I come from a modern country, where we have everything money can buy; and with all our energetic young men painting the Old World red and carrying off your best actresses and opera stars, I reckon that if such a thing as a ghost existed in Europe, we would have it at home in a very short time in one of our public museums, or on tour as a sideshow.’

‘I fear the ghost exists,’ Lord Canterville said, smiling, ‘though it may have resisted the offers of your enterprising promoters. It has been well known for three centuries, since 1584 in fact, and always appears before the death of any member of our family.’"
}

I feel obligated to tell you, Mr. Otis, that the ghost has been seen by several living members of my family, as well as by the parish rector, the Rev. Augustus Dampier, who is a Fellow of King’s College, Cambridge. After the Duchess’s unfortunate accident, none of our younger servants would stay with us, and Lady Canterville often got very little sleep at night due to the mysterious noises coming from the hallway and the library.’

‘My Lord,’ the Minister replied, ‘I will take the furniture and the ghost at a valuation. I come from a modern country, where we have everything money can buy; and with all our energetic young men painting the Old World red and carrying off your best actresses and opera stars, I reckon that if such a thing as a ghost existed in Europe, we would have it at home in a very short time in one of our public museums, or on tour as a sideshow.’

‘I fear the ghost exists,’ Lord Canterville said, smiling, ‘though it may have resisted the offers of your enterprising promoters. It has been well known for three centuries, since 1584 in fact, and always appears before the death of any member of our family.’"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_009",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Within moments, every sign of the blood-mark had vanished.

‘I was certain Pinkerton would manage it,’ he declared with pride, glancing at his impressed relatives; yet the moment he spoke, a violent bolt of lightning illuminated the gloomy chamber, a deafening crack of thunder forced them all to rise, and Mrs. Umney collapsed in a swoon.

‘What an outrageous climate!’ the American Minister remarked coolly, while igniting a lengthy cigar. ‘I suspect this old nation is so crowded that there is not enough pleasant weather to go around. I have long maintained that emigration is the only solution for England.’

‘My dear Hiram,’ Mrs. Otis exclaimed, ‘how should we handle a woman who faints?’

‘Bill her for it, just like broken items,’ the Minister replied; ‘that will stop her fainting’; and shortly thereafter, Mrs. Umney did indeed recover. It was clear, however, that she was deeply shaken, and she solemnly cautioned Mr. Otis to prepare for impending misfortune at the estate.

‘I have witnessed occurrences myself, sir,’ she stated, ‘that would cause any believer’s hair to rise, and I have spent countless nights without a wink of sleep due to the dreadful deeds committed here.’ Mr. Otis and his wife, however, kindly reassured the sincere woman that they harbored no fear of spirits, and, after she called down divine blessings upon her new employers and secured a raise in pay, the elderly housekeeper shuffled away to her quarters.


CHAPTER II


THE tempest blew with great intensity throughout the night, yet nothing of significance transpired. The following morning, however, upon descending for breakfast, they discovered the ghastly blood-stain had returned to the floor. ‘I cannot believe the Paragon Detergent is to blame,’ Washington remarked, ‘as I have tested it on everything. It must be the specter.’ He proceeded to scrub away the mark a second time, but it reappeared the next morning. It was present on the third morning as well, despite the fact that Mr. Otis had personally locked the library the night before and kept the key in his room. The entire family was now thoroughly intrigued; Mr. Otis began to wonder if he had been overly stubborn in rejecting the reality of ghosts, Mrs. Otis declared her plan to enroll in the Psychical Society, and Washington drafted a lengthy missive to Messrs. Myers and Podmore regarding the endurance of bloody marks linked to criminal acts. That evening, all skepticism regarding the physical reality of apparitions was permanently dispelled.

The day had been bright and warm; during the cool evening hours, the family departed for a drive. They did not arrive back until nine o’clock, at which point they enjoyed a modest supper. The talk never drifted toward spirits, meaning there were none of those initial states of expectant receptivity that frequently herald the arrival of supernatural events. The topics covered, as I later gathered from Mr. Otis, were simply those typical of refined American discourse, such as the vast superiority of Fanny Davenport’s acting compared to Sarah Bernhardt’s; the challenge of finding green corn, buckwheat cakes, and hominy, even in elite English residences; the significance of Boston in the evolution of the world-soul; the benefits of the baggage check method for train travel; and the pleasant quality of the New York accent versus the London drawl."
}

“What a ridiculous climate!” the American Minister remarked calmly, lighting a long cigar. “I suppose this old country is so crowded that they don’t have enough decent weather to go around. I’ve always believed that emigration is the only solution for England.”

“My dear Hiram,” Mrs. Otis cried, “what are we supposed to do with a woman who faints?”

“Charge it to her account, just like broken items,” the Minister replied; “she won’t faint after that.” Sure enough, Mrs. Umney regained consciousness shortly after. There was no question, however, that she was deeply shaken, and she sternly warned Mr. Otis to prepare for trouble coming to the house.

“I have seen things with my own eyes, sir,” she said, “that would make any decent person’s hair stand on end, and many nights I haven’t been able to sleep because of the horrific things that happen here.” Mr. Otis and his wife, however, warmly assured the honest woman that they were not afraid of ghosts. After invoking God’s blessings upon her new employers and securing a raise in her salary, the elderly housekeeper tottered off to her room.

CHAPTER II

THE storm raged violently throughout the night, but nothing of note occurred. The next morning, however, when they came down for breakfast, they discovered the gruesome bloodstain back on the floor. “I don’t think it’s the fault of the Paragon Detergent,” Washington said, “because I’ve tried it on everything. It must be the ghost.” He scrubbed the stain away a second time, but it reappeared the following morning. It was there on the third morning as well, even though Mr. Otis had locked the library himself the night before and kept the key upstairs. The entire family was now genuinely intrigued; Mr. Otis began to suspect he had been too stubborn in denying the existence of ghosts, Mrs. Otis announced her intention to join the Psychical Society, and Washington drafted a long letter to Messrs. Myers and Podmore regarding the persistence of bloodstains linked to crimes. That night, all skepticism about the reality of ghosts was removed forever.

The day had been warm and sunny, and in the cool of the evening, the family went for a drive. They did not return until nine o’clock for a light supper. The conversation never touched upon ghosts, so there were none of the usual conditions of anticipation that often precede supernatural events. The topics discussed, as I have since learned from Mr. Otis, were simply the standard subjects of cultured Americans: the immense superiority of Fanny Davenport over Sarah Bernhardt as an actress; the difficulty of finding sweet corn, buckwheat pancakes, and hominy in even the finest English homes; the importance of Boston to the development of the world-soul; the benefits of the baggage check system for rail travel; and the pleasantness of the New York accent compared to the London drawl."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_011",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "At last, he arrived at the turn in the hallway leading toward unfortunate Washington’s chamber. He halted briefly, the breeze tossing his lengthy grey hair and shaping the ghastly shroud of the deceased into bizarre, twisted forms. When the clock chimed the quarter-hour, he sensed the moment had arrived. He let out a soft laugh and rounded the bend, but immediately recoiled with a mournful cry of alarm, burying his pale countenance within his skeletal fingers. Directly before him stood a terrifying apparition, still as a statue and as grotesque as a lunatic’s vision. Its scalp was smooth and polished; its visage was circular, plump, and pallid, with a permanent, twisted sneer etched by some ghastly mirth. Crimson beams radiated from its eyes, its maw resembled a glowing pit, and a repulsive robe, mirroring his own, draped its massive frame in silent white. A sign rested upon its chest, covered in mysterious, archaic script—perhaps a list of disgraces, a chronicle of reckless transgressions, or a grim register of misdeeds—while its right hand brandished a shining steel blade.

Having never encountered a spirit previously, he was understandably paralyzed with fear. After one brief, panicked look at the dreadful phantom, he retreated toward his quarters, stumbling over his trailing burial garment as he hurried along the passage and inadvertently dropping his tarnished dagger into the Minister’s riding boots, where the butler discovered it the next day. Safely inside his own room, he collapsed onto a small cot and pulled the covers over his head. Eventually, though, the courageous spirit of Canterville regained its composure, and he resolved to address the other specter once daylight arrived. Thus, as the morning light began to silver the hills, he headed back to the location where he had first spotted the terrifying figure, reasoning that two ghosts were superior to one and that, with his new companion’s assistance, he could successfully confront the twins. Upon arriving, however, a shocking scene greeted him. Clearly, something had befallen the phantom; the glow had vanished from its empty eye sockets, the bright blade had slipped from its grasp, and it slumped against the wall in an awkward, rigid pose. He dashed forward and grabbed it, only to be horrified when the head detached and tumbled to the ground, the torso collapsed, and he realized he was holding nothing but a white cotton bed-curtain, with a broom, a meat cleaver, and a hollowed-out turnip scattered at his feet. Baffled by this strange metamorphosis, he snatched the sign with trembling hands and, in the dim morning glow, deciphered these ominous lines:

YE OLDE GHOSTE

Ye Onlie True and Originale Spook.
Beware of Ye Imitationes.
All others are Counterfeite.

The realization hit him instantly. He had been deceived, defeated, and outplayed. The traditional Canterville expression returned to his gaze; he gnashed his toothless jaws, and lifting his shriveled arms toward the ceiling, he vowed, in the colorful language of a bygone era, that once the rooster had crowed twice, violent acts would be committed, and Murder would roam with quiet steps."
}

He dashed forward and grabbed it, only to be horrified when the head detached and tumbled to the ground, the torso collapsed, and he realized he was holding nothing but a white cotton bed-curtain, with a broom, a meat cleaver, and a hollowed-out turnip scattered at his feet. Baffled by this strange metamorphosis, he snatched the sign with trembling hands and, in the dim morning glow, deciphered these ominous lines:

YE OLDE GHOSTE

Ye Onlie True and Originale Spook.
Beware of Ye Imitationes.
All others are Counterfeite.

The realization hit him instantly. He had been deceived, defeated, and outplayed. The traditional Canterville expression returned to his gaze; he gnashed his toothless jaws, and lifting his shriveled arms toward the ceiling, he vowed, in the colorful language of a bygone era, that once the rooster had crowed twice, violent acts would be committed, and Murder would roam with quiet steps."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_013",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The phantom felt a natural desire to demonstrate that he still held sway over the Stiltons, to whom he was remotely related; his own first cousin had wed the Sieur de Bulkeley, the direct ancestor of the Dukes of Cheshire. Consequently, he prepared to manifest before Virginia’s young suitor as ‘The Vampire Monk, or, the Bloodless Benedictine,’ a spectacle so terrifying that it caused Lady Startup, upon witnessing it on a fateful New Year’s Eve in 1764, to suffer piercing screams followed by a fatal stroke. She passed away three days later, having disinherited her Canterville kin to bequeath her fortune to a London chemist. Ultimately, however, his fear of the twins kept him confined to his quarters, allowing the young Duke to slumber undisturbed beneath the Royal Bedchamber’s canopy while dreaming of Virginia.

CHAPTER V

Some days later, Virginia and her curly-haired companion were riding through the Brockley meadows when she snagged her riding habit on a hedge. Upon returning, she chose the rear staircase to avoid notice. Passing the open door of the Tapestry Chamber, she glimpsed a figure inside and, assuming it was her mother’s maid, stepped in to request a repair. To her astonishment, she found the Canterville Ghost himself. He sat by the window, observing the withered, golden foliage swirling through the air and the crimson leaves drifting down the avenue. With his head resting on his hand, he appeared profoundly dejected. His forlorn and neglected state moved Virginia, who had initially intended to flee, to offer him comfort instead. Her step was so quiet and his sorrow so profound that he remained unaware of her until she addressed him.

‘I feel for you,’ she remarked, ‘but my brothers return to Eton tomorrow, and if you conduct yourself properly, no one will bother you.’

‘It is ridiculous to ask me to behave,’ he replied, turning in surprise toward the young girl who dared speak to him. ‘Utterly ridiculous. I am required to clank my chains, moan through keyholes, and roam at night. That is my sole purpose for existing.’

‘That is no reason to exist, and you know you have been very wicked. Mrs. Umney informed us upon our arrival that you murdered your wife.’

‘I acknowledge that,’ the Ghost replied peevishly, ‘but it was strictly a private family affair that concerned no one else.’

‘It is very wrong to kill anyone,’ Virginia stated, displaying a touch of the solemn Puritan morality she had inherited from a New England forebear.

‘Oh, I despise the shallow harshness of abstract ethics!’"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_013",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "The ghost was naturally eager to prove he still held sway over the Stiltons, to whom he was distantly related; his own first cousin had married the Sieur de Bulkeley, from whom, as everyone knows, the Dukes of Cheshire are directly descended. Consequently, he planned to appear to Virginia’s young suitor in his famous role as ‘The Vampire Monk, or, the Bloodless Benedictine.’ This performance was so terrifying that when old Lady Startup witnessed it on a fateful New Year’s Eve in 1764, she let out piercing shrieks that led to a fatal stroke, dying three days later after disinheriting the Cantervilles, her closest relatives, and leaving her entire fortune to her London pharmacist. At the last second, however, his fear of the twins kept him in his room, and the young Duke slept peacefully under the large feathered canopy in the Royal Bedchamber, dreaming of Virginia.

CHAPTER V

A few days later, Virginia and her curly-haired suitor went riding in the Brockley meadows, where she tore her riding habit so badly while crossing a hedge that, upon returning home, she decided to use the back staircase to avoid being seen. As she hurried past the Tapestry Chamber, the door of which was open, she thought she saw someone inside. Assuming it was her mother’s maid, who occasionally brought her sewing there, she looked in to ask for help mending her habit. To her immense surprise, it was the Canterville Ghost himself. He sat by the window, watching the golden, yellowing leaves blow through the air and dance wildly down the long driveway. His head rested on his hand, and his posture radiated deep depression. He looked so forlorn and neglected that Virginia, whose first instinct had been to run and lock herself in her room, felt a wave of pity and decided to try to comfort him. Her footsteps were so light and his melancholy so profound that he did not notice her until she spoke.

‘I feel so sorry for you,’ she said, ‘but my brothers are returning to Eton tomorrow, and then, if you behave yourself, no one will bother you.’

‘It is ridiculous to ask me to behave,’ he replied, looking around in astonishment at the pretty girl who had dared to address him. ‘Quite ridiculous. I must rattle my chains, groan through keyholes, and walk the halls at night, if that is what you mean. It is my only reason for existing.’

‘That is no reason to exist at all, and you know you have been very wicked. Mrs. Umney told us the day we arrived that you killed your wife.’

‘Well, I admit it,’ the ghost said petulantly, ‘but it was a private family matter that concerned no one else.’

‘It is very wrong to kill anyone,’ Virginia said, displaying a sweet, Puritan seriousness inherited from an old New England ancestor.

‘Oh, I hate the cheap severity of abstract ethics!’"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_014",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Initially, you seized all my reds, including the vermilion, preventing me from painting sunsets. Then you took the chrome-yellow and emerald-green, leaving me only with Chinese white and indigo. Consequently, I was restricted to moonlight scenes, which are difficult to execute and inherently gloomy. Despite my significant irritation, I never reported you, even though the situation was absurd; after all, who has ever heard of blood that is emerald-green?

'Well, truly,' the Ghost replied somewhat submissively, 'what was I to do? Obtaining genuine blood is quite challenging these days, and since your brother initiated this with his Paragon Detergent, I saw no reason to avoid using your paints. Color is a matter of personal preference; the Cantervilles possess blue blood, the finest in England, though I realize you Americans lack interest in such matters.'

'You are ignorant of the subject, and your best course is to emigrate and broaden your perspective. My father would gladly provide you with a free passage. Although there is a significant tax on spirits, the Custom House will pose no issue, as the officials are Democrats. In New York, you would certainly be a sensation. I am acquainted with many people there who would pay a hundred thousand dollars for a grandfather, and far more for a family Ghost.'

'I do not believe I would enjoy America.'

'I assume that is because we lack curiosities and ruins,' Virginia remarked sarcastically.

'No ruins! No curiosities!' the Ghost retorted; 'you possess your manners and your navy.'

'Good evening; I am going to ask my father to grant the twins an additional week of holiday.'

'Please do not leave, Miss Virginia,' he pleaded; 'I am so miserable and lonely, and I truly do not know what to do. I wish to sleep, but I cannot.'

'That is ridiculous! You simply need to go to bed and extinguish the candle. While staying awake can be difficult, particularly during church, sleeping is effortless. Even infants understand how to do that, and they are not particularly intelligent.'

'I have not slept for three centuries,' he said mournfully, and Virginia’s lovely blue eyes widened in astonishment; 'for three hundred years I have been unable to sleep, and I am exhausted.'

Virginia became serious, her small lips quivering like rose petals. She approached him, knelt beside him, and gazed up at his aged, withered face.

'Poor, poor Ghost,' she whispered; 'is there nowhere you can find rest?'

'Far beyond the pine forest,' he replied in a soft, dreamy tone, 'lies a small garden. The grass grows long and thick there, the hemlock flowers bloom in great white stars, and the nightingale sings throughout the night. model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_017",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Mr. Otis himself. While Mr. Otis held a genuine personal affection for the young Duke, he maintained a theoretical opposition to titles, stating that he feared the enervating atmosphere of a pleasure-seeking aristocracy might cause the core tenets of republican simplicity to fade. Nevertheless, his reservations were entirely set aside, and I am certain that as he escorted his daughter down the aisle at St. George’s, Hanover Square, no man in all of England felt more pride.

Following their honeymoon, the Duke and Duchess traveled to Canterville Chase. The day after they arrived, they took an afternoon walk to the secluded graveyard near the pine forest. Although the wording for Sir Simon’s headstone had initially caused significant debate, they ultimately chose to engrave only the old man’s initials and the poem from the library window. The Duchess scattered beautiful roses over the grave, and after lingering there for a while, they wandered into the crumbling chancel of the ancient abbey. The Duchess rested on a collapsed pillar, while her husband reclined at her feet, smoking a cigarette and gazing into her lovely eyes. Abruptly, he discarded his cigarette, grasped her hand, and remarked, “Virginia, a wife ought to keep no secrets from her husband.”

“My dear Cecil! I keep no secrets from you.”

“Indeed you do,” he replied with a smile, “you have never shared what occurred when you were shut away with the ghost.”

“I have never told anyone, Cecil,” Virginia replied solemnly.

“I am aware of that, but you might tell me.”

“Please do not press me, Cecil; I cannot say. Poor Sir Simon! I am deeply indebted to him. Truly, do not laugh, Cecil, I mean it. He helped me understand the nature of Life, the meaning of Death, and why Love is more powerful than both.”

The Duke stood and kissed his wife with affection.

“You may keep your secret, provided I possess your heart,” he whispered.

“You have always held it, Cecil.”

“And you will share it with our children one day, won’t you?”

Virginia turned red.

THE SPHINX WITHOUT A SECRET
AN ETCHING

ONE afternoon, I sat outside the Café de la Paix, observing the mix of grandeur and squalor in Parisian life. As I sipped my vermouth, I pondered the curious display of vanity and destitution unfolding before me, until I heard my name spoken. Turning, I recognized Lord Murchison. We had not encountered one another since our university days, nearly a decade prior, so I was pleased to meet him again, and we shook hands with warmth. We had been close companions at Oxford. I had been very fond of him; he was remarkably handsome, spirited, and honorable. We often joked that he would be the perfect man if he did not insist on telling the truth, though I suspect we respected his candor all the more for it. I noticed he had changed significantly. He appeared troubled and confused, as if grappling with some uncertainty. I doubted it was modern skepticism, as Murchison was a staunch Tory who held the Pentateuch in the same high regard as the House of Peers; therefore, I surmised it involved a woman and inquired if he had married."
}

ONE afternoon, I was sitting outside the Café de la Paix, observing the splendor and squalor of Parisian life.
