  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_017",
  "condition": "simplify",
  "rewritten_text": "Mr. Otis himself. Mr. Otis was very fond of the young Duke personally, but in theory, he objected to titles. In his own words, he was worried that, surrounded by the weakening influences of a pleasure-seeking aristocracy, the true principles of republican simplicity might be forgotten. His objections, however, were completely overruled. I believe that when he walked down the aisle of St. George’s, Hanover Square, with his daughter leaning on his arm, there was not a prouder man in all of England.

After their honeymoon, the Duke and Duchess went to Canterville Chase. The day after they arrived, they walked over to the lonely churchyard by the pine woods. There had been a lot of difficulty at first regarding the inscription on Sir Simon’s tombstone, but they finally decided to engrave only the initials of the old man’s name and the verse from the library window. The Duchess had brought some lovely roses, which she scattered over the grave. After standing there for a while, they strolled into the ruined chancel of the old abbey. The Duchess sat down on a fallen pillar, while her husband lay at her feet, smoking a cigarette and looking up at her beautiful eyes. Suddenly, he threw his cigarette away, took her hand, and said, “Virginia, a wife should have no secrets from her husband.”

“Dear Cecil! I have no secrets from you.”

“Yes, you do,” he answered, smiling. “You have never told me what happened to you when you were locked up with the ghost.”

“I have never told anyone, Cecil,” Virginia said seriously.

“I know that, but you could tell me.”

“Please don’t ask me, Cecil. I cannot tell you. Poor Sir Simon! I owe him a great deal. Yes, don’t laugh, Cecil, I really do. He made me see what life is, what death signifies, and why love is stronger than both.”

The Duke stood up and kissed his wife lovingly.

“You can keep your secret as long as I have your heart,” he whispered.

“You have always had that, Cecil.”

“And you will tell our children someday, won’t you?”

Virginia blushed.

THE SPHINX WITHOUT A SECRET
AN ETCHING

ONE afternoon, I was sitting outside the Café de la Paix, watching the splendor and shabbiness of Parisian life. I was wondering over my vermouth at the strange display of pride and poverty passing before me when I heard someone call my name. I turned around and saw Lord Murchison. We had not met since we were at college together nearly ten years earlier, so I was delighted to see him again, and we shook hands warmly. At Oxford, we had been great friends. I liked him immensely; he was so handsome, high-spirited, and honorable. We used to say that he would be the best of fellows if he did not always speak the truth, but I think we actually admired him all the more for his honesty. I found him quite changed. He looked anxious and puzzled, and he seemed to be doubting something. I felt it could not be modern skepticism, for Murchison was a staunch conservative who believed in the Bible as firmly as he believed in the House of Lords. Therefore, I concluded that it was a woman, and I asked him if he was married yet."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_018",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I unfolded it. Within lay the portrait of a lady. She was slender and tall, possessing a peculiar, striking appearance with her expansive, indistinct eyes and flowing hair. She resembled a psychic, enveloped in luxurious furs.

‘How do you interpret that countenance?’ he inquired; ‘does it appear honest?’

I scrutinized it with care. To my eyes, it was the face of a person harboring a hidden matter, though whether that secret was virtuous or wicked remained unclear. Its allure was a beauty fashioned from numerous enigmas—a psychological rather than a physical beauty—and the slight smirk hovering upon her mouth was far too nuanced to be truly pleasant.

‘Well,’ he exclaimed with irritation, ‘what is your verdict?’

‘She is the Gioconda in furs,’ I replied. ‘Tell me everything regarding her.’

‘Not at this moment,’ he stated; ‘after we dine,’ and he shifted the conversation to different topics.

Once the server delivered our coffee and cigarettes, I recalled Gerald to his vow. He stood up, paced the room a few times, then collapsed into a chair and recounted this tale:

‘One evening,’ he began, ‘I was strolling along Bond Street near five o’clock. The congestion of carriages was immense, nearly halting the flow of traffic. Near the curb sat a small yellow brougham that, for some reason, caught my eye. As I walked past, the face I displayed to you earlier peered out. It captivated me instantly. Throughout that night and the following day, I could not stop thinking of it. I paced that miserable Row, scanning every carriage and searching for the yellow brougham; yet I could not locate my beautiful stranger, and eventually, I concluded she was merely a phantom. Roughly a week later, I attended a dinner at Madame de Rastail’s. We were scheduled for eight, but by half-past eight, we remained in the parlor. Finally, the attendant opened the doors and declared Lady Alroy. It was the woman I had been seeking. She entered quite slowly, appearing like a moonbeam draped in grey lace, and to my immense joy, I was assigned to escort her to dinner. Once seated, I remarked quite naively, “I believe I spotted you on Bond Street a while back, Lady Alroy.” She turned quite pallid and whispered, “Please do not speak so loudly; you might be overheard.” I felt wretched for such a poor start and dove impulsively into a discussion about French theater. She spoke very little, always in that same soft, melodic tone, appearing as though she feared someone was eavesdropping. I fell deeply, foolishly in love, and the vague aura of mystery clinging to her stirred my most intense curiosity. When she departed, which occurred shortly after the meal, I requested permission to visit her. She paused briefly, checked to see if anyone was nearby, and then replied, “Yes; tomorrow at a quarter to five.” I implored Madame de Rastail for information about her; however, I only discovered she was a widow residing in a lovely Park Lane home, and as some tedious academic began a lecture on widows as examples of the matrimonially fittest, I departed for home.’"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_018",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "I opened it. Inside was a photograph of a woman. She was tall and slender, with a strange, striking quality to her, featuring large, unfocused eyes and loose hair. She looked like a psychic, wrapped in expensive furs.

“What do you think of that face?” he asked. “Does it look honest?”

I studied it closely. It seemed to be the face of someone harboring a secret, though I couldn't tell if that secret was good or evil. Her beauty was crafted from many mysteries—a psychological beauty rather than a physical one—and the faint smile touching her lips was far too subtle to be truly sweet.

“Well?” he demanded impatiently. “What do you say?”

“She is the Mona Lisa in furs,” I replied. “Tell me everything about her.”

“Not now,” he said. “After dinner,” and he began discussing other topics.

When the waiter brought our coffee and cigarettes, I reminded Gerald of his promise. He stood up, paced the room a few times, then sank into an armchair and told me this story:

“One evening,” he began, “I was walking down Bond Street around five o'clock. The carriage traffic was incredibly heavy and nearly at a standstill. A small yellow brougham was parked near the curb, and for some reason, it caught my eye. As I walked past, the face I showed you earlier looked out from it. It captivated me instantly. I thought about it all that night and the entire next day. I wandered up and down that miserable Row, peering into every carriage and waiting for the yellow brougham, but I couldn't find my beautiful stranger, and eventually, I started to think she was just a dream. About a week later, I was dining with Madame de Rastail. Dinner was scheduled for eight, but by eight-thirty, we were still waiting in the drawing-room. Finally, the servant opened the door and announced Lady Alroy. It was the woman I had been searching for. She entered very slowly, looking like a moonbeam in grey lace, and to my immense delight, I was asked to escort her to dinner. Once we were seated, I remarked quite innocently, ‘I think I caught a glimpse of you in Bond Street a while ago, Lady Alroy.’ She turned very pale and said to me in a low voice, ‘Please don't speak so loudly; you might be overheard.’ I felt terrible for such a poor start and recklessly dove into a conversation about French plays. She spoke very little, always in that same low, musical voice, and seemed as if she were afraid of being overheard. I fell passionately, foolishly in love, and the indefinable air of mystery surrounding her fueled my intense curiosity. When she was leaving, which she did shortly after dinner, I asked if I might call on her. She hesitated for a moment, glanced around to see if anyone was nearby, and then said, ‘Yes; tomorrow at a quarter to five.’ I begged Madame de Rastail to tell me about her, but all I could learn was that she was a widow with a beautiful house in Park Lane. As some tedious scientist began a lecture on how widows exemplify the survival of the matrimonially fittest, I left and went home.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_019",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I rapped on the entrance, and a decent-appearing lady answered. I inquired if she possessed any vacancies. “Indeed, sir,” she answered, “the parlors are technically rented; however, I have not encountered the tenant for a quarter-year, and since the payments are overdue, they are yours.”—“Is this the person?” I asked, presenting the picture. “That is certainly her,” she cried; “and when might she return, sir?”—“The lady has passed away,” I responded. “Oh sir, I truly hope not!” the woman lamented; “she was my finest tenant. She provided three guineas weekly just to occupy my parlors occasionally.” “Did she meet anyone there?” I inquired; yet the woman insisted that was not the case, noting she arrived unaccompanied and met nobody. “What on earth was her purpose?” I exclaimed. “She merely occupied the parlor, sir, perusing volumes, and occasionally took tea,” the woman replied. Lacking a response, I handed her a sovereign and departed. Now, what is your interpretation of this? You do not credit the woman’s account?”

“I do.”

“Then for what reason did Lady Alroy visit?”

“My dear Gerald,” I responded, “Lady Alroy was simply a person obsessed with intrigue. She rented those quarters for the thrill of arriving with her face obscured, pretending to be a protagonist. She craved concealment, yet she was essentially a Sphinx lacking any mystery.”

“Are you truly convinced?”

“I am certain,” I answered.

He retrieved the leather container, unlatched it, and studied the image. “I wonder?” he finally remarked.

THE MODEL MILLIONAIRE
A NOTE OF ADMIRATION

UNLESS one possesses wealth, there is little benefit in being a delightful individual. Romance is the prerogative of the affluent, not the vocation of the idle. The impoverished ought to remain grounded and dull. It is superior to possess a steady revenue than to be captivating. These represent the fundamental realities of contemporary existence that Hughie Erskine never grasped. Poor Hughie! Mentally, we must concede, he lacked significance. He never uttered a clever or even a malicious remark throughout his existence. Yet he was remarkably handsome, with his wavy chestnut hair, his sharp features, and his slate-colored eyes. He was as well-liked by gentlemen as by ladies, and he possessed every talent except that of accumulating wealth. His father had left him his military saber and a History of the Peninsular War spanning fifteen books. Hughie suspended the former above his mirror, placed the latter upon a ledge between Ruff’s Guide and Bailey’s Magazine, and subsisted on two hundred pounds annually provided by an elderly aunt. He had attempted various pursuits. He had worked at the Stock Exchange for half a year; yet what could a social butterfly achieve among aggressive financiers? He had been a tea-trader for a slightly longer duration, but soon grew weary of pekoe and souchong. Then he attempted vending dry sherry. That failed; the wine was somewhat too parched. Eventually, he became nothing, a charming, unproductive youth with an ideal visage and no career.

To exacerbate the situation, he was smitten."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_019",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "I knocked on the door, and a respectable-looking woman opened it. I asked if she had any rooms available. “Well, sir,” she replied, “the drawing-rooms are technically rented, but I haven’t seen the lady in three months, and since she owes rent, you can have them.” “Is this the lady?” I asked, showing her the photograph. “That’s her, all right,” she exclaimed, “and when is she coming back, sir?” “The lady is dead,” I replied. “Oh, sir, I hope not!” the woman said. “She was my best tenant. She paid me three guineas a week just to sit in my drawing-rooms occasionally.” “Did she meet someone here?” I asked, but the woman insisted that wasn’t the case, saying she always came alone and saw no one. “What on earth did she do here?” I cried. “She simply sat in the drawing-room, sir, reading books, and sometimes had tea,” the woman answered. I didn’t know what to say, so I gave her a sovereign and left. Now, what do you think it all meant? You don’t believe the woman was telling the truth?”

“I do.”

“Then why did Lady Alroy go there?”

“My dear Gerald,” I answered, “Lady Alroy was simply a woman with a mania for mystery. She rented those rooms for the thrill of going there with her veil down and imagining she was a heroine. She had a passion for secrecy, but she was merely a Sphinx without a secret.”

“Do you really think so?”

“I am sure of it,” I replied.

He took out the leather case, opened it, and looked at the photograph. “I wonder?” he said at last.

THE MODEL MILLIONAIRE
A NOTE OF ADMIRATION

UNLESS you are wealthy, there is no point in being a charming fellow. Romance is the privilege of the rich, not the career of the unemployed. The poor should be practical and down-to-earth. It is better to have a steady income than to be fascinating. These are the great truths of modern life that Hughie Erskine never realized. Poor Hughie! Intellectually, we must admit, he was not of much importance. He never said a brilliant or even a mean-spirited thing in his life. But then he was wonderfully good-looking, with his crisp brown hair, his clear-cut profile, and his grey eyes. He was as popular with men as he was with women, and he had every talent except that of making money. His father had bequeathed him his cavalry sword and a History of the Peninsular War in fifteen volumes. Hughie hung the sword over his mirror, put the books on a shelf between a racing guide and a magazine, and lived on two hundred a year that an old aunt allowed him. He had tried everything. He had worked on the Stock Exchange for six months, but what was a butterfly to do among aggressive traders? He had been a tea merchant for a little longer, but had soon tired of the business. Then he had tried selling dry sherry. That didn’t work; the sherry was a little too dry. Ultimately he became nothing, a delightful, ineffectual young man with a perfect profile and no profession.

To make matters worse, he was in love."
}

She paid me three guineas a week just to sit in my drawing-rooms occasionally.” “Did she meet someone here?” I asked, but the woman insisted that wasn’t the case, saying she always came alone and saw no one. “What on earth did she do here?” I cried. “She simply sat in the drawing-room, sir, reading books, and sometimes had tea,” the woman answered. I didn’t know what to say, so I gave her a sovereign and left. Now, what do you think it all meant? You don’t believe the woman was telling the truth?”

“I do.”

“Then why did Lady Alroy go there?”

“My dear Gerald,” I answered, “Lady Alroy was simply a woman with a mania for mystery. She rented those rooms for the thrill of going there with her veil down and imagining she was a heroine. She had a passion for secrecy, but she was merely a Sphinx without a secret.”

“Do you really think so?”

“I am sure of it,” I replied.

He took out the leather case, opened it, and looked at the photograph. “I wonder?” he said at last.

THE MODEL MILLIONAIRE
A NOTE OF ADMIRATION

UNLESS you are wealthy, there is no point in being a charming fellow. Romance is the privilege of the rich, not the career of the unemployed. The poor should be practical and down-to-earth. It is better to have a steady income than to be fascinating. These are the great truths of modern life that Hughie Erskine never realized. Poor Hughie! Intellectually, we must admit, he was not of much importance. He never said a brilliant or even a mean-spirited thing in his life. But then he was wonderfully good-looking, with his crisp brown hair, his clear-cut profile, and his grey eyes. He was as popular with men as he was with women, and he had every talent except that of making money. His father had bequeathed him his cavalry sword and a History of the Peninsular War in fifteen volumes. Hughie hung the sword over his mirror, put the books on a shelf between a racing guide and a magazine, and lived on two hundred a year that an old aunt allowed him. He had tried everything. He had worked on the Stock Exchange for six months, but what was a butterfly to do among aggressive traders? He had been a tea merchant for a little longer, but had soon tired of the business. Then he had tried selling dry sherry. That didn’t work; the sherry was a little too dry. Ultimately he became nothing, a delightful, ineffectual young man with a perfect profile and no profession.

To make matters worse, he was in love."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_020",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "In person, he was an odd, unrefined man, possessing a freckled complexion and a shaggy, crimson beard. Yet, once he picked up his brush, he proved a true master, and his canvases were highly coveted. It must be admitted that he was initially drawn to Hughie solely because of his physical appeal. ‘The only individuals a painter ought to associate with,’ he would remark, ‘are those who are dull and lovely, people who are an aesthetic delight to behold and a mental relief to converse with. Men who are fops and women who are darlings govern the world, or at least they ought to.’ Nevertheless, after becoming better acquainted with Hughie, he grew to appreciate him just as much for his cheerful, lively disposition and his open-handed, impulsive character, granting him constant access to his workspace.

Upon entering, Hughie discovered Trevor applying the final strokes to a magnificent life-sized portrait of a beggar. The subject stood upon a raised dais in the corner of the room. He was a shriveled elderly man, possessing a countenance resembling creased parchment and a deeply sorrowful look. A rough brown cloak, entirely ragged and torn, was draped over his shoulders; his heavy boots were mended and cobbled, and he leaned upon a crude staff with one hand while extending his battered hat for charity with the other.

‘What a remarkable model!’ Hughie murmured while greeting his friend.

‘A remarkable model?’ Trevor bellowed at the top of his lungs; ‘I should say so! Beggars like him are not encountered every day. A rare find, my dear fellow; a living Velasquez! Good heavens! What an etching Rembrandt would have created of him!’

‘Poor old man!’ Hughie remarked, ‘how wretched he appears! But I imagine, for you artists, his face is his fortune?’

‘Undoubtedly,’ Trevor answered, ‘you wouldn’t want a beggar to appear cheerful, would you?’

‘What does a model earn for posing?’ Hughie inquired, settling into a cozy spot on a sofa.

‘A shilling every hour.’

‘And what do you receive for your painting, Alan?’

‘Oh, for this piece I receive two thousand!’

‘Pounds?’

‘Guineas. Artists, writers, and doctors always receive guineas.’

‘Well, I believe the model deserves a share,’ Hughie exclaimed, chuckling; ‘they labor just as intensely as you do.’

‘Rubbish, rubbish! Why, consider the effort of applying the paint alone, and remaining on one’s feet all day at the easel! It is easy enough, Hughie, for you to speak, but I assure you that there are times when Art nearly reaches the status of physical toil. But you must not talk; I am quite occupied. Have a cigarette, and remain silent.’

Some time later, the domestic entered and informed Trevor that the framer wished to speak with him.

‘Do not leave, Hughie,’ he requested as he departed, ‘I shall return momentarily.’

The aged beggar utilized Trevor’s departure to pause for a moment on a wooden seat positioned behind him. He appeared so desolate and miserable that Hughie could not refrain from feeling sympathy, and he searched his pockets to determine what currency he possessed. All he discovered was a sovereign and a few coins. ‘Poor old man,’ he mused, ‘he requires this more than I do, though it signifies no cabs for two weeks’; and he crossed the room and pressed the sovereign into the beggar’s palm."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_020",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "He was personally a strange, rough fellow with a freckled face and a ragged red beard. Yet, when he picked up a brush, he was a true master, and his paintings were highly sought after. It must be admitted that he was initially drawn to Hughie entirely because of his personal charm. He used to say, "The only people a painter should know are those who are dull and beautiful, people who are an artistic pleasure to look at and a mental relief to talk to. Men who are dandies and women who are darlings rule the world, or at least they should." However, after getting to know Hughie better, he liked him just as much for his bright, buoyant spirits and his generous, reckless nature, and he had given him permanent access to his studio.

When Hughie arrived, he found Trevor putting the finishing touches on a wonderful life-size painting of a beggar. The beggar himself was standing on a raised platform in the corner of the studio. He was a wizened old man with a face like wrinkled parchment and a most piteous expression. A coarse brown cloak, full of tears and tatters, was draped over his shoulders; his thick boots were patched and cobbled, and he leaned on a rough stick with one hand while holding out his battered hat for charity with the other.

"What an amazing model!" Hughie whispered as he shook hands with his friend.

"An amazing model?" Trevor shouted at the top of his voice. "I should think so! You don't run into beggars like him every day. A rare find, my dear fellow; a living Velasquez! Good heavens, what an etching Rembrandt would have made of him!"

"Poor old guy," Hughie said, "he looks so miserable! But I suppose, to you painters, his face is his fortune?"

"Certainly," Trevor replied, "you don't want a beggar to look happy, do you?"

"How much does a model get for sitting?" Hughie asked as he found a comfortable seat on a divan.

"A shilling an hour."

"And how much do you get for your picture, Alan?"

"Oh, for this I get two thousand!"

"Pounds?"

"Guineas. Painters, poets, and physicians always get paid in guineas."

"Well, I think the model should get a percentage," Hughie laughed, "they work just as hard as you do."

"Nonsense, nonsense! Why, just look at the trouble of applying the paint alone, and standing all day long at an easel! It’s easy for you to talk, Hughie, but I assure you there are moments when art almost reaches the dignity of manual labor. But you mustn’t chatter; I’m very busy. Smoke a cigarette and keep quiet."

After some time, the servant entered and told Trevor that the framemaker wanted to speak with him.

"Don’t run away, Hughie," he said as he left, "I’ll be back in a moment."

The old beggar took advantage of Trevor’s absence to rest for a moment on a wooden bench behind him. He looked so forlorn and wretched that Hughie couldn't help but pity him, and he felt in his pockets to see what money he had. All he could find was a sovereign and some loose change. "Poor old fellow," he thought to himself, "he needs it more than I do, even if it means no taxis for a fortnight." He walked across the studio and slipped the sovereign into the beggar’s hand."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_021",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“This represents the likeness of Mr. W. H.,” Erskine remarked, wearing a melancholy expression. Perhaps it was merely the way the light hit him, but his eyes appeared to glisten with moisture.

“Mr. W. H.!” I cried out; “who exactly was Mr. W. H.?”

“Have you forgotten?” he replied; “observe the volume his hand is placed upon.”

“I notice there is some script there, yet I am unable to decipher it,” I responded.

“Use this lens and attempt it,” Erskine suggested, that same mournful smile still lingering on his lips.

I accepted the glass, and shifting the lamp closer, I started to decipher the difficult script from the sixteenth century. “To the onlie begetter of these insuing sonnets.” . . . “Good heavens!” I shouted, “is this the Mr. W. H. of Shakespeare?”

“Cyril Graham maintained that it was,” Erskine murmured.

“But it bears no resemblance to Lord Pembroke,” I countered. “I am quite familiar with the portraits at Penshurst. I was visiting that area just a few weeks back.”

“Do you truly hold the conviction that the sonnets were written to Lord Pembroke?” he inquired.

“I am certain of it,” I replied. “Pembroke, Shakespeare, and Mrs. Mary Fitton are the trio of figures in the Sonnets; there is no question regarding that.”

“Well, I concur with you,” Erskine stated, “though I did not always hold that view. I once believed—well, I suppose I once believed in Cyril Graham and his hypothesis.”

“And what was that?” I asked, gazing at the remarkable portrait, which had already started to exert a peculiar pull upon me.

“It is a lengthy tale,” Erskine said, reclaiming the image from me—somewhat suddenly, I felt at the time—“a very lengthy tale; yet if you wish to hear it, I shall recount it for you.”

“I am fond of theories concerning the Sonnets,” I exclaimed; “though I doubt I shall be swayed toward any fresh perspective. The subject is no longer a puzzle to anyone. In truth, I am surprised it was ever considered a mystery.”

“Since I do not subscribe to the theory, I am unlikely to persuade you of it,” Erskine said, chuckling; “but it might intrigue you.”

“By all means, tell me,” I answered. “If it is half as charming as the painting, I shall be more than content.”

“Very well,” Erskine said, igniting a cigarette, “I must start by describing Cyril Graham himself. He and I were in the same boarding house at Eton. I was a couple of years his senior, yet we were close companions, sharing all our studies and recreations. Naturally, there was far more recreation than study, though I cannot claim to regret that. It is always beneficial to avoid a strictly practical education, and the lessons I gathered on the playing fields of Eton have proven just as valuable as anything I acquired at Cambridge. I should mention that Cyril’s parents were both deceased. They had perished in a tragic yachting disaster near the Isle of Wight. His father had served in the diplomatic corps and had wed the daughter—the sole daughter, in fact—of the elderly Lord Crediton, who assumed the role of Cyril’s guardian following his parents’ passing."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_023",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "To him, just as to me, they represented verses of grave and sorrowful significance, extracted from the anguish of Shakespeare’s spirit and sweetened by the nectar of his speech. He was even less inclined to concede that they functioned as a mere philosophical metaphor, or that within them Shakespeare was speaking to his Ideal Self, his concept of Ideal Manhood, the Spirit of Beauty, Reason, the Divine Logos, or the Catholic Church. He sensed, as I believe we all must, that the Sonnets are directed toward a specific person—a particular youth whose character for some reason appeared to have flooded Shakespeare’s soul with both harrowing joy and equally harrowing despair.

Having cleared the path in this fashion, Cyril requested that I set aside any prior notions I might have held regarding the matter and grant his own hypothesis an impartial and open-minded hearing. The dilemma he highlighted was this: Who was that young man of Shakespeare’s era who, despite lacking noble lineage or even a noble disposition, was spoken to by him with such fervent devotion that we can only marvel at this peculiar adoration, feeling almost hesitant to turn the key that reveals the secret of the poet’s heart? Who was he whose bodily grace was such that it served as the foundation of Shakespeare’s craft, the primary fountain of his inspiration, and the very embodiment of his visions? To view him merely as the subject of certain love verses is to overlook the entire significance of the poems, for the art Shakespeare discusses in the Sonnets is not the art of the Sonnets themselves—which were to him but minor and private trifles—but rather the art of the playwright to which he is constantly referring. And he to whom Shakespeare declared, 

Thou art all my art, and dost advance
As high as learning my rude ignorance,

he to whom he pledged immortality, 

Where breath most breathes, even in the mouths of men,—

was surely none other than the boy-actor for whom he fashioned Viola and Imogen, Juliet and Rosalind, Portia and Desdemona, and Cleopatra herself. This was Cyril Graham’s hypothesis, developed, as you can see, entirely from the Sonnets themselves, and relying for its validity not so much on verifiable proof or structured evidence, but on a sort of spiritual and aesthetic intuition, through which alone he maintained the true significance of the poems could be perceived. I recall him reciting that exquisite sonnet—

How can my Muse want subject to invent,
While thou dost breathe, that pour’st into my verse
Thine own sweet argument, too excellent
For every vulgar paper to rehearse?
O, give thyself the thanks, if aught in me
Worthy perusal stand against thy sight;
For who’s so dumb that cannot write to thee,
When thou thyself dost give invention light?
Be thou the tenth Muse, ten times more in worth
Than those old nine which rhymers invocate;
And he that calls on thee, let him bring forth
Eternal numbers to outlive long date—

and demonstrating how thoroughly it supported his claim; indeed, he examined every one of the Sonnets meticulously, showing, or believing he showed, that under his new interpretation, passages that had appeared murky, immoral, or hyperbolic became lucid, logical, and of profound artistic value, demonstrating Shakespeare’s understanding of the genuine connection between the actor’s craft and the dramatist’s art."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_023",
  "condition": "simplify",
  "rewritten_text": "To him, as to me, they were poems of serious and tragic meaning, drawn from the bitterness of Shakespeare’s heart and sweetened by his eloquent words. He was even less willing to accept that they were just a philosophical allegory, or that in them Shakespeare was addressing his Ideal Self, his Ideal Manhood, the Spirit of Beauty, Reason, the Divine Logos, or the Catholic Church. He felt, as I think we all must, that the Sonnets are addressed to an individual—a specific young man whose personality somehow filled Shakespeare’s soul with both terrible joy and terrible despair.

Having cleared the way, Cyril asked me to set aside any preconceived ideas I might have and to give his own theory a fair and unbiased hearing. The problem, he pointed out, was this: Who was that young man of Shakespeare’s time who, without being of noble birth or noble character, was addressed with such passionate adoration that we can only wonder at this strange worship and feel almost afraid to unlock the mystery of the poet’s heart? Who was he whose physical beauty became the cornerstone of Shakespeare’s art, the source of his inspiration, and the embodiment of his dreams? To see him merely as the subject of love poems is to miss their entire meaning. The art Shakespeare discusses in the Sonnets is not the art of the Sonnets themselves, which he considered minor and private; he is always referring to the art of the dramatist. The person to whom Shakespeare said, “Thou art all my art, and dost advance / As high as learning my rude ignorance,” and to whom he promised immortality, “Where breath most breathes, even in the mouths of men,” was surely the boy-actor for whom he created Viola, Imogen, Juliet, Rosalind, Portia, Desdemona, and Cleopatra. This was Cyril Graham’s theory, developed entirely from the Sonnets themselves. He argued that it should be accepted not based on formal proof, but through a kind of spiritual and artistic intuition, which he claimed was the only way to discern the true meaning of the poems. I remember him reading that fine sonnet—

How can my Muse want subject to invent,
While thou dost breathe, that pour’st into my verse
Thine own sweet argument, too excellent
For every vulgar paper to rehearse?
O, give thyself the thanks, if aught in me
Worthy perusal stand against thy sight;
For who’s so dumb that cannot write to thee,
When thou thyself dost give invention light?
Be thou the tenth Muse, ten times more in worth
Than those old nine which rhymers invocate;
And he that calls on thee, let him bring forth
Eternal numbers to outlive long date—

He pointed out how completely it supported his theory. He went through all the Sonnets carefully, showing, or believing he showed, that under his new explanation, passages that had seemed obscure, wrong, or exaggerated became clear, rational, and artistically significant, illustrating Shakespeare’s view of the relationship between the actor’s art and the dramatist’s art."
}

Removing it, he discovered the very image currently resting on the sofa. Though it was filthy and mildewed, he succeeded in cleaning it, and to his immense delight, realized he had stumbled upon the exact object he had been seeking. It was a genuine portrait of Mr. W. H., his hand placed upon the dedication page of the Sonnets, while the frame bore the young man’s name in faint, black uncial script against a tarnished gold background: “Master Will. Hews.”

‘What was I supposed to respond? It never crossed my mind that Cyril Graham was deceiving me or attempting to validate his hypothesis through a counterfeit.’

‘But is it actually a forgery?’ I inquired.

‘Undoubtedly,’ Erskine replied. ‘It is an excellent fake, but a fake nonetheless. I recall Cyril seemed remarkably composed about the situation at the time, though he frequently insisted he personally needed no such evidence, believing his theory stood complete without it. I mocked him, insisting the theory would collapse otherwise, and I offered him my enthusiastic congratulations on his incredible find. We subsequently planned to have the image etched or reproduced as the frontispiece for Cyril’s edition of the Sonnets. For three months, we did nothing but review every line of the poems until we had resolved every textual or interpretive ambiguity. One unfortunate day, while visiting a print shop in Holborn, I noticed some exquisite silver-point sketches on the counter. I was so captivated that I purchased them, and the owner, a man named Rawlings, explained they were the work of a young artist named Edward Merton—a talented but destitute man. I visited Merton a few days later, having obtained his address from the dealer, and encountered a pale, intriguing young man accompanied by a rather plain wife, who I later learned served as his model. model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_025",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "His face drained of color as he stated, “I acted entirely for your benefit. You refused to be persuaded otherwise. This does not change the validity of the hypothesis.” “The validity of the hypothesis!” I shouted; “we should speak as little of that as possible. You never truly believed it yourself. Had you, you would not have resorted to forgery to demonstrate it.” We exchanged heated remarks; our argument was intense. I suspect I was unfair. By the following morning, he had passed away.”

“Dead!” I exclaimed.

“Indeed; he took his own life with a pistol. Some blood splattered onto the painting’s frame, right where the name had been inscribed. When I reached the scene—his attendant had summoned me immediately—the authorities were already present. He had left a note for me, clearly composed in a state of extreme turmoil and anguish.”

“What did it contain?” I inquired.

“Only that he maintained an absolute belief in Willie Hughes; that the painting was forged merely to appease me, and that this did not in any way diminish the truth of the theory; and that, to demonstrate the unwavering strength of his conviction, he intended to sacrifice his life for the mystery of the Sonnets. It was a senseless, irrational letter. I recall he concluded by stating that he bequeathed the Willie Hughes theory to me, and that it was my responsibility to reveal it to the world and reveal the secret of Shakespeare’s heart.”

“It is a deeply tragic tale,” I cried; “yet why have you failed to fulfill his request?”

Erskine lifted his shoulders. “Because the theory is entirely baseless from start to finish,” he replied.

“My dear Erskine,” I said, rising from my chair, “you are completely mistaken regarding this. It serves as the only flawless explanation for Shakespeare’s Sonnets ever devised. It is comprehensive in every aspect. I have faith in Willie Hughes.”

“Do not say that,” Erskine remarked solemnly; “I suspect there is something cursed about the concept, and there is no intellectual merit to support it. I have examined the entire subject, and I promise you the theory is utterly flawed. It holds up to a certain degree, then fails. For goodness’ sake, my friend, do not pursue the topic of Willie Hughes. It will only lead to your own heartbreak.”

“Erskine,” I replied, “you have an obligation to share this theory with the world. If you refuse, I shall. By withholding it, you dishonor the memory of Cyril Graham, the youngest and most brilliant of all literary martyrs. I beg you to honor him. He perished for this cause—do not let his sacrifice be meaningless.”

Erskine stared at me in disbelief. “You are being swept away by the emotional weight of the narrative,” he said. “You overlook the fact that a concept is not necessarily factual simply because someone dies for it. I was deeply attached to Cyril Graham. His passing was a devastating shock to me. I did not get over it for years. I doubt I ever truly have. But Willie Hughes? There is no substance to the Willie Hughes idea.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_025",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "He turned very pale and said, “I did it entirely for your sake. You wouldn’t be convinced any other way. It doesn’t change the truth of the theory.” “The truth of the theory!” I exclaimed. “The less we say about that, the better. You never even believed in it yourself. If you had, you wouldn’t have committed forgery to prove it.” We exchanged harsh words and had a terrible argument. I suspect I was unfair. The next morning, he was dead.

“Dead!” I cried.

“Yes; he shot himself with a revolver. Some of the blood splattered onto the picture frame, right where the name had been painted. By the time I arrived—his servant had called me immediately—the police were already there. He had left a letter for me, clearly written in a state of extreme agitation and distress.”

“What did it say?” I asked.

“Oh, that he believed absolutely in Willie Hughes; that the forgery of the painting was done merely as a concession to me and did not in the slightest invalidate the truth of the theory; and that, to show me how firm and flawless his faith in the whole thing was, he was going to offer his life as a sacrifice to the secret of the Sonnets. It was a foolish, mad letter. I remember he ended by saying that he entrusted the Willie Hughes theory to me, and that it was my responsibility to present it to the world and unlock the secret of Shakespeare’s heart.”

“It is a most tragic story,” I cried, “but why haven’t you carried out his wishes?”

Erskine shrugged. “Because it is a completely unsound theory from beginning to end,” he answered.

“My dear Erskine,” I said, rising from my seat, “you are entirely wrong about the whole matter. It is the only perfect key to Shakespeare’s Sonnets that has ever been created. It is complete in every detail. I believe in Willie Hughes.”

“Don’t say that,” Erskine said gravely. “I believe there is something fatal about the idea, and intellectually, there is nothing to be said for it. I have looked into the entire matter, and I assure you the theory is completely fallacious. It is plausible up to a certain point, and then it stops. For heaven’s sake, my dear boy, don’t take up the subject of Willie Hughes. You will break your heart over it.”

“Erskine,” I answered, “it is your duty to give this theory to the world. If you won’t do it, I will. By holding it back, you wrong the memory of Cyril Graham, the youngest and most splendid of all the martyrs of literature. I beg you to do him justice. He died for this—don’t let his death be in vain.”

Erskine looked at me in amazement. “You are carried away by the sentiment of the story,” he said. “You forget that a thing is not necessarily true just because a man dies for it. I was devoted to Cyril Graham. His death was a horrible blow to me. I didn’t recover for years. I don’t think I ever have. But Willie Hughes? There is nothing to the idea of Willie Hughes.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_027",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "How stinging the entire catastrophe of his betrayal and disgrace appeared now! It was a disgrace he rendered pleasant and charming through the sheer enchantment of his character, yet it remained a disgrace nonetheless. Still, if Shakespeare pardoned him, ought we not to offer our own forgiveness? I felt no desire to investigate the secrets of his transgression.

His departure from Shakespeare’s playhouse was a separate issue, which I examined extensively. Ultimately, I determined that Cyril Graham had erred in identifying the competing playwright of the 80th Sonnet as Chapman. It was plainly Marlowe to whom the text referred. When the Sonnets were composed, a phrase like ‘the proud full sail of his great verse’ could not have described Chapman’s writing, regardless of how well it might have suited his later Jacobean dramas. No, Marlowe was unmistakably the rival poet whom Shakespeare praised so highly; and that

 Affable familiar ghost
 Which nightly gulls him with intelligence,

was the Mephistopheles from his Doctor Faustus. Undoubtedly, Marlowe was captivated by the young actor’s elegance and beauty, enticing him away from the Blackfriars Theatre so he might perform as Gaveston in his Edward II. That Shakespeare possessed the legal authority to keep Willie Hughes within his troupe is clear from Sonnet LXXXVII, where he writes:

 Farewell! thou art too dear for my possessing,
 And like enough thou know’st thy estimate:
 The charter of thy worth gives thee releasing;
 My bonds in thee are all determinate.
 For how do I hold thee but by thy granting?
 And for that riches where is my deserving?
 The cause of this fair gift in me is wanting,
 And so my patent back again is swerving.
 Thyself thou gayest, thy own worth then not knowing,
 Or me, to whom thou gavest it, else mistaking;
 So thy great gift, upon misprision growing,
 Comes home again, on better judgement making.
 Thus have I had thee, as a dream doth flatter,
 In sleep a king, but waking no such matter.

Yet he would not use compulsion to retain the one he could not keep through affection. Willie Hughes joined Lord Pembroke’s troupe and, perhaps within the open courtyard of the Red Bull Tavern, portrayed the delicate favorite of King Edward. Following Marlowe’s passing, he appears to have rejoined Shakespeare, who, regardless of his partners' opinions, was quick to pardon the young performer’s fickleness and betrayal.

How masterfully, too, Shakespeare captured the disposition of the actor! Willie Hughes belonged to those

 That do not do the thing they most do show,
 Who, moving others, are themselves as stone.

He could simulate affection but lacked the ability to experience it, mimicking intensity without truly grasping it.

 In many’s looks the false heart’s history
 Is writ in moods and frowns and wrinkles strange,

but this was not the case with Willie Hughes. ‘Heaven,’ Shakespeare remarks in a sonnet of frantic devotion—

 Heaven in thy creation did decree
 That in thy face sweet love should ever dwell;
 Whate’er thy thoughts or thy heart’s workings be,
 Thy looks should nothing thence but sweetness tell.

In his ‘inconstant mind’ and his ‘false heart,’ one could readily identify the duplicity and betrayal that seem inherently linked to the artistic temperament, much like his craving for applause—that hunger for instant validation that defines all performers."
}

I had no desire to pry into the mystery of his sin.

His abandonment of Shakespeare’s theatre was a different matter, and I investigated it at great length. I eventually concluded that Cyril Graham was wrong to identify the rival dramatist of the 80th Sonnet as Chapman. It was clearly Marlowe who was being referenced. When the Sonnets were written, a phrase like ‘the proud full sail of his great verse’ could not have described Chapman’s work, regardless of how well it might have fit the style of his later Jacobean plays. No, Marlowe was clearly the rival dramatist Shakespeare spoke of in such glowing terms, and that ‘Affable familiar ghost / Which nightly gulls him with intelligence’ was the Mephistopheles from his Doctor Faustus. Marlowe was undoubtedly fascinated by the beauty and grace of the boy-actor and lured him away from the Blackfriars Theatre so he could play Gaveston in his Edward II. That Shakespeare had the legal right to keep Willie Hughes in his own company is evident from Sonnet LXXXVII, where he writes:

Farewell! You are too precious for me to possess,
And you likely know your own value:
The charter of your worth grants you release;
My legal claims on you have all expired.
For how do I hold you except by your consent?
And what merit do I have to deserve such riches?
The reason for this generous gift is missing in me,
And so my contract is returning to you.
You gave yourself, not knowing your own worth,
Or perhaps mistaken about the one to whom you gave it;
So your great gift, born of a misunderstanding,
Returns home now that you have better judgment.
Thus I have had you, as a dream might flatter,
A king in sleep, but nothing of the sort when awake.

But he would not hold by force the one he could not hold by love. Willie Hughes became a member of Lord Pembroke’s company and perhaps played the part of King Edward’s delicate minion in the open yard of the Red Bull Tavern. Upon Marlowe’s death, he seems to have returned to Shakespeare, who—regardless of what his partners may have thought—was quick to forgive the young actor’s willfulness and treachery.

‘In many’s looks the false heart’s history / Is writ in moods and frowns and wrinkles strange,’ but that was not the case with Willie Hughes. ‘Heaven,’ Shakespeare says in a sonnet of intense idolatry, ‘Heaven in thy creation did decree / That in thy face sweet love should ever dwell; / Whate’er thy thoughts or thy heart’s workings be, / Thy looks should nothing thence but sweetness tell.’

In his ‘inconstant mind’ and his ‘false heart,’ it was easy to recognize the insincerity and treachery that seem inseparable from the artistic nature, just as his love of praise reflected that desire for immediate recognition that characterizes all actors."

  "rewritten_text": "Surely no location could be more appropriate for the man to whom Shakespeare addressed the words, ‘thou art all my art,’ than this modest vineyard situated beyond the city ramparts. For did not Tragedy originate from the griefs of Dionysos? Was not the bright mirth of Comedy, characterized by its lighthearted joy and rapid wit, initially voiced by the vine-tenders of Sicily? Indeed, did not the violet and crimson marks of wine-foam upon the skin provide the initial inspiration for the allure of masquerade—the impulse for self-masking and the recognition of the importance of objectivity, thus manifesting in the primitive origins of the craft? Regardless of his final resting place—be it the small vineyard by the Gothic town’s gate or some shadowed London cemetery amidst the clamor of our vast metropolis—no grand memorial indicated his grave. His genuine sepulcher, as Shakespeare perceived, was the poet’s poetry, and his true cenotaph the enduring nature of the theater. This had been the case for others whose grace had provided a fresh creative spark to their era. The ivory form of the Bithynian slave decays within the verdant silt of the Nile, and the remains of the young Athenian are scattered across the golden slopes of the Cerameicus; yet Antinous survives through statuary, and Charmides through philosophy.

CHAPTER III

Following the passage of three weeks, I resolved to make a vigorous plea to Erskine to honor the legacy of Cyril Graham and to share with the public his extraordinary reading of the Sonnets—the sole interpretation that fully resolved the mystery. I lament that I possess no duplicate of my correspondence, nor have I managed to locate the original; yet I recall that I traversed the entire subject, filling numerous pages with fervent restatements of the evidence and logic my research had provided. It appeared to me that I was not simply reinstating Cyril Graham to his rightful position in the annals of literature, but also salvaging the reputation of Shakespeare himself from the tiresome recollection of a mundane affair. I infused the letter with all my fervor. I infused the letter with all my conviction.

In truth, the moment I dispatched it, a strange shift occurred within me. It felt as though I had surrendered my ability to trust in the Willie Hughes hypothesis regarding the Sonnets, as if something had departed from me, and I felt entirely detached from the entire matter. What had transpired? It is hard to define. Perhaps, by articulating a passion perfectly, I had depleted the passion itself. Emotional energies, much like the energies of physical existence, possess their definite boundaries. Perhaps the mere endeavor to persuade another of a theory necessitates a form of abandoning one’s own capacity for belief. Perhaps I was simply weary of the entire affair, and, my zeal having extinguished, my intellect was left to its own dispassionate assessment. However it transpired, and I cannot claim to account for it, there was no question that Willie Hughes abruptly transformed for me into a mere legend, a hollow reverie, the youthful whim of a man who, like many passionate souls, was more eager to persuade his peers than to be persuaded himself."
}

After three weeks had passed, I decided to make a strong appeal to Erskine to do justice to the memory of Cyril Graham and to share with the world his marvelous interpretation of the Sonnets—the only interpretation that fully explained the problem. I regret to say that I do not have a copy of my letter, nor have I been able to find the original; but I remember that I covered the entire subject, filling sheets of paper with passionate restatements of the arguments and proofs my study had suggested to me. It felt as though I were not merely restoring Cyril Graham to his rightful place in literary history, but rescuing the honor of Shakespeare himself from the tedious memory of a mundane affair. I poured all my enthusiasm into that letter. I poured all my faith into it.

In fact, no sooner had I sent it off than a strange reaction came over me. It felt as if I had given away my capacity to believe in the Willie Hughes theory of the Sonnets, that something had left me, and that I was completely indifferent to the entire subject. What had happened? It is difficult to say. Perhaps by finding perfect expression for a passion, I had exhausted the passion itself. Emotional forces, like the forces of physical life, have their definite limits. Perhaps the mere effort to convert someone to a theory involves some form of renouncing the power of belief. Perhaps I was simply tired of the whole thing, and with my enthusiasm burnt out, my reason was left to its own dispassionate judgment. However it happened, and I cannot pretend to explain it, there was no doubt that Willie Hughes suddenly became to me a mere myth, an idle dream, the boyish fancy of a young man who, like most ardent spirits, was more anxious to convince others than to be convinced himself."
}

Regardless of where he lay—whether in that small vineyard at the gate of the Gothic town, or in some dim London churchyard amidst the roar and bustle of our great city—no grand monument marked his resting place. His true tomb, as Shakespeare understood, was the poet’s verse, and his true monument was the permanence of the drama. This had been the case for others whose beauty had provided a new creative impulse to their era. The ivory body of the Bithynian slave rots in the green mud of the Nile, and the dust of the young Athenian is scattered on the yellow hills of the Cerameicus; yet Antinous lives on in sculpture, and Charmides in philosophy.

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_030",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Perishing for one’s religious convictions is the most regrettable way to spend a life, yet to perish for a theory of literature! It appeared beyond belief.

I checked the date. The correspondence was seven days old. An unlucky series of events had kept me away from the club for a few days, or I might have received it soon enough to rescue him. Perhaps there was still time. I hurried to my quarters, gathered my belongings, and departed on the evening train from Charing Cross. The travel was agonizing. I felt as though I would never reach my destination. Upon arrival, I took a carriage to the Hôtel l’Angleterre. I was informed that Erskine had been interred two days prior in the English burial ground. The entire catastrophe possessed a ghastly, absurd quality. I uttered various frantic remarks, drawing curious stares from the individuals in the lobby.

Abruptly, Lady Erskine, dressed in heavy mourning attire, crossed the foyer. Upon noticing me, she approached, whispered a few words regarding her unfortunate son, and began to weep. I guided her into her private parlor, where an older man was waiting. It was the English physician.

We spoke at length about Erskine, yet I remained silent regarding his reasons for taking his own life. It was clear he had shared nothing with his mother about the cause that had pushed him toward such a lethal, irrational deed. Eventually, Lady Erskine stood and remarked, “George left you a keepsake. It was an object he valued deeply. I shall retrieve it for you.”

Once she had exited, I turned to the doctor and remarked, “What a terrible blow this must be for Lady Erskine! I am surprised she handles it with such composure.”

“Oh, she was aware for months that this was inevitable,” he replied.

“Aware for months!” I exclaimed. “But why did she not intervene? Why did she not have him supervised? He must have been insane.”

The doctor gazed at me in confusion. “I do not follow your meaning,” he stated.

“Well,” I insisted, “if a mother is aware that her son intends to kill himself—”

“Suicide!” he countered. “Poor Erskine did not take his own life. He succumbed to tuberculosis. He traveled here to pass away. From the moment I examined him, I realized there was no chance of recovery. One lung was nearly destroyed, and the other was severely compromised. Three days before his passing, he inquired if there was any hope. I informed him honestly that there was none, and that he had only a few days remaining. He composed several letters and remained entirely composed, keeping his faculties until the end.”

Just then, Lady Erskine returned with the fateful portrait of Willie Hughes. “As George was dying, he requested that I present this to you,” she said. As I accepted it, her tears dropped onto my hand.

The portrait is currently displayed in my library, where it receives much praise from my acquaintances in the arts. They have concluded it is an Oudry rather than a Clouet. I have never felt the need to share its actual history. Yet, at times, when I gaze upon it, I reflect that there is truly much to support the Willie Hughes hypothesis regarding the Sonnets of Shakespeare."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "WILDE_LORD_ARTHUR_STORIES_030",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Dying for one’s religious beliefs is the worst way a person can spend their life, but dying for a literary theory! It seemed impossible.

I checked the date. The letter was a week old. Some unfortunate stroke of bad luck had kept me from the club for several days, or I might have received it in time to save him. Perhaps it wasn’t too late. I drove to my apartment, packed my bags, and caught the night train from Charing Cross. The journey was unbearable. I thought I would never arrive. As soon as I did, I drove to the Hôtel l’Angleterre. They told me Erskine had been buried two days earlier in the English cemetery. There was something horribly absurd about the whole tragedy. I said all sorts of frantic things, and the people in the lobby stared at me with curiosity.

Suddenly, Lady Erskine, dressed in deep mourning, walked across the lobby. When she saw me, she approached, murmured something about her poor son, and burst into tears. I led her into her sitting room. An elderly gentleman was waiting for her there. It was the English doctor.

We talked a great deal about Erskine, but I said nothing about his motive for committing suicide. It was clear that he hadn’t told his mother anything about the reason that had driven him to such a fatal, insane act. Finally, Lady Erskine stood up and said, “George left you something as a keepsake. It was something he valued very much. I will go get it for you.”

As soon as she left the room, I turned to the doctor and said, “What a terrible shock this must have been for Lady Erskine! I’m surprised she is holding up as well as she is.”

“Oh, she knew for months that it was coming,” he replied.

“Knew for months!” I exclaimed. “But why didn’t she stop him? Why didn’t she have him watched? He must have been out of his mind.”

The doctor stared at me. “I don’t know what you mean,” he said.

“Well,” I cried, “if a mother knows that her son is going to commit suicide—”

“Suicide!” he answered. “Poor Erskine didn’t commit suicide. He died of tuberculosis. He came here to die. The moment I saw him, I knew there was no hope. One lung was almost gone, and the other was severely affected. Three days before he died, he asked me if there was any hope. I told him honestly that there wasn’t, and that he had only a few days to live. He wrote some letters and was quite resigned, remaining clear-headed until the end.”

At that moment, Lady Erskine entered the room with the fateful picture of Willie Hughes in her hand. “When George was dying, he begged me to give you this,” she said. As I took it from her, her tears fell onto my hand.

The picture hangs now in my library, where it is highly admired by my artistic friends. They have decided it is not a Clouet, but an Oudry. I have never cared to tell them its true history. But sometimes, when I look at it, I think there is actually a great deal to be said for the Willie Hughes theory of Shakespeare’s Sonnets."
}
